Twenty-one new tests cover the rejection paths (`abs(-5)`, `__import__`, `'hello'`, `(1).bit_length`, `[1,2][0]`) and the happy paths (precedence, decimals, modulo, power, IEEE-754 honesty), and the `PGOV.TOOL_CALL_ALLOWLIST` and the AO system prompt are both updated to teach the model the new tags and when to pick each tool.

**`/ls` command** (USE-CASE-002 visibility). The TUI gained `/ls` — type it in any chat and BlarAI prints the files currently in `userdata/` that `/load` will accept, with their sizes in KB. Pure host-side operation; never reaches the model. The gateway grew a `list_userdata_files()` method that does the enumeration: skip subdirectories, skip unsupported extensions, sort case-insensitive, return `[{filename, size_bytes, size_kb}, ...]`. Five new gateway tests cover empty/missing directory, extension filtering, subdirectory skipping, size accuracy, and the alphabetical-case-insensitive sort.

**End-to-end PDF integration test** (USE-CASE-003 + USE-CASE-002 + USE-CASE-004 verification). The PDF support that landed in the previous entry was exercised by unit tests per layer — pypdf-extraction, AO grounded-context wiring, gateway document-staging — but nothing exercised the *combination*. The new `tests/integration/test_pdf_load_e2e.py` synthesises a real PDF via `pypdf.PdfWriter` in a temp `userdata/`, calls `load_document()` to extract the text, encodes a real `PROMPT_REQUEST` carrying the document, runs it through `AssistantOrchestratorService._handle_connection`, and asserts the AO's `ContextManager` has exactly one grounded chunk wrapped in the spotlighting delimiters with a `<|DOC-XXXXXXXX|>` datamarking prefix and the PDF's actual text inside (*"Hemoglobin"*, *"J. Doe"*). This is the test that catches silent regressions in any of those five layers — pypdf install, extension routing, extraction guards, IPC field plumbing, AO grounded-chunk wiring — and proves they compose. It also satisfies Blair's instruction to generate test PDFs autonomously rather than waiting for him to drop real files in.

**Paste support** (USE-CASE-004 UX, a journal `Next:` hint open for three sessions now). The Textual `Input` widget has a built-in paste action, but in some terminals Ctrl+V is intercepted by the terminal itself and Textual never sees the keystroke. So `pyperclip` is now installed (~30 KB, pure Python, OS-clipboard wrapper), and `BlarAIApp.BINDINGS` gains an explicit `Binding(KEY_PASTE, "paste_clipboard", "Paste", show=False, priority=True)`. The new `action_paste_clipboard` reads the OS clipboard, caps the text at `PROMPT_MAX_CHARS` so a huge paste cannot lock the UI, and uses `Input.insert_text_at_cursor` to drop it at the cursor. Failure paths — pyperclip not installed, clipboard-read raises — surface as red lines in the response area rather than crashing the TUI. Five new tests cover the happy path, empty-clipboard silent no-op, oversize truncation, clipboard-read-error surfacing, and the "pyperclip missing" install-hint path. The existing `test_bindings_registered` count moved 5 → 6.

A pattern in this push is worth naming. None of these four changes needed a feature branch. Each one is bounded — small in lines, focused in surface, fully tested before commit — and each adds a single visible capability to a system Blair is supposed to be using daily. Layer 3 was a security architecture that grew until it was bigger than the product it was protecting; *this* is what the "smallest thing that genuinely runs, grown one observed need at a time" working principle from the top of this file actually looks like, repeated four times in a row. The autonomous-shipping run is the apology for the previous arc and the answer to *develop BlarAI for me now*. Each of the four is real, real-shipped-on-main, and ready to use on the next BlarAI restart. *(commit `<this>`: 4 tools + args support + 21 tests; `/ls` + 5 tests; PDF integration test; paste support + pyperclip dep + 5 tests; pgov tool allowlist + AO system prompt + bindings-count test updates.)*

**Next:** what Blair *finds frustrating* on the next 24 hours of daily use is the right input for the next push. The candidates I had not yet shipped from previous journal hints are: raising the PDF size cap if real PDFs don't fit; a Whisper STT + Piper TTS voice slice now that memory headroom exists; a richer file-handling tool (`open_file` reading from a wider directory than `userdata/`); web fetch with allowlist for one specific scrape use case. The fastest sequence on the runway: drop more PDFs in `userdata/`, type `/ls` to see them, `/load` one, ask BlarAI to summarise it, and tell me what gap shows up — that's the next thing.

### 2026-06-02 — A bigger swing, on purpose: the native UI mission begins (Phase 1)

Every entry above this one is the "smallest thing that genuinely runs" principle held tight — and it worked: BlarAI went from rough to genuinely usable in the terminal. Blair's next instruction is deliberately larger. He wants the daily driver to stop being a terminal and become a *native Windows app shaped like Google Gemini* — a chat surface where you talk and attach files (PDFs, photos, screenshots, eventually video), not a command line you memorise. And he wants the four foundational use cases — the Policy Agent, the Substrate (real semantic memory), the Cleaner, the Orchestrator — extended underneath it, not just re-skinned. This is a multi-phase mission, the first time this project has taken on something that can't ship in one afternoon increment. The risk I named back to him at the gate is exactly the Layer-3 risk in new clothes: a native UI is the kind of thing that grows past the product it serves. The defence is the phase plan — each phase ends green and committed, and the screen is still the oracle.

The first decision is load-bearing and it is *not* about pixels. A WinUI 3 app is C# on Microsoft's native stack; everything BlarAI already is — the gateway, the session store, the output validator, the model orchestration — is Python. So the new front end is a separate process in a different language, and the question that matters is how the two talk. The obvious answers — a little localhost web server, gRPC — both stand up a network socket, and this project's whole privacy spine is "no listening network port." So the choice is a **named pipe**: a Windows kernel object, not a socket. No port, no DNS, nothing a misconfigured firewall rule or another local process can reach — and the server rejects any remote pipe client outright. It carries length-prefixed JSON, framed exactly like the existing vsock transport so the two read the same. That decision is written down as its own architecture record (ADR-014), because the *reasoning* — why not HTTP, why a pipe — is the portfolio-worthy part, not the code.

Phase 1 built the bridge with no UI on top of it yet, which is the honest way to de-risk: prove the plumbing before pouring the concrete. A thin Python backend hosts the existing gateway and session store behind the pipe and speaks a small remote-call protocol — list sessions, load a document, and one streaming `prompt` call that runs a whole conversational turn (send, stream the reply token by token, resolve the validator verdict, persist the answer) and emits it as a sequence of frames. That streaming call is deliberately the *single* place the chat turn is orchestrated, so when the WinUI surface arrives it stays a thin viewer instead of growing its own copy of the logic that could drift from the terminal's — the "architecture-vs-code drift" this journal has a name for, designed out instead of patched later.

The other half of Phase 1 is the multimodal groundwork. Photos and videos are the lodestar of where this is going even though BlarAI cannot *see* yet — vision is the next push, not this one. So the document loader learned image and video file types as **store-only**: it never reads the pixels, it records that an attachment exists and grounds a short note telling the model "an image is here; you can't see it yet; say so if asked." The point of doing it now is that the day a vision model lands, the *only* thing that changes is that grounded note — the load path, the eventual on-screen attachment chip, and the user's behaviour are all already shaped for it. A file picker path (`store_attachment`) copies a chosen file from anywhere on disk into the one folder BlarAI reads from, with collision-safe renaming, so the eventual UI button is thin.

The verification here is the kind I can do without Blair: the named-pipe round-trip runs headless on this machine — start the backend with a no-GPU echo stand-in, connect a client, create a session, stream a reply back, read the persisted turn — and it passes, which proves the transport works on *this* Windows, the genuinely-new unknown. The full suite is **1,364 passed, 2 skipped**, up exactly the 52 tests this phase added from the 1,312 baseline, zero regressions. What Phase 1 does *not* prove is anything on a screen, because there is no screen yet — that proof starts in Phase 2 when the WinUI app connects to this pipe. Which is the right place for the "show me it running" rule to re-enter: the backend is verified now, the GUI is verified by Blair as each phase lands. *(commit `<this>`: ADR-014 + `services/ui_backend/` named-pipe daemon + photo/video store-only in document_loader + 52 tests; full sweep 1,364 passed / 2 skipped.)*

**Next:** Phase 2 — scaffold the WinUI 3 app itself: a chat-centric, Gemini-shaped layout (narrow session sidebar, wide chat, a prominent input with an attachment button), launchable from the Start menu, connecting to this pipe and listing the real sessions from the database. Per the agreed checkpoint, I post Blair a one-paragraph description of the exact layout — sidebar width, chat arrangement, where the attachment button sits, the colour direction — *before* wiring it, so a wrong turn on the aesthetic is cheap to correct. After that layout is locked, Phases 3–7 ship without checkpoints to the close.

### 2026-06-02 — Phase 2: the window exists, and it shows real chats

Phase 1 was plumbing with nothing on top. Phase 2 puts the app there. There is now a real WinUI 3 program — `services/ui_winui/` — that builds clean and opens a window shaped like Gemini: a narrow sidebar of past conversations on the left, a wide centered chat column, and a rounded composer "pill" at the bottom with the attachment, screenshot and screen-record buttons sitting where Blair will expect them (stubbed this phase). Mica backdrop, light and dark, a blue accent that matches the rest of the project. The look is the point — Blair's instruction was that his friends should say "wow, cool," and a terminal does not earn that; a native Fluent window with proper typography and soft surfaces can.

Two things from this phase are worth keeping, both about *not* fighting the framework. First, there were no `dotnet new` WinUI templates on this machine, so the project is hand-authored — the `.csproj`, the manifest, `App.xaml`, the window. That turned out to be the *reliable* path, not the hard one: every file is something I understand rather than a generated black box, and the first build (an empty shell) compiled and produced a runnable executable in under a minute, which de-risked the whole "can C# even build headlessly here" question before a single control was placed. Second, the real build broke twice on the same underlying truth, and the fixes are the kind worth writing down because they will recur. A WinUI `Window` is **not** a `FrameworkElement`, and two common conveniences quietly assume it is: a data-bound value converter inside a list-item template needs a `FrameworkElement` to resolve the converter against, and the binding for it failed to compile against a window root. The fix was not to bolt on a workaround but to remove the need — the view-model exposes plain `Visibility` properties the template binds directly, no converter, which is cleaner anyway. The other break was subtler: the sidebar's session type was a C# *record*, and the XAML tooling generates code that tries to *set* a record's init-only properties, which the compiler refuses. Making it an ordinary class with read-only properties dissolved seven errors at once. Both are the same lesson in different costume — the generated layer has assumptions, and the cheapest fix is to stop tripping them, not to paper over the trip.

The verification is the honest split this mission runs on. I cannot open a window and look at it — a GUI does not render on a headless build server — so what I *can* prove, I proved: the app compiles with zero errors, and the backend path it will run against serves Blair's **real** twenty-three saved conversations over the pipe (checked by driving the dispatcher against the actual `sessions.db` and watching them come back, newest first). What I *cannot* prove from here — that the window looks right, that streaming feels alive, that dark mode is comfortable — is exactly what Blair verifies live, which is why the layout was agreed in one paragraph before any of it was wired. To make that live-check possible without the heavy model running, the backend grew a `--no-model` mode: it serves the real session database with echo replies, so the window shows true history and a typed message streams a placeholder answer back — enough to judge the *feel* of the thing before the GPU is in the loop. The real model arrives behind the same pipe in Phase 3. *(commits `ef3f245` scaffold, `<this>` layout + C# pipe client + sessions sidebar + streaming composer + `--no-model` backend; WinUI build clean; Python sweep unchanged at 1,364 / 2.)*

**Next:** Phase 3 — wire the real model behind the pipe (the launcher hosting the production gateway in place of the TUI), upgrade the assistant render from plain text to live Gemini-style **markdown** (headings, lists, code blocks), and bring the "Response held by the output validator" card to true parity with the terminal's policy-denial panel. Streaming stays the felt centrepiece — it is the thing Blair singled out.

### 2026-06-02 — Phase 3: a real model behind the window, and a flag that survives elevation

This is the phase where the new window stops being a demo. Three things landed, and the smallest of them taught the lesson worth keeping.

The first is the one that makes it *real*: the launcher can now host the WinUI surface instead of the terminal. The whole heavy startup — Hyper-V VM, the single 14-billion-parameter model compiled once on the GPU, the Policy Agent, the Orchestrator, the gateway, the handshake — is identical to the terminal path; only the very last step differs. Where the launcher used to construct the Textual app and call `app.run()`, a new branch starts the named-pipe backend on a daemon thread, launches the WinUI executable as a subprocess, and waits on it; when the window closes, the pipe server stops and the existing cleanup tears the VM and services down. This is deliberately the *minimum* incision into a file the brief flagged as load-bearing — the model build, the shared pipeline, the boot gates are all untouched; the default with no flag is still the terminal, byte-for-byte. The autonomy bound here was "don't touch model code," and the way to honour it was to add a surface branch at the one point where the surface is actually chosen, and nowhere else.

The second is the one Blair will *feel*: assistant replies are no longer flat text. A small hand-rolled markdown renderer turns the model's output into real prose — headings, bold and italic, bullet and numbered lists, links, and fenced code blocks rendered in a monospace card with a copy button. It re-parses on every streamed token, so the formatting assembles live as the answer arrives rather than snapping into shape at the end. I chose to write the renderer rather than take a dependency, and that was a real fork: the obvious move is a community markdown control, but the two package names I expected were not on NuGet, the maintained one's identity was uncertain, and a runtime dependency I cannot pin and verify cuts against this project's whole minimal-surface posture. Hand-rolling is more code, but it is code I understand and control, it has no supply-chain question attached, and it renders exactly the way the rest of the window looks — the same reasoning that made hand-authoring the project files the *reliable* path in Phase 2, not the hard one. The honest limit, written down rather than hidden: it covers the markdown the assistant actually emits, not the full CommonMark grammar, and an unclosed code fence mid-stream renders as an in-progress code block rather than guessing.

The third is the small one that became the lesson. To choose the WinUI surface I first wired an environment variable — `BLARAI_UI=winui` — because that is the obvious switch. Then I went to write Blair a one-double-click launcher and stopped, because the launcher self-elevates: it relaunches itself through a UAC prompt to get the Administrator rights Hyper-V needs. A Windows elevation does not carry the un-elevated shell's environment across — the elevated process starts fresh — so the variable would simply not be there on the other side, and the surface would silently fall back to the terminal. The thing that makes this worth a top-of-file lesson is *how it would have failed*: not loudly, and not anywhere a test of mine could see it. I cannot run the elevated, GPU-bound launcher on a headless machine, so every check I am able to perform would have passed while the real path quietly did the wrong thing — the screen-is-the-only-oracle trap, one layer up. I read `request_elevation` instead of trusting my mental model of it, saw that it forwards `sys.argv` but not the environment, and carried the choice as a `--winui` flag, which rides across the boundary the variable could not. The general rule is in the lessons list now (#11), and it is security-shaped: a value that must survive a trust boundary has to travel by a channel defined to cross it.

What I can prove from here, I proved: the launcher compiles and its sixty tests pass with the new branch; the WinUI app builds clean with the markdown renderer; the full suite holds at **1,364 passed, 2 skipped**. What I cannot prove from here is the thing that matters most — a real model answering in a real window — because that needs the GPU, the VM, and an Administrator token a headless build does not have. That is Blair's live-verify, and it is the first time the new window shows a genuine answer rather than an echo. *(commits `<this>`: launcher WinUI surface branch + `--winui`/`BLARAI_UI` selection + hand-rolled `MarkdownBlock` streaming renderer + PGOV denial card at parity + two demo launchers; launcher 60 tests green, full sweep 1,364 / 2; real-model render awaits live verify via `scripts/run_winui_real.bat`.)*

**Next:** Phase 4 — make attachments first-class in the window: the paperclip and drag-and-drop into the chat, with each attached PDF, photo or video appearing as an inline chip where it was dropped; the screenshot and screen-record buttons wired to capture-and-store (vision still deferred, so the reply stays "I can't see it yet"); and the slash commands kept working as a quiet power-user backdoor, never surfaced in the greeting. The store-only plumbing from Phase 1 is already shaped for exactly this.

### 2026-06-02 — The same privilege boundary, the other thing that doesn't cross it

Lesson #11 had just been written — *a value that must survive a privilege boundary needs a channel defined to cross it* — when Blair tried the real-model launch and the screen caught the boundary biting again, in a place I had not looked. He double-clicked the launcher, approved the UAC prompt, and the window exited with a clean "current process exiting" handoff line. It looked like a crash; it was half of one. The launcher self-elevates by re-running itself through Windows' "runas" verb, and the un-elevated process exiting after it fires that relaunch is correct. What was wrong was the elevated process it spawned: the relaunch invokes the launcher as a *bare script file* (`python …\launcher\__main__.py`), and a bare script puts its own directory on the import path, not the repo root — so in the elevated process `import launcher` and `import services` no longer resolve. The elevated window crashed on import and vanished before Blair could read it. The un-elevated half exited cleanly, so the only thing on screen said "exiting," which reads as failure.

This is the same boundary as #11 wearing a different coat. The environment variable did not cross the elevation; this time it was the *package import context* that did not cross it. Both are the un-elevated process assuming the elevated one inherits its world, and it does not — a UAC relaunch starts clean, with a fresh environment, a default working directory, and only the literal argv it was handed. The fix matches the lesson: stop depending on anything implicit surviving the crossing. The launcher script now becomes Administrator *first* — it elevates itself, and the elevated copy runs `python -m launcher --winui` from the repo root, where `-m` resolves imports the way a bare script never will and `is_admin()` is already true, so the fragile internal relaunch is never taken. The thing worth keeping is that I could not have caught this with any test I am able to run: the elevated, GPU-bound path only exists on Blair's hardware behind a UAC prompt, so the live screen was, again, the only oracle that could see it. *(commit `<this>`: self-elevating `run_winui_real.bat`; root cause is the elevation relaunch losing package context, the import-side twin of lesson #11's env-var loss.)*

### 2026-06-02 — Phase 4: attachments you can see, and a model that still can't

This is the phase that makes the window feel like Gemini rather than a chat box. You can now hand BlarAI a file four ways — the paperclip opens a picker, you can drag a file straight onto the chat, a screenshot button grabs the screen, and the slash backdoor still types `/load`. Whatever you attach shows up as an inline chip where you added it: a real thumbnail for an image, a file icon and name for a document, a clip icon for video. The chips sit above your message bubble the way Gemini stacks them, and the screenshot button genuinely captures the primary screen — it hides BlarAI for a quarter-second so the shot is the desktop behind it, drops the PNG into `userdata/`, and pins it as a chip.

And the model still cannot see any of it. That is the whole point, and the part worth keeping. Every one of those paths ends at the same store-only seam from Phase 1: the file is copied into `userdata/`, and what reaches the model is a sentence saying an image or video is present and it cannot read the contents yet. The experience is fully multimodal; the *capability* is not, and the two were built on purpose to come apart cleanly. When a vision model lands, not one control in this phase changes — the picker, the drop target, the chip, the thumbnail, the screenshot button all stay exactly as they are; the only thing that changes is the backend turning that placeholder sentence into a real description. I wrote this conviction up as lesson #12, because it is the governance shape Blair's cert track rewards: you decide a feature's envelope — where it plugs in, what it may touch, how it is invoked — while it is still inert, so switching it on later is one auditable change rather than a scramble. The screenshot is the sharpest example: the button that captures pixels exists *before* anything is allowed to interpret pixels, which means the privacy question ("BlarAI can now grab your screen") gets asked and answered now, in the open, not silently bundled with the vision launch.

Two smaller calls are worth recording because each was a fork. The slash commands — `/ls`, `/load`, `/unload`, `/rename`, `/trust` — still work, with a soft autocomplete that appears the moment you type `/`, but they are deliberately *not* surfaced anywhere in the greeting or the empty state. Blair was explicit that this is a conversational assistant, not a command line; the power is there for whoever wants it and invisible to everyone else. That is a real product-governance decision — keep the expert affordance, refuse to make it the front door — and it is the opposite of how the Textual terminal foregrounded the same commands. The other fork was the little "×" that removes a pending attachment before you send. The backend stages attachments as a per-session list with no "unstage one" operation, so a removed chip would still have ridden along on the next message — the screen saying one thing while the wire did another, which is precisely the failure this journal keeps cataloguing. Rather than ship that gap I made remove re-synchronise: it clears the staged set and re-stages exactly the chips that remain, so what you see in the composer is what the model receives. The cost is named — that re-sync also clears documents grounded earlier in the session — and accepted, because it only bites in an edge case (removing an attachment in a chat that already had documents loaded) and the alternative is a lie on the screen.

What I can prove from a headless machine, I proved: the whole surface compiles with zero errors, and Phase 4 is C#-only so the Python suite is untouched at **1,364 passed, 2 skipped**. What I cannot prove — that the thumbnail renders, that the drop target lights up, that the screenshot actually captures the desktop and not a black rectangle — is Blair's to confirm, and it is a lot of moving surface for one live pass, so the next message to him says exactly what to click and what each thing should do. *(commits `<this>`: attach picker + drag-drop + inline chips with thumbnails + screenshot capture-and-store + slash backdoor with soft autocomplete; `System.Drawing.Common` for the screen grab; WinUI build clean, Python sweep 1,364 / 2.)*

**Next:** Phase 5 — the Substrate (USE-CASE-002), the real one: a semantic memory that survives restarts. Reuse the bge-small embedding model the output validator already loads, index every loaded document and every approved conversation turn into an on-disk vector store (hnswlib + SQLite), and retrieve the most relevant pieces into the grounded context on each new prompt — so "what did I tell you last week about my sister?" works because the past turn is actually retrievable, not because it happened to still be in the window. The retrieved history is treated as untrusted text and runs the same injection defences a freshly-loaded document does. Isochronous-timing hardening from the spec is explicitly deferred to the multi-VM future and noted, not built — no repeat of the Layer 3 over-build.

### 2026-06-02 — A feature committed without its other half

Before Phase 5 could touch the Orchestrator's prompt loop, I had to look at why that file was already modified in the working tree — a change I did not make, sitting uncommitted since before this session began. Reading it was the right instinct: it was not stray cruft, it was the missing half of a shipped feature. The previous "four small things" run added multi-argument tool calls — `calculate(2*(3+4))` — by changing `parse_tool_call` to return a `(name, args)` pair and `execute` to take the args. But only `tools.py` and the policy allowlist were committed; the four-line edit that teaches the Orchestrator's tool loop to *unpack* that pair never got staged. So at the committed HEAD the loop still does `tool_name = parse_tool_call(...)`, receives a tuple, never sees `None`, and hands the whole tuple to `execute`, which rejects it — every tool call silently fails at HEAD. The working tree was fine, because the fixing edit lived there uncommitted, and so every test sweep ran green *against the working tree* and never reported that the committed code was broken. It is the test-as-oracle trap at the level of version control: a green suite proves the tree you ran it on, not the commit you shipped. The fix was already written; my part was to recognise it as load-bearing rather than exclude it as noise, confirm with Blair, and commit it on its own so the Orchestrator and its tools agree at HEAD before I build anything new on top. *(commit `<this>`: the tool-args unpack fix in the Orchestrator loop, repairing tool execution at HEAD; sweep stays 1,364 / 2 because the tree already had it.)*

### 2026-06-02 — The privilege boundary, a third time: elevation versus the desktop

Blair tried the Phase 4 attachments on the real hardware and gave me the split result only a live screen can: the screenshot button worked, captured his desktop, dropped the PNG into `userdata/`, and `/ls` listed it — and when he asked BlarAI to analyse it, the model correctly said it cannot see images yet, which is the store-only seam behaving exactly as designed, not a fault. But the paperclip did nothing, and dragging a file from Explorer showed the "not allowed" cursor. Both have one root cause, and it is the same boundary lesson #11 named, now wearing its third face. BlarAI runs **elevated**, because the launcher needs Administrator rights for Hyper-V — and Windows treats an elevated window as a higher-integrity object that a normal-integrity Explorer is forbidden to hand things to. That is User Interface Privilege Isolation: the medium-integrity drag source cannot drop onto the high-integrity window, and the modern WinRT file picker misbehaves in the elevated process. The first two times this boundary bit, it was an environment variable and then a package-import context that failed to cross a UAC elevation; this time it is the desktop's drag-and-drop and file-dialog plumbing failing to cross an integrity level. Same shape, three mechanisms.

The fixes follow the rule rather than fight it. For drag-and-drop, Windows provides exactly one sanctioned way to let lower-integrity input reach an elevated window — `ChangeWindowMessageFilterEx`, allowing the specific drop messages (`WM_DROPFILES`, `WM_COPYDATA`, `WM_COPYGLOBALDATA`) through the UIPI filter — so the drop is carried by a channel *defined* to cross the boundary instead of one that happens to work only within an integrity level. For the picker, I retired the WinRT `FileOpenPicker`, which is the component that does not survive elevation, in favour of the classic Win32 common dialog (`GetOpenFileNameW`), which has worked across integrity levels since long before this isolation existed. The broader thing worth recording for the governance file: elevating a process for *one* capability silently taxes *every* unrelated desktop integration it touches — a real, often-overlooked cost of "just run it as admin," and an argument for eventually running the window unelevated and letting only the Hyper-V launcher hold the token. I noted that as the proper long-term shape and did not build it now; the two targeted fixes restore the feature without the larger refactor. One more thing the live screen taught: a build can fail for a reason that is not the code at all — my rebuild errored because Blair's app was still running and holding the executable, a file lock, not a compile error. So the demo launcher now rebuilds itself on every run (closing any stale window first), which means Blair always runs the latest code from one double-click and I stop shipping him a binary a commit behind. *(commit `<this>`: UIPI drop-message filter + Win32 file dialog replacing the WinRT picker + self-building `run_winui_real.bat`; WinUI compiles clean to a fresh output dir, Python untouched at 1,364 / 2; drag-drop + paperclip await Blair's re-verify.)*

### 2026-06-02 — Phase 5: BlarAI remembers now

Until this phase BlarAI had no memory beyond the live context window. Close it, reopen it, and last week's conversation was gone; a document had to be re-loaded every session because nothing it learned survived a restart. This phase builds the first real slice of the Substrate — USE-CASE-002 — and the test of it is the question Blair named: *"what did I tell you last week about my sister?"* should work, not because the turn happened to still be in the window, but because it was actually stored and is actually retrievable. It is now: every loaded document is chunked and embedded into an on-disk vector store; every conversation turn the output validator approves is embedded and stored; and on each new prompt the most relevant pieces of that accumulated memory — a couple of document chunks, a few past turns — are pulled back by meaning and folded into the context the model reasons over. The store is a side SQLite file beside the session database, so memory is cleanly separable from the conversation log.

Three decisions in here are the ones worth the portfolio. The first is the one I keep returning to as the most important: **retrieved memory is treated as untrusted text.** It would be easy, and wrong, to wave the user's own past data through as trusted because it came from "us." A document loaded a week ago might have carried an injection no one noticed; a past turn might quote something hostile the user pasted in. So every retrieved chunk goes through the *same* Layer 1+2 defences a freshly-loaded document gets — the forged-delimiter neutralisation and the per-load datamarking from the Layer 3 work — and is grounded as data, never as instruction. I wrote that conviction up as lesson #13, because the principle is bigger than this feature: provenance is not trust, and a system that assumes "from our own database, therefore clean" launders yesterday's unnoticed injection into today's trusted instruction. For a memory system that will accumulate for years, getting that posture right at the start matters more than any retrieval-quality tuning.

The second decision was forced by reality and is the better for it. The plan named hnswlib for the vector index; it ships no wheel for this Python and would not build without a C++ toolchain. Rather than drag in a compiler and a heavyweight dependency, I looked at the actual scale — one person's accumulated documents and conversations, thousands of 384-dimensional vectors — and a brute-force cosine search is a single numpy matrix multiply that finishes in well under a millisecond at that size. HNSW earns its complexity at a hundred thousand vectors and up; here it would have been machinery for a scale that does not exist, the same over-build instinct the Layer 3 arc already taught against. So the MVP is brute-force, kept behind a private method so an approximate index can replace it unchanged the day the corpus genuinely outgrows it, and the deviation is written down in its own decision record (ADR-016) rather than left as a silent shortcut. The same record names what I deliberately did *not* build: the spec's isochronous-timing side-channel defence, which protects against a compromised agent reading retrieval latency across an inter-VM boundary — a threat that does not exist when retrieval is an in-process function call. Building it now would be defending a boundary that is not there.

The third decision is that the embedding model is **reused, not added.** The output validator already loads a bge-small sentence-transformer for its leakage check; the Substrate takes that same loaded model by injection rather than standing up a second copy — one model serving routing, leakage detection, and now memory. And the whole subsystem is wired to fail soft: if the embedder cannot load, memory simply switches off and the assistant answers exactly as it did before, because remembering is a thing BlarAI should *gain*, never a thing whose absence can break a reply. The integration into the Orchestrator's prompt loop touches a load-bearing file, so every substrate call is guarded and wrapped — a memory error logs and is swallowed, never propagates into generation.

The store is exhaustively tested headless: eighteen tests over chunking, ingest, brute-force retrieval, the document-versus-turn split, current-session exclusion, dedup-on-reingest, and survival across a close-and-reopen — using a deterministic stand-in embedder so the tests prove the *machinery* without the model. The full Orchestrator suite stays green at 379, and the whole repo holds. But the store passing its tests is not the same as memory *working*, and the proof Blair asked for is the FUT-07 shape exactly: load documents across two different sessions, close BlarAI, reopen it, and ask something that needs information from a document loaded in the earlier session — and have it answer using it. That is the live verify, and it is the first time this assistant can be tested for something it could not do at all yesterday. *(commits `<this>`: `substrate.py` store + 18 tests + ADR-016 + Orchestrator wiring [doc ingest, turn ingest on PGOV-approve, retrieval grounded through Layer 1+2, bge-small embedder reuse]; AO suite 379, full sweep green; cross-session recall awaits Blair's close-reopen verify.)*

**Next:** Phase 6 — the smaller, concrete things that make it a daily driver: confirm the four tools (time, date, day, calculator) fire from natural questions through the new window now that the tool-args fix is in; make the dark/light choice persist across restarts rather than resetting each launch; and verify the whole persistence story end to end — sessions, loaded documents, and now retrievable memory all surviving a close and reopen.

### 2026-06-02 — Phase 6: the theme remembers, and the settings surface is born

A small phase with a forward purpose. The dark/light toggle worked, but the choice evaporated on every restart — the kind of papercut that quietly says "this is a prototype." Now it persists: a tiny JSON preference file in `%LOCALAPPDATA%\BlarAI` holds the theme, written on every toggle and read at launch before the first frame, so BlarAI opens the way you left it. The four tools (clock, date, day, calculator) needed no new code — the HEAD tool-args fix already made them fire — so that half of the phase is Blair's to confirm by simply asking "what's 19 times 23?" in the window. The reason this small phase matters beyond the papercut is that it stands up the first **persisted-settings surface** — a real place for user preferences to live — and that is exactly the home the next, much larger piece needs: a voice-output toggle and a voice picker. *(commit `<this>`: `UserPrefs` JSON preference store + theme persisted across restart; WinUI builds clean, Python untouched at 1,382 / 2.)*

**Next:** voice — a deliberately large addition the User-Operator asked for after Phase 5: speak to BlarAI and have it speak back (Whisper-small STT + Kokoro-82M TTS as GPU sidecars, audio streamed over the same named pipe, mic button + voice toggle + per-message play). It is the visible "wow," and it deserves a fresh context budget to do carefully rather than a rushed tail end of a very long session — so it is handed off, with the full architecture and integration map, to a successor session (`winui-handoff-brief.md`). The Substrate, which the redirect suggested might come after voice, is already shipped (Phase 5); voice slots in as the next phase from here.

### 2026-06-02 — When memory becomes noise: don't retrieve over a fresh attachment

The first live use of the Substrate taught a lesson the unit tests could not, because the tests had no notion of *intent*. Blair attached a PDF and typed "Summarize this for me," and got back a wall of the model arguing with itself about which document "this" referred to — the fresh PDF, an old contacts file, or the conversation so far — before finally asking him to clarify. The Substrate was working exactly as built and that was the problem: it retrieved the most semantically relevant memory for the word "summarize," which pulled in older notes and past turns, and dropped them into context alongside the PDF the user had *just that second* attached. Three plausible referents for "this," so the model did the honest thing and got confused. Memory that is always on becomes noise the moment the user's intent is local. The fix is a single intent-reading guard: if a document was loaded *this turn*, skip retrieval entirely, because "this" then unambiguously means the thing just attached — pulling in memory can only muddy it. Retrieval still fires on every ordinary turn, which is where cross-session recall lives and where it already works (the same session proved "what was my sister's name" answers correctly across a restart). The broader lesson, worth keeping near lesson #13: a retrieval system has to respect the difference between "help me with what I just gave you" and "help me using everything you know" — and the freshest signal in the turn, a just-loaded document, is the clearest statement of which mode the user is in. (The same reply also showed a sliver of the model's reasoning leaking past the thinking-strip in the long-ramble case — a separate, pre-existing edge documented for the successor, not introduced here.) *(commit `<this>`: skip Substrate retrieval on turns that load a document; AO suite 379, full sweep green.)*

### 2026-06-02 — Making the journal discipline standing instruction

This entry is the rule applying to its own creation. Blair asked, again, whether the agents now and to come would automatically write the kind of journal entries his portfolio and the IAPP AIGP cert track actually need — failures kept in, trade-offs named, lessons distilled, narrative-prose-first-person, mature-not-minimal, all the shape he and I have refined across the last few weeks of entries. The right answer was not to remember it harder; it was to make it standing instruction for every agent that opens this repo. So the discipline is now codified as a new top-level section in `CLAUDE.md` titled *Build-Journal Discipline (NON-NEGOTIABLE)* — placed immediately under Project Identity, above Architecture Summary, because it governs *how* the work is communicated and that decision is more foundational than the architectural facts that surround it.

The codified section spells out the four things that compound: **when** an entry is required (anything substantive — feature, bug-with-judgment, ADR, governance flag, trade-off, failure-worth-keeping) versus when it is not (pure mechanical commits); **form** (dated section header that names the *lesson*, not the change; narrative prose first-person; mature-not-minimal; ends with `**Next:**`; commit refs in italic parens at the close); **content** (failures stay in, specific numbers, trade-offs named with the path-not-taken visible, lessons distilled at top of file as the compounding surface); and the anti-patterns that get the cert track to flinch (vague entries, changelog bullets, sanitised failure paths, `<SHA>` placeholders, *"writing it later"*). The lessons-list at the top of `BUILD_JOURNAL.md` is named explicitly as the canonical voice of the project's judgment — read those items before writing a new entry; add a new item there when the work earns one.

The honest framing this needs because it would be dishonest to leave it out. The discipline is largely the one that's been carried in working memory across sessions, paid for by Layer 3 going off the rails and by a near-quit. It worked while it stayed in the head of whichever agent was holding the keyboard; it stopped working the moment a fresh session opened without it. The codification is the apology for assuming continuity that the file system did not actually provide. Every Claude Code session that opens this repo from now on reads `CLAUDE.md` at startup and inherits the rule before it touches a single line of product code. The new WinUI agent currently in mid-push gets a one-paragraph paste-in confirming the rule because it loaded `CLAUDE.md` before this edit landed and will not auto-reload mid-session — and, gratifyingly, it has been writing entries in the right shape already, having absorbed the discipline from the working state of the journal itself when it read the substrate at session start.

The meta-lesson, since the rule asks for one, is the small sharp shape of how project culture survives across instances. **Continuity of intent across agents is a file-system property, not a memory property.** If you want the next session to do the thing this one learned, you write it down where the next session will read it — not where you will remember it. The Layer-3 over-build was, at root, the same failure mode at a different layer: a rule held in one place that should have been held in two. *(commit `<this>`: `CLAUDE.md` §Build-Journal Discipline + this entry; CLAUDE.md sweep unaffected — docs-only change.)*

**Next:** the WinUI Phase 3 wire-up the new agent was already pointing at. The journal discipline is now standing instruction; future entries land without anyone having to remember to ask.

### 2026-06-02 — Voice, the backend half: measuring the terrain before laying track

The User-Operator wants to speak to BlarAI and hear it speak back, and a generous handoff brief laid out exactly how: Whisper-small for hearing, Kokoro-82M for speaking, both riding the named pipe that already carries the chat. The brief was good enough that the wrong move would have been to trust it. Blair said as much — I had leaned on it as a map without confirming the terrain was passable — so before writing the architecture decision, I ran the three load-bearing unknowns on the real Arc 140V. The results rewrote two of the brief's premises and surfaced a hazard it never mentioned, which is exactly what a probe is for.

`WhisperPipeline` was real and fast: a converted Whisper-small loaded on the GPU and transcribed a 2.7-second clip in 0.53s with an exact round-trip — I generated the test speech with Kokoro and fed it back into Whisper, so the two halves validated each other in one shot. Kokoro was the first surprise: the brief assumed it would run on the Arc GPU, but this venv's onnxruntime carries only CPU and Azure execution providers — no DirectML, no OpenVINO provider — so Kokoro synthesizes on the CPU. The honest thing was to measure rather than panic, and the measurement was reassuring: real-time-factor 0.30, three-and-a-bit times faster than playback, with a 1.4s first-chunk on streamed sentences. The brief was wrong about the *mechanism* (GPU) and right about the *outcome* (fast enough), and the ADR now says so plainly, with the GPU-provider swap deferred behind an `ONNX_PROVIDER` override rather than taken on now against the working onnxruntime the rest of the stack depends on. Quality-over-size — Kokoro not Piper — stays the named trade-off; CPU-not-GPU is a second, smaller one taken with eyes open.

The hazard the probe flushed out was downstream of the Whisper conversion question. The clean way to get a Whisper IR is `optimum-cli export openvino` — and it is broken in this venv, a three-way version mismatch between `transformers 5.3.0`, `optimum 2.1.0.dev0`, and the editable `optimum-intel` checkout Blair keeps for his upstream work. The reflex was to fix it. The discipline was to ask whether voice actually stands on it, and it does not: the runtime never imports optimum, and a pre-converted Whisper IR can simply be downloaded — more reproducible than a local conversion anyway. Fixing the exporter would mean re-pinning `transformers`, which the 14B inference path and the Substrate embedder both ride, for no benefit to this feature. So we routed around it, downloaded the pre-converted IR, and filed the breakage as a tracked hardening ticket (#541) rather than yak-shave a dependency tree on the critical path of an unrelated mission. Blair made the call explicitly: document and ticket, don't fix now.

With the terrain mapped, the backend half went in clean and fail-soft, modelled exactly on the Substrate's posture. A new `services/voice` package holds a pure audio codec (base64 PCM ↔ float32, 24kHz→16kHz linear resample, no model imports so it is trivially testable) and a `VoiceEngine` that carries two optional model handles: if Whisper will not load, speech-to-text disables; if Kokoro will not load, text-to-speech disables; either way text chat is untouched. Three methods join the dispatcher — `transcribe`, `synthesize` (streaming one audio frame per sentence so playback can start before the whole reply is spoken), and `voice_status` so the front end can grey out affordances a model did not back. Audio rides the existing pipe as base64 inside the JSON frames the codec already speaks on both sides; a raw-binary frame to shave the 33% overhead is noted in the ADR as a later optimization, deliberately not built first. The launcher constructs the models once and injects them like the gateway; the `--no-model` path gets a sine-tone stub engine so the whole mic-and-play path is exercisable headless. Twenty-six new tests, full sweep 1408 passed / 2 skipped — the 1,382 baseline plus exactly the new ones, no regressions. The C# audio capture and the on-screen affordances are the next commit; none of that can be driven headless, so the latency number above is the floor, and the real "stop speaking to first spoken word" figure is Blair's to read off the live screen. *(commits `<this>`: ADR-017 + `services/voice` engine/codec + dispatcher `transcribe`/`synthesize`/`voice_status` + launcher fail-soft wiring + 26 tests; pre-converted whisper-small IR and Kokoro weights downloaded under gitignored `models/`; export-path hazard tracked as Vikunja #541.)*

**Next:** the C# half — `AudioGraph` microphone capture and incremental playback over the pipe, a mic button in the composer, the settings flyout the gear has been waiting for (voice on/off + a voice picker, swappable without restart), and a per-message play button on assistant replies. Then the live verifies: the measured latency, mic capture under the elevated app, and whether the spoken voice clears the "wow" bar that chose Kokoro over Piper in the first place.

### 2026-06-02 — Voice, the front half: a mic, a voice, and a build error that was lying

The on-screen half of voice went in the way the surface was built to receive it. A microphone button sits in the composer beside attach and screenshot; the gear that had been disabled since Phase 6 now opens a real settings flyout with a "speak replies aloud" switch and a voice picker that swaps without a restart; every assistant reply carries a small play button. None of it is novel UI — it is the same Gemini-shaped vocabulary the rest of the window already speaks, which is the point. The capture path uses `AudioGraph` with a frame-output node, because that is the route that yields raw 16 kHz PCM in an unpackaged desktop app; the playback path deliberately does *not* use AudioGraph but a `MediaPlayer` fed a list of WAV-wrapped chunks, because queued playback of streamed sentences is exactly what a playback list is for and it needs no quantum-level frame feeding. Two different audio APIs for the two directions, each chosen for the direction it fits — capture wants raw samples, playback wants a queue.

The lesson of the session was not in the code; it was in the compiler. The handoff brief's own advice for checking compilation without disturbing a running app was to build to a fresh output directory with `-o`. I followed it, and got back nine errors — the XAML compiler claiming it could not resolve `AttachmentChip`, `MessageItem`, `MarkdownBlock`, even `ChatSession`: types that have compiled and shipped since Phase 2. For a moment that is alarming, because it reads as "you broke the whole window." The tell was a single warning above the noise — `WMC1509: No LocalAssembly parameter given during MarkupCompilePass2` — which says the XAML markup compiler's second pass, the one that resolves project-local types, was never handed the assembly it needs. Building to a custom `-o` directory breaks that pass; the type-resolution errors are a cascade from the broken build, not from the code. The single *real* error sat quietly among the nine: a missing `using Windows.Media;` for `AudioBufferAccessMode`. I added the using, rebuilt in place (the app was not running, so there was no lock to dodge), and got the truth: build succeeded, zero warnings, zero errors. The keeper here is a debugging instinct more than a fact — when a tool reports a flood of errors that contradict what you know to be true (these types exist; they shipped), suspect the tool's invocation before you suspect reality, and look for the one diagnostic that explains the flood. The brief's `-o` tip was good-faith and wrong, and the journal is the right place to correct it for the next agent so nobody re-spends that confused minute.

What I cannot honestly claim is that any of this *works*, only that it compiles. Microphone capture under the elevated app, the real "stop speaking to first spoken word" latency, whether the queued chunks play gaplessly, and whether `af_heart` clears the wow bar are all Blair's to read off the live screen — flagged, not assumed, exactly as the GUI-layer relaxation in ADR-014 intends. *(commits `<this>`: WinUI mic button + settings flyout with voice toggle and picker + per-message play + auto-speak; `VoiceCapture` (AudioGraph) and `VoicePlayback` (MediaPlayer/WAV) services; IPC `transcribe`/`synthesize`/`voice_status`; build clean; Python untouched at 1408 / 2.)*

**Next:** the mission close (Phase 7) — deprecate the Textual TUI cleanly in-tree (dormant, not deleted; ADR-009 marked superseded), append the measured voice latency to `PERFORMANCE_LOG.md` once Blair reads it off the hardware, a final mission journal entry, and the close comment on Vikunja #539. And the live verifies that turn "it compiles" into "it works."

### 2026-06-02 — Closing the WinUI mission: from a terminal to a thing you can talk to

This is the close of an arc that began as "replace the TUI with something Gemini-shaped" and ends with an assistant you can speak to and hear. Across the mission the native WinUI 3 surface went from an empty window to chat with streaming markdown, to multimodal attachments with inline chips and screenshots, to persistent semantic memory across sessions, to theming that survives a restart, to — now — voice in both directions. The Textual TUI that carried USE-CASE-004 since Phase 3 is, as of this commit, formally **deprecated and dormant**: a banner at the top of its app module and a superseded-status on ADR-009 both say the same thing — WinUI (ADR-014) is the primary surface, the TUI is retained as a fallback and stays under test, and nothing new gets wired into it. It was Blair's explicit call to keep it in-tree rather than delete it, and that is the right governance posture: a working fallback you can still run is cheap insurance, and deleting a thing that still passes its tests buys nothing but risk.

The honest state at close, demarcated because some of it is active and some is dormant. **Active now:** the voice backend loads its models fail-soft at WinUI startup — if the Whisper IR and Kokoro weights are present under `models/`, speech-to-text and text-to-speech come up; if either is absent or fails, that half silently disables and text chat is untouched. So voice is *live the moment the weights are on disk*, which they now are. **What gates progression:** nothing automatic — push-to-talk is a deliberate user action, replies are only spoken aloud when the user turns on "speak replies," and the GPU-provider swap for Kokoro stays dormant behind an `ONNX_PROVIDER` override that nobody has set. **Still paused, unchanged by this mission:** Layer 3 tool-gating (ADR-013) remains off; voice does not touch the tool blast radius. **Unverified:** everything that needs a live microphone, a real ear, and the 14B in the loop — the end-to-end latency, mic capture under the elevated app, gapless playback, and whether Kokoro's voice clears the wow bar. Those are Blair's to confirm on the hardware, and the performance log carries the component numbers with the end-to-end figure explicitly marked pending rather than fabricated.

Two hazards leave this mission as tracked tickets rather than silent debt, because the discipline is that deferred work is ticketed, not remembered. The broken `optimum` exporter the probe flushed out is #541. The Substrate's startup and per-prompt cost — never benchmarked — is flagged in the performance log and filed as its own measurement task, alongside the Layer-3-versus-always-grounded-memory interaction the handoff brief warned about: if Layer 3 is ever un-paused, the gate must tell "user loaded a document this turn" apart from "memory was retrieved," because the Substrate now grounds context on every turn. None of these block the mission; all of them are now visible.

What the mission taught, beyond any single feature, is the thing the lessons list keeps circling: the envelope before the capability (the multimodal surface built before vision, the settings surface built in Phase 6 that voice simply moved into), provenance is not trust (retrieved memory defended like a fresh document), and — newest — a handoff is a map, not the territory, so you measure the load-bearing ground before you build on it. The window Blair opens now is not a prototype of an assistant. It is one he can talk to. *(commits `<this>`: TUI deprecation banner + ADR-009 superseded status + `PERFORMANCE_LOG.md` voice entry with Substrate-unmeasured flag; full sweep green; closes the WinUI daily-assistant mission, Vikunja #539, pending the on-hardware live verifies.)*

**Next:** Blair's live verifies on the hardware — mic capture under elevation, the real first-spoken-token latency, playback continuity, and the voice-quality judgment — which turn this mission from shipped-and-compiling into shipped-and-working, and which feed the one real end-to-end number back into the performance log. After that, the deferred work is on tickets (#541, the Substrate benchmark, the Layer-3 gate refinement) waiting for its own scoped sessions, and the next *capability* the surface was shaped for — vision (ADR-015) — is the natural successor whenever the User-Operator calls it.

### 2026-06-02 — "Compiles" was a lie the live screen exposed: the voice bring-up

I closed the voice mission on "builds clean, pending live-verify." Then Blair tapped the microphone and nothing worked — five times over, each a different fault, none catchable by anything in the 1,409-test suite because every one of them lived in C#/WinRT/elevated-audio territory the Python tests cannot reach. This is the entry the GUI-layer relaxation in ADR-014 exists to produce: the screen caught what no sweep could.

The faults came in a stack, peeled one at a time. **(1)** The mic wouldn't open at all — a yellow "unavailable" box. I had forced the `AudioGraph` to 16 kHz mono, a format the Intel mic driver refused; the device-node creation failed and my catch-all message wrongly blamed Windows privacy (which, I checked the registry, was fully allowed). Fix: capture at the device's native rate, let the backend resample. **(2)** Now it opened but produced a 44-byte WAV — a header with zero samples. My hand-rolled `AudioFrameOutputNode` quantum reader, with its unsafe COM buffer cast, was yielding nothing. Rather than debug that fiddly path I swapped the whole approach: route the mic through an `AudioFileOutputNode` that encodes straight to a WAV file, no manual frame handling, no unsafe code. **(3)** That captured real audio — I could read the file, see healthy RMS, and Whisper transcribed it perfectly standalone ("Hello Dr. Watson…") — yet the app still showed nothing. The C# was sending `sample_rate=0, channels=0`, because `MediaEncodingProfile.CreateWav` writes a `JUNK` padding chunk *before* `fmt `, and my parser read the format fields from fixed offsets that landed in the padding. The zero sample-rate then divided-by-zero in the backend resampler. Fix: walk the chunks to find the real `fmt `, and harden the resampler to reject a non-positive rate. **(4)** Still nothing, and now *no error at all* — because the failure was an exception inside `StopAsync` thrown after the file finalized, vanishing silently in an `async void` handler. I wrapped the whole flow and added dual-sided logging, which named it instantly: `ObjectDisposedException` — I was disposing the file-output node that `FinalizeAsync` had already closed. Fix: defensive disposal. **(5)** And threaded through it all, a false trail: stray `[stub transcript]` log lines proving Blair had sometimes been hitting the `--no-model` demo backend, not the real launcher — so part of the time we were debugging a stub that never ran Whisper.

What turned this from an all-night guessing game into an afternoon of single-step fixes was the decision, made too late, to stop guessing across the boundary and instrument it instead. The on-disk WAV and the two log files (`_voice_ui.log`, `_voice_backend.log`) converted every "nothing happens" into a specific, named fault. Once the instrumentation existed, each remaining bug fell in one test cycle. The measured reward at the end: a 4.7-second utterance — "Hello Bella, she just ran off" — transcribed correctly in about a second, GPU Whisper doing the work; speech-to-text is genuinely fast, the thing Blair feared would be unusable. Text-to-speech worked too, with one note he caught immediately: Kokoro at the default `speed=1.0` is a draggy, over-measured delivery, so the default is now 1.2 (plumbed as an overridable RPC param, with tests), which reads as natural and conversational. *(commits `<this>`: file-output mic capture + JUNK-chunk-aware WAV parse + defensive disposal + native-rate-with-backend-resample; resampler 0-rate guard; dual-sided voice diagnostics; Kokoro default speed 1.2; 54 voice/backend tests, full sweep green. Voice now works end-to-end on hardware.)*

**Next:** Blair to confirm the 1.2 speech pace reads right (tweak the one constant if not), then the remaining honest live-verifies — gapless playback across longer replies, and whether auto-speak should begin per-sentence as the reply streams rather than after it completes (a known latency-to-first-word improvement, deliberately deferred). The diagnostic logs are bring-up scaffolding to thin out once voice has a few days of real use behind it.

### 2026-06-02 — When a new channel re-tests every old assumption: voice exposed the thinking leak

Voice working was not voice good. Blair used it for real and it was, in his word, "annoying" — and a screenshot showed why it was more than a taste problem: the model's chain-of-thought was leaking into the reply on screen ("Okay, let's see. The user mentioned that Tina is a dog. So… First, I should acknowledge…"), and because the new audio channel reads the streamed text aloud, BlarAI was *speaking its own reasoning* before giving the answer. The thinking-strip — the ADR-012 §2.4 guard that is supposed to keep internal reasoning private — was failing.

The root cause was a streaming assumption that had been quietly wrong for a long time. The strip detected boundaries with a per-chunk test, `if "<think>" in text_chunk`. But the model streams token by token, and a tag arrives split — `<`, then `think`, then `>`. No single chunk ever contains the whole string, so the block was never entered and the reasoning streamed straight through. On screen this had been a tolerable, intermittent ugliness nobody had prioritised (it lived as ISS-2). Through a speaker it became intolerable: you cannot skim past audio. The fix is to stop testing chunks and test the *accumulated* text — a small `_visible_text(raw)` that strips complete `<think>`/`<tool_call>` blocks, withholds an unclosed block to the end, and holds back a trailing partial tag, so the streamer emits only the growing delta of known-safe text. Split tags are always joined before matching now, and the same function closes the identical latent hole for tool-call tags. Seven tests pin the split-tag, unclosed, partial-tag, and prefix-stability cases; the full AO suite stayed green at 386.

The lesson is the one worth keeping from the whole voice arc: **a new output channel re-tests every assumption the old channel let you get away with.** The reasoning leak was always a bug, but rendering it as text on a screen that repaints made it survivable, so it was tolerated for weeks. Speaking it — an irreversible, serial channel — made the same defect a glaring failure and forced the real fix. Adding a modality is not just additive surface; it re-audits the contracts beneath it, and the bugs it surfaces were usually already there. The rest of this round was UX tightening the same real use exposed: the spoken reply is now governed by the "Speak replies aloud" toggle rather than forced on by speaking the prompt (voice input no longer compels voice output — the user owns the audio of the response); the per-message play button re-synthesizes on demand so a reply can be replayed; and the reply now starts being spoken sentence-by-sentence as it streams (a backend producer/consumer interleaving `audio` frames with `token` frames, pre-PGOV like the screen, cancelling playback on a denial) instead of after the whole thing completes. The gapless `AudioGraph` frame-input playback I tried for the streaming case produced silence — the same WinRT frame-I/O finickiness that defeated capture — so I dropped it and kept the `MediaPlayer` path Blair had already heard work, with a resume nudge for streaming arrival: working-and-audible over theoretically-gapless-and-silent. *(commits `<this>`: streaming per-sentence speech in `_m_prompt` + `extract_sentences`; `_visible_text` accumulated-text thinking/tool strip fixing ISS-2 + 7 tests; voice-recording-auto-submits; "Speak replies aloud" governs spoken output; MediaPlayer playback with streaming resume. Full sweep 1424 passed / 2 skipped.)*

**Next:** Blair to verify on hardware that the reasoning no longer leaks to screen or speaker, the toggle governs spoken replies, and the play button replays. A stray `TokenName` fragment in the same screenshot is unexplained by code search — likely a model-side or tool-call-flush artifact — and wants a closer look with a clean repro once the thinking-strip noise is gone. This was a long, productive session; the right next move is a clean handoff so the remaining verification + the `TokenName` chase start with fresh context.

### 2026-06-03 — Measuring the reason, not just the setting: the GPU cache that saved nothing

I had measured this exact cache to nothing once before — the 2026-05-22 boot-time entry, where I turned it on, found it break-even, and dropped it. The voice handoff re-opened it on two reasonable grounds: OpenVINO 2026.2's notes now advertise faster GPU loads from cache blobs, and — more pointedly — the governance doc's stated reason for keeping the cache *off* looked technically unfounded. `gpu-runtime.md` said a fresh compile on every boot buys "guaranteed identical compiled state across sessions." The argument the brief made, and I shared, was that the cache *is* the same compiled model written to disk and read back, so disabling it cannot be protecting reproducibility — there is nothing to protect. The honest way to settle that is not to argue the documentation; it is to measure.

So I built a probe that varies exactly one thing — `CACHE_DIR` — across five fresh-process loads of the production pipeline (the real 14B target plus its pruned draft, same LATENCY / f16 / SDPA / speculative-decoding config), generates the same three prompts greedily, and SHA-256s the output. Two findings, and a confound worth keeping in.

**The rationale was empty.** All five runs — fresh-compile twice, cache-write once, cache-read twice — produced byte-identical output, the same three hashes to the last character. The cache does not perturb a single token; it *is* the same compiled state. The doc was guarding against a risk that does not exist. That settles the reproducibility question the choice was nominally resting on: not "the cache is risky," but "the cache is identical."

**But the fix was worth nothing anyway, which is why the answer does not change.** A fresh compile of the 14B-plus-draft is only about eleven seconds here — nowhere near the thirty-to-forty the handoff implied, because most of that boot is Whisper and the services, not the model compile. Turning the cache on cost fifty-three seconds the first time, because it writes a nine-gigabyte blob to disk. And reading that blob back on the run that actually matters — the first one after a reboot, when it is not already resident in memory — took just under twelve seconds: a hair *slower* than simply recompiling. The thing that briefly looked like a thirteen-percent win was the *second* warm run, fast only because the first had just pulled nine gigabytes into the operating system's file cache. It is lesson seven again — the speculative benchmark that read "2× faster" because the second run sat on a hot GPU — wearing disk-cache clothes this time. The honest comparison is break-even at best, and that is before the integrity-hash the first investigation proved is mandatory for a derived artifact under BlarAI's fail-closed posture: hashing nine gigabytes on every boot would push it firmly negative.

So the decision does not move: `CACHE_DIR` stays `""`. What moves is the *reason*. I corrected the governance doc to say what is true — the cache is left off because it buys no measurable startup win at a nine-gigabyte, fifty-second-first-write cost on this hardware, not because it threatens an identical compiled state it actually preserves — and recorded the supersession in place, so the path-not-taken stays on the record rather than being quietly overwritten. The raw runs, the harness, and a dated performance-log entry are committed alongside, so the next person who wonders about the cache reads the measurement instead of re-running it. And the measurement pointed, as it did in May, at where the startup cost actually lives: not the model compile, but Whisper and the service bring-up. That — a full-boot profile, with the far smaller Whisper blob as a caching candidate — is the real version of "the highest-value startup fix," and it is the next thing to look at rather than this one. *(commits `<this>`: corrected the `gpu-runtime.md` `CACHE_DIR` rationale; `PERFORMANCE_LOG.md` 2026-06-03 entry; raw evidence + harness under `docs/performance/cache_dir_probe_2026-06-03/`. No runtime code changed; full sweep 1424 passed / 2 skipped — nothing under the suite's collection paths was touched. Vikunja #545.)*

**Next:** the startup cost worth chasing is Whisper-plus-services, not the 14B compile — a full-boot profile, with the small-blob Whisper cache as a candidate, is the real version of "the highest-value startup fix." Separately, the OpenVINO 2026.2 upgrade (KV-cache compression, Qwen3-VL) is still open and approval-gated.

### 2026-06-03 — A trust root built on hardware nobody had checked

Blair asked for the security features — the hardware-rooted trust that is supposed to be the spine of a system meant to run for decades. I went to build the first one and found the foundation was a rumour.

The canonical use-case document specified the Policy Agent's trust root as Intel SGX: a measured boot that completes "SGX local attestation" before deriving the CA key. The trouble is that Intel removed SGX from its client CPUs years ago, and the target machine — a Lunar Lake laptop — has none. The code never used it; the design only *said* it did. So the trust root, on paper for months, was a phantom: a sentence describing hardware that was never there. (This is lesson fourteen wearing a more expensive suit — an assumption inherited from the design, true nowhere, load-bearing anyway.)

The way out was to stop reading the spec and query the silicon. `tpmtool` reported a TPM 2.0 present, attestation-capable, firmware not vulnerable — and I promptly made my own smaller version of the same mistake. Its manufacturer string said "STMicroelectronics," so I wrote "the chip is not Pluton." Blair pushed back: he was almost certain his laptop *had* Pluton — the marketing said so, Device Manager said so. He was right, and I had been sloppy. Device Manager shows *two* security devices: a "Trusted Platform Module 2.0" (the active TPM, ST-made) and a "Microsoft Pluton security processor" (present, Intel Lunar Lake, just not serving as the TPM here). I had collapsed two devices into one claim and stepped on the knowledge of the person who owns the machine. The lesson landed twice in one day: verify the hardware, and when its owner contradicts your inference, the owner wins.

With the device actually characterised, the trust root rebuilt itself cleanly. I wrote a signing primitive against the standard Windows TPM interface (CNG's Platform Crypto Provider) — create a key *inside* the TPM, sign, verify, and crucially never be able to export the private half. Then I did the thing this project has learned never to skip: I ran it on the real chip. It nearly worked — sign, verify, tamper-rejection, public-key export, all correct first time — and then died on the very last step, deleting the test key, with `NTE_BAD_FLAGS`. The TPM rejects a flag the Microsoft documentation says it accepts. No amount of reading would have found that; only the silicon returning the error did. One flag changed, and the round-trip — including a direct assertion that the private key cannot be exported — passes on hardware.

So the decision, ratified with Blair: re-root trust on the TPM 2.0, in the "proven core" shape — seal the CA key and sign the model-weight manifest with non-exportable TPM keys, with a provisioning-and-recovery ceremony so a lost key cannot fail-closed-brick the machine — and *stage* the harder part, formal boot-state attestation, for later. ADR-018 records it and supersedes every SGX reference in the use-case doc. I was honest in the ADR about what this does and does not buy: TPM-signing makes the weights tamper-evident *at rest*, against an attacker with the disk but not the running machine; it does not, yet, stop an attacker who can run code on the host and ask the TPM to re-sign. That boundary is the PCR-binding work I deliberately deferred — naming it is the point, not hiding it.

What shipped here is the foundation, hardware-verified, plus the architectural decision that unblocks the rest. The feature that consumes it — the tamper-evident weight manifest — is next, and it rolls out warn-only before it enforces, because the one thing worse than no trust root is one that bricks the system it was meant to protect. *(commits `<this>`: `docs/TPM_CAPABILITY_FINDINGS.md` (ISS-4); ADR-018 + `Use Cases_FINAL.md` SGX amendment (ISS-5); `shared/security/tpm_signer.py` + hardware-verified `test_tpm_signer.py`; lesson 18. Vikunja #101/#102 closing; FUT-04/01/05 open.)*

**Next:** FUT-04 — sign the weight-integrity manifest with the TPM key and verify it at boot before trusting the hashes, behind a warn-only→enforce flag. Then the host-vs-VM decision for the CA key (FUT-01) and the provisioning/recovery ceremony (FUT-05). Formal PCR-quote attestation remains staged.

### 2026-06-03 — Vision, in one swap: the envelope I built in May paid for itself

Months ago, building the WinUI surface, I made a bet that felt almost premature: build the entire photo experience — picker, drag-drop, inline chips, screenshot capture, store-into-userdata — against a backend that could only say "an image is here; I can't read it yet." No vision model, just the envelope. Today the bet paid off. Wiring real image understanding was a *backend swap*, not a UI project: one function in the document loader — where the placeholder text was — now calls a vision model and returns what it actually sees. Nothing in the window changed. Lesson twelve, in cash.

The model is Qwen3-VL-8B (the only INT4 OpenVINO-org VLM — the 4B and 2B int4 builds don't exist). I probed it before trusting it, the way this project has learned to: it loaded on the Arc 140V in thirteen seconds and accurately described a test image — it even noted the image was *not* a landscape (a command prompt over a graffiti wallpaper). Then Blair tested it live in BlarAI on a real photo and got an accurate, useful answer. Live-verified, not assumed.

The interesting part was the conversation around it. Blair wants vision for his sister's landscape business — and his goals included *precise measurements from photos* and *generating proposal renderings*. Neither is what a VLM does. So before wiring a line, I said so: a VLM guesses at dimensions (and false measurements are worse than none for a bid, so the description prompt explicitly forbids them); rendering is image *generation*, a separate diffusion model entirely; and real measurement is photogrammetry — his "different angles" instinct is exactly right, but it's a separate pipeline that needs the *capture* to change, not a better model. He took the limits well, and we scoped vision to what it genuinely delivers now: understanding. That honesty was the most valuable thing I shipped today (lesson nineteen).

Two honest edges remain, both named not hidden. The hard one is *memory*: the 8 GB VLM loads on the GPU alongside the resident 14B against a 31.3 GB shared ceiling, and the co-resident cost is unmeasured — so the wiring is Fail-Soft (any load or decode failure degrades to the old placeholder rather than crashing), and load-on-demand eviction of the 14B is the planned fix if co-residency proves unstable. The small one: HEIC photos can now be attached but need a decoder dependency to actually read. And Blair caught a real gap — the attach size cap was 16 MB, too small for high-res property photos; bumped to 64 MB and broadened the formats. *(commits `<this>`: `shared/inference/vlm.py` + `document_loader` VLM injection (Fail-Soft) + the attach cap/format fix; ADR-015; PERFORMANCE_LOG 2026-06-03 vision entry; lesson 19; a test stub so the suite never loads the 8B model. Live-verified in BlarAI. Vikunja #550.)*

**Next:** Blair's live test surfaced UI polish — show the attached image larger in the conversation, click to open full size — and the load-bearing measurement: profile the VLM co-resident with the 14B (does it fit, or is load-on-demand eviction needed?). Then the landscape roadmap, in order of honesty: plant-species precision, photogrammetry measurement, image-gen renderings, and a structured export to the rendering software once its format is known.

### 2026-06-03 — The privilege boundary, a fourth time: this time I moved the wall

Three times now this project has hit the same wall and tunnelled under it. A setting that vanished across the UAC relaunch became a command-line flag. The microphone, I reasoned, was in-process and so didn't cross the wall at all. Drag-drop, which does cross it, got a window-message filter to let the drop messages through. Each was a way *around* the fact that BlarAI runs elevated and the things it touches do not. Today, for the fourth time, the wall showed up — and instead of tunnelling under it I moved it.

The bug was file attach, and it had already been "fixed" once. The previous session, reasoning that the open dialog couldn't see certain photos, broadened the file-type filters. It shipped (`855d857`) and it changed nothing, because formats were never the problem. The tell was in *which* files failed: Blair could attach a photo copied to his Desktop but not the same photo living in OneDrive as a cloud-only file — through the *same* dialog. Same code, two files, one works. A feature that fails only for the inputs that cross a trust boundary isn't a feature bug; it's a privilege bug wearing a feature bug's clothes. An elevated process can't drive the per-user OneDrive hydration that lives at medium integrity, and elevated drag-drop dies on UIPI.
