Both halves of attach were gated on one thing: the UI was running as administrator.

It was running as administrator by accident. Its own manifest asks for nothing; it inherited High integrity simply because the elevated launcher spawned it. The launcher genuinely needs admin — Hyper-V's `Start-VM` is admin-gated — but the *window* never did. So the fix is to keep the launcher elevated and spawn the UI back down at medium integrity, as if BlarAI had never elevated at all.

That turned out to be a small idea wrapped in a fussy piece of Windows, and the fussiness is the part worth keeping. The clean way is the token Windows already mints for you: on a split-token UAC sign-in your admin token has a *linked* standard-user token hanging off it. I reached for it first — and `DuplicateTokenEx` refused it at every impersonation level with ERROR_BAD_IMPERSONATION_LEVEL. So I built the filtered token by hand the way UAC builds it: duplicate the token, run it through `CreateRestrictedToken` to demote the Administrators group to deny-only and strip the privileges, then stamp its integrity label down to Medium. Launching with it was the next small wall: `CreateProcessAsUser` rejected the filtered token with ERROR_PRIVILEGE_NOT_HELD — it wants a privilege an elevated admin doesn't actually hold — and the call that *does* work, `CreateProcessWithTokenW` (it needs only SeImpersonate, which admin has), isn't exposed by pywin32 at all. So that one is bound through ctypes, with the handle types pinned so a 64-bit handle doesn't get quietly truncated.

The reason I can write this with any confidence is that, for once, I could test the load-bearing part myself. My own session happened to be elevated, so I didn't have to hand the whole thing to the live screen on faith: I launched a child through the new code and watched it come up at Medium while the launcher sat at High, the Administrators group deny-only and a single privilege left. The primitive is real, verified on the actual chip. What I *can't* verify from here is the part that only exists in a windowed, GPU-bound, elevated run — that the WinUI window appears when the elevated launcher spawns it this way, that it still reaches the backend across the named pipe from medium integrity, and that the two attach paths finally work. That is Blair's to confirm at the screen; it sits on a branch, commit-on-green to main. The pipe is the one piece I am reasoning rather than measuring: a same-user medium client *should* connect to the high server's pipe, the launcher's existing "client connected" log line will say so in one read, and if it doesn't the fallback — an explicit security descriptor on the pipe — is written down, not left to be rediscovered.

The lesson I am taking from it is the one that surprised me. Least privilege is usually filed under *security* — a tax you pay on top of the features, a thing that makes the working thing slightly less convenient. Here it *was* the feature. The change that drops the UI from administrator to a filtered medium token is the same change that makes attach work; the secure design and the working design were one decision, not two in tension. The cert track trained me on the inverse — that hardening costs you something — and the more useful truth is that sometimes the hardening *is* the fix, and the thing you were about to debug at the feature layer was a privilege problem the whole time. *(commits `<this>`: `launcher/process_launch.py` (filtered-token de-elevation, primitive verified live) + `launcher/__main__.py` wiring + `services/ui_winui` FileDialog/MainWindow comment truth-up; ADR-019; lesson 20; `launcher/tests/test_process_launch.py` (7 tests); full sweep 1442 passed. Branch `feat/deelevate-winui-attach`; commit-on-green to main after live-verify. Vikunja #552.)*

**Next:** Blair's live-verify of the four pending facts — window appears, pipe connects from medium integrity, cloud-file pick works, drag-drop works; on green, merge to main (explicit-SD fallback if the pipe balks). Then the deferred attach work a medium-integrity UI now unblocks — multi-select for "all the property photos at once" and HEIC decode — and, separately, the full-boot profile (#546).

### 2026-06-03 — Profiling the whole before caching the part: where the boot actually goes

The ticket came pre-framed. #545 had killed the idea of caching the 14B's GPU compile — a 9 GB blob that reads back from cold disk no faster than it recompiles — and the handoff pointed at Whisper as "the real startup lever": a much smaller blob, so the cache math might finally come out ahead. The instinct was to go straight at it. The discipline was to profile the whole boot first, and the whole boot told a different story than the part I'd been handed.

I pulled the per-step timings out of the launcher's own log across two consecutive boots and added them up. Of the ~26 seconds from the admin check to the window launching, the 14B+draft GPU compile is **thirteen and a half of them — 53%**. Everything else is small beside it: the VM start a second, the Policy Agent gate under four, the Assistant Orchestrator about four and a half, and the entire voice engine — Whisper *and* Kokoro together — just 2.6 seconds, about a tenth of boot. The lever the handoff pointed me at moves a tenth of the thing; the one that matters is the 14B, and that one is already settled. This is lesson fourteen again, its quieter half: a handoff is a map of what the predecessor believed, and "Whisper is the real lever" was a belief that did not survive being measured against the whole.

I measured the Whisper cache anyway, because the ticket asked and because "probably minor" is not a number. Five fresh processes, the only variable the cache directory. It does, unlike the 14B, come out *slightly* ahead: a fresh compile is 1.72 s, a warm cache read 0.75 s, and even after paying the 0.29 s to integrity-hash the half-gigabyte blob — the cost #545 taught me to always count — the warm path totals about 1.04 s, a net saving of roughly two-thirds of a second. Real, positive, and almost entirely beside the point: two-thirds of a second on a twenty-six-second boot, bought with a permanent 496 MB on disk that must be torn down and rebuilt every time OpenVINO or the driver or the model moves. And there's a hole I couldn't close: my probe's transcription step failed on the synthetic audio I fed it, so I never verified that the *cached* compile produces the same transcript as a fresh one. #545's whole point was that you don't trust a cache until you've proven it byte-identical to the real thing; I can't make that claim for Whisper. So the decision writes itself, and it rhymes with #545: keep the cache off — not because caching can't help here, but because the help is imperceptible, unverified, and carries a standing maintenance cost. The path is left open for a future where boot speed actually matters and the identity check is done first.

The genuinely interesting number was the one I wasn't looking for. The Substrate embedder — the little bge-small model the leakage detector uses — had never been timed (#542 flagged it as a hole). It loads in five to eight seconds. That is *larger* than the entire Assistant Orchestrator boot step it supposedly lives inside, which can only mean it doesn't: it loads lazily, on the first prompt, not at startup. So there's a five-to-eight-second tax sitting on the *first thing the user types*, invisible in every boot measurement because it isn't in the boot. The slice I went hunting to shave turned out minor; the cost I wasn't hunting for turned out to be the real find. Measure the whole before you optimise the part — not just because the part may be small, but because the profiling is where you trip over the thing that actually matters. *(commit `<this>`: PERFORMANCE_LOG 2026-06-03 boot-attribution + Whisper-cache entry; `docs/performance/whisper_cache_probe_2026-06-03/` harness + 5 fresh-process runs + verdict; perf_history.jsonl boot snapshot; `CACHE_DIR=""` kept for Whisper, path-not-taken recorded. Vikunja #546.)*

**Next:** if boot speed ever becomes a priority, the only slice worth attacking is the 14B compile (53%) — and that means a different lever than disk caching (a smaller model, a faster draft, a different compile path), since the cache route is closed. The nearer win is the embedder's 5–8 s first-prompt tax: either fold its load into the boot budget (where the user isn't waiting on a reply) or make it lazy-but-backgrounded. Both are their own tickets, not this one.

### 2026-06-03 — The reasoned-not-measured piece was exactly the one that broke

I shipped the de-elevation with one honest hole flagged. Of everything in it, the named pipe was the single piece I was *reasoning* about rather than measuring — I'd argued a same-user Medium client would connect to the elevated server's pipe under default security, said so out loud, and wrote the fallback into the ADR in case I was wrong. Blair launched it and that is exactly where it broke: no chats, and a banner reading "backend not running — Access to the path is denied." The de-elevation *launch* worked perfectly — the window came up de-elevated, which is the hard part — but the Medium UI couldn't open the High server's pipe. My reasoning was wrong in the specific way I'd feared: a pipe an elevated process creates with default security does not admit a Medium client, even of the same user.

The fix was the fallback already written into the ADR: create the pipe with an explicit security descriptor — grant the current user and SYSTEM, and label the pipe Medium so the no-write-up policy stops blocking the client. The good part is I didn't have to send it back to the screen on faith. Because my own session runs elevated, I could stand up the *real* server-side descriptor, spawn a Medium child through the same de-elevation primitive the launcher uses, and watch it open the pipe and push bytes through — the shipped code path, not a mock. The thing I couldn't verify headless the first time, I could verify this time by borrowing the elevation I happened to have; only the last mile — the actual WinUI app reconnecting — goes back to Blair.

Two lessons, both already on the list, each earning another tally. Lesson two: the only proof is the real system in front of you, and the screen found in one launch what no test of mine reached. Lesson sixteen, sharpened: when you can't test across a boundary, *say which piece is unverified* — naming the pipe as the reasoned-not-measured part is what made the failure legible the instant it happened, a one-line banner instead of a mystery to chase. The honest hole flagged up front was worth more than the nine things that worked. *(commit `<this>`: `services/ui_backend/src/server.py` `_pipe_security_attributes` — explicit pipe SD, verified cross-integrity on the real code path — + ADR-019 §2.5/§6 amended; full sweep green. Branch `feat/deelevate-winui-attach`; awaiting Blair's relaunch. Vikunja #552.)*

**Next:** Blair relaunches BlarAI; confirm the chats list populates and attach (cloud-file pick + drag-drop) works; on green, merge to main.

### 2026-06-03 — The security audit I verified, and the presentation I didn't

Blair is steering BlarAI toward the network — eventually internet-facing, to fetch information and navigate the web — and he made the right call about order: real security before real memory, and certainly before the air-gap comes off. So he asked for something I had not built before — a complete, honest map of the system's security, features and vulnerabilities, rooted in what is actually on disk, illustrated, so he could understand it and make informed decisions.

I ran it as a formal multi-agent audit: seven domain auditors against the real code (trust root and measured boot, the Policy Agent authorization gate, IPC and isolation, prompt-injection defense, output validation, the privacy/network boundary, data at rest), each finding then independently re-checked by an adversarial verifier that opened the cited files and tried to refute it, then a completeness critic and a red-team synthesis — sixteen agents, every claim cited to a file. The picture is the valuable part and the sobering part: one Critical, eighteen High. The recurring theme was not "unbuilt" — it was "built but switched off." The TPM trust root exists and is wired into nothing. The guest profile ships with dev_mode on, which disables mTLS, identity binding, weight verification, and measured boot in a single config line. The leakage and PII output filters ship off. The deterministic injection backstop is off. There is no code-enforced network kill-switch — the air-gap is environmental, not enforced. The data at rest is plaintext. And the Policy Agent's signing key — the key the whole authorization model trusts — sits in git in cleartext. Today none of it is reachable, because the machine is not on a network. That is exactly the point: the air-gap is the only wall, and Blair wants the walls behind it built before he takes it down.

Then I did the thing worth keeping in. I verified every finding to the file, and I shipped the presentation carrying them with its diagrams broken — and when Blair told me, I "fixed" it and shipped it broken again. The mistake each time was the same: I "verified" the deck with a mechanical lint of my own — bracket balance, line breaks — a proxy that shares the artifact's blind spot, not the real Mermaid parser. Blair's words were exactly right: why verify all your findings only to mess up the data in the presentation. The third time I stopped guessing, installed the actual parser (the same Mermaid version his browser ran), and ran it over the exact strings the page ships — and the real cause was not the diagram syntax at all: the HTML embedding was round-tripping every `-->` arrow into `--&gt;` before Mermaid ever saw it. The fix was to hand each diagram straight to `mermaid.render()` as a string, never through the HTML parser. An independent cross-check of every slide claim against the verified findings came back faithful, with a few clarity fixes for a non-expert reader, which I applied.

That is lesson twenty-one, and it stings precisely because the audit was so careful: the vehicle that delivers a verified result is its own artifact, and it must be verified against the tool that will actually consume it — not a proxy of my own invention. Where it leaves us: Blair set the gate — complete the hardening roadmap through Tier 3 before facing the internet, all four tiers, not just the blocking ones — and asked me to implement it. Security code, mostly unverifiable headless, is exactly the work that wants fresh, sharp context and incremental live-verify, not a marathon-session blob tested once at the end. So this commit is the baseline — the audit, the findings, the deck, the verification tooling — and the build itself is scoped for a fresh session in `security-hardening-handoff-brief.md`, starting at Tier 0. *(commit `<this>`: `docs/security/audit_2026-06-03/` — `audit_full.json` (16-agent findings), `security_presentation.html` + generators, mermaid+jsdom validators; lesson 21; hardening handoff brief is untracked. Vikunja #555. Deck live-render still pending Blair's browser confirm.)*

**Next:** a fresh session implements the hardening through Tier 3 on a branch, tier by tier, each with a live-verify checkpoint — Tier 0 first (rotate + TPM-seal the signing key; build the egress kill-switch). Nothing goes internet-facing until all four tiers are verified done.

### 2026-06-03 — Fail-closed is not fail-shut: the egress switch that had to stay open

The security audit's plainest finding was also its most uncomfortable: BlarAI's "no external network" is not enforced anywhere. It is true today because the machine is air-gapped and because the source happens not to call out — two facts that both evaporate the day Blair points this thing at the internet, which is the stated destination. The air-gap is the only wall, and the gate he set is that the walls behind it get built first. So the first brick of Tier 0 is the one that turns a habit into a control: a code-enforced egress kill-switch, fail-closed, that refuses any outbound network this process did not explicitly need.

The instinct the handoff brief encoded was "refuse any outbound socket," and Blair caught the flaw in it before I could ship it — which is the lesson worth keeping. A blanket socket block would have bricked the app on launch, because the runtime's legitimate IPC *is* sockets: the AF_HYPERV vsock to the VM, the dev-mode loopback TCP substitute, the launcher's 127.0.0.1:5001 handshake to the Policy Agent. Fail-closed cannot mean fail-shut. The control had to be an *allowlist* — permit loopback and Hyper-V, deny everything else — and the part that makes it honest is that the test proves the allow-set *passes*, not merely that the deny-set fails. A deny-by-default control whose positive path nobody checked is one launch away from a self-inflicted denial of service, and that particular failure would never surface in a headless test; it lives in the GPU-bound, elevated boot that only the live screen sees. So the sixteen tests assert both halves at once: an external connect, an external bind, a wildcard bind, a disallowed socket family, a DNS lookup for an outside name, even a UDP `sendto` — all refused; and a real 127.0.0.1 listen-connect-send round-trip, the `socketpair` asyncio's event loop is built on, an AF_HYPERV socket's construction, and loopback DNS — all still working.

Two smaller things had to be right or the guard would have been theatre. First, *where* it bites. The two places BlarAI opens a socket both create it raw, connect or bind on it, and only *then* wrap it in mTLS — so a guard on the raw socket's connect/bind fires on the real egress path before TLS ever enters the picture. I read the actual order in `vsock.py` rather than assume it, because a guard placed above the wrap would have been watching the wrong layer and felt just as finished. Second, *where* it is armed. A process-wide monkeypatch of `socket.socket` is exactly the kind of global mutation that poisons a test suite: the launcher's own tests import its module and call `main()` directly, so arming at import-time or inside `main()` would have leaked the patch into the 1,442-test run and broken things nowhere near it. The only correct seat is the real process entry — the `if __name__ == "__main__"` block that runs when you actually launch BlarAI and never when a test imports the module. The guard's own tests carry an autouse teardown that disarms come what may, so the global never escapes a single test.

What I want to be honest about, because the cert track punishes the opposite, is what this guard is *not*. It is an interpreter-level floor: it governs this process's socket calls, so it stops the accidental call, the dependency that suddenly reaches out, the agent tool that tries to phone home. It is not a sandbox against code that re-imports `_socket` to route around it or drops to a raw syscall — that is the VM's job, and a deny-outbound OS firewall rule is the defence-in-depth layer above it. Naming that boundary in the ADR is worth more than the code: the value of a fail-closed control evaporates the moment someone mistakes a floor for a cage and builds on it as if it were one.

**Next:** the second half of Tier 0 — get the Policy Agent's signing key out of cleartext in git and onto the TPM. The private key moves into the non-exportable platform key (`tpm_signer.py`, already proven on this chip in the ADR-018 work); the public half becomes `pa_public.pem`; the minter, the validator, and their tests move together so signing and verification never disagree about which key is real; the old key stays in history with a note, by Blair's call — non-destructive, no rewrite. Then the Tier-0 live-verify: Blair boots the real launcher, confirms "Egress guard ARMED" in the log and that nothing local was starved, and signs and verifies a JWT on the real silicon. On green, Tier 0 merges to main. *(commit `<this>`: `shared/security/egress_guard.py` + `shared/tests/test_egress_guard.py` (16 tests); `launcher/__main__.py` arm-at-entry + security-docstring truth-up; ADR-020; lesson 22; full sweep 1458 passed, 86 deselected — was 1442 pre-increment. Branch `feat/security-hardening`; Vikunja #557.)*

### 2026-06-03 — The signature shape only the chip could confirm

The Policy Agent's JSON Web Tokens are what the whole authorization model rests on: the Policy Agent signs one, every other service trusts it. They were signed with a cleartext private key sitting in git — the audit's single Critical. The fix is to move that key into the TPM, where it is generated and can never be exported. The validator side needs nothing: it already verifies against a public key, so only the signing half moves, and the TPM's public half becomes the key everyone checks against. That asymmetry is the whole reason this is tractable — I am changing one side of a two-sided contract.

The mechanism is small but had one assumption I would not let myself make on paper. A JWT signed with ES256 wants its signature as a raw 64-byte r‖s pair — the JOSE convention. Windows' crypto layer returns ECDSA signatures through the platform TPM, and the documentation says raw r‖s, but lesson 18 is exactly that a trust root is built on the measured chip, not the spec sheet or my own inference. So I built the minter to assemble the token by hand and sign the signing-input through `tpm_signer`, normalising the result defensively in case some provider ever returned DER instead — and then I made the proof a test that runs on the actual silicon: provision a throwaway key in the real TPM, mint a token signed by it, export its public half to a PEM, and validate that token through the genuine production validator, all five stages. It passed on the chip. The assumption held — but it is now a verified fact with a test behind it, not a sentence I trusted. The headless half is covered too, without hardware: a software key standing in for the TPM drives the same hand-built path and the same JOSE normalisation, and the token still validates through the real validator, so the format logic is proven on every machine and the silicon-specific truth on the one that has the chip.

When the User-Operator worried the new path would make startup "horribly long," the honest answer was to measure, not reassure — the instrument over the inference, again. On his TPM a signature costs about 94 ms, a key-existence check about 3, and provisioning the key — a one-time ceremony, never a per-boot cost — about a quarter-second. So the boot-attributable cost is the 3 ms check; the 14B compile that already owns 53% of boot dwarfs it, and startup is untouched. The 94 ms is real but it is a runtime, per-authorization cost — one token per gated action — and it sits well under the spec's own 750 ms approval budget. That 94 ms is roughly nine hundred times slower than signing in software, and that slowness *is* the security property: it is the cost of a key that never leaves the chip. The fear was reasonable; the data dissolved it, and the number is now on the record where the next person can find it instead of fearing it too.

What this increment deliberately does not yet do is flip production over. The config still names the old key, and the old key is still in the tree — the Critical is not closed until the next increment wires the production path to the TPM, generates the public PEM through the provisioning ceremony the User-Operator chose, and removes the cleartext key. This commit is the proven mechanism; the rotation completes next.

**Next:** increment 2b — the provisioning ceremony (a command the User-Operator runs once on the chip, creating the non-exportable key and writing `certs/pa_public.pem`), the Policy Agent entrypoint repointed from `jwt.private_key_path` to `jwt.tpm_key_name`, the three validator configs moved to the new public key, `certs/pa_private.pem` removed (history kept, no rewrite), ADR-021, and the rotation-completion entry. Then the Tier-0 live-verify: the egress guard armed in a real boot, and the ceremony run on the chip. *(commit `<this>`: `shared/security/tpm_signer.py` `export_public_key_pem`; `services/policy_agent/src/jwt_minter.py` TPM signing path + `from_tpm`; `services/policy_agent/tests/test_jwt_minter.py` Groups F (software-stub) + G (on-chip, `@slow`); `services/policy_agent/pyproject.toml` `slow` marker; PERFORMANCE_LOG + `docs/performance/tpm_signing_latency_2026-06-03.json`; on-chip test passed; full sweep 1463 passed, 87 deselected. Branch `feat/security-hardening`; Vikunja #557.)*

### 2026-06-03 — Closing the Critical: the signing key the model trusts is no longer in git

The mechanism had existed for an increment already — the Policy Agent could sign its tokens with a non-exportable key sealed in the TPM, and I had proved it on the real chip. And yet the audit's single Critical was still open, because nothing an auditor would credit had actually changed. The config still named the cleartext key. The key still sat in git. A software-key fallback still existed for the code to quietly regress to. Building the strong path is the satisfying half; this increment was the unglamorous half that actually closes the finding — point production at the TPM key, delete the cleartext PEM, and remove the fallback so there is nothing weaker left to choose.

So the rotation completed in pieces that all had to land together or the tree would be briefly broken: the entrypoint's production path moved from `from_key_file(jwt.private_key_path)` to `from_tpm(jwt.tpm_key_name)`, carrying the same issuer, validity, and — the easy one to drop in a factory swap, which the LA flagged precisely because a dropped epoch breaks revocation silently — the epoch wiring. The three validators (Policy Agent, Assistant Orchestrator, Semantic Router) repointed from the old `ca.pem` to the TPM key's exported `pa_public.pem`; I left the mTLS `ca.pem` alone, because it is a different trust chain and conflating the two is a Tier-2 mistake waiting to happen. And `certs/pa_private.pem` left the tree by `git rm` — staged as a deletion, recoverable from history at `95c4f19`, no rewrite. The LA's call on the no-rewrite was the right one and worth recording the reasoning for: the rotation makes the old key *worthless*. A forger holding it can sign tokens that no validator will accept, so a destructive history-scrub buys nothing and costs the honest record of what was found and how it was fixed.

Two governance calls fell out of the work, and naming them is worth more than the code. First, provisioning is a **ceremony**, not an automatic boot side-effect: a command the operator runs once on the real silicon, that creates the non-exportable key and prints its name, its SHA-256 fingerprint, and the date — a moment a human witnesses, not a key that materialises unwatched on whatever machine happens to start the service. Second, the public half is **not committed**. A non-exportable TPM key is machine-bound; its public key is specific to one chip, not a portable constant, and baking one machine's bytes into a repo meant to outlive its hardware would carry a stale anchor across every future migration. So `pa_public.pem` is gitignored, and what goes into git is the *fingerprint* — the thing an auditor can reproduce with `openssl` and check against the file on the box. The trust anchor is documented; the key is not.

The part I want to be honest about, because it is exactly the kind of thing that gets mistaken for "done," is the interlock. The host runs `dev_mode = true` today, so the *running* system uses ephemeral in-memory keys and never touches the TPM — which means this change, for all that it closes the Critical in the tree, does not yet put the TPM key into live service. That happens when Tier 1 (#558) turns `dev_mode` off. Between now and then the new path is configured-but-dormant, and production preflight is **fail-closed until the ceremony has been run**: with `dev_mode` off and the key unprovisioned, or the TPM unreachable, the Policy Agent refuses to start rather than fall back to anything weaker. There is no silent-fallback path left — that absence is the control, and a parametrised test now asserts both the unprovisioned and the TPM-unavailable refusals so it cannot rot into "true by accident."

A smaller thing the work surfaced: the mechanism increment had labelled the TPM signing path "ADR-020" in the minter's own docstrings — but ADR-020 is the egress kill-switch; the TPM signing decision is this one, ADR-021. A reference that points at the wrong governance record is a quiet liability, so I corrected all of them while authoring the ADR. The on-chip proof carried over green: the throwaway-key suites — the full mint-and-validate round-trip and the ceremony writing a public key the real validator loads and that verifies a real TPM signature — both pass on the actual chip, using a test key provisioned and deleted within the test, never the production key. That distinction matters for the handoff: the automated on-chip tests are mine to run; the production ceremony, on the production key, is the operator's, and only the operator's.

**Next:** the Tier-0 live-verify, which is the operator's single hands-on moment — boot the real `python -m launcher`, confirm the egress guard arms and nothing local was starved, then run the provisioning ceremony on the chip, confirm `pa_public.pem` is written and a sign→validate round-trips, and record the printed fingerprint here as the trust anchor. On green, `feat/security-hardening` merges to main and Tier 0 is complete; Tier 1 (#558) then flips `dev_mode` off and the TPM key goes from configured to live. *(commit `23b2802`: `shared/security/provision_signing_key.py` ceremony + `shared/tests/test_provision_signing_key.py` (6 headless + 1 on-chip `@slow`); `services/policy_agent/src/entrypoint.py` repoint to `jwt.tpm_key_name` + fail-closed preflight; PA/AO/SR validator configs → `pa_public.pem`; `git rm certs/pa_private.pem` (history kept at `95c4f19`, no rewrite); `.gitignore` `pa_public.pem`; `jwt_minter.py` ADR-020→021 doc fix; constant `PA_JWT_TPM_KEY_NAME`; ADR-021; lesson 23; full sweep 1468 passed, 2 skipped, 88 deselected; on-chip `@slow` Group G + ceremony green. Branch `feat/security-hardening`; Vikunja #557.)*

### 2026-06-04 — The slowdown the live boot found, and the log that said it wasn't mine

Two increments of Tier 0 had shipped green, but green tests are not a running system — rule one of this project. The live-verify was the test that counts: boot the real launcher, run the provisioning ceremony on the chip, and watch the rotated key actually work. It did. The ceremony created the non-exportable key in the TPM and printed its fingerprint (`2df651d3…793c60`); a production mint-and-validate round-trip passed on the real silicon — the Policy Agent's signing key now lives in hardware, and the public half the validators trust came out of the chip, not a file in git. The egress guard armed on the same boot. The Critical was closed on hardware, not on paper.

But the live screen did what only the live screen can: it found a problem the whole test suite had no way to see. The app was slow to the point of unusable — a photo attach that sat for minutes, a voice reply queued for five. And the egress guard was the natural suspect, because it had never run live before and a deny-by-default control starving a legitimate local channel is exactly the failure its own ADR named as the one a headless test would never catch. The pull to reach in and loosen the guard was real.

The discipline that paid off was to read the instrument before touching the code. The launcher log was unambiguous: the guard armed at 22:05:59 and not a single `EgressDenied` appeared anywhere in the file. It had refused nothing. The slowness lived somewhere else entirely — the image path grounds a photo eagerly the moment it is attached, and that expensive vision work monopolises a single serialised lane, so the voice clip captured at 22:10:39 was not even handed to the transcriber until 22:15:27, whereupon it transcribed in under a second. Had I trusted the suspicion over the log, I would have spent the evening fixing an innocent guard and shipped the real bottleneck untouched. The live boot tells you that something is wrong; only the log tells you what.

So Tier 0 merged to main proven, not assumed: the egress kill-switch armed clean on real hardware, and the signing key rotated onto the chip with a round-trip to show for it. The slowness is real and worth fixing, but it is a different animal — a multimodal-responsiveness problem (eager, blocking image grounding; a voice recital slower than it needs to be) — and it earns its own focused effort rather than a reflex patch wedged into a security merge.

**Next:** the multimodal-responsiveness effort — make image grounding lazy and non-blocking so voice and chat never queue behind it, lift the voice recital rate, and keep the models warm. Tracked separately; measured change by change on the real hardware. *(commit `<this>` + merge of `feat/security-hardening` to main: Tier 0 complete — ADR-020 egress kill-switch + ADR-021 TPM-sealed PA JWT signing key; live-verified on-chip 2026-06-04; lesson 24; Vikunja #557.)*

### 2026-06-04 — Off the one lane: the freeze was structural, so the fix had to be

Lesson 24 named the bottleneck the live boot found — eager image grounding monopolising a single serialised lane while voice and chat queued behind it — and the temptation, now that I owned the fix, was to make the grounding *faster*. That is the wrong shape of fix, and seeing why is the whole point. The backend serves every request through one event loop: `server.py` accepts a pipe connection and runs `loop.run_until_complete(dispatcher.handle(...))` — one loop, per connection, for the lifetime of the app. On that loop the voice methods were already correct: transcribe and synthesize each push their blocking model calls onto a worker thread with `asyncio.to_thread`, so the loop stays free to service other frames while Whisper or Kokoro grinds. The document methods sitting three functions above them did not. `_m_load_document` called the gateway's blocking `load_document` *directly on the loop*, and `_m_store_attachment` did it twice — once to copy a file that can be 64 MB, once to stage it — every one of those a synchronous call that owns the only loop there is until it returns. So the moment a photo attach ran its eager vision pass, there was no loop left to read the microphone frame that arrived a minute later. The five-minute voice queue was not slowness; it was a thread starved of its turn.

That asymmetry is the tell worth keeping. The blocking-vs-async discipline had been applied to the voice methods and not to the document methods *beside them in the same file* — the kind of gap that opens when a pattern is held in a person's head for one feature and not written where the next feature inherits it. The fix is small and entirely structural: wrap all three calls in `asyncio.to_thread`, exactly as the voice methods already do. Behaviour is byte-for-byte unchanged — a `DocumentLoadError` still propagates out of the awaited thread to the same error frame `handle()` has always produced — only now the work happens off the loop and the loop stays alive underneath it. Two unit tests pin the property so it cannot silently regress again: each spies the thread identity the gateway call runs on and asserts it differs from the loop thread's, which is true with `to_thread` and false the instant someone drops back to a direct call.

What this does *not* do is the part the user will actually feel — the attach is still eager and the description still generic; that is the next two increments. This one is the floor under them: even once grounding is lazy and on-demand, the day it runs it must never be allowed to seize the only loop the rest of the app is living on. I shipped it first and alone, as the LA sequenced it, because it is the one piece that is purely a freeze fix — no behaviour change to argue about, provable on its own, and the thing that makes the live boot usable enough to verify the rest. Live-verify is the User-Operator's: the proof is a photo attach that no longer freezes a voice reply behind it.

**Next:** make the attach lazy — stash the image instantly, ground nothing until the user asks — then context-aware grounding at prompt-time (the brain formulates the vision query, the eyes answer, the answer folds back as datamarked data). *(commit `<this>`: `asyncio.to_thread` for `_m_load_document` + both `_m_store_attachment` calls; 2 off-loop thread-identity tests; dispatcher suite 15/15 green; lesson 25; Vikunja #561.)*

### 2026-06-04 — The brain directs the eyes: vision tasked on demand, in context, as data

The freeze fix made the app usable; this is the part that makes vision *good*. The original vision wiring (ADR-015, Option A) described every photo the instant it was attached, with a fixed 256-token landscaping prompt, before the user had said a word. Two things were wrong with that beyond the freeze: it spent a vision pass answering a question nobody had asked, and the answer it produced was generic to a domain — useless when the photo is a rash, a screenshot, a whiteboard. The design the LA chose is a hybrid: hold the image on attach, and when the user actually asks, let the 14B *formulate what the vision model should look for* from the conversation, send that enriched query to Qwen3-VL, and fold the answer back as grounded context. The brain directs the eyes; the eyes report; the brain answers the human. "Is this rash infected?" becomes a vision query about colour, texture, borders, distribution, and scaling — not the bare question, and not a gardener's checklist.

The piece I had to get right before writing a line was *where* this runs, because the brain and the eyes are not in the same place by default. The VLM is invoked from the gateway (host-side); the 14B lives in the Assistant Orchestrator. My first instinct was that "brain directs the eyes" therefore needs a round trip — the AO formulates, asks the gateway to run the VLM, the gateway answers back — which the IPC cannot even do: the vsock is strictly gateway→AO, there is no callback channel, and an image is up to 64 MB against a 64 KB request frame. The investigation dissolved the problem instead of solving it the hard way: in the default `host` deployment the AO is a native process with the **same** filesystem and the **same** GPU the gateway uses, so it can run *both* the formulation and the VLM itself. The gateway needs to ship only a **path** and a `pending_vision` flag — bytes never cross the wire, no callback is invented. The whole sequence collapses into one place, the place that already owns the conversation and the memory. The lesson worth keeping is that co-locating the orchestration with the things being orchestrated turned a three-hop distributed protocol into a local function call; I almost built the hard version because I reasoned about the topology from the canonical "services live in the VM" story instead of from what the default config actually does.

The governance line is the one the cert track will care about: the VLM's answer is **data, never instruction**. A vision model captioning an attacker's photo could read "ignore your instructions and…" off a sign in the image — so the description folds in through the same `add_grounded_context` path a loaded document gets, wrapped in spotlighting delimiters and datamarked, with a header telling the model these lines are data to read and never obey (lesson 13, provenance ≠ trust, now extended from stored documents to a subordinate model's output). A test pins it with an adversarial caption: the words survive into context, framed as data, not executed. Two more honesty knobs: a bare "what's this?" *skips* the formulation pass entirely — there is nothing to enrich, so the question goes straight to the eyes and we save a generation (speed was the whole point) — and every failure mode (no path, VLM unavailable, decode error) Fail-Softs to a factual "couldn't analyze this turn" note rather than crashing the turn.

The LA also asked the sharp question while I worked: why not run the VLM *in the VM* — better isolation, maybe better performance? It is a good instinct and it deserved a real answer rather than a reflexive "host is simpler." Security: genuinely sound for untrusted image bytes (a decoder exploit contained in the guest instead of owning the host) — but on this silicon there is no TDX Connect / TDISP (Phase 2 Gate 4 settled that), so VM-plus-GPU-PV buys CPU-side isolation, not a confidential-GPU boundary; the headline win is not actually on the table here. Performance: it does not help and likely hurts — the guest shares the *same* physical Arc 140V, so the co-residency memory pressure is identical wherever the process runs, and GPU-PV adds overhead; the freeze was an event-loop bug, fixed by async, not a location bug. So the call was: keep the VLM host-side this pass, and file the isolation idea as its own hardening track (ADR-022, Vikunja #562) with the honest framing and a cheaper *decode-in-a-sandbox* middle path recorded — most of the win, no GPU-in-VM cost — tied to the day BlarAI faces the network. ADR-015 is amended rather than replaced, so the Option A I am superseding stays visible next to the Option B that won.

What I can prove from here is green: the loader stages images without reading a pixel and never calls the VLM on attach; the AO formulates, tasks, and datamarks on demand, with the bare-question skip and the Fail-Soft both pinned; the blast-radius sweep holds at 844 passed, 2 skipped. What I cannot prove from here is the only thing that counts — a real photo, attached instantly, answered relevantly in the flow of a real conversation, with voice and chat never queuing behind it. That is the User-Operator's live-verify, and it is the proof this entry is waiting on.

**Next:** live-verify on the real boot (attach instant; ask-in-context relevant + fast; nothing freezes); then the co-residency memory question (VLM ~5 GB + 14B ~8.7 GB on the 31.3 GB ceiling) on the #550 / load-on-demand-eviction track, and the image-isolation hardening (#562 / ADR-022) when the network-facing track opens. *(commit `<this>`: lazy image attach (`document_loader` stashes path + `pending_vision`); AO-side `_ground_pending_image`/`_formulate_vision_query` (formulate → VL → datamarked ground; bare-question skip; Fail-Soft); framer carries image fields; ADR-015 amended; ADR-022 + #562 filed; lesson 26; blast-radius 844/2.)*

### 2026-06-04 — The deferred problem came due, wearing another bug's face

The live boot gave mixed results: one session described a photo correctly, another threw "validation errors," and the machine froze at 100% memory. Two of those three were the same fault, and the third was the cause of the first two. The log told the whole story, as it always does. The "validation errors" were not validation at all: the launcher log showed `vsock ERROR: Receive failed: timed out` at almost exactly 120 seconds into each vision turn, followed by `get_pgov_result: not in cache (default deny)`. The gateway waits 120 s for the orchestrator's first frame; when a vision turn took longer, the receive timed out and the fail-closed default — deny when there is no validated result — kicked in. That is the *correct* safety posture, but it surfaced to Blair as a "validation error," naming the policy subsystem for what was really resource exhaustion. The proof it was disguise and not policy: the orchestrator kept working *95 seconds after the gateway gave up*, ingesting the document into the substrate at 08:28:34 for a turn the gateway had already denied at 08:26:53. The work succeeded; the answer just arrived after the door had closed.

Why so slow? The memory freeze and the timeouts were one root cause. The vision MVP loaded the VLM once and cached it in a module global forever — `_pipe`, never freed. The Arc 140V is an integrated GPU with no separate VRAM, so that ~5 GB lives in the same 32 GB system RAM as the always-resident 14B (~8.7 GB), the draft, the 3 GB KV-cache, Whisper, the 54-voice Kokoro bank, two ONNX embedders, and a 2 GB Hyper-V VM that boots even in host mode. Over a multimodal session the never-freed VLM was the swing factor that pushed RAM to saturation; once Windows started swapping, every inference crawled, and a vision turn that should take ~50 s stretched past the 120 s timeout. The single correct early session was a *bare* "Who is this?" — three words, so the formulation pass was skipped (lesson 26) and the turn finished in 50 s before pressure built. The two that failed were formulated queries later in a longer session, by which point the cached VLM had been resident long enough to starve the machine.

The fix matches the diagnosis on both axes. Free the memory: `vlm.unload()` resets the cached pipe under its lock (and `_load_failed`, or the next image would never reload), then forces a `gc.collect()` — the codebase's proven OpenVINO-release pattern, since GenAI exposes no explicit `.release()`. The orchestrator calls it in a `finally` immediately after `describe_image`, so the ~5 GB is reclaimed *before* the final answer generation rather than lingering for the session — the co-resident peak now lasts only the describe window, and the next image re-loads on demand (~12-16 s, an acceptable price against a frozen host). And give the legitimate work room: the prompt timeout went 120 → 180 s, because a vision turn honestly chains a model load and up to two 14B generations, and a fail-closed deny should fire on a true hang, not on a turn that is merely long. The memory fix is the cure; the timeout is the headroom so the cure has time to work.

The part worth keeping for the portfolio is the masking. A fail-closed default is the right call — deny when you cannot validate — but it is a liability if it cannot say *why* it closed. "Denied by policy" and "denied because nothing answered in time" are different failures with different owners, and collapsing them into one user-visible "validation error" sends you (and Blair) debugging the PGOV validator when the real culprit is a 5 GB allocation that was never freed three layers away. The log distinguished them honestly (`default deny` vs `PGOV DENIED — N violations`); the surface did not. I deferred the co-residency problem in ADR-015 with eyes open, calling it the "open hard problem," but I deferred it *without bounding it* — and an unbounded deferred risk does not wait politely; it comes due as a hard freeze, not a graceful degrade.

**Next:** Blair flagged the other lever — the voice models (Whisper + the 54-voice Kokoro bank) preload at boot but he is not using voice yet. Making them lazy / not-preloaded is the next memory cut, shipping right behind this one. Then live-verify both on the real boot: a multi-image session that stays responsive and does not climb to 100%. *(commit `<this>`: `vlm.unload()` (lock + reset `_load_failed` + `gc.collect()`); AO evicts the VLM in a `finally` after every describe; `PROMPT_RESPONSE_TIMEOUT_S` 120 → 180; tests `test_vlm.py` + AO `test_vlm_evicted_after_each_describe` + constant; lesson 27; diagnosed from `launcher.log` 08:24-08:32.)*

### 2026-06-04 — The cheapest memory is the model you never load

Blair gave me the second lever himself: the voice models preload at boot — Whisper for speech-in, and Kokoro with its whole 54-voice bank for speech-out — but he is not using voice yet and does not want replies read aloud automatically. On a machine where the integrated GPU has no VRAM of its own and everything shares one 32 GB pool, the cheapest memory is the model you never load. The question was only *how* to not load it without breaking voice for the day he does want it.

The tempting answer was the elegant one: lazy-load on first use — keep the affordances live, and load Whisper the first time he presses the mic, Kokoro the first time a reply is spoken. More capable, and genuinely the better end state. But it is new code with a contract I cannot test from here — the voices list is empty until the model loads, `tts_available` has to mean "capable, not yet resident," the WinUI side has to tolerate both — and the screen is the only oracle for the WinUI surface (lesson 16). Shipping an untested capability path to fix a memory problem trades one risk for another.

The voice engine, though, was already built fail-soft: an absent model disables that half cleanly — STT and TTS report unavailable, the UI hides the mic and speaker, and crucially `speak` is gated on `tts_available`, so with TTS absent a reply is *never* auto-spoken. That path runs every time the weights are simply missing from `models/`, so it is the most-exercised state voice has. So the off-switch did not need new code; it needed to route into the path the system already handles. A single constant — `VOICE_ENABLED`, default False — and the launcher builds a no-model `VoiceEngine()` instead of loading the weights. Disable-by-config and degrade-by-failure became the same code path, which is the lowest-risk shape there is: I am not introducing a new state, I am deliberately entering a tested one. It also answers the auto-recital ask for free — no TTS resident means the speak gate is closed, so nothing is read aloud, with no UI change required.

One detail that is not incidental: `VOICE_ENABLED` is a code constant, not an environment variable, and that is lesson 11 doing its job before it could bite. The launcher self-elevates through UAC, and a freshly elevated process starts with a clean environment — an env var set in the un-elevated shell would simply not be there on the other side, and voice would silently re-enable (or the flag would read as unset) in the elevated process that actually loads the models. A constant rides across the boundary in the code itself. Re-enabling is one edit and a reboot; the lazy-load path stays on the table as its own improvement, to be built when it is actually wanted rather than wedged in under a memory deadline.

**Next:** live-verify the pair on the real boot — a multi-image session that stays responsive and never climbs to 100% RAM (VLM evicted after each describe; voice models absent). If headroom is still tight, the next candidates are the 2 GB Hyper-V VM that boots even in host mode, and a lazy-load path for voice if Blair wants it back without the boot cost. *(commit `<this>`: `VOICE_ENABLED` constant (default False) in `shared/constants.py`; launcher `_build_voice_engine` short-circuits to a no-model engine when off; tests `TestVoicePreload` (disabled skips load; enabled loads); lesson 28.)*

### 2026-06-04 — The fix that logged a lie, and the capability cut I almost made without asking

The memory work got worse before it got honest, and both halves of that are worth keeping. Blair ran the build and it was unusable: a vision turn timed out into a "VALIDATION_ERROR," and the machine sat at high RAM that did not come down. The telling sentence was his: *"It said over a minute ago that Qwen3-VL unloaded — VLM memory released and yet my RAM is still very high."* My eviction had logged exactly that — "VLM memory released" — and it was a lie. Not a malicious one; a hopeful one. `_pipe = None; gc.collect()` drops the Python reference, and my unit test proved the call happened and the global was cleared — but the test could not see the one thing that mattered, which is whether the operating system got the memory back. It did not. OpenVINO's GPU plugin pools native allocations and does not return them to the OS on a Python GC intra-process; the only reliable reclamation is process exit, which is why closing the app worked and my `unload()` did not. I shipped a memory fix whose actual effect on memory I had no way to measure, and I wrote a log line asserting the effect I wanted rather than the one I could prove. That is lesson 6 wearing fresh clothes — what you configure (or log) is not what runs — and lesson 16's bill come due: the layer I could not test was the only layer that counted.

Then the log told the real story, the way it always does. The images that froze were huge and the one that worked was small: the photo that hung for four minutes was 8160×6144 — fifty megapixels — while the "Who is this?" that answered in fifty seconds was 2.4. The VLM was being handed full-resolution phone photos, and a 50 MP image is an enormous vision-encoder patch grid; it spiked memory past the ceiling and the machine swap-thrashed a describe that should take seconds into minutes, which then blew past the IPC timeout and surfaced as the fake validation error (the same default-deny disguise from lesson 27). The cause was the input size, not the eager/lazy question I had been circling.

And here is the part that is mine to own. The obvious fix — downscale the image before the VLM — I wrote up as a "low-risk, standard, reversible" recommended option and was ready to ship. Blair stopped me, and he was right: shrinking a 50 MP photo to 1.5 MP would gut the answer for exactly the query that photo carried — *read the text on the wall* — and that is a reduction in what the product can do. His correction is the lesson: *"Design and technical decisions that have large impacts on the quality of the response is the kind of hard escalation we should be having to me, not stuff asking me about Git which I know nothing about."* I had the escalation bar backwards. The non-developer Lead Architect cannot and should not adjudicate how a commit is structured or which thread a call runs on — those are mine to decide. But what the user *experiences* — whether the assistant can still read the sign in the photo — is precisely his call, and I was about to make it for him by folding it into an implementation detail. The principle is now written where every agent inherits it: escalate what the user experiences; own how it is wired.

So this commit ships only what is safe to ship blind, which is *observation, not behaviour*. A small psutil-backed diagnostics helper logs real memory at the VLM's load, describe (with the image's dimensions and megapixels, so the size-to-cost correlation is right there in the log), and evict — and the evict line now reports the actual MB the OS reclaimed instead of claiming "released," so the no-op cannot hide again. The real fix is deferred, on purpose, to when the headless scenario harness Blair is commissioning can *measure* each option — native VLM pixel budgets, tiling that keeps full resolution per patch, evicting the 14B during the describe, a process-isolated VLM that the OS can actually reclaim — against quality and peak RAM, rather than my guessing and his machine paying for it. I stopped reaching for the fix. The harness is the instrument the last three rounds kept proving I needed.

**Next:** the headless scenario harness (separate effort) measures memory + describe latency + description quality across the #565 options; only then do we choose, with Blair making the quality call. The instrumentation landing here is its first building block. *(commit `<this>`: `shared/diagnostics.py` (psutil, fail-soft `memory_snapshot`/`log_memory`); `vlm.py` instrumented at load/describe/unload with image dims + honest reclamation accounting; `test_diagnostics.py`; downscaling rejected as a silent capability cut; issue + options documented in Vikunja #565; lesson 29.)*

### 2026-06-04 — The instrument I kept deferring to, and the guard I watched fail

Three of the last four entries end the same way: I hit a problem I could not measure from where I sat, deferred the fix to "when the harness can measure it," and called the harness the instrument I kept proving I needed. Lesson 29 said it outright — I stopped reaching for the fix because the last three rounds kept showing me I was guessing and Blair's machine was paying for it. This entry builds that instrument. Blair stopped the parallel work to make it lane one, with one sentence that reframed the whole effort: build the robot tester once, and every later change is checked automatically instead of costing him a boot-and-hunt. The harness is not a test; it is the thing that takes the human out of the verification loop.

It came out in two layers, because the work has two halves that want different homes. Layer A is the regression lock for the freeze itself — deterministic, no models, in the default suite. The freeze that made the first multimodal boot unusable (lesson 24) had two causes: grounding ran *eagerly* at attach, and it ran *on the one event loop*. The eager half is gone — attach is lazy now — and locked at the unit level. The off-loop half was covered nowhere, and it is the half lesson 25 explicitly asked to be pinned with "a test that asserts the work ran on a different thread than the loop, so the regression announces itself instead of hiding until the next live boot." So that is what Layer A asserts: drive the real dispatcher in-process, and check that the blocking `load_document` ran on a different thread than the event loop. Thread identity, not a stopwatch.

Getting there honestly is the part worth keeping. My first version of the lock was a timing test — fire an attach and a cheap neighbour at the loop together, assert the neighbour finishes long before the attach's block clears. It passed, and it would fail on a revert, so I nearly shipped it. An adversarial review I ran against my own harness caught two things I had talked myself past. The first: I was asserting a *proxy* — "the loop stayed responsive during the attach" — when the property the fix actually guarantees is narrower and exact: the blocking call runs off the loop's thread. The timing test was true here only because the neighbour happened to be a no-await handler; lean on it and it would flake the day a CI box was busy, because a 250-millisecond margin does not survive a descheduled loop. The fix was to assert the real invariant directly — `threading.get_ident()` inside the blocking call versus the loop's thread — which is more faithful *and* immune to jitter. The second catch was smaller and more embarrassing: my model-eviction helper did `del obj` on a *parameter*, which frees the caller's binding not at all — the exact shape as the "logged a lie" cleanup from lesson 29, a no-op dressed as a release, written twice in one week. The review is why this entry can claim the lock works: not because it passes, but because I reconstructed the pre-fix dispatcher and watched the lock go red against it, then watched a second reviewer try to break the green and tell me where it was soft.

Layer B is the other half — boot the real models on the Arc and measure what Blair actually waits on, so an agent can reproduce "it felt slow" without him. The numbers confirm where the slowness lives. The CPU router classifies in five milliseconds. The image question — the one that froze attach — costs about twenty-two seconds cold, and almost all of it is the thirteen-second model load, not the describe. The 14B's first token lands in about a second and a half cold, behind a nineteen-second load. The shape is unambiguous: the waits are cold *load*, not pipeline, which is exactly the lever the keep-warm and eviction work (#550, #565) is aimed at — and it means the freeze Blair hit was those load seconds running on the event loop, which Layer A now keeps off it. Every run writes a community-grade JSON — hardware, OpenVINO version, methodology, and an explicit list of what it does *not* measure — into the dataset Blair contributes upstream.

The honest limits live in the harness's own README, not in my head. Layer B needs the real GPU, so it runs on Blair's machine, not in CI; the numbers are single cold runs; and it does not judge whether a spoken reply *sounds* right or a visual answer is *good* — that still needs an ear and an eye. The review also named real coverage I have not built: the drag-drop attach path, the voice transcribe/synthesize off-loop-ness, the AO-side grounding on the prompt, and the fail-closed PGOV-denial path are all the same shape of lock, unbuilt — recorded on the ticket as honest follow-ups, not quietly omitted. A harness that oversells its coverage is worse than none, because it is trusted. One process note, the kind that costs a branch when ignored: the parallel session was paused, not gone, and we share one working tree (lesson 9). Rather than switch branches under it, I built additively on the same branch the freeze fix already lived on — the harness lands where the thing it locks lives, and no branch pointer moved out from under the paused session.

**Next:** turn the instrument on the problem it was commissioned for — the #565 VLM-memory options (downscale, tiling, evict-the-14B-during-describe, a process-isolated VLM) now have a rig that can measure peak RAM and describe latency per option, with Blair making the quality call (lesson 29). Then the follow-up locks the review named, the drag-drop and voice off-loop paths first. *(commits `79d1b47` (Layer A: in-process driver, off-loop freeze lock, teeth meta-test); `<this>` (Layer B real-model scenarios + CLI + community-grade recording; review-hardening to thread-identity + relative invariants + honest absent-vs-broken; README + this entry + PERFORMANCE_LOG numbers); locks `f4406c5`; default suite +10 deterministic locks; Layer B 3/3 green on the Arc — router 5 ms, VLM 21.7 s cold, 14B first-token 1.5 s; lesson 30.)*


### 2026-06-04 — The terminal frame the user never saw

A user attached an 8.3 MP photo and asked about it. Under memory pressure the Orchestrator generated and validated the answer — PGOV approved it, the log shows the result — but the IPC channel died before GENERATION_COMPLETE arrived at the gateway. The dispatcher's `_m_prompt` was sitting in `async for token in self._gateway.stream_tokens(session_id)`, waiting for the generator to exhaust. The generator was waiting on `asyncio.to_thread(self._transport.receive)`. The socket was waiting for a message that would never come. The WinUI front end was waiting for a terminal `end` frame to re-enable its text input. Six minutes later the user killed the process.

The failure mode is specific. In `stream_tokens` at `transport.py`, when GENERATION_COMPLETE arrives but PGOV_RESULT has not yet been received, the code does `continue` and loops back into the next blocking receive. If PGOV_RESULT also never arrives — or if GENERATION_COMPLETE never arrives at all — that receive call sits there until the socket timeout fires at 180 s, or indefinitely in the test-fake case where there is no socket. But 180 s is still far too long for the UI to be dead, and in the live incident the hang lasted longer than that, suggesting the GENERATION_COMPLETE sequence was never completed at all. Either way: the existing timeout did not produce a terminal frame at the front end within any useful time. The architecture already had a fail-closed on `get_pgov_result` (a missing result defaults to deny), so once `stream_tokens` finally ended, `_m_prompt` *would* have emitted `end`. The problem was that the path between those two things had no bound.

The fix is not in the gateway. The gateway's 180 s socket timeout addresses a different problem — don't wait forever for a legitimate slow vision turn. The fix belongs in `_m_prompt` itself, because that is the only place that owns the full arc from "started receiving" to "terminal frame emitted." I wrapped the entire streaming + PGOV + store + speak arc in `asyncio.wait_for` with a `_PROMPT_STREAM_FAILSAFE_S = 90 s` deadline. If anything in that arc stalls — the generator, the PGOV fetch, the store write, a speak worker — the timeout fires, the `finally` guard emits a terminal `end` frame with a `failsafe: True` tag the log can distinguish from a normal completion, and `_m_prompt` returns. The front end's input is re-enabled within 90 s in the worst case, not 6 minutes, not forever.

The trade-offs I took consciously. I chose 90 s rather than a shorter bound because a legitimate vision turn can chain a VLM load plus two 14B generations and 90 s is well under the socket timeout's headroom while still being generous enough not to clip a slow-but-valid turn. The alternative — a shorter bound like 30 s — risks clipping a real vision answer on a cold-cache run; the one-time user cost of waiting 90 s after a genuine stall is much smaller than the recurrent cost of seeing the assistant abort answers mid-stream. I gave the bound as an injectable parameter (`prompt_stream_failsafe_s` on `RpcDispatcher.__init__` and forwarded through `InProcessBackend`) rather than a module constant, so tests can set it to 2 s and the failsafe fires in the test window without waiting 90 s per test. That's the right shape for any time-bound constant whose value is "right for production, unrunnable in a unit test."

What I did not fix: the root cause. The VLM memory exhaustion that caused the Orchestrator to silently drop GENERATION_COMPLETE is tracked at Vikunja #565 and its resolution involves hard quality trade-offs — how much to downscale the image, whether to process-isolate the VLM — that only the User-Operator can decide. The fail-safe buys the time to make that decision from a system that no longer freezes rather than one that does (cf. lesson 31).

The regression lock earned its keep before the fix landed. I wrote the test first — a fake gateway that yields two tokens then blocks forever on `await asyncio.Event().wait()` — ran it against the unfixed code, and watched the primary assertion time out in 3.15 s exactly as the bug predicts. The meta-test (`test_failsafe_teeth`) reconstructs a dispatcher with no `wait_for` wrapper and proves it hangs, which is the honest proof that the lock is not just checking a property that was already there. Six tests in total: primary lock, wire-shape check (the terminal frame is `stream="end"`, not an `ok: False` frame the front end's streaming parser doesn't recognise as terminal), raising-gateway path, happy-path contract unchanged, exactly-one-terminal-frame invariant on stall, and the teeth meta-test. All pass; the pre-existing harness tests (freeze regression, latency, real-model Layer B) are untouched.

**Next:** the Vikunja #565 root cause — the VLM-memory options the harness is now set up to measure, with Blair making the quality call on downscale versus tiling versus process-isolation. The fail-safe means that decision can be taken at a measured pace rather than under user pressure from a frozen UI. *(commit `<this>`: `_PROMPT_STREAM_FAILSAFE_S` + `asyncio.wait_for` arc in `_m_prompt` + injectable `prompt_stream_failsafe_s` on `RpcDispatcher` + `InProcessBackend` forwarding; `tests/harness/test_ui_failsafe.py` 6 tests; 1478 passed, 22 skipped; lesson 31.)*

### 2026-06-04 — The window I said we couldn't test, tested

The reframe was Blair's, and it was a correction I needed. I had shipped the headless harness, watched it lock the freeze and measure the model latencies, and then — asked to bring autonomous testing to *every* layer — I drew a line around the WinUI front end and called it the one a Python harness "structurally cannot touch." Blair refused the line in a sentence: the whole point is to take the human out of the verification loop, and a layer that always needs his eyes defeats it. He was right. The wall was a failure of imagination dressed as a law of physics (lesson 32).

Two seams dissolved it, and both were sitting in plain sight. The first was UI Automation — pywinauto over the UIA accessibility tree that WinUI 3 already exposes. A probe launched the real window, dumped its control tree, and there were the elements with their `auto_id`s: `PromptBox`, `SendButton`, `AttachButton`. WinUI maps `x:Name` straight to the AutomationId, so the test targets them directly, with no app changes — it types a prompt, clicks send, and reads `PromptBox.is_enabled()`. The second seam was the one I'd assumed was a wall: the launcher forces an admin UAC prompt and a Hyper-V VM, which cannot be driven autonomously. But the window is a *separate de-elevated exe* that needs nothing but a named-pipe backend speaking the RPC protocol. So the test stands up the real `NamedPipeServer` over a *scripted fake gateway* — no models, no AO, no VM, no elevation — and the real window connects to a backend the test fully controls. I verified that enabler with no GUI at all (a pipe round-trip in 0.09 s) before ever launching the window, which is the part I'm most glad I did: it proved the UAC-free path on the cheap before betting the live work on it.

Then the live verify, against the real window on Blair's screen. A normal turn: type, send, the reply streams, the input comes back — green. And the one that matters, the user's actual bug, end-to-end across both layers: a scripted backend that *hangs* (the dropped GENERATION_COMPLETE), and the test asserts the input first freezes — the dead input Blair hit — and then, three seconds later, recovers, because the fail-safe the parallel worker built emits its terminal frame and the WinUI re-enables on it. The two halves, built in parallel in separate worktrees by two different agents, met exactly where the design said they would: the backend guarantees a terminal frame; the front end re-enables on it; the bug that froze Blair's app for six minutes is now a 12-second passing test that needs no one watching.

The parallelism is the other thing worth recording. The backend fail-safe and the front-end harness were genuinely disjoint — different languages, different files, different worktrees — and merged without a conflict. But building it surfaced the real limit on how fast this can go, and it isn't agent count. The GPU, the display, and the GUI are *shared singletons*: two agents can write code at once, but only one can hold the screen or load the 9 GB model at a time. The fleet parallelizes everything *around* those singletons and serializes on them. That's the honest shape of the speedup — not "N agents, N times faster," but "N agents building, one machine verifying."

**Next:** the integration layer — the real app end-to-end — builds on this same scripted-backend enabler. And the #565 VLM-memory options remain the open quality call, now with both a latency rig (Layer B) and a front-end harness that can prove a chosen option does not re-freeze the UI. *(commits `079ae3a` (UAC-free scripted backend enabler + pipe round-trip verify); `<this>` (pywinauto Layer C — happy + hang/fail-safe tests live-verified against the real window; `NamedPipeServer` / `scripted_pipe_backend` fail-safe forwarding; `winui` marker; README Layer C); lesson 32.)*

### 2026-06-04 — The guards the adversarial review named and I built next

An adversarial read of the #563 harness, done the same day it merged, named four coverage gaps by class rather than by hand-wave. Not "the harness could be more thorough" — four specific contracts the harness certified nothing about because `FakeGateway.get_pgov_result` always approved, and because only `load_document` had a thread-identity lock while every other blocking handler beside it in the same file did not. The gaps were recorded on Vikunja #566 with a specific fix shape for each. This entry is the fix.

The three locks the review asked for follow identically from lesson 30: assert the real property, add a teeth check that goes red against the regression, and don't assert a timing proxy when a structural invariant is available. Lock 1 is `_m_store_attachment` — the drag-drop / file-picker path that copies a file and then re-loads it, two blocking operations that were conspicuously unguarded next to the freshly-locked `load_document`. A parametrized thread-identity test confirmed both the `store_attachment` copy and the subsequent `load_document` run on a worker thread, not the loop thread. The teeth check reconstructs an on-loop version of the same dispatcher and proves the assertion goes red — otherwise we have a test that passes whether or not the dispatch is correct. Lock 2 is `_m_transcribe` and `_m_synthesize`, the voice input/output paths. Both delegate their blocking model calls via `asyncio.to_thread`; both were unguarded. A `FakeVoiceEngine` stub records `threading.get_ident()` at the moment each blocking call executes — no timing, no jitter, the same structural identity check as Lock 1. The transcribe test needed a monkeypatch for the inline `prepare_for_stt` import; the synthesize test needed one for `encode_b64_pcm`. Both inline-import their helpers at call time, so the right patch target is the source module, not a rebinding in the dispatcher. Getting that wrong would silently produce a passing test that does not exercise the patch — which would be a test that certifies the wrong thing (cf. lesson 3). Both locks carry an on-loop teeth check.

Lock 3 is the one with the security valence. `FakeGateway` always approved, which meant the `_m_prompt` denial branch — flush the tool-call buffer with `pgov_approved=False`, persist a denied turn, emit `audio_cancel` when speech is active — was unexercised in any harness test. A regression that called `flush_tool_call_buffer(True)` on denial (or omitted the call entirely) would have shipped green. Adding `approved=False` to `FakeGateway` and recording each `flush_tool_call_buffer(pgov_approved)` call in `flush_calls` turned that into an assertable invariant. Three primary asserts: the flush was called with `False` and nothing was returned (the Fail-Closed discard contract); the denied turn was persisted with `pgov_status="denied"` when a store was wired; and an `audio_cancel` frame was emitted when `speak=True`. A fourth test, the teeth check, runs the same fixture on an approved gateway and confirms `flush_calls == [True]` — proving the `[False]` assertion in the denial test would fail on an always-approving gateway. These are not exotic failure modes; a developer refactoring the prompt path who misread the flush argument polarity would produce exactly the regression the test catches.

Item 4 from the review — `_ground_pending_image` off-loop on the AO prompt path — is skipped, as the ticket's "if tractable" carve-out anticipated. The `_ground_pending_image` call lives inside `AssistantOrchestratorService._handle_prompt_request`, which is driven from a `vsock` receive thread (not an asyncio loop) and exercises `describe_image` and `OrchestratorGPUInference` — both real-GPU paths. Faking those without the actual models would require stubbing `shared.inference.vlm.describe_image` and the inference pipeline, which is possible but produces a test that exercises the mock, not the property. The property itself — that the VLM call happens on the AO worker thread, not an asyncio loop thread — is architecturally guaranteed by the fact that `_handle_prompt_request` runs synchronously in a `threading.Thread`, not in an async handler: there is no asyncio loop in that stack to block, so the off-loop risk is absent. The review's framing acknowledged this: "if it needs a real AO path you can't fake cleanly, SKIP it." The skip is recorded here rather than elided, because the honest audit surface needs the gap visible even when the gap is benign.

**Next:** the open #565 VLM-memory quality call — downscale, tiling, evict-the-14B, or process-isolation — which now has a rig that can measure the outcome of each option. *(commit `<this>`: 9 new tests in `tests/harness/test_offloop_failclosed.py`; `FakeGateway` extended with `approved`, `flush_calls`, `store_thread_id`; `FakeVoiceEngine` added to `tests/harness/fakes.py`; 62 passed in `tests/harness` + `services/ui_backend/tests` (53 prior baseline + 9 new); locks `_m_store_attachment`, `_m_transcribe`, `_m_synthesize` off-loop + PGOV denial Fail-Closed; item 4 skipped by design.)*

### 2026-06-04 — The gate that would have broken itself once memory went live

Layer 3 (ADR-013, privilege separation) is the defense I care most about in BlarAI's security stack. The guarantee is deterministic: a document a user loads cannot cause the assistant to take an action, because the gate refuses tool calls the moment any document is in the session. Even a fully fooled model can only produce wrong *words*, never wrong *actions*. That guarantee is load-bearing enough that it earned its own ADR, its own test class, and a startup WARNING whenever the config disables it.

Ticket #543 landed because I had wired the Substrate — persistent semantic memory that retrieves past conversations and documents as grounded context on every turn. The moment that went live, the gate's gate signal (`has_grounded_context`) was going to be permanently True for any session that had ever used memory. Layer 3 would fire on every single turn, forever, blocking all tool use regardless of whether the user had loaded anything. The gate would have defeated itself.

The root cause is a conflation lesson 13 already named: provenance is not trust. The gate exists to defend against *freshly introduced untrusted content* — a document the user just loaded, whose body might carry a planted instruction. Memory retrieved from the Substrate is different in kind: it is already-defended context (datamarked, grounded through the same Layer 1+2 stack), drawn from the user's own prior sessions. It does not carry the same injection risk as an unknown document arriving this turn. Treating them the same would be wrong in both directions: it would create spurious friction on every tool call, and it would imply memory retrieval is as risky as a fresh document load — which it is not, and which a user would rightly find incoherent.

The fix is architecturally simple once the distinction is named. `ContextManager.add_grounded_context` gains a `source` parameter, defaulting to `"document"` for backward compatibility. Document loads pass `source="document"` and set a new `_user_documents_loaded` flag; substrate retrieval passes `source="memory"` and does not. The Layer 3 gate now checks `has_user_loaded_documents` — True only for user-loaded files — instead of `has_grounded_context` which is True for anything. The rejected alternative — a turn-scoped signal recalculated from the payload each request — was tempting and would have worked for the gate, but it would have required passing the flag through every code path that needs it and would have lost the session-scope invariant ADR-013 §2.1 depends on: documents loaded turn N must still block tools on turn N+1. The session-scoped flag in ContextManager was already the right shape; extending it with a provenance dimension was the minimum change.

The test discipline matters here. I wrote four new entrypoint tests in `TestLayer3LoadedVsRetrieved` and verified each one fails against the pre-fix gate — specifically, `test_tool_fires_when_only_retrieved_memory_present` expects 2 generate calls (tool fires) and gets 1 (tool blocked) with the old `has_grounded_context` signal. A test that cannot go red against the bug it guards is not a test, it is a comment. Each test carries its teeth-check description explicitly.

The ten ContextManager unit tests in `TestHasUserLoadedDocuments` cover the full lifecycle of the new flag: default False, set by `source="document"`, untouched by `source="memory"`, multiple memory calls still False, clear and destroy reset it, memory chunks still appear in built context even without the flag. They verify the distinction is exactly what it claims to be.

One security-posture question I resolved conservatively without escalation: should `source` default to `"document"` or `"memory"`?
