It is a different kind of test from the handshake tests — it is testing the *wiring* rather than the *protocol*.

The sprint netted 47 new tests (the full Group J / K / L block plus the existing groups, all green — the dir-failure test later split into a write-failure and an uncreatable-dir test during the merge-gate hardening), zero suite regressions on the full run (2056 passed, 20 skipped), one new module (`shared/security/cert_provisioning.py`), one ADR (`docs/adrs/ADR-026-Per-Boot-mTLS-Ephemeral-Certificates.md`), and wiring changes in `launcher/__main__.py` and `services/ui_gateway/src/transport.py`.

The merge-gate review caught a placement bug I had not reasoned through: I had put the mint step right after the dev_mode+network interlock, which is *before* the admin-elevation block. On a non-admin launch BlarAI re-launches itself elevated and the original process returns 0 — but my mint sat upstream of that return, so the non-elevated process would mint a full cert chain, exit, and the elevated process would mint *again*. A double-mint, and worse, the short-lived non-elevated process wrote the certs first. The fix was to move the entire block to a "Step 1.5" after `admin_ok` is recorded and before the VM starts — so it fires exactly once, in the elevated process, still ahead of all service construction. The lesson here is subtle and worth keeping: in a startup sequence that can *fork and exit* (the UAC re-launch is effectively a fork), the position of a side-effecting step relative to the fork point is load-bearing. I had treated "after the interlock, before service construction" as a single contiguous region; it is not — the elevation return-0 cuts it in two, and a write-effect on the wrong side of that cut runs twice. The cheap, idempotent-looking `provision_per_boot_certs()` is in fact a disk-write side effect, and side effects belong after the last point the process can still bail out and hand off to a successor.

A second finding surfaced during the merge-gate confirmations that I want on the record because it looked alarming and turned out fine — but only after tracing it. The merge-gate asked me to confirm `git status` shows zero `.pem`/`.key` files staged. My working set was clean, but I noticed `certs/ca.pem` already *tracked* in git (committed back in `95c4f19`), which made the gitignore entries I had added for it inert (gitignore does not apply to tracked files). My first instinct was "key material in git" — but the committed `ca.pem` is `-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----`, no private bytes, and ADR-021 §2 explains why: the JWT trust anchor *moved* from `certs/ca.pem` to `certs/pa_public.pem`, and the old `ca.pem` is now used only as the mTLS `[ipc]` CA path — the exact trust chain this sprint owns, which ADR-021 explicitly deferred to "Tier 2, #559." So my per-boot mint overwriting `certs/ca.pem` is correct: it clobbers a stale public-key placeholder that no live validator reads anymore, not the live JWT anchor. The residual is real but small — because `ca.pem` is still *tracked*, a production mint will leave it showing dirty in `git status`, and a careless `git add -A` could commit per-boot cert material. The clean fix is `git rm --cached certs/ca.pem` (and the sibling mTLS paths) so the gitignore I added actually takes effect. I did NOT do that here: it is an index mutation on a file the merge-gate did not scope me to touch, adjacent to a pre-existing committed artifact, and the merge-gate is doing an independent diff review — so I am surfacing it as a finding with the recommended fix rather than acting unilaterally on tracked state.

**Next:** EA-2 flips the `dev_mode` default to `false` for the HOST deployment mode, which will make this cert provisioning code run on every real launch. Before that flip lands cleanly, the `certs/ca.pem` (and `pa_server.pem` / `pa_server_key.pem` / `gateway_client*.pem`) tracked-placeholder files should be `git rm --cached`'d so the per-boot artifacts stay untracked. EA-4 is the on-chip live-verify — running the actual production startup sequence and confirming the vsock mTLS handshake completes against the running VM.

*(commits `bcda777` (mint + module + ADR + tests), `9857ac5` (SHA fix), `3fa2315` (merge-gate: mint moved after admin-confirmed + test hardening); merged to `main` `336fcc2`. 47 new tests in test_ipc_transport.py; full suite 2056 passed, 20 skipped; the `certs/*.pem` untrack residual was handed to EA-2 and done there.)*

### 2026-06-06 — Building the door without opening it

Sprint 15 EA-2 is the smallest delivery in the sprint by line count, and the most disciplined by design. The whole mandate is to prove that production posture can be activated, demonstrate that it resolves correctly, and then deliberately not activate it. The shipped running default stays dev. This is not procrastination — it is the correct sequencing when the activation would brick the daily-driver boot if fired before the Known-Good Manifest (EA-3) and the JWT TPM ceremony key (EA-4) exist.

The mechanism itself was already present before this EA began. `resolve_dev_mode` has accepted a `dev_mode_override` since Sprint 13 Decision 8 — passing `False` explicitly already resolves production posture. What EA-2 does is: prove the capability with a regression lock (lock-b: `dev_mode_override=False` on HOST resolves `False`), document the activation path clearly in the `launcher/__main__.py` comment block, add the permanent escape-hatch note to the docstring, and lay the four locks that will survive EA-4's flip.

The blast-radius audit found two tests in `test_dev_mode_interlock.py` that called `resolve_dev_mode(DeploymentMode.HOST)` without a `dev_mode_override` and were relying on the HOST-default returning `True`. Both were in places that test interlock or banner behaviour — semantically about "what happens when dev_mode=True" — not about the HOST default itself. I applied `dev_mode_override=True` to both (`test_dev_mode_true_emits_warning` and `test_post_fix_interlock_refuses_the_same_scenario`), with inline comments explaining the blast-radius rationale. The service tests (`test_entrypoint.py` for PA and AO) are not in the blast radius because they resolve `dev_mode` from TOML config (`[security] dev_mode = true`), not from `resolve_dev_mode(HOST)`. Zero test-suite damage: 2060 passed, 20 skipped, identical to the pre-EA-2 baseline.

One other item that rode in with EA-2: `certs/ca.pem` was still tracked in git despite `.gitignore` having listed it at line 130 since EA-1. EA-1's per-boot mint auto-generates this file on every production boot (`launcher/__main__.py:568`); the tracked copy is stale from before the mint was wired. The correct framing — and I was explicit about not getting this wrong — is that `ca.pem` IS read (it is the mTLS CA, consumed via `ipc.ca_cert_path` in both PA `default.toml:40` and AO `:88`). The untrack is right *because the per-boot mint regenerates it every boot*, not because "nothing reads it." Running `git rm --cached certs/ca.pem` made the `.gitignore` entry effective without touching the file on disk.

The trade-off I considered and rejected was merging the untrack into a separate cleanup commit. The spec explicitly folds it into EA-2, and the reasoning is sound: the untrack is pre-activation hygiene — a stale tracked file in a live minting path would show as dirty on every production boot and could be accidentally re-committed. Landing it alongside the mechanism locks keeps the activation-readiness surface clean for EA-3 and EA-4.

The comprehension gate recital before any code change confirmed the working set: `shared/security/dev_mode_guard.py` (docstring only), `launcher/__main__.py` (comment region only), `launcher/tests/test_dev_mode_interlock.py` (blast-radius overrides + four locks), and `certs/ca.pem` (untrack). No service code changed. The shipped HOST default is untouched — `resolve_dev_mode(runtime_mode)` at `launcher/__main__.py:463+11` still returns `True` for HOST. EA-4 owns the one-line flip.

**Next:** EA-3 stages the Known-Good Manifest and the substrate for the JWT TPM key that EA-4's ceremony will provision. EA-2 and EA-3 are working-set-disjoint; they can run in parallel off EA-1-merged main. The four regression locks I laid here will serve as the sentinel that EA-4's activation was not fired prematurely — lock (a) specifically asserts `resolve_dev_mode(HOST)` returns `True` without override, and will need to be updated to `False` as part of EA-4's activation commit.

*(commit `cbe5bb7`, merged to `main` `bab1219` — mechanism locks, blast-radius overrides, ca.pem untrack, docstring escape-hatch note, deferred-activation comment; 2060 passed, 20 skipped; activation landed later in EA-4b.)*

### 2026-06-06 — Testing the door before handing over the key

The gap EA-3 closes is simple to state: as of EA-2, the Policy Agent would refuse to start at `dev_mode=false` for three distinct reasons — the Known-Good Manifest (KGM) path is not set, the JWT TPM key name is not configured, and the JWT CA cert path is not configured. All three were wired in `default.toml` but unverified as a cascade. EA-3 stages the minimal preconditions and builds the off-chip proof.

The most important judgment call in this EA was what "staging the KGM" actually means off-chip. There are no real Qwen3-14B weights at build time — the model directory has a manifest with placeholder digests from earlier sprint work. The temptation is to treat this as a deficiency that blocks the harness. I rejected that framing: the cascade I need to de-risk is the *structural* cascade — manifest file exists, JSON has a `digests` dict, the value keyed by `openvino_model.bin` is 64 hex chars, the format is accepted by `_validate_security_material`. Whether that digest matches the actual bytes on this particular machine is irrelevant to proving the cascade structure; that match is EA-4's job when the real weights are present and the TPM key is provisioned. The path-not-taken was staging a minimal fake binary and computing a matching digest — which I also implemented in the harness fixture, since the `test_full_boot_cascade_green_at_dev_mode_false` test needs `model_bin_path` to resolve to a file on disk for the adjudicator build to reach completion. Both the repo-level stub manifest and the harness-local dummy binary serve distinct roles: the former labels the pre-existing placeholder digests in the committed manifest; the latter gives the harness a real computable digest that passes the `_validate_security_material` regex check.

The substrate comment fix was small in code but worth naming: the old comment at `services/assistant_orchestrator/src/entrypoint.py:1012-1014` said the MINOR-3 fix was "symmetric with the audit refuse-to-start and `build_session_store`." The first half of that is wrong — the substrate path degrades gracefully (AO starts, memory off), while the audit path raises `AuditProvisioningError` and the PA refuses to start entirely. The symmetry is only with `build_session_store` (both refuse the weak sealer). Calling it symmetric with the audit path was a documentation defect that would confuse whoever later reads the code to understand the posture divergence. The fix names the asymmetry explicitly: substrate is non-load-bearing, the audit trail is a governance control, and that distinction justifies different postures.

Isolation was the sharpest edge. The lesson from Sprint 14 is that an un-isolated boot harness running `dev_mode=false` can corrupt `sessions.db` by writing dev-key-encrypted rows that the production key cannot decrypt. The design here keeps the harness entirely inside pytest's `tmp_path` fixture, which is inside the throwaway `LOCALAPPDATA` dir the root `conftest.py` creates at process startup. `BLARAI_DEK_KEYSTORE` is popped by that same conftest so no substrate factory resolves to the real production keystore. The test `test_isolation_guard_no_real_keystore_touched` checks this invariant explicitly at runtime — if someone later rewires the conftest or adds a `monkeypatch.setenv("BLARAI_DEK_KEYSTORE", ...)` pointing at the real path, the guard catches it. I wrote that as a reject-the-merge condition, not just a nice-to-have.

TPM calls at construction vs. at signing was the subtlest implementation question. `_build_audit_log` in production creates a `TpmRecordSigner` and calls `tpm_signer.key_exists` before doing so — that's the call I patch to return `True`. The `TpmRecordSigner` object itself is constructible without a TPM (`ensure_key` is called lazily on first `sign`). Since the harness never calls `adjudicate_car`, no signing ever happens, and the harness completes without any real CNG/ncrypt call. The same applies to `AgenticJWTMinter.from_tpm`: it stores the key name but doesn't sign at construction. This means I need only one patch (`tpm_signer.key_exists`) plus the real ephemeral PEM cert for `AgenticJWTValidator.from_public_key_file` — no further TPM stubbing needed.

The cascade proof result: 7 tests, all green, in 0.57s. The three core cascade tests are: `test_config_validation_passes_at_dev_mode_false` (config-validation gate), `test_validate_security_material_passes_at_dev_mode_false` (security material gate), and `test_full_boot_cascade_green_at_dev_mode_false` (full measured-boot boot returns `True`). The four regression locks confirm fail-closed behavior stays intact: missing manifest → `PA_CFG_KGM_PATH_NOT_FOUND`; missing TPM key → `PA_CFG_JWT_TPM_KEY_NOT_PROVISIONED`; stub is not FUT-04; isolation guard holds.

Full suite post-EA-3: 2067 passed, 20 skipped, 15 deselected.

**Next:** EA-4 — the on-chip ceremony. The LA provisions the real TPM JWT key (`BlarAI-PA-JWT-Signing`) and the real TPM audit key (`BlarAI-Audit-Signing-Key-v1`), computes the real Qwen3-14B SHA-256 digest, replaces the stub manifest entries with the measured values, flips `dev_mode = false` as the live default, and live-verifies that the real PA boots clean on the hardware. The stub harness de-risks all structural surprises; the remaining unknowns are chip-bound.

*(commit `e1e0c1c`, merged to `main` `b499bc6` — 3 files changed: manifest labeled, AO comment tightened, harness 7/7 PASS; 2067 passed, 20 skipped, 15 deselected. The EA-4 ceremony followed.)*

### 2026-06-06 — Making the ceremony runnable by a non-developer

Sprint 15's EA-4 on-chip ceremony was designed from the start with the Lead Architect as the operator — a non-developer who runs single copy-paste commands, not someone who hand-types Python or SHA-256 hashes. The trouble was that two ceremony steps had no such command. Confirming TPM key and keystore presence required knowing which module to import and which function to call. Staging the real-digest Known-Good Manifest meant writing a `compute_sha256` loop by hand. EA-4a was chartered to close that gap: two CLIs, no new dependencies, read-only preflight, atomic-write stager, all tested.

The preflight (`python -m shared.security.ceremony_preflight`) runs eight checks in sequence: TPM sealer availability (RSA-2048 seal path), TPM signer availability (ECDSA P-256 sign path), the three named TPM key slots (`BlarAI-DEKSeal`, `BlarAI-Audit-Signing-Key-v1`, `BlarAI-PA-JWT-Signing`), the DEK keystore file, `certs/pa_public.pem`, and the production manifest with optional digest verification. Every check prints `[  OK  ]`, `[ FAIL ]`, or `[ WARN ]` — not a traceback, not a Python exception, not a module path. The bottom line is a single sentence: "READY for production boot" or "NOT READY — N prerequisite(s) need attention." A non-developer can read it without context. The tool is strictly READ-ONLY; it calls only `is_available()` and `key_exists()`, never `ensure_key()`.

The manifest stager (`python -m shared.models.stage_production_manifest`) walks the model directory, hashes every `.bin` file with the existing `weight_integrity.compute_sha256`, and writes the JSON manifest atomically — `.tmp` file then `Path.replace()`, so a crash during write never leaves a truncated manifest. A discovery during testing: `Path.rename()` raises `WinError 183` on Windows if the target already exists, which broke the idempotence test on the second run. `Path.replace()` is the correct atomic-overwrite primitive on Windows and was substituted immediately. That's a genuine portability trap worth keeping: `rename` is POSIX-atomic-replace; `replace` is the portable cross-platform one-step overwrite.

The manifest-untrack decision was straightforward but has a gitignore subtlety worth naming. The `.gitignore` already had `models/` to ignore the large binary model directory, and the EA-3 stub manifest had been committed with `git add -f`. Untracking it with `git rm --cached` was correct. The `manifest.json.example` template needed to stay committed (it is the structure document the LA can inspect to understand the format before running the stager). Getting that single file tracked inside an otherwise-fully-ignored directory required `git add -f` on the example — the `!` gitignore negation pattern cannot override a parent-directory ignore rule; git does not descend into ignored directories to evaluate exceptions. That limitation is well-documented but easy to forget in practice. The comment in `.gitignore` names this explicitly so the next person does not waste time trying to make `!` work in this context.

The test suite found 36 new tests passing and 2103 total with no regressions. The test coverage touches every code path in the preflight (all eight check functions, all three outcomes of the manifest check — missing, stub placeholder, model-absent advisory, digest match, digest mismatch, missing manifest entry), and the stager (success with multiple files, missing dir, no .bin files, simulated read error with no-partial-write assertion, idempotence, atomic write failure with no-leftover assertion, env-override CLI path). The Windows `rename`/`replace` defect was caught in this suite on the first run — exactly the kind of platform-specific failure that CI-only testing misses because most CI runs on Linux.

I went with `[  OK  ]` / `[ FAIL ]` / `[ WARN ]` bracketed symbols over Unicode check/cross marks because terminal font support for those characters is uneven on Windows and the LA will run this on the deployment machine. Fixed-width ASCII brackets render identically everywhere. The alternative was emoji (`✓` / `✗`) — rejected for the same reason.

**Next:** The Orchestrator has the exact console output of both CLIs (captured in the merge-gate summary) and can write the novice runbook. After the LA runs the EA-4 ceremony and all eight preflight checks go green, the ceremony runbook can optionally note `python -m shared.security.ceremony_preflight` as a final verification step. FUT-04 (`require_signed_manifest = true`) remains out of scope until issue #106 is taken up.

*(commit `c834b57`, merged to `main` `217ba32` — EA-4a ceremony helpers; 36 new tests, 2103 total, 20 skipped; no regressions; manifest.json untracked, manifest.json.example committed via git add -f.)*

### 2026-06-06 — Throwing the switch: production becomes the HOST default

Sprint 15 EA-4b is the activation commit that EA-2 built toward. The mechanism has been in place since EA-2 — `resolve_dev_mode` has always accepted a `dev_mode_override`, and lock (b) verified that `dev_mode_override=False` on HOST resolves production correctly. What EA-4b does is throw the switch: change the HOST branch default from `True` to `False`, wire the explicit dev opt-in, and update every test that was waiting for this moment.

The change is three lines of runtime code and one new function. In `shared/security/dev_mode_guard.py`, the HOST branch at line 103 flips from `resolved = True` to `resolved = False`. In `shared/runtime_config.py`, a new `resolve_dev_override()` function reads `BLARAI_DEV_MODE` from the environment and returns `True` (explicit opt-in) or `None` (no override — use the mode default, now production). In `launcher/__main__.py`, the call site changes from `resolve_dev_mode(runtime_mode)` to `resolve_dev_mode(runtime_mode, dev_mode_override=resolve_dev_override())`. That is the entire runtime delta.

The docstring trade-off I weighed before writing: I could have made `resolve_dev_override()` return `False` when unset rather than `None`. Both would produce the same daily-launch behaviour — production. But `None` is semantically correct: `None` means "I have no opinion; let the mode-derived default decide." `False` would mean "I explicitly want production" — which forecloses the possibility of a future mode that has a different default. The three-way precedence (override not-None → HOST → GUEST) stays clean. The decision is named in `resolve_dev_override()`'s docstring so the next reader does not have to reconstruct it.

The `BLARAI_DEV_MODE` env var is intentionally narrow in what it accepts: only `"1"`, `"true"`, and `"yes"` (case-insensitive) return `True`. I rejected adding `"on"` — which `resolve_network_facing()` accepts — because the dev opt-in should be explicit rather than convenient. An operator who sets `BLARAI_DEV_MODE=on` and gets production by default will immediately notice and correct it; that is the right failure mode for a security-critical flag. I chose to align with the most common form (`1` / `true` / `yes`) rather than extend the alias set.

The test updates were the most sensitive part. `TestHostDefaultNotFlipped` became `TestHostDefaultActivated`, and lock (a) flipped from asserting `True` to asserting `False` — exactly the sentinel transition EA-2's docstring promised. Two new locks (e) and (f) cover the env-var path: unset returns `None`, `BLARAI_DEV_MODE=1` returns `True` and fires the loud INSECURE banner end-to-end. `TestLauncherInterlockIntegration` needed an explicit patch for `resolve_dev_mode` — after the flip, HOST + network_facing=True would no longer trip the interlock by default (dev_mode=False is safe with network_facing=True). Patching `resolve_dev_mode` to return `True` in that test preserves the interlock's test coverage without misrepresenting the daily-launch posture.

The blast-radius audit found nothing further. Every test outside `test_dev_mode_interlock.py` that calls `resolve_dev_mode(HOST)` had already received a `dev_mode_override=True` blast-radius override in EA-2. The full suite ran at 2105 passed, 20 skipped — one snapshot, no regressions, two SwigPy deprecation warnings that predate this sprint and are not attributable to this change.

The trade-off I accepted and the path I rejected: I could have left the interlock integration test as-is and added a separate test class that explicitly patches `resolve_dev_mode`. I chose to update the existing test in place because the test's intent — "the interlock fires when dev_mode=True AND network_facing=True" — is unchanged; only the means of reaching that scenario changed from "HOST default" to "explicit patch." Adding a parallel test class would have implied the old test was wrong, which it was not.

**Next:** The sprint's remaining work is the auditor SWAGR pass over all EA-1 through EA-4b deliverables, then the sprint close record. The activation is live in the worktree branch and awaits orchestrator merge-gate review before landing on main.

*(commit `94bc828`, merged to `main` `e1858a5` — HOST default flip, resolve_dev_override, launcher wiring, test suite updated with 6 locks; 2105 passed, 20 skipped.)*

### 2026-06-06 — The protocol argument the docs bury in a footnote

The defect was a single integer. `socket.socket(AF_HYPERV, socket.SOCK_STREAM)` — two arguments, protocol defaulting to 0. Windows Hyper-V sockets require `proto=1`, which the Windows documentation names `HV_PROTOCOL_RAW` and documents in a single sentence buried well below the fold. Protocol 0 is valid for nearly every other socket family Python uses; it is the textbook omission. The call would compile, pass linting, and exercise cleanly in every test environment because the dev path (`AF_INET`, TCP loopback) has no such requirement. The `AF_HYPERV` path was exercised for the first time in production when EA-4b flipped the default from dev mode to the real Hyper-V path — and the Policy Agent listener's first `socket.socket` call came back with `WinError 10041` (WSAEPROTOTYPE), which is Windows's way of saying "wrong protocol for this address family."

The fix itself is three source lines: a module constant `HV_PROTOCOL_RAW = 1` beside the existing `AF_HYPERV = 34`, and the constant threaded as the third argument into both `AF_HYPERV` socket creation sites — `VsockTransport.connect()` (~line 223) and `VsockListener.start()` (~line 436). The dev-mode `AF_INET` creations are left exactly as they were; they are correct.

What makes this entry worth keeping is not the fix but the masking mechanism. The entire vsock test suite — Groups A through L, 1001+ tests — ran against the dev path exclusively. `dev_mode=True` is the test default, TCP loopback works, and nobody had reason to reach for `AF_HYPERV` in a unit test because you cannot usefully create a Hyper-V socket in a test environment without a live guest boundary. So the production path carried a two-year latent defect in a line of code that was right for every family except the one it was about to use. The lessons list already contains the "tested dev, ran production" failure class from other arcs; this is another instance with a notably clean causal chain: EA-4b flipped the production flag, EA-4c walked in on the first boot.

Writing the regression test required thinking through the control flow. The naively obvious approach — mock `socket.socket`, build a `VsockListener` in `dev_mode=False`, call `start()`, check the captured args — fails because `VsockListener.start()` checks for mTLS cert paths before creating the socket, and returns early on line 426 (`logger.error("mTLS required in production mode"); return False`) if cert paths are absent. The transport test works because `VsockTransport.connect()` creates the socket unconditionally before the mTLS check. For the listener, the test must provision real per-boot cert material, stub `create_server_ssl_context` to return a non-None context so the guard passes, and then let the `bind()` call fail gracefully. That two-step setup is what the test documents — it proves the regression lock understands the actual control flow, not a simplified mental model of it.

The new Group M (4 tests, `TestAFHypervProtocol`) locks the protocol argument for both sites and includes a negative test confirming the dev path still uses `AF_INET` with no protocol argument. Full suite at commit `960558e`: 2109 passed, 20 skipped, 0 failures.

**Next:** Merge gate review, then this branch folds into the Sprint 15 close. The guest-boundary question — whether a full host-to-guest vsock round-trip then completes — is deferred to Vikunja #615 and is explicitly out of scope here.

*(commit `960558e`, merged to `main` `c26cf11` — HV_PROTOCOL_RAW proto fix + Group M regression lock; 2109 passed, 20 skipped; guest-boundary round-trip deferred to #615.)*

### 2026-06-06 — The transport path that was designed but never wired

Sprint 15 EA-4d closed a gap that had been accumulating since EA-1: the
production boot (dev_mode=False, HOST mode) failed because the vsock channel
tried to create an AF_HYPERV socket with a Linux-style `(str(cid), port)` address,
which Windows rejects with `bind(): bad family`. EA-4c fixed the immediate
`WSAEPROTOTYPE / WinError 10041` by adding `HV_PROTOCOL_RAW` — but that only got
as far as socket creation. The real problem was deeper: AF_HYPERV is the
guest-to-host VM-boundary transport, and in HOST mode there is no VM boundary.
All services run on the same Windows machine. The correct transport for that
topology is exactly what the signed SDV criterion #4 had been describing all
along — loopback plus mTLS, the "fidelity-2" path EA-1 already verified in
isolation. It just was never wired into the actual boot sequence.

The design was clear: two topologies, two socket families. HOST mode (the default,
all services on the same machine) uses AF_INET 127.0.0.1 + mTLS; GUEST mode
(services inside the Hyper-V VM, the eventual target) uses AF_HYPERV + mTLS,
deferred as #615 because the Windows addressing side was never finished. The
trade-off was explicit — accepting that the AF_HYPERV path stays dormant and
untested end-to-end until the VM topology work is completed, in exchange for a
production boot that works today on the hardware we have. The alternative was
to keep trying to fix AF_HYPERV's addressing before any boot could succeed; I
rejected that because EA-1 had already done the hard work of proving the mTLS
handshake over loopback, and the boot should not stay broken while waiting for
#615.

The implementation threaded a `host_mode: bool` parameter (default `True`)
into `VsockTransport`, `VsockListener`, `PolicyAgentListener`, and
`TransportGateway`. In production + host_mode=True, the listener binds
`AF_INET 127.0.0.1` with `SO_REUSEADDR` and wraps with `create_server_ssl_context`;
the connector creates `AF_INET` and wraps with `create_client_ssl_context`. Both
use the per-boot certs provisioned in EA-1 (`pa_server_cert.pem`,
`gateway_client_cert.pem`, the ephemeral CA). The `dev_mode=True` path is
completely unchanged — it was already loopback, already working, and the `host_mode`
flag is irrelevant to it. The AF_HYPERV code path is preserved intact for the
future guest-mode work; the only change was adding `host_mode=False` as an explicit
parameter so the guest path requires deliberate opt-in rather than being the
default.

The launcher (Step 6) now resolves `gateway_host_mode` from `runtime_mode ==
DeploymentMode.HOST`, and sets `gateway_port = PA_HOST_PRODUCTION_PORT` (5000,
matching `[ipc].vsock_port` in `default.toml`) for production host-mode. Dev mode
still uses `ORCHESTRATOR_HOST_LOOPBACK_PORT` (5001). The step message now says
"production host-mode: loopback + mTLS, fidelity-2" rather than "real AF_HYPERV
path", which had been wrong for months.

The new Group N tests (9 tests) lock every element of this path: the socket
family used in host-mode connect (AF_INET, not AF_HYPERV), the bind address
(127.0.0.1, not a CID string), the end-to-end VsockListener + VsockTransport
loopback mTLS handshake succeeds with valid per-boot certs and the server
extracts the gateway client's CN, the fail-closed path with absent certs, the
gateway `_connect_host_loopback_mtls()` empty-cert guard, and the `host_mode`
property on VsockTransport. The EA-4c Group M tests were updated to add
`host_mode=False` explicitly (they were always testing the AF_HYPERV path; now
that's explicit rather than implicit-from-missing-parameter).

The full suite ran at 2118 passed, 20 skipped — zero regressions across 640
lines of net change.

The remaining gap before a live boot actually completes is the PA-side production
startup sequence: it still requires the TPM signing key (`BlarAI-PA-JWT-Signing`
provisioned), the Known-Good Manifest at `models/qwen3-14b/openvino-int4-gpu/manifest.json`,
the per-boot certs placed at the `certs/` paths in `default.toml`, and the actual
model weights. The transport layer is no longer the blocker.

**Next:** the EA-4 ceremony sequence — provision the TPM JWT key and run the
manifest staging ceremony so the PA measured-boot gate can complete. That is the
final gate before a live end-to-end boot produces an `activation_evidence` PASS.
AF_HYPERV guest-mode work (#615) is explicitly deferred.

*(commit `c92d06d`, merged to `main` `04c45fd` — fidelity-2 host transport wire; 2118 passed, 20 skipped; transport layer unblocked, live boot then awaiting TPM ceremony completion.)*

### 2026-06-06 — The config and the mint were speaking different languages

EA-4d landed a functioning production mTLS transport: the Policy Agent (PA) boots, binds its loopback listener with `pa_server.pem`, and accepts connections from the gateway using `gateway_client.pem`. Both certs are per-boot ephemeral artifacts minted by `provision_per_boot_certs`. The boot sequence looked clean until the Orchestrator (AO) tried to start its own listener. It failed immediately: `Failed to create server SSL context: [Errno 2] No such file or directory`. The file it could not find was `certs/orch_client.pem`.

The gap had been sitting in the codebase since the mTLS arc began. The `cert_provisioning.py` module was written with two services in mind — the PA server and the gateway client. But `services/assistant_orchestrator/config/default.toml` `[ipc]` referenced a third cert pair (`certs/orch_client.pem` / `certs/orch_client_key.pem`) that the mint had never been taught to produce. The config and the mint were written in isolation; nobody ran a systematic audit of every `[ipc]` section against what the mint actually wrote.

When I did that audit — reading every `default.toml` across all three services — the damage was larger than one pair. `services/semantic_router/config/default.toml` referenced a fourth pair: `certs/router_client.pem` / `certs/router_client_key.pem`. Same category of defect, same root cause, never minted. The right call was obvious: fix them all at once rather than land a minimal patch that would produce a second `[Errno 2]` the moment the Semantic Router attempted to connect.

The trade-off worth naming: I could have issued the Orchestrator cert as two separate certs — a server cert and a client cert — mirroring the existing PA / gateway split exactly. I chose to issue a single cert with both `SERVER_AUTH` and `CLIENT_AUTH` EKUs instead. The reason is that the AO's role is genuinely dual: it listens (server role) and it also calls out to the PA (client role), and both connections are on the same per-boot identity. A single cert with both EKUs is simpler to manage (one pair to write, one pair to verify, one pair to pass through the `verify_per_boot_certs_exist` check), and it matches the pattern already established for `pa_server.pem` which also carries both EKUs. The accepted cost is that the CN (`blarai-orchestrator`) appears in both server and client peer-verification contexts; since the per-boot CA already bounds all trust to the current boot session, the identity overlap carries no meaningful security reduction.

The fix touched three surfaces. `cert_provisioning.py` grew four new constants (`ORCH_CLIENT_CN`, `ROUTER_CLIENT_CN`, and two file-name strings), two new `PerBootCerts` field pairs, four new key generation + issuance + write calls inside `provision_per_boot_certs`, and the nine-path list inside `verify_per_boot_certs_exist`. The module docstring's gitignore inventory was updated to name all nine per-boot files. `.gitignore` gained the four new filenames so the new per-boot artifacts stay off the committed tree. Tests in `TestCertProvisioning` gained seven new items: CN checks for both new pairs, an EKU check confirming the Orchestrator cert carries both `SERVER_AUTH` and `CLIENT_AUTH`, an SSL-context load test that is a direct regression lock for the production boot failure (the exact `ctx.load_cert_chain(orch_client.pem, orch_client_key.pem)` call that failed), and two `verify_per_boot_certs_exist` False-path tests (one per new pair). The existing five-file test was renamed to nine-file to accurately describe what the mint now produces. The `test_cert_lifetime_24_hours` test was extended to cover all four end-entity certs.

The full suite (`shared/ services/ launcher/`) after the change: 2124 passed, 20 skipped, 0 failed.

The lesson here pairs with what the mTLS arc has already surfaced twice before: the config layer and the provisioning layer must be audited as a unit. It is not enough to write a provisioner and a config in the same sprint if no test checks that every cert path named in any config is actually produced by the provisioner. The SSL context load test added here is one such check — but a broader form of this discipline would be a single test that iterates every `[ipc]` section in every `default.toml`, extracts each `mtls_cert_path`, and asserts that `verify_per_boot_certs_exist` would cover it. That is a more durable safety net than adding individual regression tests each time a new service arrives.

**Next:** merge gate and LA review. If clean, the AO listener should boot. The next live verify should confirm both the PA and the AO reach `LISTENER_READY` on the production machine.

*(commit `681b5b6`, merged to `main` `a410be9` — cert_provisioning.py: add orch + router cert pairs; 7 new tests; full suite 2124 passed, 20 skipped.)*

### 2026-06-06 — The door that answered every knock except the one that mattered

The production boot (Boot-Phase-3) sends a `HANDSHAKE_REQUEST` to the Policy Agent (PA) listener before the gateway accepts any connections. The intent is exactly what the name says: confirm the PA is alive and operational before routing traffic to it. If the PA doesn't answer, the gateway fail-closes after three retries and nothing works.

Sprint 15 EA-4d and EA-4e had just wired the full host-mode mTLS transport and the per-boot certificate provisioning for the orchestrator and router. The integration was materially complete. Then the live boot log showed: `"Unsupported message type: HANDSHAKE_REQUEST"`. The PA was rejecting the gateway's very first message.

The root cause was an oversight in `services/policy_agent/src/ipc.py::PolicyAgentListener.handle_request`. The method routes by `MessageType` — it handles `HEARTBEAT` (line 258), `ERROR` (261), and then gates everything else with `if msg_type != MessageType.ADJUDICATION_REQUEST: return error`. There was simply no `HANDSHAKE_REQUEST` case. The message type has been in the protocol enum since P1.11, and the Assistant Orchestrator (AO) handler in `entrypoint.py::_handle_connection` has handled it correctly all along — `if msg_type == MessageType.HANDSHAKE_REQUEST: respond OPERATIONAL`. The PA never got the matching branch.

Before building anything, I did a full listener audit to confirm scope: the AO `_handle_connection` and the PA `handle_request` are the only two vsock listeners the gateway handshakes. The Semantic Router is a pure library with no vsock listener. The UI Backend uses named pipes (ADR-014), not vsock. The UI Shell is a Textual frontend. So the gap was exactly one handler in exactly one file.

The fix is eight lines: insert `if msg_type == MessageType.HANDSHAKE_REQUEST: return self._framer.encode_handshake_response("OPERATIONAL", request_id=request_id)` between the ERROR case and the `ADJUDICATION_REQUEST` gate, mirroring the AO handler exactly. The rest of `handle_request` is unchanged — adjudication still requires `ADJUDICATION_REQUEST`; a `HANDSHAKE_REQUEST` does not touch `_rejection_count`, does not bypass the adjudicator, and does not affect any downstream flow.

The trade-off considered and rejected: adding the case to the catch-all `ADJUDICATION_REQUEST` rejection path rather than an explicit branch. That would have been three lines shorter but would have required parsing `msg_type` again or restructuring the routing ladder. An explicit named branch is clearer, matches the AO reference implementation, and is zero-ambiguity for any future reader. Explicit wins over terse here.

Two regression tests anchor the fix in `TestPolicyAgentListenerHandleRequest`: one confirms `HANDSHAKE_RESPONSE`/`OPERATIONAL` is returned, one confirms the response is not an `ERROR` type. Both are docstring-explained as regressions for the specific boot defect, so the failure mode is identifiable from the test name alone.

Full suite: 2126 passed, 20 skipped, 0 failures. The 20 skips are environment-gated (GPU/TPM hardware not present in the test runner), unchanged from baseline.

**Next:** This branch (`feat/s15-ea4f-pa-handshake-handler`) is ready for merge-gate review. The live boot should now complete Boot-Phase-3 successfully. If the boot log still shows a handshake failure after merge, the next place to check is the gateway's transport connection path (specifically whether the mTLS certificate the gateway presents is trusted by the PA's listener at the loopback mTLS layer — that's a cert provisioning question, not a message-type question).

*(commit `1efa5e2`, merged to `main` `5ac5f9e` — 2 files changed, 46 insertions; 37 PA IPC tests pass; full suite 2126 passed, 20 skipped.)*

### 2026-06-06 — When "fail closed" becomes a door that locks you out of your own house

The Sprint-14 encryption regression suite had two tests I found myself staring at as a decision masquerading as a defect. `test_wrong_key_on_existing_db_fails` asserted that `list_sessions()` must raise under a wrong key. `test_no_plaintext_fallback_on_auth_failure` asserted that constructing the store with a tampered turn must raise at construction time. Both of those tests locked what I would describe as a sensible instinct — "authentication failure is an unrecoverable state" — but an instinct that had been applied at the wrong level of granularity.

The defect that surfaced it was the dev-to-production key transition. Practice-era sessions in `sessions.db` were encrypted under the dev SoftwareSealer key. The production TPM-sealed DEK, which is healthy and correct, refuses them with AES-GCM `InvalidTag`. That is the cipher doing exactly what it should. The problem is that all three bulk readers — `list_sessions`, `get_session_turns`, and `_backfill_empty_titles` — call the leaf helpers `_dec_session_title` / `_dec_turn_content` inside a loop, and those leaf helpers raise `RuntimeError` on `FieldCipherError`. So the first legacy row in the table aborts the entire loop, and the entire app is bricked. Every session — the new ones too, the ones written after the key transition, the ones whose plaintext is perfectly accessible under the production key — is denied because one old row failed to decrypt.

That is a denial-of-service. A self-inflicted one, and one that the availability component of the CIA triad says you should not accept without very good reason.

The recalibration took some thinking. The leaf helpers are correct: `_dec_session_title` and `_dec_turn_content` must raise when they cannot decrypt. If a single-record caller — `set_title_if_empty`, `update_session_title` — fails to decrypt the record it was explicitly asked to fetch, the loud RuntimeError is the right signal. The caller named that one record. Something is wrong with exactly that record, and the caller should know. Hard fail-closed is correct there.

But a bulk reader is not a single-record caller. It is a sweep across everything in the table on behalf of the application startup sequence or a session list render. The application did not ask for any particular row; it asked for "all sessions." When one row is un-decryptable — because it was written under a prior key, or because someone tampered with its ciphertext — the right answer is to exclude that row, log a greppable structured event (`SESSION_ROW_DECRYPT_QUARANTINE`), and continue. The good rows are served. The bad row is absent from the result. Plaintext is never returned from the bad row; tampered data is never trusted. The quarantine event is actually more observable than the old crash: instead of a generic RuntimeError propagating up through three stack frames, you get a WARNING log line with a stable event code, the session or turn ID, and a short reason — greppable, countable, and actionable if the count is unexpectedly high.

That is the synthesis: the leaf helpers have one correct fix (fail-closed, always); the bulk loops have a different correct behaviour (quarantine-and-continue). The distinction is the unit of accountability. A leaf helper is accountable for one row. A bulk reader is accountable for the health of the whole store, which includes not letting a single sick row infect all the healthy ones.

The two Sprint-14 regression tests needed to be rewritten, not just relaxed. The old `test_wrong_key_on_existing_db_fails` asserted that `list_sessions()` must raise — after the fix, it asserts that `list_sessions()` must not raise and must return zero rows (both quarantined), while a direct call to `_dec_session_title` on the same bad blob still raises. The old `test_no_plaintext_fallback_on_auth_failure` asserted that construction must raise when a backfill turn is tampered — after the fix, it asserts that construction succeeds (the backfill quarantines the bad turn), the store is usable afterwards, and a direct call to `_dec_turn_content` on the corrupted blob still raises. The "never return garbage" invariant is preserved. Only the blast radius changes.

The doc amendment was necessary to match the code. ADR-025 §2.7 originally said "fail-closed" without qualification, and that language had been applied uniformly across both single-record and bulk-read paths by the Sprint-14 implementation. I added an explicit amendment block to §2.7 naming the corrected posture: bulk reads quarantine, single-record calls remain hard fail-closed. The Sprint-14 SDV criterion 3 also referenced "wrong key → fail-closed" without the distinction, so I added an amendment note there as well. Both documents now say what the code does, not what was intended in a first-pass design that hadn't yet met the dev→prod key transition.

The path I rejected: escalating this as a posture decision rather than a defect fix. I thought about it. The confidentiality and integrity invariants are genuinely unchanged — no plaintext leaks, no tampered data is trusted. The only property being tuned is availability, and the tuning is for the bulk path only. The single-record path is unchanged. That combination — clear defect, one correct fix, no capability change, no quality change — is the definition of "fix it, don't escalate it." The test suite confirms the properties that matter: the quarantine event code is stable and greppable; confidentiality tests (`test_turn_content_is_not_plaintext_on_disk`, `test_wal_sidecar_contains_no_plaintext_content`, etc.) all remain green.

**Next:** This branch (`worktree-agent-ad71811c1605f639d`) is ready for merge-gate review. After merge, the production boot path that was bricking on legacy-key rows will recover cleanly — `list_sessions` will quarantine those rows and serve everything else. If the quarantine log count is nonzero on first boot after key transition, that is expected and correct; a future cleanup pass (wiping the practice-era rows after confirming their irrelevance) would silence the logs. That cleanup is not urgent — the sessions today hold only dev scaffolding.

*(commit `145f2b1`, merged to `main` `4af2033` (#618) — 3 bulk readers patched in `session_store.py`; 2 regression tests rewritten + 1 new resilience test file (7 new test functions); ADR-025 §2.7 amendment + Sprint-14 SDV §3 criterion 3 amendment; orchestrator merge-gate re-verified 105 tests.)*

### 2026-06-06 — When the fix was already written — applying it to the sibling store

The session-store bulk-read quarantine (see the preceding session-store entry) named the lesson cleanly: fail-closed applied uniformly to bulk loops converts a confidentiality control into a self-inflicted availability DoS. The session store has three such loops; after the fix they quarantine bad rows rather than aborting. The argument is in that entry. What this entry records is the moment I looked at `substrate.py` and saw the same defect sitting in the sibling store, untouched.

Two sites. `_load_embed_cache` runs at construction time — it iterates over every row in `substrate_chunks`, decrypts each embedding, and loads it into the in-RAM boot cache. One encrypted row from the practice era (dev SoftwareSealer key, before the ceremony) causes `FieldCipherError` to propagate out of the constructor. The AO substrate store doesn't open. AO doesn't start. Boot-time analogue of the session-store `_backfill_empty_titles` crash, but worse: the embedding cache is the entire retrieval substrate, not just title repair.

`_search_kind` is the second site — the per-hit text and source decrypt inside the top-k loop. One un-decryptable hit aborts the entire retrieve() call, denying the caller all results. The session-store analogue is `get_session_turns`.

The fix is identical in shape. In `_load_embed_cache`: try/except around the per-row decrypt, skip + log `SUBSTRATE_ROW_DECRYPT_QUARANTINE` with the row id and reason, continue. Quarantined rows are absent from `_embed_cache` — they are never scored in any subsequent query, so they never reach the text-decrypt path either. In `_search_kind`: the same try/except guard around the text decrypt and separately around the source decrypt, each logging the same stable event code with the field name, the natural row id, and the reason.

One judgment call: the `_search_kind` scoring loop now excludes rows absent from `_embed_cache` rather than substituting a zero vector. The existing code already had a cache-miss fallback using a zero vector, which would have allowed a quarantined row to score at the bottom of the ranking and then fail on text decrypt — invoking the quarantine path there too. Both approaches are correct, but excluding the row from the candidate set entirely is cleaner: the quarantine happens once at boot rather than being deferred to query time, and the `_search_kind` loop is never asked to score a row it cannot serve.

The test that needed correction was `test_wrong_dek_raises_at_boot_or_retrieve` in `test_substrate_encryption.py`. It previously asserted `pytest.raises(FieldCipherError)` around the store constructor with a mismatched key — locking the exact boot-crash behavior this sprint fixes. The corrected test (`test_wrong_dek_at_boot_quarantines_not_crashes`) asserts the inverse: construction succeeds, the store is usable (count() works, retrieve() returns an empty list rather than hitting quarantined rows), and a `test_single_record_decrypt_still_raises_with_wrong_key` test confirms the confidentiality lock is intact at the leaf level.

The new `test_substrate_decrypt_resilience.py` covers the three scenarios explicitly requested: (a) boot quarantine — cipher-A rows in a store opened with cipher-B succeed construction; (b) one corrupted chunk among several good chunks — retrieve() returns the good hits, omits the bad one, logs the event; (c) single-record decrypt — the leaf `_cipher.decrypt` still raises `FieldCipherError` on wrong key, tampered ciphertext, and mismatched AAD.

I rejected adding a factory-level `build_substrate_store` wrapper analogous to `build_session_store` — that already exists in the AO entrypoint as `_build_substrate`, and the production-wiring regression test (`test_build_substrate_returns_encrypted_store`) covers it. The scope here was the quarantine guards on the bulk decrypt sites, not the factory shape.

ADR-025 §2.7 now names both stores explicitly: the bulk-quarantine event codes (`SESSION_ROW_DECRYPT_QUARANTINE` for the session store, `SUBSTRATE_ROW_DECRYPT_QUARANTINE` for the substrate store), the methods covered in each, and the single-record carve-out. The amendment previously named only the session-store methods; after this sprint it is accurate for both encrypted stores.

The class-audit lesson is the one the sibling entry already articulated: when you fix a defect of this shape, audit the rest of the class before closing. The session store and the substrate store were built in parallel under the same ADR, with the same pattern, and with the same failure mode. Finding one should have triggered a check of the other at the time. It did not. This sprint closes that gap.

**Next:** The branch is ready for merge-gate review. After merge, a boot following a dev→production key transition will quarantine any practice-era embedding rows rather than bricking AO startup. The quarantine log count on first post-ceremony boot is expected and correct. A future cleanup pass to delete the practice-era rows (after confirming they hold no real data) will silence the logs; that cleanup is deferred and low-urgency.

*(commit `3f61b41`, merged to `main` `6fe1fcc` (#618 class-audit sibling) — 2 bulk decrypt sites guarded in `substrate.py`; `test_substrate_encryption.py` corrected (1 brick-behaviour test rewritten + 1 new single-record fail-closed lock); new `test_substrate_decrypt_resilience.py` (10 test functions, 3 scenarios); ADR-025 §2.7 amendment extended to cover both stores; orchestrator merge-gate re-verified 63 tests.)*

### 2026-06-06 — Mocks pass, seams break — and the harness that mocked the seam too

I was wrong in the same shape twice tonight, told the operator a third thing that was also wrong,
and the three together are the lesson worth keeping.

The setup was a green suite I trusted too much. Two store-resilience fixes had landed, the default
suite was 2163 passing, and I had — more than once — called the production path "verified" on the
strength of that number. Then the operator ran the only test that counts (lesson 2, again): he
booted the real thing. The window opened, he typed a prompt, and the Orchestrator answered
"Unsupported message type: PROMPT_REQUEST." A 2163-green suite, and the first sentence a human
typed broke it.

The cause was humbling in its smallness. The launcher wires the gateway to a port; in dev it
points at the Orchestrator on 5001 — which is why every prompt in every dev session and every test
had always worked — and in production host-mode it pointed at the Policy Agent on 5000. One branch
of one `if` in `launcher/__main__.py`, dev and production diverging on a single line, and the
gateway was handing every prompt to a service that does not handle prompts. Worse, the boot looked
healthy: an earlier fix had taught the Policy Agent to answer the cheap startup handshake, so the
gateway's "are you there?" succeeded against the wrong service and nothing complained until a real
prompt asked it to do a thing it cannot. A shallow gate certified a path the deep path did not
have; a downstream fix masked an upstream bug. The repair is a single source of truth — one
`resolve_gateway_port` that returns the Orchestrator's port for both dev and production, the
constant regression-locked against the service's own config so the two declarations of one
physical port can never drift again — and it was reproduced red before green: stand the real
Policy Agent on 5000, drive the real gateway at the pre-fix port, watch the handshake succeed and
the prompt come back rejected with zero tokens, then flip the port and watch it stream.

Then I made my own mistake, and it is the one I most want to keep. The operator asked how the UI
gets tested — "the window pops up and you click the buttons, right?" — and I told him, with the
confidence of a man who had just grepped, that the WinUI app has no automated tests at all. I had
scoped my check to the C# project and stopped. Before writing that into this journal I checked the
whole repository, and the claim fell apart: `tests/harness/` is a deliberate three-layer harness
(Vikunja #563, built a sprint ago — this is lesson 32's "the window I said we couldn't test,
tested"), with in-process dispatcher locks, real-model latency on the Arc GPU, and real pywinauto
UI automation driving the actual window. I had forgotten the very thing I was once proud of
building and nearly carved my ignorance into the permanent record. The lesson inside the lesson: a
claim about your own coverage is worth exactly as much as any other unverified claim — which is to
say nothing, until you have checked.

And the harness's existence does not rescue the night; it sharpens it. The routing bug slipped
past the harness too, for the same reason the unit tests missed it: every layer mocks the boundary
the bug lives past. The dispatcher layer drives a fake gateway, so the real gateway→Orchestrator
routing was exercised by nothing. The window layer drives a fake backend, so the GUI against the
real model-loaded path is not a tested path. An integration test that mocks the seam where the bug
lives has precisely the blind spot of the unit test beside it (lesson 3, in integration clothing).
Coverage is not how integration-flavoured a test looks; it is which seam it actually exercises —
and the one seam that mattered, the launcher's production port-wiring, was exercised by nothing at
any layer. A `slow` marker had even hidden two integration files of bit-rotted tests from CI
entirely; a test that does not run is not coverage, however green the suite that excludes it.

The operator's response reframed the whole night, and it is the governance lesson. He did not ask
me to fix the bug faster. He said every major aspect of BlarAI needs automated testing so
production is stable and development is fast with as little human in the loop as is reasonable. The
problem was never the bug; it was that finding it was a manual, one-at-a-time job that landed on
the most expensive tester on the project — him — because no test reached the integrated production
path. The fix for that is not another fix. It is a standard.

So the durable output of tonight is not the routing patch. It is `TEST_GOVERNANCE.md` §2.7: every
major subsystem has an automated test of its real integrated path that *exercises* the production
seam rather than mocking it; a subsystem is not "done" until that test exists, the same standing as
a journal entry; and minimal-human-in-the-loop is the design target, with manual testing reserved
for genuine visual judgment and nothing else. I placed it beside §2.5 (test in production posture)
and §2.6 (never touch real user data) as the third load-bearing clause. The trade-off on the
record: I could have left this as the tickets it spawned (#619 the production-parity boot lane,
#620 the routing fix and the model-loaded preflight gate, #621 growing the #563 window harness to
the full critical path against a real backend). Tickets get done when someone reads them; doctrine
binds every sprint. The operator pre-approved making it doctrine, because a blind spot this
structural is not a bug to fix — it is a class to outlaw.

**Next:** the routing fix lands — verified red-to-green in a new real-listener integration test,
with the launcher's model-loaded prompt preflight (which it already had, and had left disabled —
the very gate that would have caught this at boot) flipped on by default in production so the boot
fails closed with the model loaded before the window ever appears. Then I run that real generation
here, so the operator sees end-to-end proven in the harness rather than handed to him as a boot to
try; the coverage audit (#622) maps every major aspect to the fidelity that reaches its seam; and
the window harness grows from one fake-backed scenario to the full path against the real model. The
standard exists now; the burn-down is the work.

*(commits: `TEST_GOVERNANCE.md` §2.7 + this entry; routing fix on `worktree-agent-af96…` verified
red→green, merge-gate pending; harness `#563`; initiative `#622`, members `#619`/`#620`/`#621`.
Default suite 2163-green and blind to the launcher's production port-wiring at every layer, unit
and integration alike — the entire point. Lesson 56.)*

### 2026-06-06 — The fix for the prompt that went to the wrong door

The discovery, the GUI-harness misstep, and the coverage mandate it forced are in the entry above ("Mocks pass, seams break," lesson 56); this entry is the fix itself — and the mechanism that had hidden the bug. The misroute was one wrong line in `launcher/__main__.py`: the gateway opens a single transport port and reuses it for two jobs — the Policy-Agent liveness handshake and the prompt connection both read the same `self._port` — and the production host-mode branch resolved that port to the PA (5000) instead of the Orchestrator (5001). In production the gateway therefore handshook the PA *and* handed it every prompt; the PA owns `ADJUDICATION_REQUEST`, has no branch for `PROMPT_REQUEST`, and rejected it with exactly that string. Dev had always worked because dev pointed at the right door.

The part worth keeping is *why nobody noticed*, because it is a second lesson sitting underneath the seam one. Three days earlier EA-4f fixed a real defect — the PA did not answer `HANDSHAKE_REQUEST`, so Boot-Phase-3 failed loudly — and that fix was correct on its own terms. But it also taught the PA to answer the gateway's handshake *even when the gateway was wrongly pointed at the PA*. Before EA-4f the misroute would have fail-closed at boot; after it, the boot's only probe of the 5000 path was a handshake the PA now answered, so the misroute went silent and the failure migrated from "won't boot" to "boots fine, every prompt fails." A correct downstream fix moved the symptom past the gate that used to catch it — the most dangerous kind of regression, and lesson 65.

The fix has two halves. Behaviour: extract a pure `resolve_gateway_port(*, dev_mode, host_mode) -> int` that returns the Orchestrator loopback port for *both* dev and production host-mode (mTLS, not the port, is the only prod/dev difference on this path) and 0 for guest-mode; the launcher's three-branch inline selection collapses to one call. Durability: the gateway's prompt port and the AO's listener port are two independent declarations of one physical port, so I locked them together — a regression test reads the AO's production config and asserts `ORCHESTRATOR_HOST_LOOPBACK_PORT` equals its `vsock_port`, and CI fails if either drifts. I weighed the two-character hardcode (ships today, leaves the divergence-prone shape behind) against the function-plus-lock (more surface now, this class of misroute fails in CI forever); on a long-horizon platform I took the lock. The proof is reproduce-first: an integration test stands the real AO listener (GPU stubbed so Qwen3-14B never loads) at the resolved port and asserts a real `STREAM_TOKEN` round-trips, while a characterization test points the gateway at the real PA and reproduces the literal `Unsupported message type: PROMPT_REQUEST`. Revert the mapping and the round-trip goes red; apply the fix and it streams.

**Next:** merge-gate, then a live production host-mode boot completes a real prompt end to end — the first time that path has had a test guarding it. The rule for the next seam-hunt: when a downstream fix makes a previously-loud failure quiet, ask what *else* that path was implicitly checking, because a control that starts answering more messages can stop being a tripwire. The live-verify this fix unblocked is the entry below.

*(commit `762fbf9`, merged to `main` `ecbd991` (#620) — `resolve_gateway_port` extraction + call-site fix + comment correction; `launcher/tests/test_resolve_gateway_port.py` 9 wiring-lock tests; `tests/integration/test_prompt_round_trip_host_mode.py` 3 real-listener round-trip + PA-rejection tests; red→green demonstrated by reverting the mapping; orchestrator merge-gate re-ran 32 fast + 26 slow incl. the real round-trip. The model-loaded prompt-flow preflight was flipped default-ON in production in the same arc. Lesson 65.)*

### 2026-06-06 — The night BlarAI first answered a prompt in production

Lesson 2 of this journal is *"Show me it running."* Tonight it ran. For the first time, BlarAI answered a real prompt in full production posture — TPM-sealed keys, per-boot mTLS, the dev-mode flip activated — on the operator's actual hardware: a prompt came back, a photo attached through the paperclip was described correctly, and the vision model evicted from RAM after the turn. That is the bar the whole air-gap campaign is built around ("production is the only 'works'"), met for the first boot.

It did not come easily, and the arc is the lesson. The dev→production flip turned on, and the first real boot failed — then the next, and the next — each on a different production-only seam that the green 2,163-test suite never touched, because the bugs lived in the seams the unit tests mock. The encrypted session store bricked on dev-era rows the new production key could not decrypt (one bad row → an app-wide "backend not running"); past that, the gateway rejected every prompt because the launcher had wired it to the Policy Agent's port and an earlier handshake fix had masked the misroute. Each fix got the boot one seam further and exposed the next — the exact whack-a-mole the operator named, and the reason he turned the night's real output into a standard (§2.7) rather than three patches. (The decrypt-quarantine and routing entries above carry each of those fixes in full.)

Two of my own missteps belong in here, because they are the parts worth showing. First, when the decrypt-brick surfaced I reached to *escalate* it — I handed the operator an A/B decision (reset the data vs. change the store's behaviour) as though a Sprint-14 regression test that asserted the buggy "brick the whole list" behaviour made it a posture I could not touch. He collapsed it in one line: *"Fix this so it doesn't keep happening — is there really more than one correct answer?"* There was not. A test can lock a *defect*, not just a feature, and "it's test-locked" is not proof "it's a decision" (lesson 66). Second, asked how the UI is tested, I told him with grep-confidence that there were no automated UI tests — and there is a whole three-layer pywinauto harness I had built a sprint earlier and forgotten. Verify-first caught that one before it reached the permanent journal; it is the cautionary half of lesson 56. Both mistakes share a root: I trusted what was in my head over what was on the disk.

The durable outputs landed and were merge-gated — the decrypt-quarantine posture, the single-source `resolve_gateway_port`, and the model-loaded prompt preflight now default-ON in production so the boot self-verifies the full prompt path with the model loaded, fail-closed, before the window ever opens. The next broken prompt path will fail a boot, not reach a person. And the operator closed the night on the discipline itself: make the journal entries now, leave no loss if the session ends. He was right to — this entry was the one thing still only in my head.

**Next:** second-boot continuity (does a plain re-boot stay clean), then the Sprint-15 close — fold every `2026-06-*` fragment into `BUILD_JOURNAL.md`, the SCR, the Auditor SWAGR, the ledger entry, and close #616. Then the campaign continues toward the #598 gate: Tier-3 egress, the deferred guest-boundary handshake (#615), weight integrity (#106).

*(live-verify boot-1 PASSED on real hardware, LA-confirmed 2026-06-06: prompts + vision + VLM-eviction. Fixes this sprint: decrypt-quarantine `4af2033`/`6fe1fcc` (#618), prompt routing `762fbf9`→`ecbd991` (#620) + model-loaded preflight default-on, coverage mandate `0834d44`/`8206800` (§2.7 + lesson 56). Fragment first recorded `f3d4411`; folded into the journal at Sprint-15 close. Lesson 66.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The Automation Wave: building the locks before the changes that need them

Sprint 15 paid down the production-posture debt and named the testing debt: a green unit suite that mocks the boundary is not coverage of the boundary, and the project had near-zero standing enforcement of the "test the real seam" mandate. Sprint 16 is the answer — front-load the automation so the security tiers after it self-verify, and, because the working sets were genuinely disjoint, land two gate-critical hardening items in the same wave.

Seven worktree builders ran under one serial merge gate (the autonomous fleet stays paused; this is the Orchestrator + specialist-subagent shape). Two read-mostly audits went first: Stream E walked the security-roadmap §5 gate criteria against the code and found the tracker stale in both directions; Stream F mapped every subsystem's real-integrated-path coverage. Then four code builders in parallel: B1 closed the single-file blind spot in weight integrity; B2 wired and staged the signed-manifest mechanism (default off — no brick — with the on-chip ceremony batched for later) and wrote the non-developer runbook; C bounded the security-critical dependencies; D built the production-parity lane — the key-transition tests and the boot-cascade smoke that lock the Sprint-15 cascade before Sprint 17 modifies it. Stream A came last (it builds on the test harness), extending the GUI automation to the full critical path.

Two honesty lines run through the wave. First, the model-loaded runs — D's real-GPU boot-smoke and A's GUI tiers — are BUILT and SCRIPTED this sprint but not yet green: they need hardware this session does not have, so their first green run is a Sprint-17-kickoff prerequisite, not a claim of done. The stubbed tiers run green now precisely so that first real run is a confirmation, not a debugging session. Second, #106/FUT-04 stays PARTIAL: the multi-entry weight sweep landed and the signed-manifest mechanism is staged, but the gate-critical closure (a real TPM-signed manifest enforced on the hardware) is the later ceremony.

The merge gate held its shape: every builder diff was reviewed against its criterion and the Layer-A suite re-run by the Orchestrator (2172 -> 2187, zero regressions), never trusted from a builder summary — which is how a builder's report of "41 collection errors" was caught and corrected to the real figure of 4, all pre-existing and confined to tooling tests outside the runtime. The air-gap stays up; #598 remains the GO/NO-GO gate. The seven stream entries below carry the per-stream detail.

### 2026-06-07 — The checklist that had never been verified

Sprint 16's Stream E was a read-mostly audit task: walk every gate criterion in
`SECURITY_ROADMAP_air_gap_removal.md` §5 against actual on-disk code and config, and
rewrite §5 as the authoritative verified tracker. The stale doc had a flat checkbox
list that had not been updated since the roadmap was authored; by Sprint-16 start,
several of those boxes still showed as unchecked when the work they described had
shipped one to three sprints ago.

The two most load-bearing stale findings: the tamper-evident audit stream (`shared/security/audit_log.py` — hash-chained, TPM ECDSA P-256 signed, refuse-to-start if the audit key is unprovisioned) had shipped in Sprints 13 and 14 and was listed as remaining. The runtime raw-socket egress guard (`shared/security/egress_guard.py`) was arm()ed at the process entry point (`launcher/__main__.py:1131`) — also listed as remaining when it was in fact live. Conversely, one item the doc implied was mostly done (model weight integrity) was in reality only half built — the primary `.bin` is hash-checked at load, but every other manifest entry is not, and the signed-manifest mechanism (the 4th TPM key, `BlarAI-Manifest-Signing`) has not been provisioned on-chip. That is a genuine gap that the code builders in Stream B needed to see clearly before starting their work.

The deeper discovery was about what "attestation" means in the measured-boot path. The `boot.py` docstring says "Verify TPM/Pluton attestation" but the implementation is a security-material validation — key/manifest present and valid — not a PCR-based hardware quote. The `dev_mode` parameter is accepted but immediately discarded (`_ = dev_mode`). This distinction matters for the #598 gate scope: if PCR attestation is the intent, the work has not started; if key/manifest validation is the scope, it is largely done. That is a genuine open decision for the LA, and the updated §5 names it clearly as an open question rather than papering over it.

The trade-off in the rewrite was depth versus readability. A flat six-item checklist fits on a phone screen but cannot carry the evidence needed for the gate audit. I chose a structured format with subsections, file:line evidence, and explicit DONE/PARTIAL/REMAINING labels — harder to scan but impossible to misread at a gate review. The alternative (preserve the flat list and annotate it) would have hidden the evidence inside footnotes and preserved the illusion that everything was uniform progress.

**Next:** Stream B uses this §5 tracker directly — the PARTIAL on weight integrity + the explicit "not in ceremony_preflight" note for `BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` tells the builder exactly what the gaps are. The Orchestrator uses the STALE-GATE SURPRISES section to confirm no scope changes are needed before the other four builders finish their work. At Sprint 18 this tracker becomes the gate-SWAGR input.

*(commit `2800d53`; read-mostly; no code changed; doc only)*

### 2026-06-07 — Closing the single-file blind spot in weight integrity

The weight integrity check that has guarded PA and AO since Sprint 12 had a
silent gap: it verified only `openvino_model.bin` against the manifest, leaving
every other `.bin` in the model directory — draft-model weights, tokenizer
embeddings, any future shard — unverified at load time. An attacker with write
access to the model directory could swap a secondary weight file or inject a new
`.bin` entirely, and the boot gate would pass regardless. Sprint 16 SDV criterion
#2 named this the deliverable for Stream B1: extend the check to cover every
manifest entry, in both the PA and AO `load_model()` paths.

The implementation adds `verify_all_manifest_entries(model_dir, manifest_path)`
to `shared/models/weight_integrity.py`. It iterates the full `digests` dict from
the manifest, hashing each on-disk `.bin` and comparing against the expected
digest. Then it does a second pass: any `.bin` present in the model directory but
absent from the manifest is a fail-closed rejection. That second pass is the one
that surprised me as I read the existing code — the single-file check had no
analogue at all for "extra files not in the manifest." The attack path (swap the
primary binary, add a new filename with injected weights) would have bypassed the
old check entirely.

The trade-off I took was to keep `verify_weight_integrity` (the old
single-file function) intact and export both. The call sites in both
`gpu_inference.py` files now call the new sweep, and I store the
`openvino_model.bin` per-file result back into `self._integrity_result` for the
existing `integrity_result` property contract — so callers that reach through that
property continue to see a coherent `IntegrityCheckResult` for the primary binary.
The alternative was to remove the old function entirely. I rejected that because
other call sites outside the load-model paths (the per-request re-hash path,
tests) call `verify_weight_integrity` directly, and those paths are not Stream B1
scope. Removing it would be a cross-stream collision risk.

One manifest quirk worth naming: the manifest's `digests` dict uses bare
filenames as keys (e.g. `"openvino_model.bin"`), not full paths. The sweep uses
`model_dir / filename` for each key, which means the manifest is anchored to a
specific model directory at verify time. This is correct and intentional — the
`manifest_path` and `model_dir` are both caller-supplied, so the relationship is
explicit. But it means that if someone were to copy a manifest file into a
different model directory (wrong model, right manifest), the sweep would either
fail on digest mismatch or on missing files. That is the right behavior.

The 8 new tests in `shared/tests/test_weight_integrity_sweep.py` cover all four
fail-closed paths: all-pass, swapped digest, extra `.bin` on disk, missing
manifest entry. The PA and AO integration tests mock the OpenVINO pipeline so they
exercise the real `load_model()` control flow without hardware. The full Layer-A
scope ran at 1539 passed, 2 skipped, 15 deselected — zero regressions.

**Proposed lesson:** The "verify only the primary binary" pattern is a common
failure mode for integrity checks on multi-file artifacts. A manifest covers a
set; the check must cover the same set — and must also assert no extra members.
Checking less than the manifest claims to cover is a false assurance; checking
less than the directory actually contains is a silent injection window.

**Next:** #106/FUT-04 remains PARTIAL/OPEN. The sweep now covers all manifest
entries and rejects extra `.bin` files. What remains for gate-critical closure is:
(a) a real TPM-signed manifest enforced on the LA's hardware (`require_signed_manifest=true`
via the `BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` TPM key), which is staged as Stream B2 and the
on-chip ceremony batched for later, and (b) Stream E's §5 tracker update marking
weight integrity as PARTIAL.

*(commit `59fa496`; 8 new tests; 1539 passed, 2 skipped, 15 deselected on the builder's PA+AO scope — zero regressions; integrated Layer-A on main 2187 after merge `5b9123b`.)*

### 2026-06-07 — Wiring the key you already had, not building the one you thought you needed

Sprint 16 Stream B dispatched me to "wire the signed-manifest machinery into the boot cascade." Reading the brief carefully, I expected a substantial build task — code to write, new hooks to add. What I found on-disk was almost the full picture already standing.

`manifest_signer.verify_manifest_signature()` existed, fully implemented, with both the fail-closed and the warning branch. `load_manifest_verified()` in `weight_integrity.py` called it, threading `require_signed` through from its caller. Both service entrypoints — Policy Agent and Assistant Orchestrator — already called `load_manifest_verified(require_signed=resolved.require_signed_manifest)` in both their `_validate_security_material` gate and their `_verify_weight_integrity_gate`. Both `default.toml` files already had `require_signed_manifest = false` with a comment naming FUT-04 and explaining the staged intent. `provision_manifest_signing_key.py` existed in full. What was genuinely missing: (1) the preflight script did not yet check the fourth key, (2) the four mechanism-lock tests did not exist, and (3) the novice runbook had never been written.

The trade-off the sprint ratified — and that I want named explicitly here — is staged-not-activated. The mechanism is real and wired. The default stays `false` so no boot can brick before the ceremony runs. The alternative was to flip the default to `true` now, which would fail closed on every machine that has not yet run the signing ceremony, including the production host. The sprint brief, the SDV, and the LA's D-1/D-3 decisions all say: build + stage; run the ceremony in a later batched session; only then flip to `true`. I accepted that the mechanism ships as a capability that cannot yet be seen working in production — the test suite verifies it works in software; the live flip is the moment it becomes operationally active.

The ceremony preflight addition is small but structurally correct: `_MANIFEST_SIGNING_KEY = "BlarAI-Manifest-Signing"` lives alongside the three keys already in the file, and a fourth `_check_key(...)` call in `run_preflight()` brings it into the checklist. The preflight now reports all four TPM keys symmetrically. A FAIL for the manifest key is expected and honest pre-ceremony — the same way JWT key showed FAIL before Sprint 15's EA-4 ceremony ran.

The four mechanism locks test the code path that will matter on the day of the ceremony. Lock (a) proves the shipped default is genuinely permissive — the WARNING is emitted, the digest dict is returned, nothing breaks. Lock (b) proves the production signal resolves cleanly when the stub signer produces a valid .sig. Lock (c) proves the fail-closed path is reached without an exception escaping the gate. Lock (d) proves the end-to-end chain — stub-sign the manifest, call the gate with `require_signed=True`, get the digest dict back. The software stub mirrors the on-chip signer's interface exactly; when the TPM takes over, the test architecture does not change.

The novice runbook mirrors the EA4_ceremony_runbook.md standard the LA ratified in Sprint 15: full-path commands, paste-the-output cadence, no code editing, idempotent steps, fail-closed framing, low-stakes tone.
