The key discipline: the `provision_manifest_signing_key` script is idempotent — if the key already exists, it re-signs rather than failing. The runbook makes that explicit so the operator feels safe re-running it.

No new ADR was authored or amended. The staged-manifest design is recorded in the SDV §4 criterion #3, ratified as LA decision D-1 (2026-06-07), and does not require a separate ADR — the decision is a scoping ratification of the existing FUT-04 / ADR-018 intent (build + stage, activate later), not a new architectural direction. The DECISION_REGISTER does not need updating: no new DEC or ADR was authored.

**Next:** the Orchestrator merges this stream and, at a later batched on-chip session, drives the ceremony runbook line-by-line. After the signing ceremony runs and the LA confirms the preflight shows all-OK, I flip `require_signed_manifest = true` in both service configs and the signed boot is live. That flip is the moment FUT-04 moves from PARTIAL to COMPLETE in the §5 tracker. #106 stays OPEN — mechanism advanced, full closure (the live flip on the LA's hardware) is the batched ceremony session.

*(commit `046b5fc` (merge `66f6b61`); 4 mechanism-lock tests added, ceremony_preflight.py updated to check BlarAI-Manifest-Signing, novice runbook at docs/runbooks/manifest_signing_ceremony.md; awaits merge-gate + live ceremony.)*

### 2026-06-07 — Closing the unbounded upper-bounds on the security surface

Sprint 16 Stream C landed pins or upper-bounds on the security-critical runtime dependencies across
all six `pyproject.toml` files. The motivation was simple: `cryptography>=41.0` is a five-major-
version-wide window; `PyJWT>=2.8` permits a 3.x major with unknown signing semantics; `openvino>=2024.0`
allowed a three-year span of the model-loading runtime to silently change under us. The root
`pyproject.toml` had already made this call correctly for `cryptography` in Sprint 14 (EA-2,
ADR-025 §5.1 #3), but the five service pyproject.toml files had not caught up. Stream C closes
that gap uniformly.

The work itself was not glamorous — it is the maintenance posture work that security governance
frameworks demand and that tends to get deferred when features have more visible pull. The judgment
here was calibration: which deps sit on the security boundary, and therefore warrant a tight pin,
versus which are framework conveniences that can tolerate a wider window. My ordering: `cryptography`
and `PyJWT` are unambiguously on the trust boundary (they produce the tokens and the
encryption envelopes); `pydantic` and `onnxruntime` parse attacker-controlled or
supply-chain-controlled binary/structured data and belong in the medium-high tier; `openvino`
controls model loading so a version surprise there is integrity-adjacent; `numpy` lands in the AO's
privacy leakage detection path, which is security-adjacent. `textual` and `tomli` are in the
UI-rendering and configuration-reading buckets — real software, but not boundary nodes.

The thing I had to be honest about is hash verification. PEP 508 — the dependency specification
format that pyproject.toml uses — simply does not support hash syntax. The `--require-hashes` flag
and the `hash=sha256:...` annotations live in pip-tools / uv lock files, not in package metadata.
The pins here create a version-containment boundary (no silent jump across a major); they do not
prevent a supply-chain substitution at the same version number. The coverage gap is named in the
rationale doc; the next logical step is a `uv lock` or `pip-compile --generate-hashes` run and a
committed lock file — that is a separate, deliberate act and falls outside Stream C's scope per
SDV §5.3 ("pin existing deps; add none").

One environmental finding worth naming: the worktree-mode baseline differs from the main-tree
baseline by 20 tests (2152 passed / 22 skipped in the worktree vs 2172 / 2 on main). The delta is
pre-existing and reproducible with and without my changes — confirmed by stashing the pyproject.toml
edits and rerunning. The cause is that the semantic_router model-loading tests compute the model
file path relative to the calling working directory, which differs under `git worktree` from the
repo root. This is a coverage gap already identified in the Stream F audit; it is not a regression
this stream introduced. The worktree result (2152 + 22 = 2174 collected; 0 failed) equals the
worktree pre-change baseline exactly.

**Next:** A lock-file generation pass (`uv lock` or `pip-compile --generate-hashes`) would extend
the supply-chain assurance from version-containment to hash-verification. That is the natural
follow-on to this sprint's work, and the coverage-gap doc from Stream F should call it out as a
Sprint-17 candidate.

*(commit `639c9b0`; worktree baseline 2152 passed 22 skipped 0 failed from
`worktree-agent-a0e93e02bc8d20a15`; main-tree baseline 2172/2/0 confirmed pre-change.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The Two Locks That Let the Next Sprint Modify Instead of Discover

Sprint 15 live-verify drove out ten production-only defects one boot at a time at the LA's terminal. The core lesson (lesson 56 in BUILD_JOURNAL): a green suite that mocks the boundary is not coverage of the boundary. This entry documents the Sprint-16 Stream D work that installs the first two automated locks so Sprint 17's #615/egress changes can modify a tested cascade rather than discover it was broken all along.

**Lock (i) — key-transition integration tests.** The specific production gap Stream D closes: no test in the pre-Sprint-16 suite exercised the real integrated store open/decrypt path across a key rotation. In production, a dev→prod ceremony provisions a new TpmSealer key and rotates the DEK. Both the `EncryptedSessionStore` (sessions.db) and the `EncryptedSubstrateStore` (substrate.db) see cipher_new at open time; cipher_old data cannot decrypt. The old posture (before ADR-025 §2.7 amendment) would brick AO startup — the store constructor would raise a `FieldCipherError` before the user could interact with anything. The amended quarantine posture is correct: old rows are quarantined and logged, the store opens, new sessions are immediately readable.

What I chose not to do: use a mocked store or a mocked cipher. The test assertion is at the seam — the real `EncryptedSessionStore` constructor, the real `_backfill_empty_titles` path, the real `list_sessions` quarantine loop, the real `get_session_turns` decrypt. The `SoftwareSealer` stands in for the production `TpmSealer` (the same pattern every existing decrypt-resilience test uses), so the test runs on any machine without TPM access. That's the right boundary: mock the hardware, not the application logic.

The dual-store-same-session test (`test_dual_store_same_session_serves_not_bricks`) is the closest thing to a production integration path: it opens both stores with a post-rotation cipher in the same "session" (same process, as the launcher's Steps 3-5 do), writes new content to both, and asserts both return readable results. This was the exact failure mode that would have bricked the LA's terminal — and it now has an automated regression lock.

**Lock (ii) — boot-cascade smoke.** The decision I had to make carefully: what does "lock the cascade" mean for a test suite that cannot load Qwen3-14B? The LA directive was explicit: maximize what is verified WITHOUT a GPU; the stubbed tier must genuinely exercise the cascade logic, not be a trivial smoke that passes even if the cascade is broken.

The stubbed tier (ii-a) runs the REAL production functions in the correct production order: `provision_per_boot_certs()` (real X.509 generation, tmp_path dir), `AssistantOrchestratorService.start()` (real VsockListener, real serve loop, real handler — GPU monkeypatched to `_StubInference`), `build_session_store()` (real DEK creation), `TransportGateway` construction, `gateway.check_pa_status()` (real TCP handshake), and `_run_uat2_prompt_flow_preflight()` (real prompt round-trip through the AO's `_handle_prompt_request`, real PGOV check, real session write-then-delete). The ONLY stubbed component is `OrchestratorGPUInference` — everything else is real code executing real logic.

One production-seam finding surfaced during development: `_run_uat2_prompt_flow_preflight()` uses `asyncio.run()` internally (it is the launcher's synchronous bridge into the async prompt path). Calling it from inside a `pytest-asyncio` test loop causes "cannot run nested event loops." The fix was to make `test_full_prompt_flow_preflight_step` a synchronous test and run the async handshake (Step 6a) in a fresh `asyncio.new_event_loop()` before the synchronous preflight call (Step 6b). This is not a defect in the production code — the launcher calls it correctly from a synchronous `main()`. But it surfaces a real constraint: the preflight function is not safe to call from an async context. That constraint is now documented in the test's docstring and is visible to any future test author.

I went with driving the cascade's component functions from tests (stubbing the model) rather than adding a production hook to `entrypoint.py`. This keeps the test entirely within the test-owned working set and avoids touching Stream B2's `entrypoint.py`. The trade-off accepted: a production hook would be marginally cleaner to read but would widen the file-ownership surface and create a merge risk. Driving the component functions directly is the established pattern in this codebase (`test_prompt_round_trip_host_mode.py` does exactly this).

The real-GPU tier (ii-b) is built and scripted. Its first run is a Sprint-17 kickoff prerequisite before the first #615/egress edit. The `@hardware` marker ensures Layer-A DESELECTS it. The runbook is in the test's class docstring — three paragraphs covering what to run, where models live, and what "PASS" means.

**The GPU-free maximization decision.** I could have written a much simpler smoke: start the AO service, confirm it returns True, stop it. That would have passed the quality gate. I rejected it because it would not have caught the production misroute bug from Sprint 15 (which was in `resolve_gateway_port` returning the wrong port, not in service startup). The test that locks the cascade is `test_full_prompt_flow_preflight_step`: it runs the real prompt through the real handler and the real PGOV. If that test is green, the prompt path is confirmed working. That's the bar the LA's directive set, and it's the bar the tests meet.

**Next:** The Orchestrator merges Stream D onto main, re-runs the full Layer-A suite on the integrated tree (regression-gate held), and opens the Sprint-17 kickoff. The first Sprint-17 action before any #615/egress edit is the real-GPU run of `TestBootCascadeSmokeRealModel` — that run confirms the cascade and activates the lock.

*(commit `2df25d8` (merge `469a442`) — 17 new tests (9 key-transition, 8 boot-cascade stubbed; 1 deselected hardware); Layer-A suite: 2245 passed, 22 skipped, 114 deselected on the worktree.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The GUI harness the sprint-15 live-verify session should have had

Sprint 15 found ten production-only defects one boot at a time, at my terminal, because the
green suite mocked the boundary the bugs lived behind. The dead-input freeze. The PGOV denial
card that only appeared under a real PGOV result. The session-lifecycle paths that the scripted
backend never actually walked. Each required a human looking at a screen to discover. The
Sprint-15 SCR named this honestly: `TEST_GOVERNANCE` §2.7 is a mandate with near-zero standing
enforcement. Stream A of Sprint 16 is the enforcement.

The existing Layer-C harness (from #563 and #569 and the Sprint-12 follow-up) proved the
pattern: stand up the real `NamedPipeServer` over a scripted `FakeGateway`, launch the real
`BlarAI.Desktop.exe`, drive the real window with pywinauto. Two scenarios existed: the
dead-input re-enable test and the `/external` fall-through test. Both were load-bearing — they
caught real bugs. The question this sprint asked was: what else belongs in that list, and what
automation handles are missing for those scenarios to work?

The gap analysis was straightforward. Looking at the XAML through the eyes of the automation
harness, the elements with `x:Name` already resolve via UI Automation (WinUI maps `x:Name` to
the AutomationId peer automatically for top-level named elements). But: elements inside
`DataTemplate`s do not inherit `x:Name`-based IDs the same way, and some elements I needed to
assert on had no stable handle at all. The `GreetingPanel`, the `MessagesList`, the `SuggestBox`,
the `StatusBar`, the `PgovDenialCard` inside the assistant message template, the composer buttons
(`AttachButton`, `MicButton`, `ScreenshotButton`) — all needed explicit
`AutomationProperties.AutomationId` declarations. I added fifteen of them across `MainWindow.xaml`.
The rule I held: non-behavioral only. No logic changes, no visibility changes, no new event
handlers. Each attribute is a pure automation annotation on an element that already existed and
already did what it did. The `dotnet build` confirmed: `Build succeeded. 0 Warning(s). 0 Error(s).`

The thirteen critical-path scenarios cover the full list from SDV §4 criterion #1: app-launch
and connect; empty-state greeting; new-chat via `NewChatButton`; send-prompt→stream→render;
PGOV approved (no denial card); PGOV denied (denial card visible); session lifecycle (two
sessions in sidebar); streaming accumulation; document (AttachButton reachable + enabled);
settings flyout (opens, toggle present); mic-button state (disabled without voice); slash-command
autocomplete (SuggestBox appears on `/`); and the StatusBar handle for degraded-state assertions.
Each test is self-contained: it launches its own window, drives it, and asserts one observable
property. No test depends on another's state.

The design choice I had to make explicitly: what does "degraded state" mean in a test you cannot
run headlessly? The real degraded path (backend disappears post-connect, StatusBar opens with an
error) requires the backend to actually go away while the window is running — which means timing
two concurrent processes precisely. That is a live-machine scenario that the stub-backend cannot
fake cleanly without sleeping and terminating the pipe mid-test. I took the pragmatic line:
test 13 verifies the `StatusBar` automation handle is stable and reachable (it exists, it has
an AutomationId), which is the prerequisite for a full degraded-state assertion. The
live-machine run can extend this to a real degraded-state check once the handle is confirmed
stable. That is a one-test extension, not a design gap.

The model-loaded tier (Layer C + Layer B) is the higher bar. The stub-backend tier proves the
GUI renders correctly under a scripted gateway. The model-loaded tier proves the full path: a
real Qwen3-14B reply, generated on the Arc 140V, streamed through the dispatcher, rendered in
the real window. Two tests: the cold-GPU turn that confirms a reply appears and the input
re-enables; and the PGOV approved path that confirms no denial card fires for a benign prompt
through the real pipeline. Both are marked `winui + hardware`, which deselects them from the
canonical Layer-A suite and from standard `winui`-only runs. The `pytest --collect-only` confirms
15 tests collected, 0 errors, 0.08s — importable and syntactically valid in the headless agent
session.

The trade-off I accepted: the model-loaded tier's `_RealAOGateway` wraps the real
`OrchestratorGPUInference` engine but uses `FakeGateway`'s scripted PGOV result (always approved,
no real Policy Agent call). This means the model-loaded tier tests the AO chat path and the
window's rendering, not the full PA+AO+PGOV stack. The full stack is the production live-verify
(the human boots BlarAI normally). I named this in the docstring rather than hiding it:
"PGOV = scripted stubs; this tier tests the AO chat path only." That is an honest scope
statement, not a gap to be embarrassed about.

The deferred run is the Sprint-17-kickoff home, batched with the #615 hardware boot. The
runbook (`docs/runbooks/sprint_16_gui_devrun_runbook.md`) is non-developer-grade: one command
at a time, paste-back confirmation at each step, explicit "you never click inside BlarAI," and
an explicit note that the window opening and closing repeatedly is correct behavior, not a crash.
That last point came from the EA4 runbook's lesson: a first-time observer of a test framework
driving a GUI can easily panic when the window appears and disappears. Naming it proactively is
the difference between a confirmation session and a debugging session.

**Next:** the deferred dev-machine run — Tier 1 (stub-backend, 13 scenarios) then Tier 2
(model-loaded, 2 scenarios) — lands at the Sprint-17 kickoff home, batched with #615.
When those 15 tests pass green against the real window on the real machine, the automation
mandate from TEST_GOVERNANCE §2.7 has standing enforcement for the GUI path. The next gap to
close is the production-parity test lane (#619, Stream D of this sprint) extending coverage to
the boot cascade itself.

**Proposed lesson:** The automation handle design decision — which elements get an explicit
`AutomationProperties.AutomationId` vs. relying on `x:Name`-to-automation-peer inference — must
be made from the perspective of the test suite, not the UI code. Elements inside `DataTemplate`s
do not reliably propagate `x:Name` to UI Automation peers; asserting on them requires an
explicit ID. Walking the XAML as a test author (not a UI developer) is the only way to find the
gaps before the first live run, because the gaps are only visible when you write the test that
fails.

*(commit `2c4d85e` (merge `aed13ad`); 13 Layer-C scenarios added; 15 AutomationIds added to MainWindow.xaml;
model-loaded tier defined + runbooked; `dotnet build` clean; `pytest --collect-only` 15 tests
in 0.08s; live runs deferred to Sprint-17-kickoff dev-machine session.)*

### 2026-06-07 — Reading the full test suite and seeing which seams it actually exercises

This entry documents the Stream F coverage audit for Sprint 16 — a read-mostly pass
over every test file in the repo to produce a prioritized map of which subsystems
have real-integrated-path tests and which do not.

The mandate (TEST_GOVERNANCE.md §2.7, Sprint-15 lesson 56) is blunt: a green unit
suite that mocks the boundary is not coverage of the boundary. That was the finding
after Sprint-15 EA-4 drove out ten production-only failures one at a time at the
LA's terminal, with 2163 tests passing in CI. The coverage audit's job is to name
every seam that is still mocked away, ordered by how badly the project has already
been burned or is likely to be burned.

I read every test file across `services/*/tests/`, `shared/tests/`, `launcher/tests/`,
`tests/integration/`, `tests/harness/`, and `tests/security/`. Fifteen major
subsystems and cross-cutting seams mapped. The result is sobering but expected given
the timeline: 14 of 15 have unit test coverage, 7 of 15 have a real-integrated-path
test (real socket, real IPC seam, no mock at the boundary), and 2 of 15 have any
form of E2E / model-loaded automation.

The HIGH-priority gaps cluster around the same failure class the project already lived:
boot wiring (GAP-1), GUI against real backend (GAP-2), the AF_HYPERV real VM boundary
(GAP-3), weight integrity at load (GAP-4), the model-loaded prompt round-trip in
production posture (GAP-5), and the IPC routing regression lock in model-loaded
conditions (GAP-6). The seventh HIGH gap — the shared/security boot cascade as an
integrated automated sequence (GAP-7) — is the generalisation: the Tier-2 ceremony
chain has been unit-tested component by component but never as a composed automated
sequence, and Sprint-15 found failures in that composed chain that none of the
components caught.

The gap-cross-reference to open tickets is straightforward: Sprint 16's four code
builders (A, B, C, D) are already closing four of the fourteen gaps, partially or
fully. The remaining ten gaps fall into two burn-down clusters for Sprints 17–18:
the Boot Cluster (GAP-3 + the rest of GAP-1 + GAP-7 + GAP-12) aligns with the #615
and egress work already committed to Sprint 17; the pre-gate sweep (GAP-5, GAP-6,
GAP-8, GAP-9) aligns with the "make the #598 gate audit a scripted run" objective
named in the LA's planning decisions.

The lesson this audit confirms is one the project has earned at some cost: unit
coverage and integration coverage are not interchangeable, and the seams that most
need testing are exactly the seams that are easiest to mock away in a unit test —
the cross-service IPC boundary, the boot wiring, the real model's streaming path,
the GUI render. Every time the project mocked one of those seams in a unit test, it
implicitly decided "the LA will catch failures in this seam at the terminal." The
coverage mandate (§2.7) is the explicit counter-decision: automate the seam, or
accept that the seam is a manual regression test.

**Next:** the Orchestrator folds this audit into the Sprint 16 SCR. Sprint 17's
kickoff should pick up GAP-1 (part 2), GAP-3, GAP-7, and GAP-12 as the "Boot
Cluster" — the coherent set that directly enables the #598 gate audit to be
scripted rather than manual. GAP-5 and GAP-6 (the model-loaded prompt round-trip
and the IPC routing lock in production posture) belong to Sprint 18's pre-gate
sweep. The gap list lives at `docs/sprints/sprint_16/coverage_audit.md`.

**Proposed lesson:** Every seam that has burned the project in production earns an
automated test that exercises it — not one that mocks it. The inverse is also true:
the seams that are easiest to mock in a unit test are precisely the seams most likely
to hide a production-only defect, because the mock is a claim that the boundary
behaves as the test author assumed, not evidence that it actually does.

*(commit on worktree-agent-a291d05443f6f29c4 branch: `[sprint:16][F] coverage audit —
prioritized real-integrated-path gap list`; 15 subsystems mapped; 7/15 with real-integrated-path
tests; 7 HIGH-priority gaps identified; Sprint 17+ burn-down seeded.)*


### 2026-06-07 — Closing the debt the sprint existed to close

Sprint 16's locks all landed green, but I closed the sprint reporting one of them only half-wired: the production-parity lane lived in `tests/integration/`, which the canonical gate command excluded, so a green gate said nothing about whether the lane passed. I wrote that up as lesson 70 — a lock only locks if the gate runs its scope — and then, instead of fixing it, escalated it to the operator as a "governance/baseline-scope decision." His reply cut straight through it: "why are you even considering not closing the debt fully? obviously that is the goal here; the goal is to have these actually working." He was right, and the framing was the lesson: changing a baseline's *scope* sounds like governance, but folding a just-built lock into the gate so it does its job is not a trade-off the operator needs to weigh — it is the work.

So I closed it. The standing gate is now `shared/ services/ launcher/ tests/integration/ tests/security/ -m "not hardware and not winui and not slow"` (2187 -> 2212 green), and the default `pytest` marker filter (`addopts`) deselects `hardware`+`winui`+`slow` so the locks fire on the ordinary run, not only when someone remembers a long command. Wiring it surfaced the same gap one layer worse: `tests/security/` — the posture guards that fail loudly if dev-mode ships as the default, if the test-isolation conftest is removed, or if an external-network import appears — was in *neither* the gate *nor* `testpaths`. Those are the most consequential locks in the repo, and they were orphaned. They are folded in now too. Doctrine (TEST_GOVERNANCE §1/§4, CLAUDE.md, copilot-instructions, pyproject) is synced; the one step left toward a world where a bare `pytest` *is* the gate is the four pre-existing `tools/tests` collection errors (#626).

**Next:** Sprint 17 — the Boot Cluster (#615 -> egress -> the full production boot test). Its first action is the deferred real-GPU boot-smoke run against this now-enforced cascade lock, before any #615/egress edit.

*(commit `<this>` — pyproject addopts+testpaths; TEST_GOVERNANCE §1/§4; CLAUDE.md; copilot-instructions; SCR/ledger MINOR-4 closed; standing gate 2212 green.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The rulebook for the door before we cut it

The air-gap will not stay welded forever — #598 is the gate where it becomes a *guarded door*, and after it BlarAI gains web search and page-reading (#556). Before Sprint 17 builds that door's machinery, it needs the rules. I stepped the operator (a non-developer) through the egress-policy decision as four plain forks — who is on the visitor list, does the guard wave them through or check each time, what is the panic button, do we frisk outgoing packages — and at every fork he chose the strict option. The policy is now ADR-027: deny-by-default allowlist (Kagi first, grown one vetted endpoint at a time); the Policy Agent auto-approves within the list and logs every call (no per-call nagging — the guarantee is the tight list, not friction); the kill-switch stays off by default, auto-trips and alerts on any anomaly, and only the operator re-arms; and every outbound payload is screened for secrets/PII and blocked if any is found. Four independent fail-closed layers; a single failure does not open the door.

The honest framing that made it teachable: it changes nothing today (ADR-020's guard stays armed, allowlist = loopback + vsock only). It is a decision recorded now, while the choice is fresh, so Sprint 17 builds to a written spec rather than the operator's recalled intent. It also sharpened one prior open item — Decision-5's "redact at the egress boundary" became *block* at the egress boundary — and closed two of the §5 gate-tracker's open-LA-decision rows (egress policy + PII posture).

**Next:** Sprint 17 (the Boot Cluster) implements ADR-027 alongside #615 + the production boot test; the egress machinery (allowlist-widening, the PA carve-out, the auto-trip, the exfil screen) is built then and enforced only when the first web feature ships, after #598.

*(commit `<this>` — ADR-027 + DECISION_REGISTER index + SECURITY_ROADMAP §5/§6 reconciliation; not-yet-active by design.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The two bugs that hid behind one "deferred" label

The #615 guest boundary had been carried for several sprints as a single deferred item — "Windows AF_HYPERV
addressing fix" — preserved-but-not-exercised in `vsock.py` and `transport.py`. Activating it, I found the
label hid *two* distinct defects that had been masking each other, and the reason neither had been caught is
exactly why a never-green lock locks nothing: the dormant path was never run, so its two faults sat
undiscovered behind the one word "deferred."

The first defect was in the shared transport. Both `VsockTransport.connect()` and `VsockListener.start()`
built the AF_HYPERV address as `(str(self._config.address.cid), self._config.address.port)` — a stringified
integer CID and an integer port. That is the *Linux* `AF_VSOCK` shape. Windows Hyper-V sockets do not address
by `(cid, port)`; the winsock `AF_HYPERV` sockaddr is a pair of **GUID strings** — `(VmId, ServiceId)` — and
the host literally cannot reach a guest with the integer form. The oracle that settled this was not
documentation or memory: it was `phase2_gates/evidence/vsock_validation.json`, the empirical validation run
on this exact hardware (Windows 11 Build 26200, zero NICs attached) back in February, which records the
working topology as `vm_id 9c7f986f-…` + `service_guid 0000c350-facb-11e6-…` + `HV_PROTOCOL_RAW`. The
evidence file *was* the spec. I taught the addressing to a single helper, `_hyperv_sockaddr()`, that builds
the GUID pair and fails closed (ValueError, caught → False) when either GUID is absent — because the one
thing worse than an un-addressable socket is one that addresses *blindly*. `VsockAddress` grew two optional
GUID fields (`vm_id`, `service_guid`, default empty) so the change is backward-compatible — every existing
`VsockAddress(cid=…, port=…)` call still constructs.

The second defect was subtler and is the portfolio-worthy one: the gateway's `_connect_hyperv()` already had
the *correct* address — it connected to `(ORCHESTRATOR_VM_ID, VSOCK_SERVICE_GUID)`, the GUID pair — but it
created the socket with `socket.socket(AF_HYPERV, SOCK_STREAM)` and **no protocol argument**. Omitting proto
(defaulting to 0) raises WSAEPROTOTYPE / WinError 10041 on Windows before the address is ever used. So the two
code paths failed for *opposite* reasons — the shared path had the right proto but the wrong address; the
gateway path had the right address but the wrong proto — and because the whole thing was dormant, neither
had ever thrown. A "deferred" label is a place bugs go to hide from each other.

The launcher wiring was the easy half, and I kept it that way deliberately. Host-mode is BlarAI's default
topology and the guest path is an *added* capability, never a replacement — so `resolve_gateway_topology()`
returns host-mode unconditionally for `DeploymentMode.HOST`, and flips to the guest boundary only when GUEST
is requested *and* a cheap AF_HYPERV capability probe succeeds; otherwise it logs the downgrade and falls
back to host-mode rather than wedging the boot. The fell-back boot is recorded distinctly in the activation
evidence (`gateway_topology_downgraded`) so a guest-requested-but-fell-back boot is never silently
indistinguishable from a native host-mode one — committed-but-fell-back honesty, the same discipline as
"committed ≠ done." A pleasing confirmation: when I probed on this dev machine, the topology resolved to the
guest boundary (`host_mode=False`) — the box genuinely can create the AF_HYPERV socket — so the real-VM
verify in the on-chip session has a live path to exercise.

The tiering split the way the SDV asked. The addressing fix, the proto fix, the fail-closed guards, and the
topology-flip-with-fallback are all locked in the standing gate with mocks (Groups O/P in the vsock suite;
`TestResolveGatewayTopology` in the launcher suite) — 15 new addressing/protocol tests plus 7 topology tests,
all green, and the two pre-existing AF_HYPERV proto-lock tests updated to supply GUIDs (they previously
constructed an address with none, which now correctly fails before socket creation). The one thing mocks
cannot prove — that a real Windows host reaches a real Alpine guest over the corrected sockaddr — is the
`@pytest.mark.hardware` round-trip test, deselected by the gate, carrying a copy-paste HOW-TO-RUN for the LA
on-chip session. That test is the seam; everything else is the scaffolding that proves the seam is wired
right before we spend the human's terminal time on it.

This earned lesson 73 — *a long-"deferred" code path is where bugs hide from each other; activating it is a
discovery exercise, not a one-liner.*

**Next:** the C1 real-Hyper-V round-trip (`tests/integration/test_guest_boundary_hyperv.py -m hardware`)
runs green in the LA on-chip session against the running guest (prerequisite: `guest_deploy` has placed the
vsock echo responder on service port 50000); then C2's boot-integration test composes against this now-live
guest topology. Committed ≠ done until that hardware tier is green.

*(commit `<this>` — #615 C1: `_hyperv_sockaddr` GUID-pair addressing + `VsockAddress` GUID fields in
`shared/ipc/vsock.py`; `HV_PROTOCOL_RAW` + GUID address in `transport.py:_connect_hyperv`;
`resolve_gateway_topology` flip-with-fallback in `launcher/__main__.py`; 22 new unit/stub tests green +
2 pre-existing proto-lock tests updated; the real-VM round-trip is `@hardware`, awaiting the LA on-chip
session.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The production boot that two services boot differently

Sprint 17 stream G2, criterion C2: the composed *production-mode* boot
integration test, the spine's second half on top of G1's post-#615 topology.
The dev-posture cascade was already locked in Sprint 16 (`test_boot_cascade_smoke.py`):
cert-mint → PA → AO → handshake → preflight → prompt → teardown, all in
`dev_mode=True`. The gap C2 closes is the posture that actually ships and
actually fail-closes: `dev_mode=False`, where the AO's `_validate_security_material`
demands a Known-Good Manifest + a JWT CA public key, the gateway connects over
real mTLS, and the PA refuses to start without TPM-backed keys. That posture is
exactly what bit stream J at the merge gate this same sprint — a production gate
that fail-closes without material is correct, but until something *composes* the
whole production path with the gate armed, "it boots in production" is a claim no
test backs.

The design fact that shaped everything. I went in expecting a symmetric "start
PA + AO in production, stub the GPU" test and the code corrected me. The two
services are not symmetric in their hardware coupling at all. The **AO** production
gate is satisfiable entirely off-chip: a manifest carrying a 64-hex digest entry
for `openvino_model.bin` (matched by *name*, not by reading the file — and with
`OrchestratorGPUInference` stubbed, `load_model()` never runs the real weight
sweep), a JWT CA *public key file* (no private key, no TPM), and mTLS cert paths.
So the AO can run its genuine production posture in the standing gate. The **PA**
cannot: its production path needs a provisioned TPM JWT key *and* a provisioned
TPM audit key (`_build_audit_log` refuses to start without it — ADR-025 §2.8(a)),
plus a measured-boot weight gate over the real model directory and a real model
load. None of that stands in. That asymmetry *is* the tier split: the gate tier
composes the production cascade around the AO in real production posture and the
real mTLS handshake the gateway performs against it; the full-production PA boot
(TPM + model) and the real Qwen3-14B generation are the `@pytest.mark.hardware`
tier. I made the cascade docstring carry that reasoning explicitly so the next
reader doesn't re-derive why the gate tier "only" starts the AO — it is not a
shortcut, it is where the production/hardware boundary actually falls.

The stand-in material is deliberately not a mock that tiptoes around the gate. The
manifest carries the *real* sha256 of a tiny stand-in model bin, so it passes
`load_manifest` *and* a real `verify_all_manifest_entries` sweep — I added a
test (`test_standin_material_satisfies_production_manifest_gate`) that asserts
exactly that, so the positive cascade can't be a silent no-op. And a negative
control (`test_production_cascade_refuses_without_model_digest`) drops the digest
entry and asserts the AO refuses with `AO_CFG_KGM_MODEL_DIGEST_MISSING` — proving
the gate the whole tier leans on actually fail-closes.

The over-assertion I had to walk back. My first cut asserted the gateway's stored
transport carried `peer_cn == GATEWAY_CLIENT_CN` after the handshake. Red:
`peer_cn` was `None`. For a moment that read like a broken mTLS handshake — but
the handshake had *succeeded* (gateway OPERATIONAL, prompt round-trip green), so
the contradiction was the tell. The peer-CN is extracted on the **server** side
of the TLS accept (`VsockListener.accept` calls `getpeercert` and stashes the CN
on the *accepted* transport); the **client** side — which is what the gateway
holds — never extracts the server's CN. I was asserting a field that is None on
the client by design. The honest proof that mTLS verified was already in hand:
reaching OPERATIONAL is impossible without a `CERT_REQUIRED` mutual handshake (the
negative control in `test_security_cascade.py` proves a non-chaining cert yields
no channel), plus the prompt round-tripped over that same socket. So I replaced
the wrong assertion with the right seam: the stored transport is `dev_mode=False,
host_mode=True` — the production mTLS path, not a dev-loopback shortcut — and left
the server-side peer-CN check to stream I, where it belongs.

The real defect, and it was mine. The hardware tier is supposed to be safe to run
in `-m hardware` on any box — skip if under-provisioned, never fail. My first skip
guard only checked `tpm_signer.is_available()`. This dev box *has* a real TPM 2.0,
so the guard passed, the test ran, and it FAILED at the PA measured-boot weight
gate because this worktree has no models on disk (`PA_CFG_KGM_PATH_NOT_FOUND`). A
hardware tier that fails on a TPM-present-but-models-absent machine is a broken
hardware tier — it turns "I ran the hardware suite on an incomplete box" into a
red instead of a skip, which is exactly the noise the marker exists to prevent. I
fixed the guard to check the *full* prerequisite set: TPM reachable AND the
production model material present (`models/qwen3-14b/.../manifest.json` +
`openvino_model.bin`, resolved from repo root the way the production config
resolves it) AND the production audit key provisioned. Now it skips cleanly here
and only runs where the cascade can actually complete. Caught it because I ran
`-m hardware` on my own box instead of assuming the marker would spare me — the
gate-honesty discipline cuts both ways: a hardware tier you never tried to run is
as untrustworthy as a stand-in tier you never ran green.

Stayed strictly inside the working set: one new file,
`tests/integration/test_production_boot_integration.py`. `shared/`, `services/`,
`launcher/` were imported and exercised, never modified — the whole cascade is
built from existing public APIs (`resolve_gateway_topology`,
`resolve_gateway_port`, `provision_per_boot_certs`,
`AssistantOrchestratorService`, `TransportGateway`,
`_run_uat2_prompt_flow_preflight`). No production-code change was needed. The gate
tier is 5 tests, green and deterministic across repeated runs (the AO listener
port is released on teardown every time); the full `tests/integration/` folder
stays green (25 selected) with no port contention against the sibling boot/round-
trip files. The model-loaded tier is built, scripted, and confirmed to *skip*
cleanly off-hardware — its first green run needs an on-chip / Sprint-18 home.

This earned lesson 74 — *a `@hardware`/`@slow` tier must SKIP on every axis it
needs, not just the headline one.*

**Next:** the Orchestrator homes the model-loaded tier
(`TestProductionBootCascadeRealModel`) on the §7 on-chip track / Sprint-18 — it
is the production complement to C1's real-VM verify and C7's manifest ceremony,
and like them it ships committed-≠-done until its first green run on a fully
provisioned box (real TPM + real Qwen3-14B + the LA-provisioned keys). When that
session runs, set `BLARAI_PROMPTFLOW_PREFLIGHT=1` and the tier walks the real PA
(TPM + model) + real AO (model) + real mTLS handshake + real generation end to
end.

### 2026-06-07 — Building the door before there is anything to let through

ADR-027 settled the policy for the network-facing era — deny-by-default allowlist, PA auto-approval
within it, a kill-switch that auto-trips on anomaly, an exfil screen that blocks on detection. My job
on Sprint 17's H-a stream was the egress *machinery* for the first and third of those, plus the seam
the exfil-screen stream (H-b) plugs into — and to ship it all **dormant**. That last word is the whole
discipline of this piece. The active allowlist stays loopback + AF_HYPERV, the external list stays
empty, the kill-switch stays default-off, no screener is registered in runtime code. Nothing about
what BlarAI can reach changes this sprint. The temptation in security work is to flip the thing on the
moment you have built it; the mandate here was the opposite — build it, test it hard, leave it welded,
so that when #598 clears and the first Kagi call ships post-#556 the machinery is already proven rather
than freshly improvised. Same pattern as manifest signing: the ceremony is built long before it runs.

The interesting engineering call was the screener seam. H-b owns the exfil-screen module and I merge
first, so the two have to connect across a merge boundary without a circular import — `egress_guard`
can't import a module that imports `egress_guard`. I went with a registration pattern over the
tempting alternative of having `egress_guard` reach into a known module path: the screen module
imports me and calls `register_screener(...)`, and a second hook, `register_arm_hook(...)`, lets it
defer that wiring to `arm()` time rather than import-time side effects. `arm()` runs the hooks after
installing the socket guard, so a screener that itself opens a loopback resource does so under the
armed allowlist. The launcher's `_arm_egress_guard()` imports `shared.security.exfil_screen` if it's
present and then arms — and if the module is absent on a checkout (it is, on my branch), the baseline
guard still arms. That "fail toward the *more* restrictive posture" choice runs all through the seam:
a screener that raises is treated as a detection, an arm-hook that raises trips the kill-switch. A
broken screen must never silently become an open door — the most dangerous failure mode in an exfil
control is the one where the screen is gone but egress still flows.

The kill-switch shape took a deliberate decision too. I made `trip()` *latch*: once tripped, every
egress operation is cut — loopback and vsock included, not just the external attempt that triggered it
— and only an explicit, LA-only `rearm()` clears it. The softer alternative (trip only the offending
destination, auto-recover) is wrong for this threat: on a detected anomaly the safe state is "nothing
leaves," and choosing to resume is a human decision, never automatic. `EgressTripped` subclasses the
existing `EgressDenied`/`OSError` so the runtime's `except OSError` fail-closed paths (the vsock
transport, `socket.create_connection`) degrade to a refused connection rather than crashing — the same
courtesy the original guard extended. One thing I checked rather than assumed: that auto-tripping on
every external attempt wouldn't break the *existing* baseline tests, which deliberately attempt
external connects and expect `EgressDenied`. It didn't, because I made `disarm()` release the latch and
the autouse fixtures disarm between tests — but that's exactly the kind of global-state bleed that
would have passed locally and detonated three tests later in the suite, so I ran the existing
`test_egress_guard.py` + `test_no_external_egress.py` + the boot-cascade smoke together with my new
file to prove no cross-test pollution. 59 green together; 522 green across `tests/security/` +
`shared/tests/`.

The blast-radius lock is the one I'd point an auditor at. The original guard's contract is that arming
rebinds *exactly* two symbols — `socket.socket` and `socket.getaddrinfo` — so the Windows named pipe
and every non-socket surface stay untouched. It would have been easy to widen that surface adding
payload screening; I added screening inside the already-guarded `_GuardedSocket.send`/`sendall`/
`sendto`/`sendmsg` instead, and kept a test asserting the rebind set is still exactly those two. The
machinery grew the guard's *behavior*, not its reach.

This earned lesson 75 — *cross-stream seams that merge separately must connect by registration, not by
import.*

**Next:** H-b builds `shared/security/exfil_screen` + the PA `DENY_EXTERNAL_NETWORK` carve-out + the
PGOV PII path and registers its screener through `register_screener()` / `register_arm_hook()` — the
exact signatures are frozen in `tests/security/test_egress_core.py::TestInterfaceAnchorContract` and
documented in `docs/security/egress_activation.md` §5. The Orchestrator merges H-a first (interface
anchor), then H-b. The live allowlist stays empty until W4 Kagi ships post-#556; the first
`allow_external_endpoint(...)` call is a future governance act, runbooked in the activation doc §3.

*(commit `<this>` — egress_guard ADR-027 machinery: allowlist-widening + latched anomaly auto-trip +
the `register_screener`/`trip`/`register_arm_hook` seam, all STAGED/DORMANT; launcher `_arm_egress_guard`
boot wiring; `tests/security/test_egress_core.py` 33 tests; `docs/security/egress_activation.md`.
Standing-gate-adjacent: 59 green with the existing egress + boot-cascade locks, 522 green across
tests/security + shared/tests. Awaits H-b's screener + the post-#556 first allowlist entry.)*

### 2026-06-07 — Building the screen for a door that is still welded shut

Sprint 17, stream H-b: build the half of the egress machinery that screens an outbound payload and lets the
Policy Agent auto-approve an allowlisted call — the exfil screen (ADR-027 §4) and the PA
`DENY_EXTERNAL_NETWORK` carve-out (ADR-027 §2). The whole thing ships dormant: no external egress is
permitted this sprint, the air-gap stays up, and what I built is the mechanism that *would* adjudicate and
screen the day web features land post-#556. Building a control you deliberately wire to nothing is its own
discipline — the temptation is to half-build it because "it doesn't run yet," and the antidote is to lock it
as if it did.

The first real finding was where the rule I was told to amend actually lives. The brief and the SDV both say
"PA `rule_engine` carve-out," and there is indeed a `services/policy_agent/src/rule_engine.py` — but
`DENY_EXTERNAL_NETWORK` is not in it. The deterministic egress deny is RULE 3 of
`DeterministicPolicyChecker.check()` in `gpu_inference.py`, and that matters more than a filename quibble:
that one classmethod is the single source of truth enforced at *both* the PA boundary and the AO tool loop
(`_adjudicate_tool_dispatch` calls it in-process so the assistant's own tool calls get the same deny). Put
the carve-out anywhere else and I would have either missed the AO path or forked the rule into two copies
that drift. So the carve-out went where the rule is, behind a keyword-only `egress_allowlist=` parameter that
defaults to an EMPTY class-level frozenset. Empty allowlist means RULE 3 behaves byte-for-byte as it does
today — every external URL still denied — which is the dormancy guarantee made mechanical rather than
promised. I wrote a `TestPaEgressCarveOutDormant` lock that fails loudly if that default is ever populated
without the gate, the same staged-default-OFF pattern the manifest-signing work used in Sprint 16.

The second finding was a fail-open I built and my own test caught. My first cut extracted the URL host for
*any* external scheme, so an allowlisted host reached over `gopher://` or `ftp://` got auto-approved. That is
exactly the smuggling vector RULE 3 was written to stop — gopher for SSRF, ftp for file exfil, ws for C2 —
and an allowlist that names "the web endpoints a feature needs" has no business green-lighting them. The test
I had written asserted the stricter contract (non-web schemes stay denied even to an allowlisted host), it
went red, and rather than soften the test I fixed the code: the carve-out now only considers `http`/`https`.
This is the proactive-defect rule in miniature — the check surfaced a clear fail-open with one correct fix,
so I fixed it instead of shipping the permissive version with a note. The screen is for web egress; the other
protocols stay where ADR-027 §1 leaves them, denied.

The reuse call was the satisfying one. The exfil screen had to detect "secrets + PII," and BlarAI already has
a mature, Luhn-gated, context-aware PII recognizer set in the PGOV output-validator (`pgov.find_pii_spans`).
Reinventing it would have been two recognizer sets drifting apart — one of them the security-critical one,
silently weaker. So the screen lazy-imports `find_pii_spans` (lazy to keep `shared/security/` free of the AO
dependency chain at module load, the same discipline the rest of that package uses) and adds only a thin
credential layer for the high-value formats PGOV does not target: PEM private-key blocks, JWTs,
GitHub/Slack/Google tokens, and gated `key=value` secret assignments. When PGOV's PII detection improves, the
egress screen inherits it for free. Everything fails closed — an undecodable payload or a recognizer that
throws yields `blocked=True`, because a payload you cannot prove clean is a leak — and the `Detection` carries
labels and offsets only, never the raw secret, so the alert that fires on a catch does not itself become the
leak.

The seam to the kill-switch is the one piece I could not fully run here, by design. Stream H-a owns
`egress_guard` and merges first; its `register_screener()`/`trip()` interface does not exist in my worktree
base (main@148f3e1). So I wrote `screen_and_enforce()` against the documented contract — screen, and on a
block call `egress_guard.trip(reason)` — and the seam test that exercises the real wiring is guarded with
`pytest.importorskip` plus a capability check, so it skips cleanly here and the Orchestrator verifies it
runs-and-passes at my merge-gate once H-a has landed. 21 mechanism locks green now, 3 seam locks skipping
pending integration, and zero regressions across the 113 existing `DeterministicPolicyChecker` tests and the
AO tool-loop tests that lean on the same rule. The air-gap import scan still passes too — the `urllib.parse`
I use to pull a host is parsing-only and already on that scan's allow-list.

This earned lesson 76 — *put a carve-out where the rule actually fires, not where its name says it lives.*

**Next:** the Orchestrator merges H-a first, then H-b; at the H-b merge-gate the three seam tests must run
green (not skip), proving the screen→trip wiring fires over H-a's real `egress_guard`. After #598 and when
W4 Kagi search ships under #556, the activation sequence in `docs/security/egress_machinery.md` §5 is the
runbook: add the host via H-a's allowlist-widening mechanism, register the screener on the send path, arm the
kill-switch for that feature.

*(commit `<this>` — `shared/security/exfil_screen.py` (new), the PA RULE 3 carve-out in
`services/policy_agent/src/gpu_inference.py`, `tests/security/test_egress_screen.py` (21 locks + 3 seam),
`docs/security/egress_machinery.md`; STAGED/DORMANT, no runtime behaviour change; seam green awaits H-a merge.)*

### 2026-06-07 — Walking the whole trust chain at once, and the rotation check that lied to me

Sprint 17 stream I, criterion C4: close GAP-7. The Sprint-16 coverage audit had
named the gap with uncomfortable precision — every link in the boot security
chain (TPM sign, TPM seal, per-boot cert mint, mTLS handshake, signed audit
record) was unit-tested in isolation, but nothing walked the *seams between them*
as one automated sequence. That gap is exactly the shape of the Sprint-15
cert-mount failure that burned the LA at a live terminal: each component green on
its own, the composed boot path never exercised until a human watched it break.

So this stream is one test that walks the entire cascade end to end:
provision-key → cert-gen → boot (the at-rest DEK comes up) → per-boot mint →
mTLS handshake → TPM-signed audit record. Two tiers, mirroring the
boot-cascade-smoke pattern that landed in Sprint 16: a SoftwareSealer stand-in
tier that is green in the standing gate (no chip, no GPU, no hardware marker),
and a `@pytest.mark.hardware` real-TPM tier that the gate deselects. The
discipline here is the one the project keeps re-learning — a lock that never went
green locks nothing, but the first real-hardware run must be a *confirmation*
(harness proven on the stand-in tier first) not a *discovery* (harness broken
because nobody ever ran it). The stand-in tier proves the walk; the hardware tier
confirms it against the chip.

The honest part. My rotation assertion — "a second per-boot mint must produce
distinct cert material" — failed on the first run, and for a moment I thought I'd
found a real defect: two PEM files byte-identical, same serial number, even
though `provision_per_boot_certs` calls `x509.random_serial_number()`. Then I read
my own ordering. `provision_per_boot_certs` rotates *in place* — it overwrites the
same `certs/` paths. I had captured `first_certs.pa_server_cert_path.read_bytes()`
*after* the second mint had already overwritten the file, so I was diffing the
current file against itself. The rotation was real the whole time; my test was
reading the wrong moment. The fix was three lines — capture the first boot's cert
bytes immediately after step 2, before step 4 re-mints — but the lesson is the one
that keeps earning its keep: a red test is a claim that needs its own scrutiny
before you escalate it as a code defect. I nearly reported a fail-to-rotate bug
that did not exist. (The real-TPM tier already had the ordering right, which is
how I knew where to look.)

The payoff exceeded the bar. The hardware tier didn't just collect-and-skip on
this box — it ran green against the real STMicroelectronics TPM 2.0: the real
provisioning ceremony minted a non-exportable key and exported its public half,
`TpmSealer` did a real RSA-2048 OAEP seal *and* unseal of the DEK through the
envelope (create → persist → unseal → reload-from-disk → unseal-again), and
`TpmRecordSigner` put a real TPM ECDSA-P256 signature on the audit record that
`verify()` then walked. So C4's real-TPM tier is not merely "built and scripted,
awaiting the on-chip session" — it is confirmed green on hardware today. I still
flag it for an explicit on-chip/Sprint-18 home because the *gate* must never
depend on a chip being present, and other dev boxes won't have this TPM; but the
committed-≠-done risk on this one is already retired on the reference unit.

Two seams I made sure were not no-ops. The mTLS step uses the *minted* per-boot
certs (PA-server identity as listener, gateway-client identity as connector,
`CERT_REQUIRED` both ways, verified against the per-boot CA) over the dev-mode TCP
fallback — dev_mode only swaps AF_HYPERV for AF_INET as the transport; the TLS
handshake itself is the real production one because the cert paths are set. And I
added a negative control: a client presenting a cert from a *different* per-boot
CA must not get a working channel. Without that, "mTLS passed" could mean the
trust check silently degraded — the exact fail-open class these security sprints
exist to prevent. The teardown deletes only the two cascade-scoped TPM key names
(`BlarAI-Cascade-Test-*`), never the operator's production keys, and I verified no
stray keys remain on the chip after the run.

Stayed strictly inside the working set: `shared/security/*` and `shared/ipc/vsock`
were imported and exercised, never modified (stream H-a owns `egress_guard.py`,
stream K owns `dek_envelope.py`). The cascade is built entirely from existing
public APIs — no shared/security change was needed.

This earned lesson 77 — *a red test is a claim about your test before it is a claim
about the code.*

**Next:** the Orchestrator homes the real-TPM tier explicitly on the §7 on-chip
track / Sprint-18 (it is green on the reference unit but must be re-run wherever
the gate is audited, and stays out of the standing gate by the `hardware` marker).
Stream J (production-posture runtime guard, C5) can lean on this file's
SoftwareSealer + minted-cert pattern. When stream K's key-recovery work (C6)
lands, a future pass could extend this cascade with a dead-chip branch
(unseal-via-recovery after the TPM path is denied) to walk the break-glass seam
end to end too.

### 2026-06-07 — The recovery key was already there; what was missing was proof it works on a dead machine

Sprint 17, Stream K (C6, SECURITY_ROADMAP §5.5): "recover the at-rest DEK from
the offline recovery key after TPM/chip loss or hardware migration + a
tested-recovery lock (a fresh environment decrypts via the recovery key)." This
is gate-critical for #598 — the at-rest-encryption criterion's last open box.

I went in expecting to *build* the offline-recovery path and found it had shipped
in Sprint 14. `dek_envelope.py` already dual-wraps the DEK — TPM-sealed primary
plus an AES-256-GCM recovery wrap under a 256-bit off-box key — with
`unseal_via_recovery` (recovery-wrap-only, never touches the sealer),
`reseal_dek` (re-bind the *same* DEK to a new chip, no new DEK), and a
`provision_dek_keystore.py` ceremony carrying both `--rotate` and `--recover`
modes. The §5.5 tracker even records the *mechanism* as DONE. So the honest
framing of C6 was never "build the path" — it was "the mechanism exists but
nothing proves it survives the actual disaster it's for." That gap is the whole
point of the criterion, and it's the kind of gap this project has been bitten by
before: a green suite that mocks the boundary is not coverage of the boundary.

The tell was in the existing tests. Every one of them simulates "the TPM is
gone" by `monkeypatch`-ing a live `SoftwareSealer`'s `unseal` to raise mid-test —
the chip is "dead" but the same process that sealed the DEK is still running with
the sealing key one attribute-set away. That is not hardware migration. The
disaster the recovery key exists for is a *fresh environment on new hardware*
where the only artifacts that crossed the gap are the keystore file and the
recovery key, and there is no TPM anywhere that can unwrap the primary wrap at
all. None of the suite modelled that.

So Stream K is really two things: a small new module and the lock that was
missing. The module — `shared/security/recovery_key_store.py` — owns the
recovery-key *material*: generation, a human-transcribable encoding (bare hex,
dash-grouped, and a checksummed-grouped form so a mis-keyed nibble is caught on
re-entry before the all-or-nothing AES-GCM unwrap is even attempted), Fail-Closed
parsing, and a `redact()` that reveals only a length and a short fingerprint so a
recovery key can be referenced in a log line without leaking. The
64-hex-character validation had been living inline inside the ceremony's
`recover()`; centralizing it means the envelope, the ceremony, and any future
recovery tool parse the operator's input one way instead of three. I added one
method to my own file, `DekEnvelope.unseal_via_recovery_hex`, to close that loop —
the real break-glass UX hands the operator a *string*, not 32 raw bytes, and now
the envelope can be driven straight from that string through the validated
parser, re-raising the store's `RecoveryKeyError` as `DekEnvelopeError` so one
call site catches one type and no key fragment escapes through a differently-typed
error.

The lock — `tests/security/test_key_recovery.py`, 34 tests — models the dead
machine honestly. I wrote two stand-in sealers, `_NoTpmSealer` (raises
`TpmUnavailable`, the off-chip/no-provider case) and `_DeadChipSealer` (raises
`TpmSealingError`, the provider-present-but-key-absent case), so a test that
reaches the TPM branch fails exactly the way production would on the real dead
chip. The headline tests do the full arc: provision on machine A, carry *only*
the keystore file and the recovery key to a fresh machine B whose sealer cannot
unseal, and recover. Not just the DEK bytes — a real `FieldCipher` payload
encrypted on A decrypts byte-for-byte on B, because "a dead chip must not lose
decades of data" is a claim about *data*, not about a key comparison. Then the
migration completes: `reseal_dek` re-binds the recovered DEK to B's new TPM with a
fresh recovery key, the data is readable via the new fast path after a simulated
reboot, and the old recovery key is now rejected by the new keystore. The
fail-closed half locks the refusals: wrong key, all-zero key, no-key-and-no-TPM,
a foreign ciphertext the recovered DEK must *not* decrypt, and a tampered
recovery wrap on disk — all REFUSE, none returns plaintext.

The trade-off I want on the record: this is the **gate tier**, `SoftwareSealer`
standing in for the TPM, which is exactly what the SDV scopes C6 to ("green in
the gate (stand-in sealer)"). I deliberately did **not** expand scope to a
real-TPM recovery verification. A real chip would prove the RSA-OAEP seal/unseal
against the Platform Crypto Provider, which the software stub does not — but the
*recovery* path by construction never touches the sealer (it unwraps the AES-GCM
recovery wrap directly), so the stand-in exercises the recovery logic with full
fidelity; only the primary-wrap round-trip is stubbed, and that is already
covered by the ceremony's on-chip live-verify. I judged a real-TPM recovery test
genuinely worthwhile as a `@hardware`-marked on-chip item and flagged it for an
on-chip home rather than building it here — chosen over silently treating the
gate tier as the whole story, which would have been the dishonest version of
"done."

99 tests green from the worktree (34 new + the 65 existing field_cipher/
dek_envelope tests, confirming my envelope extension regresses nothing); the 20
`provision_dek_keystore` tests stay green too, and I confirmed no import cycle
despite `recovery_key_store` importing the key size from `dek_envelope` (the
back-reference is a function-local import). Isolation verified active: the root
conftest redirected `LOCALAPPDATA` to a throwaway temp dir and unset
`BLARAI_DEK_KEYSTORE`, so nothing in this stream went near the real keystore.

This earned lesson 78 — *mechanism-exists is not disaster-tested; the missing
catastrophe test is the actual deliverable.*

**Next:** the merge gate (re-run the standing gate, branch-guard before the
main-tree merge). For the gate's honesty column: the real-TPM recovery
round-trip is the `@hardware` follow-on — recover a keystore provisioned by a
real `TpmSealer` on a machine where that seal key has been deleted, proving the
break-glass path against the actual chip, not the stub. It belongs in the LA
on-chip batch, not the gate tier.

### 2026-06-07 — The posture the file swears to versus the posture the boot resolves

`test_secure_defaults.py` has guarded `dev_mode = false` since Sprint 12. It reads
the shipped TOML and asserts the *file* still declares production. That lock is
real, but it is static: it proves the config on disk is secure and stops there.
It cannot prove that a boot in production posture actually *resolves* dev_mode to
False at runtime, nor that the production-only fail-closed behaviours engage. A
regression that left the file correct but short-circuited the resolution — an
inverted override default, a stray `BLARAI_DEV_MODE` leak into the resolver, a
service quietly ignoring `dev_mode_override` — would sail straight past the static
lock and only surface at the LA's terminal. That gap is exactly what the Sprint-16
coverage audit logged as GAP-12 (#600), and it is my Sprint-17 criterion (C5):
the *dynamic* complement that boots production posture and asserts dev_mode=False
at runtime.

The design question was where to put the assertion so it is both honest and
hardware-free. The naive read of "boot production and assert" is a full
`PolicyAgentService(...).start()` with `dev_mode=False` — but in production that
path fail-closes without a provisioned TPM (JWT signing key + audit key) and a
Known-Good Manifest. A test that can only run on the chip locks nothing in the
gate; a lock that never goes green is the lesson the boot-cascade smoke already
paid for. So I split the assertion the way the SDV's tiering invites. The gate
tier asserts the *resolved* posture through the exact resolution chain the
launcher runs — `resolve_dev_mode(HOST, dev_mode_override=resolve_dev_override())`,
the literal call at `launcher/__main__.py` ~:547 — and the PA's real
`_load_entrypoint_config()` against the real shipped config (the PA defers
`_validate_security_material` to `start()`, so config resolution returns a
production config with no TPM/model/cert dependency). The slow tier carries the
real full `.start()` boot, `@pytest.mark.slow`, with a HOW-TO-RUN and an explicit
on-chip / Sprint-18 home — never the *only* place the dev_mode=False assertion
lives.

The part I am most satisfied with is that the gate tier proves a runtime
*behavioural* divergence, not just a resolved flag. The static lock can only ever
say "the file says false". The dynamic guard shows the system *acting*
production: `_prompt_flow_preflight_enabled(dev_mode=False)` is ON; the
dev_mode/network_facing interlock admits the production combo and still refuses
the insecure one; and — the sharpest one — `build_session_store(db, dev_mode=False)`
with no `BLARAI_DEK_KEYSTORE` REFUSES TO START (`StoreProvisioningError`, ADR-025
§2.8(a)) where the same call in dev mode happily builds a SoftwareSealer-backed
store. That is a real fork in runtime behaviour driven by the resolved posture,
and no config read could ever demonstrate it. I paired every production assertion
with a dev-mode control (override flips to True; dev store succeeds; dev AO skips
the security-material gate) so a future bug that makes resolution *always* return
False can't make the production assertions pass vacuously.

The failure worth keeping: my first dev-mode AO control test blew up not with a
clean assertion error but with `dataclasses.FrozenInstanceError: cannot assign to
field '__traceback__'`. The cause was a stricter-than-expected validator — my tmp
dev config omitted `[gpu].priority`, which `_validate_config_data` requires even
in dev mode, so `_load_entrypoint_config` raised a `ConfigResolutionError` the
test wasn't expecting. And because `ConfigResolutionError` is a *frozen dataclass*
that also subclasses `ValueError`, pytest's attempt to attach a traceback as the
exception propagated through the `with`-block machinery hit the frozen wall and
masked the real message. Two lessons compounded: a stricter validator is a
feature (it caught my incomplete fixture), and a frozen-dataclass exception type
turns an unexpected raise into a confusing secondary error — so a dev-mode
"control" config has to be *complete*, not minimal. Completing the config fixed
it cleanly; all twelve gate-tier assertions execute, zero skips.

This earned two lessons — lesson 79 (*a static config lock and a runtime posture
guard are not redundant — they catch different regressions*) and lesson 80 (*a
frozen-dataclass exception type masks unexpected raises*).

**Next:** the slow tier (`TestProductionPostureFullBoot`) needs its first green
run on a provisioned chip — the LA on-chip session (Sprint 17 §7) or the Sprint-18
pre-gate sweep. Until then it is BUILT + SCRIPTED, not done. The gate tier is the
load-bearing runtime lock and is green now.

### 2026-06-07 — The posture test that passed because the worktree was empty

Stream J shipped the production-posture runtime guard (C5 / #600) green in its
isolated worktree — twelve tests, every one executing its assertion rather than
skipping. The merge gate, run on the real main tree, turned one of them red within
nine seconds: `test_assistant_orchestrator_production_security_gate_fires` — DID
NOT RAISE.

The test asserted that the Assistant Orchestrator's production security-material
gate fail-closes, and it proved that by constructing the AO against the shipped
`default.toml` and expecting the Known-Good Manifest to be missing "in the test
tree." In J's worktree that held — a `git worktree` has no `models/` directory,
because the multi-gigabyte OpenVINO weights are not in git. But on the dev
machine's main checkout the real manifest sits right there at
`models/qwen3-14b/openvino-int4-gpu/manifest.json`, so the gate found it, accepted
the unsigned manifest (`require_signed_manifest=false` — the staged-OFF pre-C7
state, which is the *correct* current behaviour), and resolved cleanly. The test
wasn't exercising the gate; it was measuring whether the machine happened to have
models installed.

Fixing it taught a second, sharper lesson than I expected. My first instinct was
to force the manifest absent — point `weight_manifest` at a tmp path, then drop it
from the config entirely so `manifest_path` would resolve to `None`. Neither made
the dev box trip at the manifest: the AO's config resolution still produced a
present, valid manifest, and the gate moved past it to fail-closed on the *next*
missing material — the JWT CA public key (`AO_CFG_JWT_CA_PATH_MISSING`) — a
different code than the `AO_CFG_KGM_*` I had pinned. The exact sub-check that fires
is genuinely host-dependent: a provisioned dev machine trips on the JWT CA (manifest
present); a bare worktree trips on the manifest. Chasing a specific code was the
same mistake one layer down.

So the fix asserts the *invariant*, not the host-decided code: production posture
must fail-closed at a security-material gate — `AO_CFG_KGM_*` **or**
`AO_CFG_JWT_CA_*`, both reachable only past the `dev_mode` early-return in
`_validate_security_material` — while the same construction in dev mode skips the
method entirely (the control test). The test config provides no JWT CA, so the
gate always fires in production regardless of host; either code proves exactly what
GAP-12 cares about — the production security-material gate is engaged at runtime.
The runtime gate was never wrong; the test's premise, then its assertion, were
environment-fragile.

This is the precise shape of seam the gate-honesty discipline exists to catch: a
worktree's emptiness silently stood in for a control that behaves differently on a
provisioned machine. It is also exactly why the Orchestrator re-runs the standing
gate on the merged main tree and never trusts a builder's in-worktree green — the
builder ran on a tree that *structurally could not* show the failure.

This earned lesson 81 — *a posture test must own the outcome it asserts on.*

**Next:** none for this test — it is deterministic now (it owns the outcome it
asserts, not the host's provisioning state). Standing watch item: any
posture/security test that asserts a fail-closed control must force the precondition
AND assert the invariant rather than a host-specific error code, because the dev
machine is provisioned and a worktree is not, and the two disagree exactly where
these tests assert.

*(Merge-gate catch on stream J / C5; fix on branch `sprint17/j-posture-fix`,
folded into the C5 record. Verified 12 passed on the gate tier; re-gated on the
merged main tree below.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The on-chip session: the alarm I withdrew, and the tiers the chip locked

This was the night Sprint-17's hardware tiers stopped being scripts and became
evidence — and the night I re-learned that a symptom read correctly can still ship
a wrong conclusion.

It opened on the FUT-04 ceremony, the one governance act in the set. I provisioned
the fourth TPM key (`BlarAI-Manifest-Signing`), signed the weight manifest, and
flipped `require_signed_manifest = true` in both service configs. The trust anchor
is `508defe5…`; the private key never left the chip. The proof it works was quiet
but decisive: the signed manifest verifies under enforcement, the standing gate held
at 2342 — and *un-skipped two signed-manifest tests that had been dormant while
signing was off* — and at the real production boot the Policy Agent cleared the
manifest gate end to end. From that boot onward BlarAI fails closed if its weights
aren't validly signed. One line, reversible, but a posture I can point to.

Then #615 nearly cost me my credibility. The guest-boundary test failed
`connect(): bad family`, and I read the symptom right — this Python has no
`socket.AF_HYPERV` — but concluded the wrong thing entirely: that the approach was
broken, that it wanted a ctypes rewrite, maybe a re-scope of the #598 gate. I
escalated that. The LA pushed back in four words — *"didn't we test AF_HYPERV
already?"* — and he was right. The Phase-2 evidence held a real host→Alpine
AF_HYPERV round-trip; the venv is simply Python 3.11.9, which predates CPython's
`AF_HYPERV`, while the system's 3.14 has it and the connect dials the guest. A
mundane interpreter-version gap, not an architecture failure — and the gateway that
performs the host-side connect imports zero OpenVINO, so it runs ≥3.12 cleanly with
no GPU-stack collision. I withdrew the alarm, fixed the *real* defect it had masked
(the `getattr(socket,"AF_HYPERV",34)` fallback that let a creation-only probe lie
about guest-mode — now it refuses to claim guest-mode and says why, loudly), and
recorded the corrected diagnosis on #615. The design had been sound the whole time;
what I owed was verification before escalation. That is the lesson I am keeping
(lesson 82): a conclusion alarming enough to escalate gets checked against the
evidence already on disk first — and the same instinct, applied a beat later,
correctly called the next failure *test-tier* rather than real.

That next failure was C2, the mocks-pass-seams-break classic. The model-loaded
production-boot tier failed the gateway→PA mutual-TLS handshake with
`CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED`. The tell was that the stubbed-GPU tier ran the identical
handshake and passed — so this was environmental, not a real mTLS gap. The test
minted per-boot certs into a tmp dir while the *real* PA (started via
`from_runtime_mode`) read the shipped `certs/`; two CAs, no trust. Fixed to mint into
the production `certs/` location exactly as the launcher does at boot, and the full
real cascade — real PA with TPM JWT + TPM audit + measured boot, real Qwen3-14B, real
per-boot mTLS, real prompt round-trip — went green. Proven test-tier, not a gate gap.

C4 was the cheap, honest one: the real-TPM security cascade re-verified on the
engaged chip in 1.2s, and I captured the evidence artifact (structured JSON + raw run
log) that the Sprint-16 SWAGR's MINOR-2 had asked for — the builder's "green on the
reference TPM" claim is now independently re-verified in-tree.

And the GUI took four rounds, each one the screen catching what a headless agent
could not theorise — the full account is the entry below this one. The short version
is that no app bug surfaced at any step across four rounds; every failure was
UI-Automation projection literacy (the window not foreground, no-peer containers, an
unprojected empty `ListView`, a templated `x:Name` that WinUI never exposes), and
twice the regression I had to fix was a "fix" I had *just* shipped. Anchoring on
control identity and on text — and dumping the UIA subtree instead of guessing —
closed it at 13/13 critical-path, 2/2 model-loaded. That arc earned lesson 83.

The thread through all of it: the first real run finds what no mock holds. The chip,
the screen, and the LA's memory each caught something the scripts could not — and the
discipline that paid was verifying before concluding, every time.

**Next:** C1's full guest↔host round-trip is the single open tier, and it stays
honestly PENDING — it needs the deployment runtime on Python ≥3.12 and the Alpine
responder up, both tracked on #615 and inherited by the VM-occupant sprint. Host-mode
is operational, the air-gap is unchanged, and the #598 gate is unaffected. Everything
else — C7/#106, C8a, C8b, C2, C4 — is green and banked.

*(commits `09c62b1` (FUT-04 flip + C8a + C1 honest-detection), `f4bdea4` (C2 cert-fix +
C4 evidence), `d3fe427` (GUI 13/13); standing gate 2342 passed / 0 failed; C4 evidence
at `docs/sprints/sprint_17/evidence/`; earned lessons 82 and 83.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The harness that had never met the window it was driving

Six of the thirteen GUI critical-path scenarios in `test_winui_critical_path.py` were
failing, and the reflex read was "the WinUI app has bugs." It does not — every failure sat in
the *harness*. The reason they had survived to be found at all is the interesting part: these
tests are marked `slow` + `winui`, deselected by default, and they skip the instant
`BlarAI.Desktop.exe` is absent. So on every dev-box gate run, on every collection, they
collected green and ran *never*. Authored, committed, counted as coverage — and had never once
driven the real window they claimed to drive. A test that has never executed is a hypothesis,
not a regression lock, and this was a folder of hypotheses. Fixing them took three screen runs,
and the gaps between them — each a different wrong assumption about how WinUI surfaces a control
to UI Automation — are the part worth keeping.

**Run one** attacked what looked like the whole problem: a backgrounded WinUI 3 window has an *unrealized*
UI-Automation tree, so even controls that exist are absent from the UIA projection until the
window is the foreground, active window and the render pass has run. The harness's
window-activation was a bare `win.set_focus()` in a best-effort `try/except` — exactly the
call WinUI 3's XAML-island host loves to silently no-op from a background thread. I built a
real foregrounding seam (new `tests/harness/winui_foreground.py`: `bring_to_foreground` =
`set_focus` then a Win32 `ShowWindow`/`SetForegroundWindow` nudge through `ctypes` on
`user32.dll`, no `pywin32` dependency, no-op off-Windows; `foreground_and_wait` = foreground
then poll for the target, re-asserting foreground each tick) and routed every lazily-rendered
assertion through it. The operator ran it on the screen: **7 → 9 passing.** Real progress,
and the foregrounding was genuinely necessary. But four still failed — and they were not
foregrounding failures at all.

**Run two** is where I learned the thing I had wrong. The remaining four — `RootGrid`,
`GreetingPanel`, `PgovDenialCard`, `SuggestBox` — share a single root cause that foreground +
poll can *never* fix: **WinUI gives layout panels (`Grid`, `StackPanel`) and bare decorations
(`Border`) no AutomationPeer**, so they are never directly findable by AutomationId — Visible
or not, foreground or not. The nine tests that passed had, by luck of their authoring, all
anchored on genuine *Controls* (Button, TextBox, ListView, ToggleSwitch — each with a native
peer); the four that failed had each anchored on a `<Grid>` (`RootGrid`), a `<StackPanel>`
(`GreetingPanel`), or a `<Border>` (`PgovDenialCard`, `SuggestBox`). The fix is to anchor on a
child that *is* a Control, or on child TEXT (a `TextBlock` surfaces its text as the UIA Name):
`RootGrid` → `PromptBox`; `GreetingPanel` → its "Hi Blair" headline TextBlock (found by text);
`PgovDenialCard` → its `PgovDenialHeading` TextBlock; `SuggestBox` → the `SuggestList` ListView
inside it. The sharpest lesson sits in `RootGrid`: that anchor was *my own* from run one — when
I corrected the closed-`InfoBar` test (a closed WinUI `InfoBar` renders nothing and is
correctly absent, so asserting its presence was wrong), I re-anchored it on `RootGrid` as a
"stable host container." `RootGrid` is a `<Grid>`. It has no peer. My correction was itself an
instance of the very bug the other three failures were — I fixed one no-peer assertion by
introducing another, and only the screen caught it. The slash test had a second defect on top:
it drove '/' with `set_edit_text`, which sets the value without raising WinUI's `TextChanged`,
so `OnPromptTextChanged` never fired and the box never opened; switched to a real `type_keys`
keystroke. I expected this run to finish the job. It did not: **9 → 11**. `RootGrid` and
`SuggestBox` went green; the greeting and the denial card did not — and again for a *different*
reason than I had assumed.

**Run three** found the third seam, the one hiding behind the first two. The greeting test
also asserted `MessagesList.exists()` "for good measure" — but in the empty state
`UpdateGreetingVisibility` sets `MessagesList.Visibility=Collapsed`, and **WinUI does not
project an empty or Collapsed ListView into the UIA tree at all**. So the test proved the
empty-state greeting was visible and then immediately failed demanding the presence of a
ListView the empty state is specifically designed to hide. The fix is to drop that second
assertion entirely: the greeting headline being found *is* the empty-state proof; requiring an
empty ListView alongside it is self-contradictory. The denial card was the same projection rule
seen from the other side. I had been treating its failure as virtualization, but I had never
actually confirmed the denied message was being *added* — so run three makes the test prove it
first: after the denied send, poll `MessagesList` for ≥1 item (it flips Visible and reachable
