the instant it is non-empty), and only then poll for `PgovDenialHeading` inside the now-realized
item. And I stopped guessing in the dark: both stubborn tests now call a `_dump_uia_subtree`
helper on their failure path that prints every AutomationId-bearing descendant plus the
`MessagesList` item count, so if either is still red the next foreground run is *diagnostic* —
it shows exactly what the tree exposes and whether the message was even there — instead of
another round of me theorising about a framework I cannot see.

Two of the original six were plain *logic* bugs, unrelated to either realization seam.
`test_two_sessions_appear_in_sidebar` clicked `NewChatButton` twice expecting two rows, but
`OnNewChat` (MainWindow.xaml.cs L146) only *clears* the active session — a row is born on
**prompt-send**, where `SubmitPromptAsync` calls `CreateSessionAsync` (L229-234) the first
time `_activeSessionId` is null (correct ChatGPT-style UX: an empty chat you never typed into
is not a chat). Rewrote it to drive two real prompt-sends across a new-chat reset.
`test_new_chat_button_is_present_and_enabled` carried the identical latent assumption and had
been passing only by leaking state from a prior test in run order — a quietly load-bearing
accident — now it asserts the real contract. I confirmed, before touching anything, that the
denied-PGOV state the card test needs is genuinely producible on the Layer-C stub:
`FakeGateway(approved=False)` → the dispatcher emits a `pgov` frame with `approved:false`
(`_m_prompt` L325/L355) → `BackendClient` surfaces `verdict.Approved=false` → `IsDenied=true`
→ `DeniedVisibility=Visible`. No model-loaded tier required; the state flips on a real
denied send, exactly as the test now drives it.

What the three runs teach together, and what I am distilling as lesson 83: the UIA tree is not
the visual tree and not the XAML — it is a *projection*, gated on three separate preconditions a
hand-driven dev session hides from you. *Realization* (foreground + render pass). *Peer
existence* (only Controls get a peer; layout panels and bare decorations do not). And
*non-empty/visible projection* (an empty or Collapsed ListView is not in the tree at all). Each
run taught me one and I mistook it for the whole; the screen corrected me three times, twice by
failing a "fix" I had just shipped (the `RootGrid` re-anchor in run two, the contradictory
`MessagesList.exists()` in the greeting). The deeper failure under all three was the same: three
foreground runs in, I was *still* reasoning about a framework I cannot see instead of
instrumenting it. That is what the `_dump_uia_subtree` helper is really for — it converts the
next failure from "theorise again" into "read the tree." The honest move every time was to treat
"committed but never run unattended" as unverified (the same standing as a green test that
shares the code's blind spot, lesson 3) and to let the screen, not my model of the framework, be
the arbiter.

**Next:** the operator re-runs `pytest -m winui tests/harness/test_winui_critical_path.py`
foreground on the dev box (and `-m "winui and hardware"` for the model-loaded tier) — the
deferred #621 green run, which came back 13/13 critical-path and 2/2 model-loaded on the
engaged chip the same session. If a denial-card-shaped regression ever recurs, its failure path
now prints whether the denied message reached `MessagesList` and what the tree exposes — which
decides between "the denied state isn't being produced" and "the templated child is genuinely
unreachable via UIA" (the latter being a real finding: assert via the item's accessible
Name/text, or move it to the model-loaded tier). Green-on-screen is the only thing that turns
these hypotheses into regression locks, and committed is not done until that run passes.

*(commits — run 1: new `tests/harness/winui_foreground.py` foregrounding helper (7→9); run 2:
re-anchor 4 failures off peer-less containers onto child Controls/text + real-keystroke slash
(9→11); run 3: drop the contradictory empty-state `MessagesList.exists()` from the greeting
test, make the denied-card test prove a transcript item was added before polling
`PgovDenialHeading`, and add a `_dump_uia_subtree` failure-path diagnostic to both — all in
`test_winui_critical_path.py`; `test_winui_model_loaded.py` unchanged; landed under `d3fe427`
with the on-chip close; operator's 13/13 + 2/2 foreground verify for #621 banked. Earned
lesson 83.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The control that guards a different door

Preparing Sprint 17, I surfaced two "decisions" to the operator and he was right to be annoyed at one
of them. The §5.9 measured-boot attestation scope was genuinely his — but the Sprint-17 *sizing* question
I put next to it (build the egress machinery in Sprint 17 or Sprint 18?) was not. That second one changes
nothing that gets built and no answer quality; it is sequencing — how I pack disjoint work across waves —
and dressing it up as "…or we could defer half to Sprint 18" read to him exactly as it should have: as
suggesting he stop short of finishing. He has trained this calibration before (own the obvious call; a
just-built lock folded into the gate is not a governance fork, lesson 72); this is its sibling. A
sequencing/packing choice that changes nothing built is the orchestrator's to own, not an operator fork.
I took it back and owned it: Sprint 17 builds the whole Boot Cluster — #615 + the boot/posture locks +
the egress machinery — in parallel, and finishes.

The attestation decision itself was the teachable one, and the thing that made it a *real* decision (not
an obvious "do the strong thing") was that measured-boot guards a different door than the gate is about.
The #598 gate exists to clear the threat that *removing the air-gap* introduces — network attack surface.
True TPM PCR measured-boot attests the firmware/bootloader/OS boot-chain, defending *physical* tampering
(evil-maid / bootkit) — orthogonal to the network threat, and carrying a decades-of-use cost a novice
could not be expected to know: PCR values change on every legitimate firmware/OS update, so strict
measured-boot fails-closed-on-boot until re-baselined (the reason BitLocker asks for a recovery key after
firmware updates). The operator chose, informed: the PA's existing security-material validation (manifest
+ keys + certs present and valid; refuse-to-start, hard-lock ×3) IS the #598 attestation bar — it already
fail-closes on the forged/missing *trust* material that the network threat would target — and PCR
measured-boot becomes a deliberately-designed post-gate hardening item (#627). Matching the control to the
threat the gate addresses, with the stronger control captured and tracked rather than rushed to a date.
Recorded as ADR-028, indexed in the register, and reconciled into the SECURITY_ROADMAP §5.9/§3 gate
tracker so the criterion is no longer "open."

**Next:** Sprint 17 scopes at kickoff with §5.9 settled and both the egress (ADR-027) and attestation
(ADR-028) postures decided; #627 tracks the post-gate measured-boot build (including the `boot.py:9`
comment-accuracy fix that lands with it).

*(commit `cd86442` — ADR-028 + DECISION_REGISTER index + SECURITY_ROADMAP §5.9/§3 reconciliation + #627
tracking ticket; the attestation-scope decision, LA-made 2026-06-07. Earned lesson 84; folded from journal
fragment at the Sprint-17 close.)*

### 2026-06-07 — The briefing before the irreversible door

The operator drew a line under the #598 gate that I had drawn in the wrong place. I had #612 — the
capstone security presentation — sequenced *after* the gate, the way the ticket body read it: an
after-the-fact "here is the verified AFTER posture" bookend to the 2026-06-03 audit deck. He corrected
it: #612 is his **real deep-dive into the residual risks of removing the air-gap, even after all the
hardening** — and it comes **before** he signs off, not after. He wants the thorough presentation, he
wants to ask questions, and he wants any work that surfaces during the Q&A *tasked and resolved* before
the air-gap comes down. The sign-off is not a rubber-stamp at the end of an automation run; it is a
decision he makes only once he understands what he is deciding.

That is the right governance shape and I had it backwards. Removing the air-gap is the single biggest
threat-model shift in the project — the one irreversible-feeling door — and the human who owns that
go/no-go should take his understanding pass *before* the decision, with a remediation loop so his
questions can still change the outcome. A capstone scheduled after the gate can only explain a decision
already made; scheduled before it, it can still stop or reshape it. The #612 ticket's eight must-cover
requirements were already strong; what was wrong was the timing and the role. Recorded as a new gate
criterion (SECURITY_ROADMAP §5.13, the sign-off precondition), §5.12 amended to depend on it, §4 sequence
updated, and both the #612 and #598 tickets amended. The emphasis for whoever builds #612 also sharpened:
the residual-risk register + the network-facing threat-model scenarios are the heart of it, not the
victory lap.

**Next:** unchanged for Sprint 17 (the Boot Cluster wave is in flight; this is gate-sequence, not
Sprint-17 scope). Sprint 18 = the pre-gate automation sweep; then the #612 capstone phase (build →
present → LA Q&A → resolve surfaced work); then the #598 sign-off.

*(commits `452ac98` (§5.13 + the #612-before-#598 amendment) + `021ffda` (the §4 capstone-phase
restructure); the #612-gates-#598-sign-off gate sequence, LA-directed 2026-06-07. Earned lesson 85;
folded from journal fragment at the Sprint-17 close.)*

### 2026-06-08 — The on-chip session I told the agent to make the operator run

The Sprint-17 hardware tiers needed a live chip, and the SDV had them as an "LA on-chip session" —
runbooked, Orchestrator-driven, one command at a time. I handed that framing to the execution agent
verbatim and drafted the operator a "guide me through it, give me the first command, I'll paste each
output back" kickoff. He stopped me cold: *"It's having me do testing! Why would it do that? All testing
should be automated. My time is being wasted, again."* He was right, and the root cause was an assumption
nobody had re-checked. The "LA-driven, one-command-at-a-time" script is correct for exactly one situation
— a *guiding* session with no path to the hardware (which is what I am). The *execution* agent was running
on the dev box with full shell access; it could run every one of those commands itself. The framing
carried the "operator runs X, pastes Y" phrasing through four layers — the runbooks, the SDV §7 on-chip
track, the execution brief, my kickoff message — and at no layer did anyone ask whether the agent
executing it could simply do the work.

The fix is a standing principle, not a patch: an agent on hardware it can reach runs every step itself,
and a step is routed to the human *only* when proven to need human hands — an OS-level elevation the shell
cannot obtain, a physical action, an irreducible presence assertion — never because a runbook is phrased
for a human operator. Where the blocker is one-time access (shell elevation), it is obtained once, not
routed through the human each command. The burden of proof sits on routing a step *out* to the human, not
on automating it. The agent self-corrected immediately and ran the entire on-chip session itself — the
FUT-04 ceremony, the boot tiers, the model-loaded cascade, the screen-driven GUI harness — surfacing only
results. The operator's later clarification sharpened the boundary: by "testing" he means *all* automated
verification of any kind is the agent's, including the screen-taking GUI harness once he has cleared the
screen; his own role is the occasional hands-on verification of a major feature, which is a different act
from running a test suite.

**Next:** the on-chip doctrine reframe is tracked as Vikunja #629 — propagate the automate-first /
human-exception principle across BlarAI CLAUDE.md, the SDV §7 on-chip template, the runbooks, and the
devplatform operating-model — so no future sprint inherits the "operator runs it" fiction. Sprint 18 (the
pre-gate automation sweep) is authored under it: every tier the fleet can reach, the fleet runs.

*(commit `<this>` — the BUILD_JOURNAL fold of the Sprint-17 governance + on-chip-guidance lessons; the
automate-first reframe, Vikunja #629, LA-surfaced 2026-06-08. Earned lesson 86.)*

### 2026-06-08 — The skip that wasn't a skip: when a process-leak masquerades as a coverage decision

The standing gate had been reporting a clean 2342 passed / 0 skipped for weeks. Then it would occasionally read ~2333 passed / 9 skipped, with no obvious cause — no code change, no model change, no environment difference anyone had touched. The first instinct was to treat those ~9 skips as expected: the boot-cascade tests skip when a live BlarAI instance is detected, and surely someone had the app open. But the flag kept appearing on automated gate runs where no one had opened BlarAI at all.

The root cause was a process-teardown gap in the WinUI harness, and the reason it was hard to see is that the gap was invisible at the harness tier. The four WinUI test files each called `proc.terminate()` to stop `BlarAI.Desktop.exe` at the end of a GUI automation run. Correct — except that the .NET app immediately spawns a Python backend child when it boots, and that child binds AO loopback port 5001. A bare `proc.terminate()` kills only the parent. The Python child becomes an orphan, continues holding port 5001, and on the *next* pytest invocation — which might be the standing gate — every test that guards on "is a live BlarAI instance running?" sees a held port and defensively skips. The skips are correct. The problem is that the "live instance" is a ghost: a leaked child with no parent, no window, serving no one.

This is a class of bug worth naming carefully. It is not a failure the suite reports as a failure; it is a failure the suite reports as a perfectly reasonable skip. From inside the gate run, the skip looks valid — port held, operator probably has the app open. No assertion error, no exception, no noise. You only notice it if you run the gate twice with a WinUI harness run in between and think to compare skip counts — and even then the natural read is "someone opened the app." The ghost process is a red herring made out of a real herring.

The fix was two-part. First, every bare `proc.terminate()` in the four harness files became a call to a new `tests/harness/process_tree.py` helper — `terminate_process_tree(pid)` — that uses psutil (already in the venv at 7.2.2) to walk the full descendant tree before terminating: children in reverse BFS order, then the root, all wrapped so it never raises out of a `finally`. I chose psutil over the `taskkill /T /F` fallback because it gives a deterministic, auditable tree-walk; the fallback remains for a psutil-absent future. Second, a session-scoped autouse fixture in the root `conftest.py` captures whether port 5001 is held at session start, yields, then evaluates the free→held delta at teardown. The design is deliberately asymmetric: held→held (a genuine live instance) and free→free (a clean run) both pass silently; only free→held fires, because that is the only transition representing a leak *this session caused*. On every clean gate run 5001 is free at both ends and the fixture is a silent no-op — it does not move the 2342/0 baseline at all; it only fires on the broken run that would otherwise drift to ~2333/9.

The trade-off I rejected was "just kill the leaked PID at teardown and be done." Cleanup-only hides the leak: if the Part-1 teardown fix ever regresses — someone adds a fifth harness file with a bare `terminate()`, a .NET change spawns a second child — cleanup-only masks it and the gate passes cleanly, leaving no record. Fail-loud keeps the signal. I kept the cleanup in the fixture too (belt-and-suspenders, so the next run is clean) but it is secondary to the failure. I factored the decision into a pure `port_leak_verdict(held_at_start, held_at_end)` with a four-case unit test locking the no-false-positive contract; the one that matters most is `test_free_to_free_is_silent_pass` — if it ever fails, the detector has broken the gate for everyone.

**Next:** the real verification of the teardown fix is a WinUI harness run on the dev machine (exe present, display available) followed immediately by the standing gate — the 2342/0 should hold where it previously dropped to ~2333/9. That is the live confirmation the worktree cannot provide; the determinism criterion itself (the gate reproduces 2342/0 without a manual process-kill) is met and was re-confirmed twice from main.

*(commits `5698936` (C6 teardown fix + fail-loud detector) + `2c1f373` (docstring honesty) merged `f6193d1`; Orchestrator re-ran the standing gate from main twice at 2342/0; live WinUI teardown verify deferred to a dev-machine GUI run. Earned lesson 87.)*

### 2026-06-08 — The two ways `dev_mode` lies, and the worktree that wasn't where I wrote

Sprint 18 stream M was two locks that look like one: prove the gateway→AO prompt round-trip survives the REAL Qwen3-14B, and prove it does so in *production posture* — not the comfortable dev path the existing tests have always taken. Building them taught me where the fidelity actually lives, and it nearly cost me the work to a directory I wasn't watching.

Two tests already touched this territory and both quietly stepped around the hard part. `test_prompt_round_trip_host_mode` stands up the real AO listener and a real `TransportGateway`, sends a real `PROMPT_REQUEST`, asserts a real `STREAM_TOKEN` comes back — but with the GPU stubbed and `dev_mode=True`, so no model and no mTLS. `test_sprint12_real_model` drives the real 14B through the full turn path — but via a `_FakeTransport` that never crosses a socket, so again no mTLS, no listener, no real wire. Each is honest about what it covers; neither covers the seam the Sprint-15 terminal actually burned on, which is the *composed* production walk: a real socket, a real mTLS handshake, the signature-verified manifest boot, real generation, real PGOV, streamed back. `dev_mode=True` is the single line that makes both existing tests easy and both incomplete. C1 sets `dev_mode=False` on *both* ends so the real `create_server_ssl_context`/`create_client_ssl_context` `CERT_REQUIRED` handshake runs, the `load_manifest_verified(require_signed=True)` detached-`.sig` check fires at config-load, and the digest sweep fires at `load_model()`. The only thing loopback changes from true production is AF_INET vs AF_HYPERV — the security code path is the production one.

Production mTLS needs a server identity (the AO listener) and a client identity (the gateway) that chain to one CA the test controls. Two candidates: the real ceremony certs in `certs/`, or the per-boot `provision_per_boot_certs()` mint into a tmp dir. I chose the per-boot mint, and the reasoning matters: it is not a hand-rolled stand-in — it is the *actual* production cert-minting function the launcher calls every boot (ADR-026), so it exercises the same material production mints, just into `tmp_path`. The real `certs/` material is per-chip, ceremony-generated, gitignored, and not guaranteed present in the right role-pairing on the GPU box; depending on it would make C1 skip for cert-absence reasons unrelated to the model. `dev_mode` stays `False` either way, so the production mTLS path executes regardless — the more robust choice loses nothing on fidelity. (The JWT validation key is a separately-generated EC P-256 public key, because the AO's `jwt_ca_cert_path` must load as an `EllipticCurvePublicKey` and a CA *certificate* is not a bare public key — a small distinction that fails closed if you conflate them.)

The failure that stayed in: my env said the working directory was the worktree, but every reference path in my brief pointed at the main checkout (correct for *reading*). I let that path leak into my *writes*. Both files — the new C1 harness and the C2 edit — landed in the main checkout, the one tree I was told explicitly not to touch. Collection failed against the worktree, which surfaced it. The recovery was the non-destructive discipline the project insists on: copy both files into the worktree, SHA-verify the copies byte-identical, preserve a backup, *then* correct main — the new C1 file (untracked) moved out and preserved, the C2 edit confirmed identical-SHA between main-HEAD and worktree before a single `git restore` returned main to pristine with the work already safe in the worktree. The pre-existing `docs/guide-workstreams/README.md` change — not mine — I left untouched. The lock that caught it was the same path-discipline reflex that has caught worktree confusion before; this time it was the *write path*, not the branch, that wandered.

When the production tier first ran on the chip it fail-closed at boot with `AO_CFG_KGM_PATH_NOT_FOUND` — and that was the gate working, not breaking: the test wrote a `tmp_path` config with relative model paths, but the AO derives `repo_root` from the config file's own location, so under tmp the relative `models/...` resolved to a dir with no weights and the signed-manifest boot refused to start. The fix was absolute paths in the test config (`_resolve_path` passes absolutes through); production fidelity is unchanged. With that corrected, C1 and C2 went green on the Arc 140V.

**Next:** the model-loaded tiers are agent-run green (C1 load 18.4 s / first-token 3.10 s / total 3.59 s, PGOV-approved; C2 total 2.97 s, no misroute), captured to `PERFORMANCE_LOG.md` + `docs/performance/`. The production-posture round-trip is now an automation-verifiable lock, not a manual terminal walk.

*(commits `e51119d` (C1+C2 build) merged `b1d7c96`; path-fix `e3dbee0` merged `dedf341`; C1/C2 agent-run GREEN on the real chip. Earned lessons 88 and 89.)*

### 2026-06-08 — The mocked seam that hid the wiring gap

Sprint 18, criterion C3 (GAP-8): prove the real bge-small semantic router is invoked inside a real AO turn. This sounds like a wiring test; it turned out to be a gap-exposure test — and the gap was more interesting than the wire.

I started by grepping every file under `services/assistant_orchestrator/src/` for `SemanticRouter`, `semantic_router`, and `.classify(`. The only hit was a comment in `pgov.py` noting the `LeakageDetector` "mirrors `SemanticRouter._embed_raw`" — not a call, a comment. The AO entrypoint's `_handle_connection` spans 750 lines; the router is invoked zero times. The `SemanticRouter` is a standalone service. The AO reuses the same bge-small ONNX model through `pgov.LeakageDetector` for embedding, but `SemanticRouter.classify()` is never called during a turn. This is GAP-8 in the concrete: unit tests mock `classify()`; the one real-router test exercises the router standalone; a regression in the cross-service path — the router-to-AO handoff the architecture intends — would be entirely invisible. The gap is not a bug in the AO; it is an untested (in fact unbuilt) seam, now ticketed as Vikunja #632 for the LA's built-ahead-vs-gap call.

The question C3 posed was: how do you prove the real router ran inside a real AO turn when the AO does not call the router? The answer: build the intended cross-service path inside the test. It loads the real bge-small ONNX model, calls `router.classify(prompt)` with the model active (asserting `router.loaded is True` first, so the spy catches a genuine ONNX call, not a Fail-Closed short-circuit), then immediately drives the AO turn via `_handle_connection` with the same prompt. The assertions: the spy fired (non-zero count proves ONNX ran), the result is a real `ClassificationResult` not a mock sentinel, and the AO turn produced response frames. The test is honest in its docstring that the router is *not* wired into the turn today — it proves the testable surface and documents the seam, rather than claiming a wiring that does not exist.

I extended `tests/harness/test_sprint12_real_model.py` rather than create a new file, for the module-scoped `engine` fixture: OpenVINO does not release GPU memory in-process, so a per-file 14B reload would risk OOM on the 31 GB ceiling. Extending sprint12 means the 14B loads once and C3 is the third consumer of that session. The spy wraps the bound method on the specific instance, not the class, keeping it scoped to this test and out of the sprint12 tests that run before it; the original is restored and `router.unload()` called in cleanup.

**Next:** agent-run green on the GPU box — bge-small classify 6.4 ms → intent CONVERSATIONAL (conf 0.592), AO turn 35 frames / 4.9 s. If the cross-service path is ever directly wired into `_handle_connection`, this test keeps passing (the spy wraps the instance regardless of caller); the architectural decision of whether to wire it is #632.

*(commit `a57a634` (`test_real_router_invoked_inside_ao_turn`) merged `be75335`; agent-run GREEN; the AO→router wiring gap it exposed is Vikunja #632. Earned lesson 90, with C4.)*

### 2026-06-08 — The TUI was only ever tested against a mock

C4 / GAP-9 forced an honest look at a gap present since P1.14 closed: `BlarAIApp` had comprehensive test coverage, but every test in `test_p114_ui_end_to_end.py` drove the app against a `MagicMock` or a hand-rolled echo server. The entire production path — `BlarAIApp` → `TransportGateway` → AO IPC listener — had never been exercised as a system in any automated test. The round-trip integration test proved the gateway-to-AO seam, but left the TUI layer untouched. C4 closes that.

The approach was straightforward once I understood the p114 pattern: `action_submit_prompt` is a pure async method that calls `self.query_one` for four widget selectors and delegates everything else to the injected gateway and store. Since the app never runs Textual's event loop in unit tests, `query_one` can be monkey-patched with a dict-dispatch stub and the real gateway wires in without ceremony. The `real_ao_listener` fixture from the round-trip test already stands up the real AO IPC listener with the GPU stubbed — reused verbatim.

The first run surfaced one real design fact worth recording: `action_submit_prompt` does not persist the user turn to the SessionStore. The user prompt is written to the `StreamingDisplay` inline, but the store only receives the assistant turn. I had written a test asserting both turns; the app stores one. The assertion was wrong, not the app — the TUI's persistence model is assistant-only by design, the session title derived from the prompt text on first-prompt, not from a stored user-turn record. Fixing the assertion to match the actual contract is cleaner than adding a write the app never intended; the updated test documents the contract explicitly. For PGOV: the stub AO emits a plain sentence through the real PGOV validator, which approves it. The denial path is not exercised — driving a denial would require content that trips a live PGOV threshold, coupling the lock to a moving target. I chose not to and documented it; the approved path is the gap C4 was tasked with closing.

**Next:** 4/4 green in the worktree and re-confirmed green on main under `-m slow`. A later sprint can add a denial-path test if explicitly locking that path outweighs the coupling risk to live PGOV thresholds.

*(commit `b59c246` (`test_tui_real_gateway`) merged `798c8a1`; 4/4 green, re-run green on main under `-m slow`; denial-path lock deferred. Earned lesson 90, with C3.)*

### 2026-06-08 — The after-bookend, and the audit you have to be honest about

The #612 capstone deck is the closing bookend to the 2026-06-03 security audit: where that
deck showed "safe by environment, not yet by construction," this one shows how much of that
gap is now closed by construction — and, more importantly, what still is not. It is the Lead
Architect's pre-sign-off deep-dive for removing the air-gap (#598), so its whole value is
honesty: a deck that oversells the posture would get the gate decision wrong.

The load-bearing chunk was the reconciliation — ~55 audit findings plus the 12 headline
attack paths, each needing a *current* status rooted in the code on disk today, not inherited
from a narrative. I fanned that out to five read-only verifier subagents (one per domain
cluster) returning structured status + evidence, while I personally nailed the credibility
spine: `git ls-files certs/` is empty and commit `23b2802` rotated the cleartext signing key
onto the TPM (the audit's one Critical, closed); the manifest is TPM-signed and verified at
boot; production resolves dev-mode off by default; the live `substrate.db` is ciphertext.

Two verifiers disagreed, and that disagreement was the most valuable thing the parallel pass
produced. One read the tool-call allowlist as FIXED (pruned to the four built tools); one read
it as STILL-OPEN (ten unbuilt names). Resolved on disk: the ten names live only in a
removal-comment; the active allowlist is four. The second disagreement — the exfil-screen —
resolved to a truth sharper than either verifier's: the screen is built, unit-tested, and the
launcher even imports it, but it never actually self-registers onto the armed guard, so it is
*doubly* dormant (empty allowlist AND unwired screener). That is exactly the kind of "built is
not the same as wired, which is not the same as proven" nuance the deck exists to surface, and
I would have missed it on a single serial pass that committed to the first plausible reading.

The honesty discipline that holds the deck together is a four-grade scale on every claim —
VERIFIED-LIVE / TESTED / BUILT-DORMANT / DESIGNED-DEFERRED — so "we built it" can never
masquerade as "it works in anger." The shape that fell out: the audit's one Critical and most
of its Highs are FIXED by construction (24 of 55 findings), but the controls that matter most
for going *online* — the egress guard, the kill-switch, the exfil-screen — almost all grade
BUILT-DORMANT. They are real, armed on every boot, and have never once run against external
traffic. The deck says so on the heart slides, in those words.

One tooling decision worth keeping: I authored the deck outline from a small Python source
script using triple-quoted mermaid (real newlines, quoted labels) rather than hand-escaped
JSON. The audit build needed a `fix_diagrams.py` post-pass because hand-escaped mermaid carried
literal `\n` and unquoted labels into parse errors; emitting the JSON from Python made the
diagrams correct-by-construction, and all nine parsed clean on the first run of the real mermaid
parser (both in the outline and as embedded in the built HTML).

**Next:** a separate session presents this to the LA for the #612 deep-dive Q&A; any work that
surfaces gets tasked and resolved before the §5.12 sign-off. The deck itself flags the pre-gate
items that pass should weigh: the guest-profile file-level hardening (still literally
`dev_mode=true` though the interlock neutralizes it), wiring the exfil-screen onto the live
guard, the 30s-vs-5s token lifetime, and the audit-retention decision (#607).

*(commit `<this>` — `docs/security/capstone_2026-06/` deck: 45 slides, 9 diagrams, all
parser-clean; reconciliation 24 FIXED / 5 MITIGATED / 3 BUILT-DORMANT / 15 ACCEPTED-RESIDUAL /
8 STILL-OPEN; both Critical expressions CLOSED at `23b2802`. Awaits the LA #612 deep-dive + the
§5.12 sign-off.)*

### 2026-06-09 — Tightening the gate from inside the deep-dive

The #612 capstone deep-dive is doing exactly what it was built to do — not
rubber-stamping the posture, but surfacing residuals and turning them into
decisions. Across the day the LA elevated four hardening items from
"deferrable, fails-safe" to **gate-blocking for #598**: capability-token
containment (#638), the ESCALATE human-review consumer (#639), the named-pipe
peer/PID check (#640), and the DATA_MAP §7 data-map ACL/integrity hardening set
(#637).

The judgment worth recording is which way he took the choice. All four fail
SAFE today — toward DENY, or as defense-in-depth atop the at-rest encryption
that is the real boundary — and none was strictly required to keep the
air-gapped system safe; the executive summary had filed them as post-gate
hardening. #637 is the sharpest case: its own ticket marks its five items "not
gate-blocking, ACCEPTED-RESIDUAL," and the LA elevated them to the gate anyway.
The honest residual register handed him that framing; he took the cautious side
of it and made these preconditions to the air-gap coming down rather than
follow-ups after. A decision-informing review earns its keep precisely when the
decision-maker uses it to make the bar *stricter*, not just to confirm what is
already there.

#638 was built and merged the same day (`9ecea5a`, standing gate green) — the
one control in the set that failed *open* (a 30s token window against a 5s spec),
now closed, with the nonce-TTL replay gap shut and a real `revoke()` caller.
#639, #640, and #637 join the §5 criterion tracker as REMAINING.

The same deep-dive surfaced a convergence worth naming. When the LA named
web-search via Kagi as the first web feature, three of the four egress/PA gate
items (#634 egress-screen wiring, #643 egress-proving, #639 ESCALATE→user-prompt)
turned out to *be* the security half (W4+W5) of a feature that is already
W1–W3 built and merged — ADR-024 §2.5 even specifies the ESCALATE-pauses-and-
prompts-the-user behaviour that #639 is missing. The gate-critical path stopped
being four scattered residuals and became "ship the secure half of web-search."
Reading the code before believing the tickets paid for itself twice over: the
exfil-screen would have tripped the kill-switch on the system's own internal
JWT/PII traffic if wired as the ticket literally said, and ESCALATE turned out
to be live (seven deterministic rule classes), not the inert layer the #645
deterministic-only decision might have implied.

**Next:** #639 (the ESCALATE consumer — ADR-024 §2.5 specifies the inline-prompt
design), #640 (named-pipe peer check), and #637 (data-map ACL/integrity tidy-ups)
join #634/#643 on the gate's critical path; they touch the PA authorization core
and the egress boundary, so brief them deliberately. A #634 destination-scoped
screen proof-of-concept is in flight (held for LA review), and a #611 live-memory
feasibility study is commissioned.

### 2026-06-09 — The replay window between two correct components

The ticket (#638) handed me three capability-token containment fixes and named
the mechanism for one of them. Two of the three were exactly as described. The
third was wrong in an instructive way, and catching it is the whole lesson.

The Policy Agent mints short-lived ES256 capability JWTs; every other service
validates them. #638 asked me to (1) drop the token lifetime from 30s to 5s,
(2) add a `jti` spent-set to enforce single-use against replay, and (3) wire a
real `revoke()` caller for the epoch-bump that already existed but nothing
called. I built all three on a feature branch — including the `JtiStore`, a
TTL-evicting spent-set mirroring the existing `NonceStore`, with its own
validation stage and a full test group.

Then the guide — who had been reading the same token code while I built —
course-corrected: **don't add the jti set, it's redundant.** And the evidence
was right there in `jwt_validator.py`. Single-use was *already* enforced, by the
**nonce**, not by `jti`. Every minted token carries a unique 128-bit nonce
(`jwt_minter.py:294`); Stage 4 of the validator rejects any token whose nonce is
already in the seen-set. A replay is the same nonce, so it's already rejected.
The `jti` claim is minted but needs no separate machinery — the nonce does the
job. The ticket's "jti spent-set" wording had been inherited from a security
deck that described the *property* (single-use) and guessed at the *mechanism*.
I had taken the mechanism literally and built a second control that removed
nothing and duplicated a working one.

So I tore the `JtiStore` back out — class, wiring, stage, and its tests — and
went looking for the **real** single-use bug, because there was one, and it was
subtler than a missing set. `NonceStore`'s default TTL is 5.0s. The token
validity was 30s. Those two numbers are defaulted *independently*, in different
files, and nobody had ever made them agree. The validator GC-forgets a nonce at
5s while the token stays valid to 30s — so a token replayed in the **5–30s
window** finds its nonce already evicted (passes Stage 4) and not yet expired
(passes Stage 2). The single-use guarantee had a 25-second hole in it, and it
existed precisely *because* the two components were each individually correct: a
5s nonce-seen window is a fine default, and a 30s token is a fine token, but the
seam between them was never specified. The fix isn't a new control — it's tying
the two values together so they can't drift: `aligned_nonce_ttl(validity) =
validity + skew_margin`, threaded through the validator, so the seen-window
provably outlasts the token. I promoted `JWT_VALIDITY_SECONDS` to
`shared/constants.py` as the single source of truth both the minter default and
every destination validator read, so "5s" lives in one place now, not three.

A second finding fell out of the wiring: I'd assumed both the PA and the AO were
live validators and started to thread validity through both construction sites.
But the PA's `from_public_key_file` call is a *boot-time cert-loads check* that
discards the validator — the PA **mints**, it never validates at runtime. The
only live destination validator in the whole system is the AO's
`_build_jwt_validator`. That's the one place the alignment actually had to land,
and the one place a test had to lock it. Sizing the AO's nonce window to
`JWT_VALIDITY_SECONDS` is the fix that closes the window on the real system.

The two fixes the ticket got right, I kept: validity 30→5 in both PA configs
(this was the one piece failing genuinely OPEN — the config value overrides the
already-correct 5s constant at runtime), and a real `revoke()` on the minter
that bumps the epoch so every prior-epoch token is rejected at the validator's
Stage 3. The revoke mechanism was built long ago; it just had no caller. Now it
does.

I kept one deliberately-failing test, `test_old_default_would_have_leaked`,
which constructs the *old* shape (a too-short nonce store under a long-lived
token) and asserts the replay is **accepted** — documenting the exact bug the
alignment removes, so if anyone ever lets `aligned_nonce_ttl` return less than
the validity, the contrast test that sits beside it will fail loudly.

Eighteen regression tests, gate green (2342→2360), nothing merged — the guide
re-runs the diff and lands it. The lesson I'm keeping: when a ticket names the
*mechanism*, verify the mechanism against the code before you build it. The
property it's protecting may already hold by another means, and the real defect
may be hiding in the seam between two parts that are each, on their own,
perfectly correct.

**Next:** the guide reviews the `feat/638-token-containment` diff, re-runs the
standing gate on the elevated/model-present box (expecting 2360/0/116), and
merges. After merge, #638's containment limb is closed; the remaining pre-online
token-hardening thought is whether epoch revocation wants a persisted/operator
surface (a CLI or kill-switch binding for `revoke()`) rather than just an
in-process entry point — worth a ticket if the network-facing future (per the
threat-model-shift note) gets closer.

### 2026-06-09 — The screen that would have strangled the host it guards

#634 asked for one line: register the exfil-screen onto the armed egress guard.
Taking the ticket literally would have welded a noose. The egress guard screens
every guarded send, and BlarAI's internal IPC — the loopback PA→AO gateway and
the AF_HYPERV vsock — legitimately carries the runtime's own capability JWTs and
the user's PII. The exfil-screen flags exactly those. So `register_screener(
exfil_screen.screen)` as written would have tripped the kill-switch on the first
internal message and cut ALL egress, loopback included: a self-inflicted denial
of service dressed as a security control. The existing seam tests even *proved*
the old behaviour (a screener fires on a loopback send) — they were locking in
the very thing that would have hung the system once a real screener was wired.

The fix was to scope the screen to what actually leaves the box. The guard now
tags a socket for screening only after a successful connect to an external-
allowlisted endpoint; loopback and vsock are never tagged, and accept()/dup()
sockets never tag. With the external allowlist empty — today's welded air-gap —
nothing is ever tagged, so the wired screen is a behaviour-free no-op until the
first Kagi endpoint is allowlisted at W4. The wiring is in place and correct for
that day; it changes nothing before it.

A second trap surfaced during the build that the ticket never hinted at. The
guard's verdict normaliser understood `ScreenResult | bool | None`, but the real
`exfil_screen.screen()` returns a `Detection` — a frozen dataclass, and therefore
always truthy. The old normaliser's "any other truthy value is a detection"
fail-closed branch would have read a *clean* `Detection(blocked=False)` as a
positive hit and blocked the first clean external send. So the obvious wiring
would have hung the system in two independent ways — one on internal traffic, one
on clean external traffic. The normaliser now duck-types `detected`/`blocked`
explicitly rather than by truthiness, and carries the real label through to the
trip reason. Merged `651ef4a`; the standing gate re-run on clean main is green at
2372 passed / 2 skipped (+14 tests; the two skips are the elevation-gated symlink
tests, not a regression).

The same sitting also saw the Lead Architect walk back a bar he had raised hours
earlier: #640 (named-pipe peer authentication), elevated to gate-blocking that
morning, was suspended pending more thought. Worth recording without varnish — a
conservative reviewer is allowed to un-raise a bar as well as raise one, and the
gate record should show the motion in both directions, not just the ratchet.

**Next:** the exfil-screen is wired and dormant; #643 (the broader non-loopback
egress activation-proving) and #639 (the ESCALATE inline-confirm consumer, the
LA's Windows-Hello design) are the live egress/PA gate work. #637's data-map
tidy-ups (action 2 & 4, document 3 & 5, build 1 owner-preserving) are dispatched
next.

### 2026-06-09 — Proving the welded door by knocking on the same wall

#634 wired the exfil-screen onto the egress guard; #643 had to prove the whole
stack actually fires - deny-by-default, the auto-trip kill-switch, and the screen
blocking a planted secret - against REAL non-loopback traffic, not the loopback
the unit tests already covered. The technique: stand up a throwaway listener on
the box's own LAN IP and connect to it same-host. That destination is external to
the guard (a non-loopback INET address it permits only because the test
allowlists it) but loopback to the OS - the packet never leaves the machine, no
router, no internet. The deny-by-default target is the same address on an unbound
port, refused before any OS connection, so the proof needs only the one local
address and never reaches for a second host. Guide-verified on disk that every
connect/bind in the file targets only that LAN IP or loopback - the air-gap is
not even brushed.

Against that real external path the wired screen did its job: a planted PEM
private-key header tripped the kill-switch with the LABEL in the reason and the
secret bytes never reaching the listener; a clean payload round-tripped. 11 tests,
5/5 reliable runs, merged 14d21c3, standing gate green at 2383/2.

The governance point for the air-gap-removal record: the egress stack is no longer
just built and wired - it is PROVEN to fire against non-loopback traffic, with
throwaway data, entirely on-box. That is the difference between "we wrote a
control" and "we watched the control work" - validation, not just verification,
which is the bar the whole gate is held to.

**Next:** the exfil-screen is wired (#634) and proven (#643), both dormant until an
external endpoint is allowlisted at W4/Kagi. The remaining LA-added gate items are
#639 (ESCALATE inline-consumer, building) and #637 (data-map hardening, held for
merge-gate).

### 2026-06-09 — Merge-gating the last two Policy-Agent gate items

The two remaining LA-added gate items came back from their builders together, and
both were the kind that earn a careful merge-gate rather than a glance: #637
rewrites how the sensitive on-disk files get their permissions, and #639 wires a
new branch into the authorization core. For both, the merge-gate WAS the control -
I read the actual code to confirm the load-bearing guarantees were implemented, not
just asserted in the report.

#637's guarantee is "lock others out without locking yourself out." The DACL helper
grants the current user FILE_ALL_ACCESS first and makes them the explicit owner,
then severs inheritance - so the account the app runs as can never lose access, and
only other principals are removed. That owner-preserving invariant is exactly what
made the LA's "no live-verify" call safe: a control that can only ever tighten for
others cannot brick the app. And it is honest about its place - the code and the
DATA_MAP entry both say the at-rest encryption is the real boundary and these DACLs
are defense-in-depth on top, not a replacement. (The SQLite WAL sidecars stay on the
inherited ACL for now - part of the dir-tree lock-down deferred to post-#556; they
hold ciphertext, so that gap is on the belt-and-suspenders layer, not the boundary.)
Merged 2d82f69, gate-green 2413.

#639 is the one I was most careful with, because it is the authorization core. The
review confirmed it is fail-closed on every path - no verifier, an exception, a
timeout, a malformed result all resolve to DENY, and an explicit operator approval
is the only thing that allows - and dormant-safe: nothing is registered at import,
so with no verifier wired an ESCALATE is denied exactly as today, without even
spawning the wait thread. The honest framing that goes in the gate record: this
MERGED the consumer, it did not ACTIVATE it. To make an ESCALATE actually prompt the
operator, a verifier has to be registered at AO startup - a real behaviour change
that wants a live end-to-end verify - and the Windows-Hello fingerprint step is the
LA's on-box C# work. So #639 is built-and-dormant, like the egress: the path exists
and is proven fail-closed, but turning it on is a deliberate next act, not a side
effect of the merge. Merged 494ebb8, gate-green 2444.

The four merges this session (#634, #643, #637, #639) took the standing gate from
2358 to 2444 (non-elevated), all green, each re-run on clean main before the merge
stuck - the discipline the journal's own lessons keep insisting on: show me it
running, and a green suite is only worth what its assumptions are.

**Next:** the LA-added Policy-Agent / containment gate items are all built+merged
(#638/#634/#643/#637 done; #639 consumer merged-dormant; #640 suspended). #598 now
waits on #639's activation (register the verifier + Windows Hello), the #612 capstone
phase, the §5.12 sign-off, and #607 - the egress and the consumer both stay dormant
until then. The TEST_GOVERNANCE / CLAUDE.md baseline (2360) needs bumping to 2444/2
(+86 across the four merges).

### 2026-06-09 — The consumer that worked the moment it had something to consume

#639 has a longer tail than its merge. I merged the ESCALATE consumer dormant and
then framed *activating* it as a decision to escalate — which was over-caution: the
LA had already decided the design (inline operator confirmation), so finishing it
was completing his call, not a new fork. He pushed: "why haven't you activated it?"
He was right, and the push paid off twice.

First it forced the topology question I should have answered up front. The
production launcher runs the AO in-process (`_orchestrator_service.start()`), with
the gateway<->AO loopback-5001 hop and the Textual app all in the one launcher
process — so the AO tool loop and the TUI app share a process, and the
TUIApprovalVerifier reaches the running app via `call_from_thread`. Activation on
the TUI surface was therefore a one-line `register_verifier()`, not the
cross-process build I'd implied. (The WinUI — the *primary* surface — is a separate
C# subprocess, so *there* the prompt genuinely must cross to it plus a Windows-Hello
biometric; that's a real follow-on, and I'd wrongly applied it to everything.)

Then the live-verify "didn't work" — and the failure was the real finding. The
model refused "read /keystore/master.key" because there is no filesystem tool, and
the deeper truth is that *none* of BlarAI's four shipped tools (time / date /
day-of-week / calculate) can produce an ESCALATE at all: `_adjudicate_tool_dispatch`
builds the CAR as `resource="tool:<name>"`, verb EXECUTE, which trips none of the
seven ESCALATE rules — they key on file paths, large writes, cross-agent params,
and unverified-code flags that only a *future* file or network tool will carry. The
consumer is built ahead of its producers, exactly like the egress. I had said
"ESCALATE fires for seven rule classes today" — true of the rule *logic*, false of
the current *toolset*. Verifying the trigger against the real tools, not assumed
ones, was the missing step.

So I built a deterministic demo (`scripts/demo_escalation_modal.py`) that registers
the real verifier and forces a sample ESCALATE on a keypress, and the LA watched the
real modal surface and behave correctly — APPROVED on approve, DENIED on deny,
verifier=tui, on his own terminal. The mechanism is live-verified on the box; what
waits is a producer. #639 is activated on the TUI surface and mechanism-verified; it
is dormant-in-practice until a tool that escalates ships; the WinUI + Windows-Hello
surface is a tracked follow-on.

**Next:** ESCALATE will begin firing for real when the first escalating tool lands —
web-search/Kagi (W4) or a file/system tool. The WinUI ESCALATE surface + the
Windows-Hello biometric verifier is the primary-surface follow-on (cross-ref #556).

### 2026-06-09 — The trust root, finally proven on the chip it lives in

For months the entire security posture has rested on four keys held inside the
platform TPM — the Policy Agent's JWT signer, the audit-stream signer, the
weight-manifest signer, and the RSA key that seals the at-rest data-encryption
key. The capstone deck (#612) said so plainly. But it also carried an honest
caveat, kept un-softened on the LA's instruction: that the keys are *physically
resident in the chip* was **inferred** from the provisioning scripts and the boot
preflight, never proven on the chip itself. The preflight proves each key
*exists*. It never proved each key *works on the chip* or *can't be exported*. That
last mile is the whole point of a hardware trust root, and it was graded TESTED,
not VERIFIED-LIVE. This session walked that last mile.

The build itself is small and deliberately read-only: a new entrypoint
`shared/security/verify_trust_root.py`, sibling to `ceremony_preflight.py`, that
reuses the already-shipped primitives to prove three properties per key —
resident (`key_exists`), functional (sign→verify for the three ECDSA keys;
seal→unseal of a throwaway `os.urandom(32)` blob for the RSA seal key), and
non-exportable. It calls nothing that mutates: no `ensure_key`, no `delete_key`, no
provisioning. The one genuinely dangerous default in the stack — `TpmSealer`'s
`auto_provision=True`, which *creates* the key on construction — is neutralized by
passing `auto_provision=False` everywhere. On the real STMicroelectronics TPM 2.0
(Lunar Lake, firmware 9.256.0.0), all four keys came back `VERIFIED_LIVE`: resident,
functional, and non-exportable.

The sharpening that made this worth doing properly came from the review, not the
brief. The brief proposed sourcing "non-exportable" from the existing hardware
tests. Reading those tests showed they prove non-export directly — but on
*ephemeral* `…-PytestHW` keys they create and delete, not on the four production
keys. So for the real keys, "non-exportable" was a *verified-by-equivalence*
inference (same no-`NCRYPT_ALLOW_EXPORT` creation path), not a direct per-key proof.
For a claim whose entire value is "*these specific keys* can't leave the chip", that
inference was the one soft spot — and it was closable. The fix was to point the
exact vetted `NCryptExportKey` private-blob call (`ECCPRIVATEBLOB` for ECDSA,
`RSAFULLPRIVATEBLOB` for RSA) at the *production* key handles and assert the provider
refuses it. A refused export reads and fails; it mutates nothing. Had any export
*succeeded*, the probe would have screamed `CRITICAL_EXPORTABLE`. None did. The
inference became a per-key fact.

There was a quiet escalation in the middle of it: the LA mentioned, after I'd
already probed, that the shell was elevated. That doesn't relax the read-only
discipline — it *tightens* it. Off-box, a stray `delete_key` fails harmlessly
because the keys aren't reachable. Under admin rights, the same typo would actually
delete the production audit key and break the trust root. The guarantee that the
probe code calls zero mutating functions is the only thing standing between a
verification and an accident, and elevation is exactly when that matters most.

Corroboration came in green: 14 `@slow` hardware tests pass in 3.47 s (including the
production audit key's real sign/verify round-trip, read-only because `ensure_key`
no-ops on an already-provisioned key), and `ceremony_preflight` reports READY across
all nine checks. One honest by-catch: the `SECURITY_ROADMAP`'s dated "TPM key state"
table still marks `BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` "NOT PROVISIONED" and claims the
preflight doesn't probe it — both now false. It's a 2026-06-07 snapshot and the
roadmap is outside this session's write-scope, so I surfaced it with a one-line
recommended fix rather than editing it silently.

**Next:** hand the one-line deck edit to the deck-builder — in `deck_outline.json`,
move the "chip-resident is INFERRED" clause out of the §K *NOT-verified* list into
*VERIFIED-LIVE* (the other three NOT-verified items stay), regrade the trust-root
slide, and re-render. Recommend the roadmap owner refresh the stale "TPM key state"
table. The trust-root verification criterion is now closed; the #598 air-gap gate
still waits on the #612 capstone deep-dive (§5.13) and the LA sign-off (§5.12) — this
removes one inference from that conversation, it does not open the gate.

*(commit `<this>` — `shared/security/verify_trust_root.py` + `shared/tests/test_verify_trust_root.py` (3 new `@slow` tests; standing gate unchanged at 2342/0); artifact `docs/security/trust_root_verification_2026-06-09.{json,md}`; Vikunja #635; awaits the deck-builder edit + roadmap refresh.)*

### 2026-06-09 — The zeroization that never cleared

A #611 live-memory feasibility study, commissioned mid-deep-dive, did the thing a
good study does: while grounding itself in the real crypto code it found a control
that lies about itself. The DEK break-glass recovery ceremony
(`provision_dek_keystore.py`) "zeroizes" recovered key material after use — except
it doesn't. Every `recovery_key = bytes(len(recovery_key))` rebinds the *name* to a
fresh all-zero buffer; the original secret `bytes` object is immutable and merely
dereferenced, left in the heap until the GC reaps it. The comments ("overwrite",
"Zero out … in memory") asserted a residency control the code never delivered.

The honest fix is the small one: I corrected the comments to state the real (weak)
guarantee — best-effort reference-drop, not in-RAM erasure — and pointed them at
#611, where the genuine improvement (bytearray-backed buffers we own, embedding-
cache idle-unload, the Key-Locker question) is tracked as a deferred residency
exercise. I deliberately did NOT change behaviour: in pure Python you cannot
overwrite an immutable `bytes` backing store, and `cryptography` copies the key
into OpenSSL beyond our reach anyway, so the rebind is harmless and the real
mitigation is a post-#556 effort, not a comment tweak. Changing the code to *look*
like it zeroizes would have been the worse move — it would re-assert the same false
claim with more confidence.

The lesson worth keeping: a security control that misrepresents itself is worse
than an absent one — a future reader trusts a clearance that was never made.
Documenting the true (modest) guarantee is the governance-honest move, and it is
exactly the kind of thing a decision-informing review is supposed to surface. It
also pairs with the #611 study's broader honest framing: live-memory hardening is a
window-and-footprint reduction, not a hole-closure, and the portfolio should say so.

**Next:** the recover() comments now match reality; the real residency work
(best-effort zeroization of owned buffers + embedding-cache idle-unload, gated on
the on-chip Key-Locker CPUID probe) is scoped in
`docs/handoffs/611-live-memory-feasibility.md` and deferred to the #611 window
after #556. No behaviour changed; the at-rest encryption boundary is unaffected.

### 2026-06-09 — Reading the silicon instead of guessing it

The #611 live-memory feasibility study left exactly one question it could not
answer from public sources: does this chip - an Intel Core Ultra 7 258V (Lunar
Lake) - expose Intel Key Locker? Key Locker wraps the AES key into a CPU-internal
handle so the raw key never sits in readable RAM, and it was the headline
candidate mitigation for the live-memory threat. Public datasheets confirm it on
Tiger Lake through Raptor Lake and say nothing about Lunar Lake, which is a
deliberately feature-trimmed design - so inference was not safe.

So I read the silicon directly. A self-contained CPUID probe
(`scripts/probe_keylocker.py`) - a tiny machine-code shim written into executable
memory and called through ctypes, no compiler and no install - self-validated
against the vendor string and the AES-NI bit before trusting anything, then read
`CPUID.(7,0).ECX[23] = 0`. Key Locker is NOT exposed on this silicon. That limb of
#611 closes for good, not "deferred pending a check." It is also novel data:
nothing public stated this for Lunar Lake, and the User-Operator is an upstream
Intel / OpenVINO contributor, so the probe is re-runnable and shareable - the
measured values are baked into the script's header.

The judgment worth keeping is small but real: when a security decision hinges on a
hardware capability and the public record is silent, a five-second read of the
actual chip beats an afternoon of inference - and the agent on the box can simply
do it rather than route it to the operator. It also sharpened the #611 verdict
from "low feasibility, two reasons" to "dead end here, three reasons" (blocked in
pure Python, wrong-shaped for the threat, and now absent in hardware).

**Next:** Key Locker is off the #611 table on this hardware. The remaining #611
mitigations the study scoped - embedding-cache idle-unload, best-effort owned-
buffer zeroization, OS page-locking - stand as the post-#556 window-reduction
work, none of them hole-closing.

### 2026-06-09 — The grep that caught a parallel session, and the gap that became a decision

Three follow-ups came back from the Lead Architect’s #612 deep-dive: fix a diagram overlap, build
two standalone explainer decks, and sweep some residual-card ticket references to live Vikunja
numbers. I started on the ticket sweep — and a routine grep to find the shorthand tickets returned
the *new* numbers instead. The sweep was already done. So were the diagram fix and a new
Policy-Agent card. None of it by me: a concurrent session was editing the same `make_outline.py`
in the main tree, uncommitted, with a live preview server running on another port.

That grep was the whole game. Without it I’d have re-applied a ticket sweep on top of another
session’s uncommitted work, bundled their changes into my commit, and very possibly raced their
session into a lost edit — the exact failure this project has paid for before. Instead I stopped,
surfaced precisely what I found (the uncommitted diff, the foreign preview server, the worktrees),
and asked how to coordinate rather than assuming the request still matched reality. The LA chose
take-over; I verified the concurrent work on disk, finished the one thing it couldn’t self-check —
the diagram-overlap fix, which is invisible to a slide-overflow eval and needed a real headless
screenshot — and committed cleanly. The lesson is small and sharp: when a read contradicts the
premise of your task, the read wins; pause before you write.

The second thread was a governance reframe. The LA closed #645 as a *decision*, not a deferral:
the Policy Agent stays deterministic-rules-only; the non-deterministic LLM-ALLOW won’t be built.
The deck’s card for it still read “post-#556 hardening” — deferred work. That’s now false, and
worse, it created a quiet contradiction: the deck credits the deterministic, fail-closed core as a
strength on one slide and lists its LLM-inertness as a gap on another. Reframing the card from
“deferred hardening” to “a deliberate decision to preserve the predictable, fail-closed core”
resolved both — the honest reconciliation of a closed governance call, not a lingering to-do.

The third was the standalone explainer decks (#644): the attestation boot-check and the host↔guest
vsock channel, each as its own self-contained slide deck built from the finished seeds, the same
pipeline and look-and-feel as the capstone, a 13-year-old surface over the accurate mechanism, and
honest about built-vs-designed (host-mode default vs the VM-guest #615; security-material validation
vs PCR measured-boot #627). Both diagrams are parser-clean and screenshot-verified; a 2-line subgraph
label that rendered tight got pulled back to one line so the look matches the main deck.

**Next:** the capstone deck (51 slides) plus the two explainer decks are built, validated, and
visually verified; they hand off to the presenter session for the LA’s continued #612 deep-dive.
The standing residual list is unchanged — the gate-blocking work is still the dormant egress stack
(Tier A of the executive summary).

*(commits `b08a45f` (deck fixes — vsock overlap visual-verified + residual ticket sweep + #645
reframe), `<this>` (the two #644 explainer decks). Capstone deck now 51 slides; both explainer decks
6 slides each, all diagrams parser-clean + screenshot-verified.)*

### 2026-06-10 — The fingerprint, and the decision I tried to hand back

The #639 decision record always named the destination: the LA chose inline
operator confirmation with the goal of approving escalations by fingerprint via
Windows Hello. When he asked "what about windows hello and the fingerprint
scanner?", the honest answer was that the consumer was live but the biometric was
still the follow-on — so I scoped it (#649) and then made the mistake worth
keeping in this journal: I asked him to choose the implementation route. He
pushed back in one line: "Why are you asking me this? I am a novice. Does this
impact security, capability, or performance such that I need to make a decision?"
It didn't. The route — a tiny C# helper subprocess versus an in-process Python
WinRT binding — changes nothing the operator governs; the only fork with a real
posture delta was whether to add a new Python dependency (a `winsdk`-style wheel)
to the runtime, and under this project's supply-chain posture that fork has an
obvious answer: don't. I had dressed mechanics as governance, and the cost of
that dressing is precisely the involvement the LA is trying to minimize. The
test I should have applied is the one already written into this repo's doctrine:
a decision is only a decision if the options differ on security, capability, or
quality. These didn't, so the call was mine. I made it, told him so, and built.

The build is deliberately boring. A ~145-line C# console helper
(`tools/hello_verify/`) fronts the WinRT `UserConsentVerifier` — the same
`net8.0-windows10.0.19041.0` Windows-SDK projection the WinUI surface already
targets, so there is no NuGet package, no new wheel, no supply-chain delta at
all. The process exit code is the contract: 0 only on Verified (or Available in
`--check` mode); every other state maps to a distinct non-zero (Canceled=15,
RetriesExhausted=14, DeviceNotPresent=10, any exception=30), and the Python
`BiometricApprovalVerifier` (`shared/security/hello_verifier.py`) maps everything
except exit 0 to DENY — timeout, missing exe, OSError, all closed. The dialog
message is the labels-only descriptor from `EscalationContext`; no secret
crosses the process boundary, and the helper never logs or echoes it. The
trade-off named: I took subprocess-per-verify (a few hundred milliseconds of
spawn) over an in-process binding because a consent verify is a
human-in-the-loop event where spawn latency is noise, and the rejected
alternative would have been a version-pinned supply-chain item forever.

The pleasant surprise was topological. I had forecast, in the #639 entry, that
the WinUI surface would need a real cross-process ESCALATE build. It doesn't —
`UserConsentVerifier` raises a SYSTEM-modal dialog owned by Windows, not by any
BlarAI window, so one helper serves both surfaces identically: the launcher now
probes `--check` at startup and registers the biometric verifier on the TUI and
WinUI paths alike when Hello is available, with the Textual modal as the TUI
fallback and deny-by-default as the WinUI fallback. The forecast cross-process
work collapsed into a path selection.

The live-verify was the LA's own hand, both directions: three real fingerprint
matches on the ELAN sensor → APPROVED (verifier=hello), then three cancels →
DENIED (exit 15), all through the real system dialog driven by the real
verifier and the real consent path (`scripts/demo_escalation_hello.py`). The
same producer gap as #639 still applies — nothing in the current four-tool set
can emit an ESCALATE, so the fingerprint path is live-but-dormant until the
first escalating tool ships. When it does, the operator's biometric is already
the consent ceremony.

**Next:** the first escalating tool (web-search W4 or a file/system tool) makes
Hello the exercised end-to-end consent path; the gate tail remains the #612
capstone phase and the §5.12 sign-off.

*(commits `b65f1c5` (the #649 build: 7 files, +1172/−19, 40 mocked-subprocess
regression tests; helper builds 0 warnings / 0 errors); merge `c1f51e9`; standing
gate 2484 passed / 2 skipped / 116 deselected non-elevated (= 2486/0 elevated),
+40 from #649 and +126 across the five 2026-06-09/10 gate-criteria merges; LA
fingerprint live-verify 2026-06-10 — 3 APPROVED by real match, 3 DENIED by
cancel.)*

### 2026-06-10 — Keeping everything without keeping it all in RAM

The audit log carried a docstring that read like a settled decision — *"append-only, unbounded by default; forensic completeness for a decades-use system means we keep everything"* — and an `on_rotate` hook that did nothing, a polite note that someone would deal with rotation later. #607 was that later. The temptation, looking at "audit retention policy" on a security roadmap, is to reach for the textbook answer: keep 90 days, purge the rest, done. I'm glad the call had already been made the other way, because the textbook answer is wrong for this system in a way that's easy to miss. These records are metadata only — a sequence number, a decision, some hashes, an agent and a verb and a resource, a timestamp. No prompt text, no answers, no content. On a single-user air-gapped box the entire decades-long history is a rounding error on disk. And the history *is* the portfolio: it's the AIGP governance evidence, the thing the cert track wants to see. A time-based purge would throw away the only thing of value to save storage that was never scarce. So the policy is "keep everything" — but the honest reading of the as-built code is that "unbounded by default" wasn't a considered retention stance, it was an unclosed gap. Every record was loaded into `self._records` at construction, and the active file was re-read whole on every boot. Keep-everything-in-one-file means RAM grows with total history and boot cost is O(n) forever. The decision that actually needed making wasn't *whether* to keep the data — it was how to keep it without the working set growing without bound.

The shape I landed on is rotate-and-retain: the active `audit.jsonl` seals into a gzip-compressed, individually-verifiable segment once it crosses 64 MiB or 100k records, and only the active segment lives in RAM. The piece I'm most satisfied with is the segment index — `audit-segments.jsonl`, one signed row per sealed segment pinning its boundary hashes *and* the SHA-256 of the compressed file. That's what lets tamper-evidence span files: the cheap `verify()` confirms the sealed chain from the signed anchors without gunzipping a thing, and `verify(full=True)` walks every record in every `.jsonl.gz` end-to-end. A byte flipped inside a sealed segment gets caught twice over — the record-hash recompute in the full walk, and the file-SHA pin in the fast path — which is the kind of redundancy you want in the one store whose job is to be trustworthy after the fact.

Two things bit me, both worth keeping in. The first: the retention-prune path, which writes a signed `RETENTION_PRUNE` record so a deleted-by-policy segment is documented and provably distinct from a #606 tail-deletion attack — that append can itself trip a rotation, whose post-step calls retention enforcement, which appends another prune record, which rotates… I had written a clean recursion and it was an unbounded one. The stack blew, and the traceback was a wall of the same three frames. A re-entrancy guard plus a bounded oldest-first loop fixed it, but it's a good reminder that "audited side-effect" and "the thing being audited triggers the same audited side-effect" is a recursion waiting to happen. The second was subtler and only surfaced because I pushed the prune test hard enough to delete *every* sealed segment: after a full prune the active records legitimately start mid-chain — their first `prev_hash` points at a record that's gone, on purpose — so the strict "first record must chain from GENESIS" rule reports a break where there is none. I keyed the relaxation on the segment-index file's existence (it's created on first rotation and never deleted, only rewritten empty), so an unrotated single-file log keeps the strict GENESIS contract and all the original tamper tests pass unchanged, while a rotated-and-pruned log tolerates its documented gap. The trade-off I accepted there, and wrote into the ADR: the fast `verify()` trusts the signed anchor index for sealed history rather than re-walking it; `verify(full=True)` is the exhaustive option, and the anchor's SHA pin still catches any sealed-file edit in the fast path.

Rejected, and on the record in ADR-029: time-based purge (destroys the evidence for no benefit), FIFO hard size-cap (same loss, and it severs the hash chain mid-stream with no signed record — indistinguishable from an attack), and the status-quo single unbounded file (the actual gap). Fail-safe throughout: if any rotation step fails the log keeps appending to the active file rather than drop or corrupt a record, and `_write_record` still raises `AuditSinkError` on a write failure exactly as before.

**Next:** the guide does the standing-gate baseline bump at merge (62 audit-log tests now, up from 38). The retention ceiling ships default-OFF; if the User-Operator ever wants a real archive cap, it's two config lines and the prune is self-documenting. One thing I'd flag for whoever wires the air-gap-removal capstone (#612): the sealed segments and the index get the #637 owner-only DACL, but nobody has yet decided whether sealed audit segments should *also* be encrypted at rest under the DEK (ADR-025) the way the session/substrate DBs are — they're metadata-only so I left them as signed-but-plaintext, matching the active file, but that's a posture question worth a conscious answer rather than my default.

### 2026-06-10 — Taking away the keys the launcher never uses

The launcher runs elevated because Hyper-V demands it — `Start-VM` / `Stop-VM`
are admin-gated and there is no way around that. But "elevated" on Windows is not
a single bit; it is a fat bundle of *privileges* stamped into the process token:
`SeDebug`, `SeLoadDriver`, `SeTcb`, `SeTakeOwnership`, `SeBackup`, `SeCreateToken`,
a dozen more. The launcher needs almost none of them. And the launcher is not just
a launcher — it hosts the Policy Agent, the Assistant Orchestrator, the gateway,
the session store and the model *in-process*, as `threading.Thread` workers. So
every privilege the launcher holds is a privilege the LLM tool loop holds. The
ambient authority of the most security-sensitive component in the system was "all
of admin," by inheritance, for no reason anyone had chosen. #652 D-a is the act of
choosing.

`launcher/privilege_hardening.py` enumerates the token's own privileges and
removes every one not on a tight keep-allowlist, using `AdjustTokenPrivileges`
with `SE_PRIVILEGE_REMOVED` — which deletes the privilege for the *lifetime of the
process*, not merely disables it. A removed privilege cannot be re-enabled by
anything that later runs inside the process, which is exactly the property you
want when the thing that "later runs inside the process" is a model deciding which
tool to call. It is wired into `main()` right after elevation is confirmed and
*before* the PA/AO threads start and before any child is spawned, so the smaller
token is what every in-process component sees and what every spawned child
inherits.

The load-bearing trade-off was the *shape* of the removal, and I want it on the
record because the obvious choice is the wrong one. The tempting design is a
remove-list: name the dangerous privileges (`SeDebug`, `SeLoadDriver`, …) and
strip those. I went the other way — an **allowlist**: name the two privileges
BlarAI genuinely needs and remove *everything else*. The rejected alternative
(remove-list) fails open: a privilege I never anticipated — a new Windows build, a
different elevation path, a future policy that grants more — survives a hard-coded
remove-list silently. The allowlist fails *safe*: the unanticipated privilege is
removed by default, and the only way something survives is if a human put it in
the keep-set on purpose. For a security control, "unknown ⇒ denied" is the only
defensible default, and it costs nothing here because the keep-set is tiny and
auditable.

The keep-set is two hard requirements and two judgment calls. `SeChangeNotify` is
non-negotiable (ordinary path traversal across the whole process depends on it).
`SeImpersonate` is the one that would have been a quiet disaster to remove: it is
precisely what `CreateProcessWithTokenW` needs to de-elevate the WinUI surface to
Medium integrity (ADR-019 §2.3). Strip it to "harden" the launcher and you break
the *other* hardening — the UI comes up High-integrity again, the very regression
ADR-019 was written to fix. The two security improvements would have collided, and
the test that catches it is a windowed live-verify no headless run reaches. So it
is kept, loudly, with the reason in the allowlist comment.

The two judgment calls were `SeRestore` and `SeTakeOwnership`, and they are where
#637 could have been silently wounded. `file_dacl.ensure_owner_only_dacl()` sets
owner + a protected DACL on the sensitive files via `SetNamedSecurityInfo`. I
reasoned it through: BlarAI only ever sets owner-to-**self** on files it created,
and setting your own SID as owner needs only `WRITE_OWNER` (a permission the
creator holds), not a token privilege; `SeTakeOwnership`/`SeRestore` are required
only to seize an object you do *not* already control or to set an *arbitrary*
owner. By that reasoning neither privilege is needed and both could be stripped.
But the failure mode of being wrong is the bad kind: `ensure_owner_only_dacl` is
fail-safe, so a missing privilege would not crash — it would log a warning and
*skip*, flipping #637's defense-in-depth from "applied" to "quietly off" on a
deployment we do not live-verify. Weighed against keeping two privileges out of
~twenty, conservative-keep-on-doubt wins cleanly. I kept both, documented the
reasoning in full, and flagged the removal as a future tightening once #637 is
live-verified to keep working without them. This is the "decision vs defect"
boundary in practice: stripping a clearly-unused privilege is a defect-fix I just
do; stripping one that *might* silently disable a sibling security control is a
judgment I make conservatively and write down.

One thing I deliberately did **not** do: lower the *integrity level* of the
launcher (the way ADR-019 lowers the UI's). The PA and AO are not separate
processes I could de-elevate; they are threads inside this process, and the
process must stay High to drive Hyper-V. Integrity is a per-process property and
there is no per-thread integrity to lower. Privilege-stripping is the tool that
*does* apply here — it shrinks what the High-integrity process is allowed to do
without touching the integrity it runs at. Naming that distinction matters: the
right lever for the in-process services is privileges, not integrity.

The second, smaller deliverable (B) is orphan-guarding the spawned children via a
kill-on-close Job Object (`launcher/orphan_guard.py`) so a crash can't leave a
windowed UI running with no backend. I shipped it as best-effort: the de-elevated
WinUI child is launched via `CreateProcessWithTokenW`, which runs it through the
secondary-logon service and can place it in a system job that refuses nesting, so
the assignment is allowed to fail and degrade — the child runs unguarded rather
than the surface being risked. Verified headlessly that the job creates and that
an ordinary child (`cmd.exe`) assigns successfully (`AssignProcessToJobObject` →
True); the `CreateProcessWithTokenW` interaction is the one the LA's live-verify
will confirm or show degrading, and the log line says which happened either way.

Everything is fail-safe to the point of never blocking boot: every ctypes step is
guarded, the public `strip_unused_privileges()` returns a report in every branch
and raises in none, and there is an outer try/except at the call site as
belt-and-suspenders. On this non-elevated dev box the strip is a faithful small
demonstration — it removed 4 privileges (`SeIncreaseWorkingSet`, `SeShutdown`,
`SeTimeZone`, `SeUndock`), kept `SeChangeNotify`, and a second run found nothing
left to remove, proving the removal is permanent. On the elevated launcher it will
remove ~15 including the dangerous set. The before/after is visible headlessly via
`scripts/show_launcher_privileges.py`; run it from an elevated shell for the full
production-shaped strip.

**Next:** the LA's on-box live-verify — run `scripts/show_launcher_privileges.py`
from an elevated PowerShell to see the real ~15-privilege strip, then a full
`python -m launcher` boot to confirm (a) the WinUI surface still de-elevates
cleanly (proving `SeImpersonate` survived the strip), (b) the #637 DACL hardening
still applies to the sensitive files (proving the `SeRestore`/`SeTakeOwnership`
keep was sufficient — and, if it works, that they could later be removed too), and
(c) the orphan-guard log line shows whether the WinUI child was job-assigned or
degraded. If #637 holds without the two owner/DACL privileges under live-verify,
