tightening the allowlist to just `SeChangeNotify` + `SeImpersonate` is the
follow-up.

*(commits `<this>` — `launcher/privilege_hardening.py` + `launcher/orphan_guard.py`
+ wiring in `launcher/__main__.py` + `scripts/show_launcher_privileges.py` +
`launcher/tests/test_privilege_hardening.py` (16) + `launcher/tests/test_orphan_guard.py`
(10); worktree gate `launcher/ shared/ services/ -m "not hardware and not winui and
not slow"` = 2330 passed / 22 skipped (20 semantic_router model-absent + 2 symlink-
privilege, both benign worktree deltas) / 0 failed. Awaits the LA's elevated-shell
live-verify of the production-shaped strip + the WinUI de-elevation + #637 DACL
hold + the orphan-guard assignment.)*

### 2026-06-10 — The latch that only a fingerprint may lift

The egress kill-switch has always had a deliberate asymmetry built into it: it
trips itself automatically on an anomaly, but it will only re-arm by hand.
`egress_guard.rearm()` is a bare function with a docstring that all but shouts
"Lead-Architect-only" — the safe state after an anomaly is *nothing leaves*, and
clearing it has to be a human decision, never a runtime path. What the latch was
missing was the *authorisation*: a bare function anyone in-process could call is a
governance gap, not a control. #653 closes that gap by making the re-arm cost a
real fingerprint.

The tempting move was to invent a small auth path of my own — a confirm dialog, a
password check, something egress-specific. I didn't, and the trade-off is the
whole point of the entry. The ESCALATE consumer (#639) already defines the
operator-approval contract end to end: the `ApprovalVerifier` protocol, the
`ApprovalResult` shape, the fail-closed bounded-wait worker-thread machinery in
`request_escalation_consent`, and a single-verifier registry that the live (WinUI)
surface already wires the #649 Windows-Hello `BiometricApprovalVerifier` into at
boot. Re-arming the egress latch is the *same shape of decision* as approving an
ESCALATE — a single-user-local, human-in-the-loop approve/deny. So `egress_rearm.py`
reuses the same verifier, the same result type, and copies the same bounded-wait
pattern beat for beat. Inventing a second trust surface would have doubled the
audited attack surface and let it drift out of step with the one that already has
40 tests behind it. I went with reuse-the-#649-mechanism because a second auth path
is a second thing to get wrong, accepting that `egress_rearm` now hard-depends on
the escalation-consent module's shapes (a dependency I think is correct — they are
the project's canonical approval primitives).

The fail-closed posture is strict and deliberate: *only* an explicit `approved is
True` calls `rearm()`. No verifier configured → stays LOCKED (a box with no
operator surface cannot self-clear its own kill-switch — it mirrors the #649
dormant default exactly). Verifier raises, times out, returns `None`, returns a
non-`ApprovalResult`, or returns a deny → stays LOCKED. The one concession to
ergonomics is the not-tripped case: re-arming an un-tripped guard is a no-op, so I
return a no-op allow *without* raising a Hello dialog — there's no reason to make
the operator dismiss a prompt for a latch that isn't set. The trip reason rides
into the operator-facing descriptor (`"Re-arm egress kill-switch — clear the lock
(trip reason: …)"`) so the operator sees *why* egress locked before they approve
clearing it; that's safe because `trip()`'s own contract already forbids a secret
in the reason string.

The part that earned its keep was DELIVERABLE 4 — the WinUI surface — because the
honest answer was "you can't get there from here," and proving it mattered more
than coding around it. The brief asked: can an "Egress LOCKED — reason" banner plus
a "Re-arm" button be added by *extending* an existing WinUI↔backend channel? I read
the whole pipe stack on both sides. The C# `PipeClient.CallAsync` writes one
request and reads exactly one reply under a one-permit `SemaphoreSlim`, with **no
background reader** — the WinUI only ever reads a frame immediately after it sent a
request. The Python `server.py` is symmetric: read a frame, dispatch, reply. There
is no server-push path, and even if the backend wrote an unsolicited frame, the C#
side has nothing listening for it outside an active call (and the gate would block
it). So a backend→WinUI banner push *does not exist and cannot be bolted onto the
existing channel* — it needs net-new cross-process notification plumbing. The brief
was explicit that the wrong response here is a fragile one-off, so I didn't build
one. Instead I implemented the launcher-side seam the brief asked for:
`request_egress_rearm_on_surface()` — one function the surface layer (or a future
WinUI re-arm command handler) calls. It works *today* without any push channel
because the AO tool loop and the Hello verifier both run in the launcher process
and the Hello prompt is a **system** dialog: calling the seam from the launcher
pops the fingerprint prompt for the operator regardless of which surface is in
front, exactly like the #649 ESCALATE verifier wiring already does in
`_run_winui_surface`. The banner + button are the missing piece, and they are the
*same* server-push plumbing the deferred WinUI-native ESCALATE prompt needs — which
is the argument for building it once, reusably, rather than a re-arm-specific hack.
That's a posture call for the LA, so it's in the report as a plan, not a
half-built channel.

One small thing the screen would have caught if I'd skipped it: the not-tripped
no-op path. My first instinct was to always run the verifier and let `rearm()` no-op
if not tripped — but that raises a Hello dialog every time the button is pressed,
even when there's nothing to clear. Returning the no-op allow *before* prompting is
the correct ergonomics and the test (`approving.seen == []`) locks it: the verifier
must not be consulted when there's nothing to re-arm.

Eleven tests, all green: not-tripped no-op (verifier untouched), no-verifier stays
locked, approve clears the latch, the descriptor carries the trip reason,
deny/error/malformed/None/timeout all stay locked, and a late answer can't
retroactively clear an already-denied latch. The demo (`scripts/demo_egress_rearm.py`,
modelled on `demo_escalation_hello.py`) runs two samples — approve→UNLOCK,
cancel→STILL LOCKED — and leaves the latch cleared so it doesn't poison global
state; it's the LA's on-box fingerprint live-verify and is currently gated behind
the un-built #649 helper exe on this worktree (the same one ESCALATE uses).

**Next:** LA to live-verify the fingerprint re-arm on the box (build the #649
helper, run `demo_egress_rearm.py`, tap once to clear and once to cancel). Then the
forward decision the report surfaces: build the net-new launcher→WinUI server-push
channel ONCE — banner + button for egress re-arm AND the WinUI-native ESCALATE
prompt share it — or keep the launcher-seam-only posture for now. This is dormant
until a web feature post-#556 can actually trip the kill-switch in production; the
re-arm path is ready ahead of that, fail-closed by default.

### 2026-06-10 — Teaching the journal its own lessons, and the vocabulary that had rotted under me

The lessons deck is the journal turned around to face its author. The capstone
deck answered "is the system safe?"; this one answers the question the operator
actually asked me to serve: *help me understand each lesson, one at a time —
the failure that caused it, what I must understand to grow from it — with the
AIGP certification in mind.* So every one of the 103 study units (the 90
numbered lessons plus 13 drafted from the June fragment inbox, deliberately
unnumbered because numbers are assigned at fold-in) teaches in four beats:
the failure with its real numbers kept in, the distilled lesson, the mechanism
underneath it, and the governance idea it exemplifies — named in the IAPP
Body of Knowledge's own vocabulary. A prologue (lesson 1, the 244-of-259
sessions that shipped only markdown) opens the arc; seven thematic acts and
the unfolded June work close it. 323 slides, generated from eight per-act JSON
files by the capstone's outline-as-data pipeline, vendored mermaid, study
state in localStorage only.

The first finding of the build was about my own head. I "knew" the AIGP exam
as seven domains; before letting a single mapping be drafted I verified
against live sources, and the Body of Knowledge had been restructured to
**four** domains in v2.0.1 (February 2025), with v2.1 effective 2026-02-02 —
ISO/IEC 42005 added, agentic architectures added. Every framework claim in the
deck would have been quietly stale at the level the certification actually
tests. The fix became doctrine for the artifact: `aigp_vocabulary.md` is the
only vocabulary the content may use, verified against named sources, and
`validate_acts.py` enforces it mechanically — alongside the anti-hallucination
check that every cited journal entry and every commit SHA in the teaching
content must exist verbatim in `BUILD_JOURNAL.md` or the source fragment.
Eight drafting agents wrote in parallel against that contract; the validator
came back 103 units, zero failures, and the one judgment pass left for me was
the per-unit domain mappings, reviewed by hand off the generated report.

Mid-build, the journal taught me lesson 82 about itself. A worktree glob
returned zero fragments where thirteen should have been, and I had half-written
the conclusion — a parallel session folded them, my Act VIII scope is stale —
before doing what the lesson says: check the instrument against evidence
already in hand. `git ls-files` showed all of them tracked and present; the
glob's path handling was the liar. Twenty minutes earlier I had been compiling
that exact lesson into a teaching unit. Being teachable by your own curriculum
mid-lesson is, I suspect, the strongest sign the curriculum is real.

The visual pass earned its keep the same way it did on the capstone, one layer
deeper. My geometry sweep — scroll sizes, bar clearance, across all 323
slides — came back clean, and was wrong. The how-to slide's mermaid loop had
PAINTED its last three nodes over the keyboard-hints list: the SVG's layout
box ended at y=631 while its paint reached y=1001, because `useMaxWidth`
emits a max-width style with no height attribute and the browser sized the
box inconsistently with the drawing. A layout-geometry check cannot see paint
spill; only measuring the painted boxes (or eyes) catches it. I switched
mermaid to explicit dimensions (`useMaxWidth:false`), re-swept for paint-vs-
layout divergence everywhere, and then made the honest design call: the study
loop was never diagram-shaped — as a left-right mermaid chain it rendered a
1299-pixel-wide ribbon scaled to 45 pixels of illegible text — so it shipped
as a plain HTML strip in the deck's beat colors, full-size type, zero
rendering risk. I kept mermaid only where it earns its place (the domains
map, 829×399, clean). Reuse the pipeline, refuse the tool where it fights the
content; the deck now records that trade-off in its own README.

What ships: `docs/learning/lessons_deck/` — the deck, the eight act files,
the vocabulary sheet, the schema/contract, the validator with its generated
mapping report, a README that makes regeneration a two-command habit, and the
`learning_log.md` template for the presenter session that will tutor the
operator through it one lesson at a sitting. Built entirely in an isolated
worktree while five gate-criteria merges landed on main around it; Vikunja
#650 tracks both sessions.

**Next:** the LA starts the presenter session (Part B of the 2026-06-09
brief) whenever he wants the first sitting; at the next fragment fold-in, Act
VIII's units take their assigned numbers and migrate into their thematic acts
(`README.md` documents the two-command regeneration).

*(commit `<this>` (deck v1: 103 units, 323 slides, validator 0 FAILs); merge
to main pending an LA-confirmed quiet window — parallel development active.)*

### 2026-06-10 — The one store I chose not to encrypt

The audit-retention work (#607) surfaced a posture question its builder was
right to escalate rather than answer: the new sealed segments inherit the
active log's existing on-disk shape — TPM-signed, owner-only-DACL'd, but
*plaintext* — while `sessions.db` and `substrate.db` are DEK-sealed (ADR-025).
The reflex, and this project's standing lean, is encrypt-everything-at-rest; I
have been overridden before for proposing a plaintext residual, so the honest
move was to escalate it with a recommendation rather than quietly ship either
default. The LA ratified the recommendation: keep the audit log
signed-and-locked, not encrypted.

The reasoning is the part worth keeping, because this is a genuine *exception*
to a good default, not a lapse from it. An audit log's whole value is being
*independently* checkable — a reviewer, or a future operator, must be able to
verify the trail without holding the data-encryption key, and the trail must
survive that key being lost or compromised. Encrypting the audit log couples
the witness to the very thing it witnesses: the one record meant to outlive a
key incident would die with the key. And the cost side is low — the records
are metadata (decisions, resource identifiers, hashes), not conversation or
document content — so the confidentiality encryption would buy here is small.
Signed + owner-DACL'd + air-gapped is the right amount of protection for *this*
store. What I did not do is pretend the question is closed forever: the moment
post-#556 tools begin writing sensitive resource identifiers — real file paths,
URLs — into `resource`, the metadata stops being low-sensitivity and the
encryption trade-off should be re-weighed (with the keyed-hash-for-index
pattern that keeps a ciphertext log verifiable). The decision and its expiry
are both on the record.

**Next:** no code changes — the as-built signed-plaintext posture stands,
ratified; the revisit trigger is wired to the #556 network-facing work. #607
closes.

*(commit `<this>` (ADR-029 ratification note + this entry + lesson 104); no
product code changed — a governance decision recorded.)*

### 2026-06-10 — The lock I proved, then declined to fit

A proof-of-concept on 2026-06-09 had closed a long-standing "feasible but
unproven" caveat: it sealed a secret under a TPM `PolicyPCR` on this exact chip
and watched the silicon refuse to unseal it when the bound PCR value differed —
measured-boot key-binding *enforces* here, it does not merely decorate. The
natural reflex on a green capability proof is to reach for it. The LA did the
opposite, and correctly: on 2026-06-10 he decided the production trust-root keys
will **not** be PCR-bound.

The reasoning is the part worth keeping, because "we proved it works and chose
not to use it" is a stronger governance position than either "we couldn't" or
"we did it because we could." PCR-binding a *key* is the strongest form of
measured boot — the key itself will not open outside a known-good boot state —
and that strength is exactly its operational poison on a decades-use daily
driver: PCR values move on every legitimate firmware and OS update, so a
PCR-bound trust root would brick itself after a routine BIOS or Windows update
until a recovery ceremony re-sealed it, fighting head-on the key-loss-recovery
posture the platform requires (ADR-025). And the threat it buys down — someone
booting a different OS to *use* the keys — is physical and boot-local,
orthogonal to the network surface the #598 gate is about, and already mitigated
in depth: the keys are non-exportable and chip-bound (not extractable at all),
the box is air-gapped and physically held, and the keystore is owner-only-DACL'd
(#637). A brittle fourth layer against a residual sliver of an already-covered
physical threat is a bad trade. So the keys stay hardware-resident,
non-exportable, DACL-locked — not PCR-gated — and the PoC stands as the recorded
evidence behind the *informed* decline, not a half-built feature.

**Next:** recorded as ADR-028 Amendment 1 + DECISION_REGISTER + roadmap §5.9.
#627's deferred PCR boot-attestation-*check* (a distinct, weaker mechanism than
key-sealing) is unchanged by this — neither advanced nor closed. The 2026-06-09
PoC artifacts (the report + `verify_pcr_seal.py`) await an LA disposition call
(commit as the evidence-of-record, or leave as a standalone PoC); they are
untracked and were not touched.

*(commit `<this>` (ADR-028 Am.1 + register + roadmap + this entry + lesson 105);
no product code changed — a governance decision recorded. PoC artifacts left
untracked pending the LA's keep/commit call.)*

### 2026-06-10 — The secret you can actually erase

ADR-025 gave BlarAI at-rest encryption, and then — honestly — recorded the limit it
could not cross: while the process runs, plaintext-derived secrets necessarily live
in RAM. The #611 feasibility study walked that surface and named the worst offender
precisely. It is not the DEK. It is the substrate's embedding cache:
`_load_embed_cache` decrypts *every* document and turn vector once at store
construction into `self._embed_cache` and holds the lot for the store's entire
lifetime. Those vectors are semantically invertible (vec2text-class attacks reconstruct
text from embeddings), so this is the largest and longest-lived genuinely-sensitive
plaintext-derived blob the running system holds — and the one with the biggest 32 GB
footprint, too. This is the limb of mitigation 2 the study marked "fully feasible, the
cleanest win," and this commit builds it.

The lever the study found is a distinction I had not drawn sharply before: **numpy
arrays are mutable; Python `bytes` are not.** The DEK and its subkeys are `bytes`, and
the existing ceremony "zeroization" that rebinds their names to fresh zero-`bytes` is
*ineffective* — it dereferences the secret and leaves its backing store in the heap
until the GC maybe reaps it (the study's §2.3 honesty defect, separately ticketed).
You cannot overwrite an immutable object's storage in place. But `arr[:] = 0` on a
numpy array *does* overwrite the backing buffer. So the embedding cache is the one
secret in this system I can genuinely erase, not merely drop. `unload_embed_cache()`
walks the cache, zeroes each vector in place, then clears the dict and marks a
"needs reload" flag; the next `retrieve()` re-decrypts the same rows lazily under the
store lock. An opt-in daemon timer (default ON, 900 s window, `<= 0` disables and
starts no thread) tracks last-retrieval time and fires the unload after the idle
window. The proof that mattered in the test is not that the dict empties — a
dereference does that too — but that the *captured references* to the ndarrays read
all-zero afterward. That assertion is the difference between a control that works and
a comment that claims one.

The trade-off, stated plainly because the AIGP track wants the path-not-taken on the
record: **idle-unload reduces the exposure window and the footprint; it cannot touch
the in-use window.** While a retrieval is actually running, the vectors must be
plaintext in RAM to be scored — no zeroing changes that, and the study is explicit
that only TEE-class isolation would. I went with window-and-footprint reduction
because it is the honest, tractable win on this air-gapped single-user box (where #611
has no remote ignition path until #556 anyway), accepting two costs: a one-time
re-decrypt latency on the first query after an idle period, and the irreducible in-use
window. The rejected alternative — per-request DEK unseal/discard — buys a sliver more
window reduction for a TPM round-trip on every operation, which is the wrong trade at
single-user scale. I reused the existing `_load_embed_cache` reload path rather than
duplicating it, and kept the write-path `_invalidate_embed_cache` distinct from the
idle path: invalidate means *data changed* (rebuild eagerly, stay resident); unload
means *data unchanged, just freed* (re-decrypt the same rows on next touch). An
`RLock` serialises every cache transition so the monitor thread and a concurrent
retrieval cannot race; the monitor swallows every exception so it can never crash a
retrieval or the store, and `close()` joins it cleanly and scrubs whatever was still
resident.

One thing I deliberately did *not* do: measure the real reload latency. The mechanism
is fully headless-testable and 18 synthetic-embedding tests cover it (zeroing,
idempotence, lazy-reload equivalence, the concurrency storm, the timer firing and the
disabled path, write-invalidation still intact, close lifecycle, on-disk reopen). But
the *actual* first-query-after-idle cost on the real bge-small embedder over the real
corpus is an on-box number, and that number is what should size the 900 s default
sensibly. 900 s is a placeholder, labelled as such in the config comment and here.
Measuring it headlessly would be inventing data, which the Testing-Data-Capture rule
exists to prevent.

**Next:** on-box, re-measure the embedding-cache reload latency on the Arc 140V over a
representative corpus and record it to `PERFORMANCE_LOG.md` + `docs/performance/`
(community-grade — novel Lunar Lake data), then revisit whether 900 s is the right
default. The deferred sibling work stays where the study put it: the `bytearray`
conversion of the DEK/subkeys we own (still leaves the un-zeroable OpenSSL copy) and
the OS page-locking / Key-Locker limbs belong to the #556 window when a remote
code-execution path actually exists. The ineffective ceremony `recover()` zeroization
honesty fix already landed (`b45b91e`).

*(commit `295cb69` (substrate `unload_embed_cache` + idle timer + lazy reload +
`[substrate].embed_cache_idle_unload_s` knob default 900; AO entrypoint wiring); +18
tests in `test_substrate_idle_unload.py`; standing gate 2563 passed / 2 skipped (=
2565/0 elevated); on-box reload-latency re-measure awaits live verify.)*

### 2026-06-10 — The gate-blocker that was already green

The #612 deep-dive's residual register named one thing as the gate's last
technical blocker: A.3, the controlled sandboxed egress exercise — prove the
armed egress stack denies by default, auto-trips off-allowlist, and blocks a
planted secret, against real (non-loopback) traffic. Told to complete it, I went
to build it and found it already built. `tests/security/test_egress_sandbox_proving.py`
— the #643 work merged the day before — does exactly that, and it is in the
standing gate, green. I ran it deliberately as the formal exercise anyway: 11
proofs, 11 passed, real same-host non-loopback round-trips on the box's own LAN
IP (air-gap-safe by construction — the OS loops it back, no router, no internet;
a loopback-only box would skip, not fail; the planted secrets are throwaway fakes).

So the honest answer to "complete the egress exercise" was not a build but a
reconciliation: the deep-dive's "BUILT-DORMANT, gate-blocking, the one remaining
proof" was currency drift — the proof exists and passes. What the register had
folded *into* A.3 and what genuinely remains are different things: the full
AO-tool-driven chain (an AO tool requesting egress → PA mediation → guard →
screen) cannot be exercised end-to-end because no egress tool exists yet — and
the first egress tool is itself the deliberate going-online act, so requiring its
integrated proof *before* the gate that authorizes going online is circular. The
component-level proof (guard + screen on real traffic) is the exercisable bar,
and it is met; the integrated run belongs to the first egress tool's bring-up,
day-one-online. I recorded the formal run as gate evidence and put the scope
question — component-bar vs full-chain — to the LA rather than declaring the gate
criterion satisfied myself.

**Next:** the LA settles A.3's gate bar (component proof vs full chain); on the
component-bar reading it is MET and the #654 doc-sync updates roadmap §5 + #598 +
the deep-dive register to match disk. The integrated AO-driven egress proof rides
the first egress tool (web-search/Kagi W4), under observation, day-one-online.

*(commit `<this>` (gate-evidence record `docs/security/egress_exercise_2026-06-10.md`
+ this entry + lesson 107); the A.3 exercise `tests/security/test_egress_sandbox_proving.py`
ran 11/11 — no product code changed, a gate-evidence run + reconciliation recorded.)*


### 2026-06-10 — The deck went stale before the meeting started

The #612 capstone deck is the artifact the Lead Architect reads before he signs off on
removing the air-gap — the largest threat-model change the project will ever make. I
spent this session walking him through it, top to bottom, as presenter and sparring
partner. The rule I set for myself was simple and it turned out to be the whole point:
ground every claim against the code on disk before I say it out loud, never against the
deck's own narrative.

That rule paid for itself three times. The deck (`b08a45f`) and its executive summary,
both barely two days old, had already drifted from the tree. The summary's single
sharpest finding — the exfil-screen "structurally disconnected, `register_screener` has
no runtime caller" — was no longer true: `#634` had landed `wire_into_egress_guard()`
into the launcher boot path at `launcher/__main__.py:1410`, so the screener registers via
the arm-hook seam now. Token containment, the one control the deck flagged as failing
*open*, was closed (`#638`: 5s lifetime, revocation wired, +18 tests). And PCR-bound
sealing, graded "feasible, unproven," had been demonstrated on the actual ST TPM the
evening before. None of these were small caveats — they were the shape of the decision.
Re-grounded, the Tier-A gate-blocking work collapsed from a finish-list to essentially a
single proof: the egress stack has been built, wired, and harness-tested, but it has never
once run against a real, hostile, controlled payload. That bootstrap — you cannot fully
prove an egress defense without allowing some egress — is the honest crux of the whole
go/no-go, and it survived the reconciliation intact when the rest of the list shrank.

The other half of the session was discipline about role. The LA asked, off a single line
in the containment section, what OS-level privilege separation the Assistant Orchestrator
could actually get — Job Objects, AppContainer, restricted tokens. That is a genuine
engineering question with a defect-vs-decision boundary running through it: the cheap,
low-risk hardening (drop integrity High→Medium, wrap a Job Object that forbids
child-process spawning, strip unused privileges) I can just recommend; whether to pay the
AppContainer cost — which most likely breaks GPU or TPM access on a young Lunar-Lake
driver stack — is a trade-off only he weighs. I kept those separate, recommended the cheap
tier now while we are still air-gapped (so a broken inference path has no external blast
radius), and filed the network-egress firewall as `#651`, coupled to the gate. Nothing got
"fixed" live; everything surfaced got a ticket or a line in the close-record. A review that
quietly edits the thing it is reviewing is not a review.

**Next:** the LA's §5.12 sign-off is the live act, and it should be made against an accurate
gate tracker — so `#654` (deck/#598/roadmap currency sync) sequences before it. The one
remaining gate-blocking proof is the controlled sandboxed egress exercise (allowlist a
throwaway endpoint, plant a secret, watch the screen block and the kill-switch trip). Close
that and the recommendation flips from NO-GO to GO, per-endpoint, Kagi first.


### 2026-06-10 — The biggest dependency since OpenVINO, and the door we welded shut on arrival

Stage B of the #655 ingest program needed the thing BlarAI has avoided for its entire
life: a real HTML parser. The Cleaner's first job (ADR-030 §1 — data-normalizer first,
sanitizer second) is turning raw article pages into boilerplate-free text worth storing
for decades, and the LA weighed the options at the comprehension gate: stdlib
`html.parser` heuristics (+0 packages, hand-rolled boilerplate removal — badly), justext
(+3, no metadata), readability-lxml (+5, no date/byline), trafilatura (+9, the de-facto
standard with title/byline/date extraction). He chose extraction quality over
supply-chain thinness, and I executed the ceremony that choice demands: the W2/Kagi
precedent, scaled up threefold. `pip install --dry-run --report` resolved the exact tree
against the live venv (9 new packages — trafilatura 2.1.0, courlan 1.4.0, htmldate
1.10.0, dateparser 1.4.0, jusText 3.0.2, lxml 6.1.1, lxml_html_clean 0.4.5, tld 0.13.2,
tzlocal 5.3.1); the PyPI JSON API supplied wheel AND sdist sha256 per package
(`requirements/ingest-cleaner.txt`); the OSV database returned zero advisories across
all nine pinned versions; and the vetting record
(`docs/research/trafilatura_supply_chain_vetting.md`) says the uncomfortable parts out
loud — lxml is a 4 MB C-extension binary whose job here is parsing attacker-authored
bytes, the largest attack surface BlarAI has accepted since OpenVINO; three of the nine
packages share one maintainer; the trafilatura release was three days old at vetting
time. Hash-pinning, the ADR-030 §3 guest-homed-parsing containment, and naming the risks
are the mitigations — not pretending the surface is small.

The posture decision that matters most is what we did NOT take from the package.
trafilatura ships its own fetcher (`fetch_url` / `fetch_response` /
`trafilatura.downloads`) — one convenience call that would bypass the PA adjudication,
the exfil screen, and the kill-switch simultaneously. It is forbidden on arrival, and
the prohibition is enforced, not documented: a new AST scan in
`tests/security/test_no_external_egress.py` fails the standing gate on any runtime
import of `trafilatura.downloads` or any reference to the fetch names, and a subprocess
lock proves that importing `services.cleaner.src.pipeline` constructs no socket and
resolves no hostname. I wanted the stronger lock — *never even load* the downloads
module — but probing killed it: even `trafilatura.core` imports `downloads` at module
level (verified 2026-06-10), so the honest, enforceable property is behavioral
(no sockets), and the scan's scope-honesty note says exactly what a dynamic `getattr`
could still evade and which armed runtime layer catches that instead.

The pipeline itself (`services/cleaner/src/pipeline.py`) is deliberately boring:
`bare_extraction` (metadata on, favor_recall off, comments off — reader comments are
chrome-grade noise for a knowledge store), NFC + zero-width/bidi/control normalization,
then sanitization that REUSES the repo's two canonical primitives rather than copying
them — the gateway's `scan_for_injection` pattern table and the AO's
`_neutralize_delimiters`, imported across service boundaries on purpose, with an
identity-assert regression lock so a rename fails loudly instead of drifting silently.
One ordering subtlety earned its own test: the injection scan runs on normalized text
BEFORE the delimiter strip. Strip first and a forged `<|GROUNDED_CONTEXT_BEGIN|>`
vanishes before the scanner sees it — the verdict gets laundered, the operator reviews a
"clean" document that arrived armed. And normalization must precede both, because a
zero-width space inside "ignore previous instructions" defeats a naive phrase scan;
the evasion test proves the order does real work. The quarantine verdict starts
conservative per the LA's ratified "tunable, start conservative": five stable reason
codes (`EXTRACTION_FAILED`, `LOW_TEXT_LENGTH`, `LOW_EXTRACTION_RATIO`,
`LOW_EXTRACTION_CONFIDENCE`, `INJECTION_PATTERN_DETECTED`), floors at 80 words / 0.2%
text-to-raw ratio / 0.5 composite confidence for HTML, a far looser 10-word floor for
the operator's own pastes — and quarantined results still carry the cleaned text,
because the operator's in-chat review IS the override (there is no auto-approve path
either way, ADR-031 L0). The rejected alternative — blocking quarantined content from
preview entirely — would have made the operator approve documents he cannot read.

Numbers: 46 new tests (29 corpus + 15 text-path + 2 security locks), all green alongside
the untouched egress scans (48 passed in the targeted run). The 7-fixture synthesized
corpus (nav/ads chrome, code blocks, NFD-and-ZWSP-bearing unicode, a paywall teaser, an
injection-bearing article, a comments section, a listicle) extracts in ~2.1 ms per
article, 14.94 ms mean per full corpus pass (20 runs, stdev 3.3 ms, Python 3.11.9,
trafilatura 2.1.0, lxml 6.1.1, CPU-only on the Core Ultra 7 258V) —
`docs/performance/cleaner_pipeline_2026-06-10.json` records the methodology and names
what is NOT measured: real fetched pages are 50–500 KB and will be slower; these are
synthesized fixtures, re-measure at fetch activation. A `PERFORMANCE_LOG.md` entry is
deliberately left to the fragment integrator — the parallel Stage-B sibling owns
gateway/entrypoint files and shared-file appends collide on merge, which is the whole
reason fragments exist.

**Next:** the sibling Stage-B agent wires `/ingest` paste + file through the gateway to
this pipeline's `CleanResult` (the interface contract is frozen); `/ingest <url>` stays
behind the dormant-fetch message until W4 ships `guarded_fetch.fetch_external` and #598
signs off; quarantine floors get recalibrated against real fetched pages once the fetch
limb goes live; the fresh-environment hash-pin gap (eight already-satisfied transitives,
named in the vetting doc §3) folds into the standing lockfile decision from Sprint 16.

*(commits `9121653` (dependency ceremony — requirements/ingest-cleaner.txt + vetting
record + venv install) and `7531abe` (pipeline + security locks + 46-test corpus + perf
capture); worktree standing gate 2763 passed / 20 skipped (documented bge env-skips) /
116 deselected / 0 failed; live verify rides the Stage-B gateway integration.)*

**Post-review fix (same day).** The review called the fresh-environment hash-pin gap I
had left as a "documented decision" and made me close it instead — rightly: a
supply-chain artefact that only installs into the one venv that already has everything
is identity-pinning theatre for the rebuild case it exists to protect. Extending
`requirements/ingest-cleaner.txt` with the eight already-satisfied transitives (PyPI
JSON API wheel + sdist sha256, exact shared-venv versions) surfaced a ninth the review
itself had missed: `six`, python-dateutil's dependency, which never appears in a
satisfied-venv dry-run and only refused under the `--ignore-installed` fresh-env
simulation — the gap-checking method had the same blind spot as the gap. The file now
covers the full 18-package closure; both verifications pass (`pip install
--require-hashes --dry-run` resolves cleanly, and the fresh-env simulation downloads
and hash-verifies every artefact, including the cp311-win_amd64 C-extension wheels,
installing nothing). The vetting doc §3 carries one-line identity notes for the nine;
their deep vetting stays with the Sprint-16 shared-venv pinning rationale
(`docs/sprints/sprint_16/dependency_pinning_rationale.md`) rather than being
re-performed — these are long-established staples whose risk posture that review
already owns.

**LA-verdict addendum (same day).** An independent LA-session verification (2026-06-10)
caught two things this branch owed the record. First, the vetting doc's headline lxml
mitigation — guest-homed parsing — read as if it were live, when it is a Stage-C
deliverable; meanwhile Stage B host-parses operator-consented LOCAL HTML files. A
mitigations list that leads with a control that does not exist yet is the polite cousin
of a fail-open: anyone auditing the lxml surface would have credited containment that
is not there. The doc now states the interim plainly — host-side parsing of the
operator's own files, bounded by the hash-pins, the egress locks, and ADR-030 §2's
path-scope controls, until the Stage-C guest parser lands as the durable fix. Second,
the first real-page run (the BleepingComputer UniFi OS root-CVE article, 92,152 raw
bytes → verdict clean, confidence 1.0, 666 words, title/byline/date all extracted,
4,480 cleaned chars at a 4.86% ratio, deterministic across re-runs) existed only in
ticket comments — exactly the "unrecorded test results are an incomplete task" failure
mode. It is now a repo record (`docs/performance/cleaner_realpage_validation_2026-06-10.md`),
re-measured from disk rather than copied from the comment (the re-run corrected the
comment's 4.87% ratio rounding to the pipeline's 4.86%), with the known imperfection
kept honest: the trailing Picus sponsored-content block survives extraction and is
tracked as #658 item 1. One page is a validation, not a benchmark — the record names
single-page scope, the absent fetch path, and the synthetic-fixture floor calibration
as unmeasured.


### 2026-06-10 — The encrypted store that kept a plaintext fingerprint of its own contents

An independent LA-session verification of the knowledge-bank branch (LA
verdict 2026-06-10) caught something I had shipped without seeing: the
`knowledge_docs` table stored the SHA-256 of the full cleaned plaintext as a
plaintext `content_sha256` column — sitting directly beside the AES-256-GCM
ciphertext it fingerprints — and the signed-plaintext ingest audit log carried
the same digest as `car_hash`. Every individual piece had a justification
(the digest powers the staging integrity cross-check; the audit chain wants a
content reference; "it's only a hash"), and the composition was still wrong.
Under the stolen-DB threat model a deterministic content hash IS content
metadata: an attacker holding `knowledge.db` runs any public article through
the deterministic in-repo cleaner, hashes the output, and tests membership —
which is exactly the attack the keyed `source_hash` two columns away was
designed to deny. The store denied "did he ingest this URL" while answering
"did he ingest this article" for free. The embarrassing part is the proximity:
the right pattern was already in the same CREATE TABLE.

The fix is the pattern the table already knew. `content_sha256_keyed` now
stores `cipher.keyed_index` (HMAC under `k_idx`) over the plaintext digest
hex — same ADR-025 §3 equality-leak residual as `source_hash`, unguessable
without the key. The verification capability cost is zero, and that is the
part worth recording: the AO's integrity cross-check (recompute the SHA over
the decrypted staging plaintext, compare against the INGEST_SUBMIT frame's
digest) runs in RAM BEFORE the insert, exactly as before. Only the at-rest
form changed. The rejected alternative — encrypting the digest instead of
keying it — would have cost the deterministic-equality property for nothing;
nobody ever needs the plaintext digest back out of the row.

The audit limb was a delegated sub-choice (the LA handed it to the
orchestrator) and the call was KEYED there too: `car_hash` on ingest records
is now the keyed hex, because a plaintext content digest in a signed-PLAINTEXT
JSONL file would simply re-open the same oracle one directory over. ADR-029's
ratified plaintext exception covers action/identity labels — verbs, doc
UUIDs, decisions — never content-derived hashes; the decision is now written
into both module docstrings so the next reader inherits the boundary, and
DATA_MAP row 8 got the honesty pass it needed: the old claim that
`published_date` was "the one deliberate plaintext metadata column" was false
(word_count, cleaner_version, timestamps, lifecycle state were all plaintext
too), and the row now points at a full per-column enumeration with a
one-line rationale each. A data map that flatters the store is worse than no
data map. Regression locks: raw `knowledge.db` bytes and the audit JSONL
bytes are scanned for the plaintext digest of a known ingested article
(absent, in every spelling), the keyed form is asserted present, and a
genuinely tampered staging file (validly re-encrypted different content under
the same doc identity) still bounces off the cross-check.

The same verdict landed a second, unrelated decision: the LA INVERTED the
launcher's zero-NIC posture from assumption to precondition. The guest VM
must remain NIC-less under the one-door host-side-fetch composition, so
`start_vm` now enumerates the VM's adapters (`Get-VMNetworkAdapter`, the
existing `_run_ps` pattern) and refuses fail-closed — before Start-VM, and
deliberately also for an already-RUNNING guest, because a running VM with an
adapter attached is the violation itself, not a grandfathered state. An
enumeration failure refuses too: "could not check" is not "checked clean".
Mocked tests cover zero-proceeds / one-refuses / error-refuses, plus the
never-issued-Start-VM assertion on the refusal paths. The check is
self-contained in `vm_manager.py` so it composes cleanly with the #657
stop-on-exit work that landed on main after this branch forked.

**Next:** fold this into the #655 merge-gate review; the UX-side twin of this
finding (the /ingest paste persisting into sessions.db as a forwardable user
turn) is fixed on the stacked `feat/uc003-ingest-ux` branch with its own
fragment.


### 2026-06-10 — The door we cut into the wall

For its whole life BlarAI has been air-gapped by *absence*: no runtime module imported a network
client, and the `egress_guard` welded that shut at the socket layer so even a stray `connect`
auto-tripped a latched kill-switch. The Lead Architect has now decided to remove the air-gap (the
#598 GO), and the first thing that wants to reach out is UC-003 — read a URL, clean the article,
hand back text. The temptation, when you add a network feature, is to let *that* feature open *its*
socket. I built the opposite: one door. `shared/security/guarded_fetch.fetch_external` is the single
sanctioned external-fetch path, and every future web tool fetches through the same guarded seam. The
wall isn't torn down; we cut exactly one opening and put a guard on it.

The guard runs ADR-027's four rules as a strictly-ordered, fail-closed pipeline: an SSRF check
(https only, no `userinfo@`, no raw-IP hosts, no loopback/private/link-local/CGNAT, ports locked to
443 + an explicit 8443), then the Policy Agent's per-URL verdict, then a charset-correct fetch behind
a per-fetch allowlist widen that is *always* auto-revoked, then an injection scan of the body. The
load-bearing posture the LA handed me is "URL = authorization": when the operator initiates a fetch,
the PA verdict governs and that verdict alone — ALLOW proceeds with **no** extra fingerprint, DENY
blocks, and only ESCALATE routes to the #639 Windows-Hello consent path. I had to resist the reflex
to bolt a mandatory consent onto every ALLOW; the reason it's wrong is subtle and worth keeping. A
fingerprint on every fetch trains the operator to rubber-stamp, which corrodes the exact signal the
ESCALATE tier exists to carry. So consent is reserved for the tier the PA *escalates*, not levied as
a blanket tax. That's rule 2's "held hybrid-consent option" from the original ADR finally being spent
— and I recorded it as an ADR-027 amendment rather than letting it live only in code, because it is a
governance decision (where the human gate sits), not a mechanical one.

Two implementation decisions inside the door earned their own scrutiny. The first is the
registration seam. `shared/security` is leaf-level and must never import the Policy Agent service, so
the door takes its adjudicator by registration — `register_url_adjudicator`, mirroring exactly how
`egress_guard` takes its screener and `escalation_consent` takes its verifier. The consequence I
chose deliberately and flag loudly: `guarded_fetch` ships with **no** adjudicator wired, so today
every fetch DENIES. There is no honest in-process callable shaped `(url, purpose) -> verdict` to
register against — the PA classifies a Canonical Action Representation, not a URL string — so the
alternative was to fabricate a verdict in runtime code, and fabricating a verdict is a silent open
door. Better a documented TODO that the UC-003 caller wires the real PA (build a CAR, run
`HybridAdjudicator`, map `AdjudicationDecision` onto the local `Verdict`) than a stub that says ALLOW
when nobody asked the PA. The seam is real; the verdict is not faked.

The second is the one that would have auto-tripped the kill-switch on the very first real Kagi
connect, and it's a good example of a control fighting the thing it's supposed to permit. A standard
HTTP client resolves a hostname via `getaddrinfo` and then connects to the **numeric IP** it got
back — and that IP is not the literal string on the allowlist, so the guard would see an off-allowlist
destination and cut all egress. The fix (salvaged and verified from two prior stranded drafts) is
hostname-resolution pinning: when an *allowlisted* name resolves, `egress_guard` records its resolved
IPs (`ip → {hostnames}`) and admits a later numeric connect to one of them — but only at the
allowlisted port, and only for an IP that an allowlisted name genuinely resolved to. Deny-by-default
survives intact: an IP nobody resolved has no pin and still trips. The pin is not a second allowlist;
it's a memory of a legitimate resolution, dropped the instant the endpoint is revoked, the allowlist
cleared, or the guard disarmed — and a pinned-IP connect still tags the socket so the exfil screen
fires on real outbound bytes. I also made the body decode honor the page's *declared* charset
(Content-Type, then `<meta charset>`), not a blind UTF-8 assume, because a page that sends ISO-8859-1
or Shift_JIS is not a hypothetical and a blind decode mangles it.

One thing the building of the door forced was a deliberate, reviewable nick in a foundational
control. `tests/security/test_no_external_egress.py` fails the build on *any* runtime import of a
network client — that's how the air-gap is proven, not assumed. `guarded_fetch` must import `httpx`.
Rather than weaken the scan, I carved out exactly that one file by path and added a companion test
asserting the carve-out is one file wide and that the file imports *only* `httpx` — so the single-door
property is now itself locked by a test, and a second module reaching for a network client trips the
build just as before.

**Next:** the UC-003 URL-mode wiring consumes this — it must call `register_url_adjudicator` at its
entry point with a callable that runs the real Policy Agent (CAR → `HybridAdjudicator` →
`AdjudicationDecision` → `Verdict`), and an operator surface must register a Windows-Hello verifier so
ESCALATE actually prompts rather than failing closed to DENY. Until both are wired the door stays
shut, which is the correct dormant state. The live-verify that matters is the first real PA-ALLOWED
Kagi fetch on the box: confirm the resolution pin admits the connect at 443, the exfil screen tags
and screens the real outbound traffic, and the allowlist is empty again the instant the fetch
returns.

*(commits `b7471dd` (D1 resolution-pinning, 21 tests + #643 11/11 unchanged); `663c7fa` (D2
`guarded_fetch` one-door seam, 38 tests + the single-door import lock); D3 ADR-027 Am.1 + DECISION_REGISTER
+ this fragment. Awaits the UC-003 caller wiring the real PA adjudicator + a Windows-Hello verifier, then
a live Kagi-fetch verify on the box.)*

### 2026-06-10 — Three things the merge gate caught before the door ever opened

A merge-gate review of the door — done while it is still DENY-by-default, so nothing here was ever
live — surfaced two real defects and one gap, and I closed all three before the door can be wired
live. Reviewing your own freshly-cut hole in the wall, with fresh eyes, is exactly when the cheap
catches happen; doing it before the adjudicator exists meant zero exposure window.

The real one was an SSRF the first layer half-closed. `_validate_url` refuses a raw-IP *literal*
host, but a **named** host that DNS-resolves to an internal address sailed straight through, and
`egress_guard._record_resolution_pins` pinned *every* resolved IP except loopback — so a name pointed
at `169.254.169.254` (the cloud-metadata classic), a `10.x`/`192.168.x` box, or `100.64.x` CGNAT got
its internal IP pinned and the connect admitted. I closed it in both layers, deliberately, for defense
in depth. In `egress_guard` the pin recorder now skips *any* blocked range, not just loopback (a new
`_is_blocked_pin_ip` mirroring the door's own predicate): an allowlisted name that resolves internal
simply gets no pin, so the later numeric connect stays off-allowlist and the guard denies and trips.
In `guarded_fetch` I added a pre-fetch resolve-and-recheck: before widening the allowlist, the host is
resolved once and the whole fetch is refused if *any* returned address is blocked — and this one also
catches a name resolving to **loopback**, which the guard permits globally for IPC, so the door has to
refuse that itself rather than lean on a guard that is (correctly) lenient about 127.0.0.1.

A wrinkle worth keeping: the tests had been using RFC-5737 documentation IPs (`203.0.113.x`) as stand-in
"public" addresses, but Python 3.11's `ipaddress` classifies those ranges as `is_private` — so once the
blocked-range skip went in, the happy-path pins stopped recording and the suite went red in a way that
*looked* like a regression but was the new control working. The fix was to move the public-path test
constants onto a genuinely-global address. The lesson there is small but real: a "documentation" IP is
not automatically a "public-for-classification" IP, and a security predicate will tell you so.

I did **not** fully close DNS rebinding, and I wrote that down rather than imply I had. The pre-fetch
recheck and httpx's own connect-time resolution are two separate lookups; a rebinding attacker can
answer the check with a good IP and answer the connect with a bad one (a TOCTOU). The resolution-pin
layer narrows it — httpx connects to a numeric IP that must be pinned by an allowlisted name, and an
internal IP is never pinned now — but the robust close is a custom httpx transport that validates the
*actually-connected peer IP* against the blocked ranges before sending. I named that as a follow-up in
the module docstring and here, and deliberately left it out of scope: it is a real piece of work, not a
one-liner, and shipping it half-built would be worse than naming it honestly.

The second defect was a memory-exhaustion DoS hiding in plain sight. `_fetch_body` did
`response.content[:_MAX_BODY_BYTES]` — but `client.get()` reads the *entire* body into memory before
that slice ever runs, so the 8-MiB cap was decorative: a hostile (PA-allowed) host streaming gigabytes
would exhaust host memory before the cap applied. I switched it to `client.stream("GET", url)` and read
chunks up to the cap, stopping the instant it's reached so the unread remainder is never pulled in. An
over-cap body is truncated-at-cap and logged WARNING, never raised. One small trap cost a test cycle:
httpx forbids re-iterating a consumed stream, so the "is there more past the cap?" peek had to advance
the *same* iterator with `next()`, not call `iter_bytes()` a second time. Everything else — status,
content-type, charset-correct decode, the always-revoke `finally`, the trip/timeout/HTTP-error handling
— is preserved.

The third was a gap, not a bug: `_scan_and_annotate` logged the injection-scan flags but `FetchResult`
had nowhere to carry them, so the UC-003 cleaner could never act on them — they died in the log. I
added `injection_flags: tuple[str, ...]` to the frozen result and populate it from the scan via
`dataclasses.replace`. The annotate-not-block contract is unchanged (the body is returned intact
regardless); the consumer can now *see* the flags and decide, which is the whole point of a
defense-in-depth signal.

**Next:** unchanged from the door's first entry — the UC-003 caller still wires the real PA adjudicator
and a Windows-Hello verifier, and the live Kagi-fetch verify on the box still gates going live. The one
addition to that punch-list: build the **peer-IP-validating httpx transport** to close the DNS-rebinding
TOCTOU before the door is ever wired live (a named follow-up, ticketed against #577's egress hardening).

*(commits `0a3fc04` (fix 1 — named-host SSRF, both layers; +TestBlockedRangeResolutionNotPinned +
TestNamedHostResolutionSsrf); `ca3eca4` (fix 2 — streamed body cap; +TestBodyCapStreaming); `36128c0`
(fix 3 — `injection_flags` on `FetchResult`; +TestInjectionFlagsSurfaced). Standing gate green; the
#643 sandbox 11/11 and the one-door import lock both unchanged. Residual: peer-IP-validating httpx
transport for the DNS-rebinding TOCTOU — named follow-up, not built here.)*


### 2026-06-10 — The paste that walked in through the front door

The independent LA-session verification of the ingest-UX branch (LA verdict
2026-06-10) found the channel I had built around every defense and then left
open beside them: the gateway persisted the operator's full `/ingest` message
— up to ~40 KB of raw, pre-cleaning web text — as a `role='user'` turn in
sessions.db, and the prompt-history filter forwards ALL user turns verbatim
into later prompts. So the knowledge bank could clean, quarantine,
approval-gate, encrypt, datamark, and ground that article as
UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL — while an identical uncleaned copy rode straight into the
model's context as a trusted user turn on the very next message. Not a
bypass of the defenses; a parallel path that never met them. The irony is
that the assistant-side preview was already correctly excluded (the
INFORMATIONAL marker keeps it out of history) — I had defended the reply and
left the request wide open.

The fix persists a labels-only STUB user turn for ALL `/ingest` modes:
`/ingest <article: {N} words, doc {uuid8}>` on a successful submit, a
`not submitted` variant when the command refused or the URL limb is dormant.
Two deliberate negatives are doing the security work. First, NO content-hash
prefix in the stub — the orchestrator decided this (LA-delegated): a
truncated digest would re-seed into sessions.db the same content-fingerprint
membership oracle the knowledge-bank branch just closed at rest, and the
opaque doc_uuid already gives the transcript a join key to the audit chain.
Second, the persisted assistant turn for a successful submit is also a
labels-only summary, because the preview reply embeds the full CLEANED
article body — and "the paste never persists in sessions.db" is not honest
if a lightly-cleaned copy of it persists one row down. The live reply is
untouched: the operator sees the full preview in the moment; a session
reload shows the summary. I accepted that transcript-fidelity cost over the
alternative (persisting the body under some new excluded-from-history
marker), because markers rot and scope-creep — absence cannot be forwarded.

Chasing the same digest one level further turned up a smaller leak of the
identical shape: an untitled paste's pending label fell back to its
`source_ref`, which for pastes is `paste:<content-sha256>` — so the
`/approve` confirmation ("Approved — "paste:abc123…"") would have persisted
the full plaintext content digest into sessions.db through the decision
message. The label now falls back to `pasted article (doc {uuid8})`.
`/approve` and `/reject` themselves persist verbatim — short commands, no
content. Regression locks: a marker-tagged paste is absent from a full dump
of the session store (turns AND titles, raw and cleaned forms both); the
stub format is locked exactly; later-prompt history carries the stub only;
decision turns are unaffected; the untitled-paste decision path carries no
digest. Gate for this worktree: ui_gateway + ui_backend + ui_shell +
test_no_external_egress = 549 passed, 0 failed.

**Next:** the merge-gate review for #655 should re-run the dump-scan tests on
the merged tree (this branch is stacked on the knowledge-bank branch, whose
own fragment covers the at-rest membership-oracle close); the TUI inline
ingest orchestration named in the Stage-B follow-ups inherits this stub
behaviour for free via `handle_ingest_command`.


### 2026-06-10 — The front door gets a doorbell: explicit commands, one pending slot, and a turn that is not a turn

Stage B of the #655 knowledge-bank program handed me the operator-facing half:
the path from "I have an article" to "it is in the encrypted bank" had a store
(Stage A), an IPC contract (INGEST_SUBMIT / INGEST_DECISION / INGEST_RESULT),
and an encrypted staging handoff — but no way for a human to drive any of it
from chat. This session built that surface: gateway-parsed `/ingest`,
`/approve`, `/reject`, the preview-in-chat flow, and the WinUI passthrough,
all fetch-free (the URL limb stays behind the #598 glass).

The first decision was inherited, not made, and it shaped everything else:
**explicit commands, gateway-side**. ADR-030 §7 locks the doctrine — network
and ingest actions are never inferred from conversational text, and the
`_parse_external_command` precedent (`services/ui_gateway/src/transport.py`)
puts command parsing in the gateway so every UI surface gets one
implementation. I followed it exactly: `parse_ingest_command` lives in the
new `services/ui_gateway/src/ingest_coordinator.py`, `TransportGateway.
handle_ingest_command` is the single interception point, and the WinUI's only
contribution is a deliberate passthrough list (`/external`, `/ingest`,
`/approve`, `/reject` travel to the backend as prompt text; every other slash
command still errors client-side). The rejected alternative — a dedicated
`_m_ingest` RPC method, which the dispatcher's `_m_*` convention makes cheap —
would have been WinUI-only: the TUI never speaks the named pipe, and a
second command path per surface is exactly the drift the gateway-side pattern
exists to prevent. One asymmetry is worth recording honestly: the dispatcher
(`_m_prompt`) calls `handle_ingest_command` before `send_prompt`, so the
WinUI gets the feature now, while the TUI — which orchestrates its turns
inline — needs the same one-call hook added at its submit point. The parsing
parity is real; the wiring parity is a named follow-up, not an accident.

The second decision was the **one-pending-slot simplification**. A session may
have exactly ONE outstanding pending ingest; a second `/ingest` is refused by
name ("…reply /approve or /reject before starting another"). The rejected
alternative was a queue — and it was genuinely tempting, because the AO-side
bank happily holds any number of pending rows. But a queue drags in everything
the flow does not need: `/approve <n>` disambiguation, ordering and display
rules, and a stale-preview problem where the operator approves a document
whose preview scrolled away three articles ago. The whole point of this flow
is "read the preview you just asked for, then decide" — a slot, not a queue,
is the honest model of that. The slot lifecycle has one nuance the first
draft got wrong: I initially kept the slot on EVERY failed decision, then
realised a deterministic AO refusal (`INGEST_DECISION_REFUSED` — unknown doc,
already decided; never transient) leaves the operator trapped with a slot
nothing can clear. The shipped policy: clear on success AND on deterministic
refusal; keep only on transient failures (transport error, bank disabled)
where the decision never reached a verdict. The slot is in-memory keyed by
session_id, which buys a restart consequence I documented rather than hid: a gateway restart
forgets the slot but the pending ROW survives in the bank; re-running
`/ingest` with the same source replaces it per the dedup contract (keyed
source-hash → delete + insert), so nothing accumulates for the same source.
Orphaned pending rows from *other* forgotten sources remain AO-side —
`list_pending` exists, and a `/pending` listing command is the named gap.

The third decision took the longest to get right: **what is the preview,
ontologically?** It echoes into the transcript like an assistant message, but
it is not model output — it is deterministic tool output that was never
PGOV-validated, and pretending otherwise corrupts two ledgers at once (the
turn record claims a validation that never ran; the history budget re-feeds a
256 KB article into a 40 KB window). The representation I landed on: persist
it as an assistant turn with `pgov_status='informational'` (a new value —
`turns.pgov_status` carries no CHECK constraint, so it is a pure data
convention, constant `INFORMATIONAL_TURN_STATUS` in `session_store.py`). Two
properties fall out for free and are now regression-locked: the prompt-history
filter forwards only `'approved'` assistant turns, so previews never re-enter
the model context; and the WinUI's denial styling keys on
`pgov_status == 'denied'` only, so informational turns reload as plain
assistant messages with zero C# changes. On the wire the dispatcher emits the
preview as ONE token frame plus the terminal `end` — no pgov frame, no audio
frames regardless of the speak preference. The single frame is not just
protocol hygiene: the WinUI `MarkdownBlock` rebuilds its whole visual tree on
every Markdown change, so streaming an article-sized preview token-by-token
would pay an O(n²) rebuild the recon dossier explicitly warned about. One
frame, one rebuild. The rejected alternative — let the preview ride the
normal `send_prompt` → `stream_tokens` → PGOV arc by pre-caching a synthetic
approved verdict — would have given both surfaces wiring parity for free, and
I dropped it because it forges a PGOV approval for text PGOV never saw. A
cheaper integration was not worth a dishonest audit surface.

The **bare-URL nudge** (LA requirement, same day) rounds out the UX: a message
that is SOLELY one URL gets a deterministic gateway response offering
`/ingest <url>` — no model call, no fetch. The shape check is exact
(`https?://\S+` full-match on the stripped text); a URL inside a sentence
flows to the model untouched, because rewriting conversational text into
commands is precisely what the explicit-command doctrine forbids. `/ingest
<url>` itself returns the dormant-fetch message naming the activation gate
(#598 sign-off + ADR-027 Amendment 1) and the interim paste path — built
behind glass, refusing loudly, the ADR-024 pattern.

Two smaller calls worth their ink. First, the staging cipher: the encrypted
staging file must encrypt under the same DEK envelope as everything else
(ADR-025 §2.1), and the resolution policy for that envelope lives in
`build_session_store`. Rather than duplicate the three-way sealer/keystore
policy (the bug farm of every copied policy), I exposed a read-only
`field_cipher` property on `EncryptedSessionStore` — the gateway reads the
cipher off the store it already holds. No new surface: the derived subkeys
were already in process memory; the property only spares a second unseal.
Second, FILE-mode classification is deterministic and refuses to guess: an
absolute path that does not exist is a loud "file not found", never a
fall-through to paste mode — pasting a path string as an "article" is an
operator-error trap, and a single filename-shaped token gets the same
treatment. The ingest byte cap is the 262,144-byte `staging_max_bytes` bound,
deliberately NOT the document loader's 16 KB grounding cap (ADR-030 §9 —
storing for retrieval is not grounding a turn). `.pdf` is a named deferral:
the Cleaner contract has no PDF path in v1.

One late catch from re-reading my own diff: the interception ran OUTSIDE the
`_m_prompt` 90 s fail-safe, so a stalled ingest IPC call would have frozen the
front end's input for the raw 180 s socket timeout — exactly the class of
open-ended hang the fail-safe exists to bound (Vikunja #565). The fix wraps
the interception in the same `wait_for` deadline and emits the terminal frame
pair on timeout; a 0.05 s-deadline test locks it.

Everything ships against the cross-agent `CleanResult` interface contract with
the pipeline constructor-injected: the sibling agent's
`services/cleaner/src/pipeline.py` does not exist on this branch, so the real
wiring lazily imports it and fails with a loud "cleaner unavailable" message,
and all 71 new tests (45 coordinator, 19 gateway, 7 dispatcher) run against
fakes — clean, quarantined, cleaner-missing, encrypted-staging round-trip,
INGEST_SUBMIT payload shape with the mandatory `content_sha256`, the
pending-slot state machine, bare-URL shapes, the informational marker, the
single-frame emission, and the fail-safe bound. Quarantined results still
stage, still submit, and still preview — with the reasons displayed — because
the L0 pending state IS the ingest quarantine and `/approve` is the override
(ADR-030 §6, "conservative, operator reviews in chat"). Full standing-gate
selection green in this worktree before commit — 2788 passed / 20 skipped /
116 deselected (the 20 skips are the known missing-ONNX-model worktree
artifact on the semantic_router tests; they pass on the main checkout) — and
the WinUI project builds clean (0 warnings, 0 errors).

**Next:** wire the TUI submit hook to `handle_ingest_command` (one call, named
above); a `/pending` command listing AO-side pending rows that survived a
gateway restart; merge-time integration test against the real cleaner once
the sibling branch lands (replace the injected fakes with the genuine
`CleanResult` in one cross-module test); and the Stage-C URL limb — fetch via
`guarded_fetch.fetch_external` once W4 ships it, behind the #598 gate.

*(commit `dd466b9` — gateway ingest coordinator + preview/approve flow + WinUI
passthrough; 71 tests added; live verify awaits the real cleaner pipeline +
an on-box end-to-end /ingest→/approve run after the Stage-B merge.)*

**Post-review fixes (same day).** The adversarial review caught three things
worth naming honestly. First, a byte-cap seam I had built wrong: the gateway
capped the PLAINTEXT at `staging_max_bytes` while the AO caps the on-disk
CIPHERTEXT at the same number — AES-256-GCM staging adds exactly 29 bytes
(version + nonce + tag), so any plaintext in that last 29-byte band passed my
checks and bounced at the AO with a cap error quoting the same number it had
just passed, the most confusing possible refusal. The fix is gateway-side only
(the AO's Stage-A read cap deliberately does not move): a
`CIPHER_ENVELOPE_OVERHEAD_BYTES` constant derived from the field_cipher format
constants (never a hardcoded 29), subtracted in both the cleaned-text check
and the FILE raw-size precheck, with refusals naming the effective plaintext
cap; boundary tests prove at-cap plaintext stages within the staging cap via
the real `write_staged` output and one-byte-over refuses at the gateway.
Second, a genuine air-gap hole: absolute-path FILE mode accepted UNC paths —
`/ingest \\host\share\doc.html` would have performed an off-host SMB read
from an air-gapped runtime. Both the raw and the resolved form are now
refused on the `\\` prefix (covering `\\?\UNC\` long paths; the raw check
runs BEFORE `resolve()` so the share is never touched), and the docstring now
says what absolute mode actually is — a deliberate containment bypass for
LOCAL paths only — instead of claiming document_loader parity. The named
trade-off: `\\?\C:\` extended-length local paths are also refused (operators
give plain drive-letter paths; a simple prefix check beats namespace-parsing
cleverness). Third, the "TUI hook is a named follow-up" deferral did not
survive review — the TUI is dormant-but-supported, so it got the same
interception the dispatcher has: `handle_ingest_command` before the
`send_prompt` arc, one informational message, no PGOV panel, with an
`iscoroutinefunction` guard so MagicMock-style test gateways stay on the old
arc. 16 new tests across the three fixes; all four affected suites re-run
green.


# Journal fragment — UC-002/003 integration merge (fold into BUILD_JOURNAL.md)

### 2026-06-10 — Two sessions wrote the same amendment, and the merge had to make them one law

The UC-002/003 program landed on main tonight — four branches, ~11,300 inserted
lines, the knowledge bank, the cleaner, the ingest UX, and the governance that
binds them. The merge itself was the easy part: of the four `--no-ff` merges
(ADRs → knowledge-bank → cleaner → ingest-ux, onto an integration branch off
main `8703dae`), three went through without a single conflict and the fourth
conflicted only in two governance documents. The interesting work was everything
a green merge does not prove.

The conflict was its own lesson. Two parallel sessions had each authored
"Amendment 1" to ADR-027 — the egress-door session wrote Am.1 as the *mechanics*
(the one-door `guarded_fetch` seam, "URL = authorization" verdict tiers,
hostname-resolution pinning, charset-correct decode), and the UC-003 program
session wrote Am.1 as the *policy* (the per-action `INGEST_FETCH` adjudication
class, the paste-is-the-consent posture, the LA-verdict-reconciled activation
preconditions). Same section number, same date, two halves of one decision,
written blind to each other. Git surfaced the Status line as a conflict but
happily placed both full sections in the file fifty lines apart — a reader would
have found two competing "Amendment 1"s in the canonical egress policy. I unified
them into a single record (policy decision first, mechanics as its implementing
subsections, the reconciled preconditions governing) and recorded the unification
in the DECISION_REGISTER trailer. The alternative — renumbering one limb to
"Amendment 2" — was rejected: both limbs were LA-ratified as Amendment 1 in their
own contexts, and the register + commit history already cite them that way;
renumbering would have falsified the provenance to make the file tidier.

Then the semantic gate, because the independent review's sharpest observation was
that a clean *textual* merge could silently reintroduce the membership oracle the
program's MAJOR fix had just closed: the ingest-ux branch forked before the
knowledge-bank branch keyed the content digest, and still computes a plaintext
`sha256(cleaned_text)`. Three parallel verification lenses ran on the integrated
tree. The oracle trace proved the design holds: the plaintext digest lives only
in RAM and the IPC frame (the ADR-031 staging-integrity contract *requires* it
there, fail-closed at encode), and every persistence surface — the `knowledge.db`
column, the WAL bytes, the audit `car_hash`, the sessions.db paste stubs — gets
the HMAC-keyed form, with `submit_pending` deliberately keeping the
`content_sha256` param name and keying internally. The combined oracle + ingest
regression set (the locks from both branches, meeting for the first time on one
tree) ran 206/206. The egress lens proved the auto-merged
`test_no_external_egress.py` — a security lock file git merged *without asking* —
kept all three locks intact: the door's one-file `httpx` carve-out, the
exactly-one-importer companion, and the cleaner's trafilatura extraction-only
AST scan (+137 lines, zero deletions, nothing weakened).

The governance lens failed my own merge, which is exactly what it was for. I had
retensed ADR-027's stale "being rebuilt on `feat/w4-w5-going-online-2`" claim to
the merged fact and then claimed in the register trailer that the stale reference
was resolved — but ADR-030 carried the same pending-state claim in two more
places, inside the activation-precondition list a future Stage-C activation
decision will read, and I had missed both. A half-reconciled precondition wearing
a "reconciled" label in the SSOT index is worse than an unreconciled one. Fixed
in `bf517ac` (plus retensing the two "zero-NIC assertion lands in parallel"
forward-pointers to the landed `verify_vm_zero_nic` fact the verifier confirmed
in `launcher/vm_manager.py`). One MINOR residual was ticketed rather than fixed
(#658): the live preview renders the plaintext paste sha on screen —
contract-compliant (display is not persistence) but worth taking off the
operator-visible surface.

The numbers were derived, not eyeballed, per the review's instruction: main
collects 2661 gate-selected tests, the integrated tree 2984 — the program's
measured contribution is +323, against the build agent's pre-door estimate of
≈307. The full standing gate on the integrated tree came back green at exactly
the derived expectation: 2984 passed / 0 skipped / 116 deselected / 0 failed in
2:46 (the shell held the symlink-create privilege, so even the standing ±2
symlink skips passed), and the landing on main re-ran it clean. I went with
merge-not-rebase for the four branches because they are
another agent's published refs, possibly checked out in its worktrees — rewriting
them is the 2026-05-22 race shape; the cost is four merge bubbles in history,
accepted.

**Next:** the doctrine baseline fold (CLAUDE.md / TEST_GOVERNANCE still said
2565 before this session; reality is now post-program), then Stage C — the guest
VM parser mini-sprint (#655 c.1045) — and at its end the wiring of
`ingest_coordinator`'s URL mode through `guarded_fetch.fetch_external`, the PA
adjudicator registration at the gateway entrypoint, and the first live
`/ingest <url>` with the LA at the screen.

*(commits `e8aba5c`/`c709183`/`84ef47d`/`179369d` (the four --no-ff merges),
`bf517ac` (governance retense); semantic gate: oracle 206/206, security suite
227/0; full standing gate on the integrated tree + clean main: see
TEST_GOVERNANCE §1 baseline updated in the landing commit.)*


### 2026-06-10 — A third failure posture, and the index that lives only in RAM

Stage A of the UC-003/UC-002 program (#655) is the knowledge-bank vertical: the
encrypted, approval-gated store that the future paste-a-URL flow will land
cleaned articles into. I shipped it end to end behind glass — `knowledge.db`
as a sibling of `substrate.db`, the IPC verbs, the encrypted staging handoff,
the AO wiring, the ingest audit chain, and the 512-token re-embed migration —
with no fetch anywhere in sight (that limb stays governance-gated behind
ADR-027 and the #598 sign-off; this stage never touches a socket).

The first decision was where the documents live. The tempting move was a third
`kind` in `substrate_chunks` — one store, one boot cache, one retrieval path.
But the substrate carries `CHECK(kind IN ('doc','turn'))`, which SQLite cannot
ALTER (a full table rebuild on the operator's live memory store, to share a
schema that has no approval state, no provenance record, and no dedup-by-source
column). I went with a sibling DB under the SAME DEK envelope (ADR-025's
one-DEK rule), accepting a second boot-decrypt pass and a second retrieval path
in exchange for a lifecycle the substrate simply does not have: rows move
`pending → approved|rejected`, pending rows hold cleaned content only (no
chunks, no embeddings), approval is the moment chunking + embedding + indexing
happen, and rejection is a tombstone that RETAINS content — retention is a
later lifecycle decision I deliberately did not take. Dedup is the substrate's
keyed-HMAC trick over ciphertext: a re-submitted identical source replaces a
prior pending row, replaces a rejected tombstone (an explicit re-submit is a
fresh-decision request), and NEVER touches an approved document — that conflict
returns a distinct `already_ingested` result instead.

The hairiest design fork was lexical search. UC-002 has always promised
hybrid retrieval, and FTS5 is compiled into the project's Python 3.11.9 — but
an FTS5 table on disk indexes plaintext, which would park recoverable article
text right next to the AES-256-GCM ciphertext it defeats (the Sprint-14
strict-residuals override is exactly on point). An encrypted-at-rest index
file was the second option; it costs a rebuild-or-rewrite on every approve and
buys nothing at personal scale. I took the third path: at DEK-unlock the bank
builds an IN-MEMORY FTS5 index (`:memory:` connection) over the decrypted
chunk text of approved docs — the exact pattern the substrate's embedding boot
cache established — and extends it incrementally on each approve. Hybrid
retrieval is brute-force cosine over the in-RAM vectors plus FTS5 BM25, merged
by reciprocal-rank fusion with the canonical k=60. Plaintext exists only in
RAM, only after unlock, which is precisely the boundary the rest of the system
already accepts. (Free-form queries are word-split and double-quoted before
they reach MATCH, so FTS5 operator syntax is not an injection surface.)

Embeddings got their overdue width fix. The substrate has been embedding
2048-char chunks through the leakage detector's 128-token window — roughly
three quarters of every stored chunk never informed its vector. Knowledge
chunks embed at 512 tokens (bge-small's native max) via a NEW
`LeakageDetector.embed_documents` method on the same loaded session; the
leakage path's 128-token `_embed` is untouched because PGOV Stage-5 thresholds
are calibrated there, and a regression test now locks its output to a
reference computation. Writing that lock taught me something embarrassing: my
first stub session emitted `hidden[s,d] = ids[s] * scale[d]`, and L2
normalisation cancelled the token dimension entirely — the 128-vs-512 test
could not fail no matter what the code did. The stub had to become
non-separable (`(ids[s] + d) % 13`) before the property under test was even
visible. The existing `substrate.db` gets the same width via a runnable
re-embed migration (`python -m services.assistant_orchestrator.src.reembed_substrate`):
decrypt text → re-embed at 512 → re-encrypt under the same AAD, bump
`substrate_meta.embed_max_tokens`, verify every row decrypts to a unit-norm
384-vector, idempotent on re-run. It will be executed manually on the live box
— tests drive it only with stub embedders.

The process seam is deliberately boring: three new IPC verbs (INGEST_SUBMIT /
INGEST_DECISION / INGEST_RESULT) whose payloads are labels only. Content
crosses in an encrypted staging file
(`%LOCALAPPDATA%\BlarAI\ingest_staging\<doc_uuid>.bin`, same DEK, AAD bound to
the doc identity), so the 64 KB envelope is never pressured and a staged blob
cannot be replayed under a different document. One security call worth
recording: the submit payload carries a `staging_path`, but the AO never
dereferences it — the read side derives the canonical path from the validated
UUID (anything traversal-shaped dies in `uuid.UUID()` before a path exists)
and merely cross-checks the claim. A path that arrives over IPC is input, not
an address.

Failure posture was a genuine decision, so I am naming the alternatives. The
substrate degrades silently (memory off, AO starts); the session store refuses
to start. The knowledge bank takes a deliberate third posture: feature-level
LOUD disable — construction failure (including a production audit-chain
failure: TPM key unprovisioned, keystore missing) logs ERROR and every
INGEST_* frame returns a clear `KNOWLEDGE_BANK_DISABLED` error, but chat boots
untouched. An ingest feature the operator can see is broken beats both a
silent absence and a bricked assistant. In the same register: the AO now emits
its first ADR-029 audit events — its own file, its own chain
(`ingest_audit.jsonl`), the PA's adjudication log untouched — mapping
submit→ESCALATE (held for human review, which is literally what pending
means), approve→ALLOW, reject→DENY, with doc_uuid + source-hash prefix as the
resource and never a byte of content. A decision whose audit append fails
returns an error frame rather than silently succeeding unrecorded. Two smaller
deliberate divergences: WAL is ON for knowledge.db (interactive writes
alongside reads; the substrate's default journal stays as-is), and retrieved
knowledge is grounded UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL with datamarking REGARDLESS of the
stored provenance column — operator approval curates content into the bank, it
does not promote web text into the trust boundary (ADR-023, lesson 13). Since
today's four tools are all SAFE-tier, the Layer-3 lock this engages costs the
daily driver nothing.
