Numbers: 815 lines in `knowledge_bank.py`, 335 in the re-embed module, 206 in
the staging helper, plus the protocol/entrypoint/pgov edits; 146 new tests
(106 under `services/assistant_orchestrator/tests/`, 40 under `shared/tests/`)
all green with no model files present, and the three touched suites
(AO + shared + ui_gateway) pass 1610→1756 with one existing message-type lock
updated in-change, 17 deselected, 0 failures. `docs/security/DATA_MAP.md` §2
gained rows 8–10 (knowledge.db, ingest_staging/, ingest_audit.jsonl).

**Addendum (same day) — the review that found real corruption.** An
adversarial review of this vertical reproduced a CRITICAL defect I am keeping
on the record exactly as found: `submit_pending` issued its dedup-replace
DELETE and could then raise on the doc_uuid-collision check — python's sqlite3
left that DELETE in an open implicit transaction, and the next healthy
operation's commit flushed it, silently destroying an unrelated pending
document (submit u1/X, submit u2/Y, submit(doc_uuid=u1, source=Y) raises; u2
is gone after a later submit + reopen). `approve()` carried the same
no-rollback hole. The fix is structural, not local: every mutating method now
runs all deterministic checks and ALL encryption before any DML, and the DML
runs inside `with self._conn:` (commit-or-rollback); the exact reproduction is
a regression test, alongside a mid-INSERT IntegrityError rollback lock. The
review also caught the audit chain recording AFTER the mutation — a dead audit
sink left an approved, retrievable document with no governance record. I
inverted to audit-first (PA precedent: an unauditable action never takes
effect), accepting the documented opposite residual — a record may exist for a
mutation that then failed — resolved by a best-effort `<verb>_FAILED`/DENY
compensating record, with read-only prechecks so deterministic refusals never
write records at all. Three more MAJORs: the re-embed migration stamped
`substrate_meta` even when every row failed (now stamped only on a clean run,
with `--accept-quarantined` for confirmed dev-key remnants); the AO still
bound the substrate embedder at 128 tokens while the migration stamps 512 —
post-ceremony ingests would have re-created the exact mixed-depth store
ADR-031 §3 rejects (now meta-driven at build time, and the knowledge bank
refuses retrieve/approve on a stored-vs-configured window mismatch); and my
"vector" retrieval tests were all satisfiable by BM25 alone — the suite passed
with the cosine limb deleted (the new pinned-vector lock and the strengthened
punctuation test both fail with the limb commented out; I verified that before
restoring it). Minor hardening rounded it out: `content_sha256` is now
required fail-closed at both the encoder and the AO seam, plus WAL-sidecar
plaintext-scan, FTS5-stays-in-RAM, and AAD-swap locks. Suites grew 1756→1782
across AO + shared + ui_gateway, zero failures. The lesson already in this
entry — prove the lock can fail — applied to my own tests within hours of
writing it.

**Next:** Stage B builds the operator-facing limbs this vertical is shaped
for: the gateway-side `/ingest` UX (staging writer is already shared), the
trafilatura cleaner behind its hash-pinned vetting record, the in-chat
preview/approve flow over the new INGEST verbs, and — strictly after the #598
sign-off and the ADR-027 Amendment 1 per-action adjudication — the VM-homed
fetch. The substrate re-embed ceremony (`--dry-run` first) wants a slot in the
next live-box session, and the bank's embedding cache should eventually adopt
the #611 idle-unload pattern the substrate already has.

*(commits `8efbffc` (encrypted knowledge-bank core + 512-token embed_documents + re-embed migration), `0605329` (AO wiring: build/dispatch/retrieval + ingest audit chain + DATA_MAP rows), `f5e8bfc` (adversarial-review fixes: transaction discipline + audit-first + meta-driven embed windows + vector-limb lock), `34c3e34` (V-1 membership-oracle closure: keyed content digest at rest + in audit; zero-NIC launcher gate); merged to main in `c709183`; branch-tip gate 1668/0.)*


### 2026-06-10 — The option we held three days ago found its feature

Today the UC-003/UC-002 program (#655) got its governance substrate: two new ADRs, one
amendment, and the register rows that index them — written before the builders' code
lands, so the code is built against a ratified design instead of the design being
reverse-engineered from the diff. The work itself was documentary, but every paragraph
in it is a decision the LA made at the comprehension gate this morning, and the shape of
that gate is worth keeping.

The gate ran as four named forks, each presented with its alternatives, each answered
explicitly. One: build now or wait for the #598 sign-off — answered *build now, behind
glass*, on the ADR-024 precedent (the whole web-search skill merged mocked and dormant
behind the armed egress guard; construction was never the gated thing, activation is).
Two: how does an arbitrary pasted news URL clear an egress policy whose rule 1 is "named
endpoints, one vetted at a time"? — answered by exercising the option ADR-027 itself had
explicitly *held* on 2026-06-07: the hybrid per-action-consent refinement "if a feature
warrants it (e.g. fetching arbitrary user-directed URLs)." Three days later the exact
feature the parenthetical predicted showed up, and adopting the held option took one
amendment (ADR-027 Amendment 1: the paste is the consent for that ONE URL; PA adjudicates
a new `INGEST_FETCH` action class per fetch + logs it; GET-only with no user-content echo
outbound; kill-switch and exfil screen untouched) instead of a re-litigation of the whole
egress posture. Three: placement — VM-homed fetch and HTML parsing from day one, per
roadmap Decision 3, rejecting the faster host-side-first build because an HTML parser
eating hostile web bytes inside the process that holds the unsealed DEK is exactly the
thing the HYBRID topology exists to prevent, and lesson 73 says the "move it later" note
never costs what the note implies. Four: trafilatura, +9 hash-pinned packages,
extraction-only with a named static-scan lock against its fetch functions — the LA
weighed the largest supply-chain addition since openvino against what stdlib heuristics
would store into a decades-horizon knowledge bank, and chose quality.

ADR-031 carries the design contract for the store itself, and its load-bearing calls all
have the rejected path on the record. A sibling `knowledge.db` under the same DEK rather
than new kinds in `substrate_chunks` (SQLite cannot ALTER a CHECK constraint; curated
documents and disposable conversational memory should not share a migration history). The
128-to-512-token embedding fix as a *new* method so PGOV's leakage path — calibrated at
128 — stays byte-identical, plus a re-embed migration utility for the existing
`substrate.db` rows whose vectors currently see only the first quarter of each 2048-char
chunk. Lexical retrieval via FTS5 (probe-verified compiled into the project Python,
SQLite 3.45.1) but **in RAM only**, rebuilt at DEK-unlock beside the existing embedding
boot cache — an on-disk FTS5 index is a plaintext shadow of the ciphertext store, and the
keyed-token alternative leaks term-level equality across the whole corpus for no BM25 in
return. Hybrid merge by reciprocal-rank fusion at k=60 because cosine and BM25 scores do
not share a scale. Content crosses processes in an encrypted staging file rather than by
raising the 64 KB IPC envelope to 1 MB — a transport-wide loosening for one feature was
the rejected path — and ingest deliberately does not inherit the 16 KB `/load` cap (it
gets its own 262,144-byte staging limit; storing for retrieval is not grounding a turn).
And a third failure posture, named and argued: *loud-disable* — knowledge-bank failure
kills ingest and knowledge retrieval with clear errors but never blocks AO boot — sitting
deliberately between the substrate's silent-degrade (right for best-effort memory, wrong
for a store the operator curates) and the session store's refuse-to-start (which would
hold chat hostage to a feature). Retrieval of approved knowledge stays untrusted and
datamarked forever; the provenance column is for the record, not for trust — approval is
a quality judgment, not a security clearance.

One mechanical note for the record: the brief assumed ADR-030/031 were the next free
numbers; verified on disk against `docs/adrs/` (ADR-028 measured-boot scope and ADR-029
audit retention both exist; 030/031 free) before writing a byte.

Same-day postscript: the reconciliation pass. The ADRs and the Stage-A code were drafted
in parallel sessions, and review caught the drift between them before either had a chance
to ossify — the drafted audit section did not yet carry the implemented decision mapping
(`INGEST_SUBMIT` held as ESCALATE for human review, APPROVE as ALLOW, REJECT as DENY),
the audit-first ordering (append the record before the bank mutation commits, with a
best-effort compensating DENY if the mutation then fails, so the chain never shows an
unresolved ALLOW), or the deliberate dedup carve-outs. And on the other limb, before any
fetch code existed at all, a cross-session integration contract (epic #577 comment 1029,
LA-relayed) replaced my drafted fetch-in-guest wording with the one-door composition:
the socket lives host-side inside `guarded_fetch` — the single PA-gated egress door W4
ships — while the hostile-HTML parsing stays guest-homed per Decision 3. I folded both
back into ADR-030/031, the ADR-027 amendment, and the register the same day. The cheap
version of the lesson: parallel drafting drifts, always, and the review that catches it
has to land on the record — an ADR that describes a fetcher nobody will build is worse
than no ADR, because it reads as ratified.

Second same-day postscript: the LA-verdict pass. An independent LA-session verification
(2026-06-10) read these ADRs against the code that actually exists, and the corrections
it mandated are worth keeping in, because two of them are my own drift. First: the
activation lists named a Cleaner `fetch_enabled` config flip. No such flag exists — and
none was ever going to, because the dormancy the builders actually shipped is *stronger*
than a flag: the fetch limb is structurally absent until Stage C writes it through
`guarded_fetch.fetch_external`. The docs described the safer design as the weaker one,
and worse, they would have sent a future agent hunting a switch that is not there. The
fix was to reframe dormancy as absence-of-code everywhere the flip appeared. Second: the
"guest VM NIC provisioning" precondition was ratified under the drafted fetch-in-guest
design and survived my same-day reconciliation to the one-door host-side composition —
exactly the half-reconciled state the first postscript warned about. Under one-door, a
guest NIC is not a prerequisite; it is the exposure the zero-NIC posture defends. The LA
struck it and INVERTED it: the guest stays NIC-less, and the launcher asserts zero NICs
at VM start, failing closed if one appears. Third, the good news the docs were too stale
to carry: the #598 §5.12 sign-off is no longer future — the GO was recorded on #598
comment 1026 today; what remains is Am.1 in force (verified against the real W4 code,
now rebuilding on `feat/w4-w5-going-online-2`) plus the Stage-C limb itself. The same
pass recorded the content-digest decision in ADR-031 (at-rest `content_sha256` and the
audit `car_hash` carry the `keyed_index` HMAC hex, never the plaintext digest — a
plaintext content hash in a stolen DB or audit file is a membership oracle, and the
ADR-029 plaintext carve-out covers action labels, not content-derived hashes) and named
two ADR-030 residuals the LA accepted with bounds: local-absolute-path file ingest (UNC
refused raw+resolved, extension allowlist, strict UTF-8, size cap, preview + approval)
and the Stage-B lxml interim (host-parsing operator-consented LOCAL HTML files until the
Stage-C guest parser, the durable fix). The lesson rhymes with the first postscript's:
a reconciliation pass that updates the mechanism but not the preconditions ratified
under the old mechanism has only half-reconciled, and the preconditions are where a
future activation decision will actually look.

**Next:** the Stage-A builders land the store, IPC types, staging handoff, AO wiring, and
retrieval against this contract; Stage B installs trafilatura (hash-pinned, vetted) and
builds the gateway/preview UX; the fetch limb stays unwritten until Stage C wires it
through `guarded_fetch.fetch_external` under the reconciled §8 preconditions (sign-off
RECORDED; Am.1 verify-against-real-W4-code; zero-NIC invariant). This fragment folds into
BUILD_JOURNAL.md at the next quiet point.

*(commits `63f3736` (ADR-030 + ADR-031 + ADR-027 Am.1 + register rows + this fragment), `bbf62d6` (register reconciled to the implemented audit/embed contracts + the #577 c.1029 one-door integration contract), `df51b8d` (LA-verdict reconciliation: structural dormancy, NIC inversion, sign-off RECORDED, keyed content digest, named residuals); merged to main in `e8aba5c` with the two parallel Am.1 limbs unified at the integration merge.)*


### 2026-06-10 — The VM that owned itself out of being stopped

The symptom the Lead Architect reported was concrete and a little maddening: close BlarAI
cleanly, and the 2 GiB `BlarAI-Orchestrator` Hyper-V VM stays `Running`. An on-demand
assistant that does not give its memory back when you dismiss it is, on a 32 GB single-user
box, a real cost — and the most annoying kind of bug, because the *next* clean exit doesn't
fix it either. It just sits there.

The root cause was an ownership ratchet hiding in the launcher's cleanup, and it is the sort
of logic that reads as obviously-correct until you trace the second boot. At startup the
launcher set `_vm_was_started = vm_initial_state != VMState.RUNNING` — "I only own the VM if
I was the one who turned it on" — and `_cleanup` then stopped the VM only `if _vm_was_started`.
Reasonable in isolation: don't stop a VM somebody else is using. But the flag is sticky in the
wrong direction. The moment the VM is *ever* left running across a launcher start — a crash, a
hard console close that kills the process before the up-to-30s `Stop-VM` can finish, a host
reboot that resurrects it, another tool starting it — the *next* launch sees it already
`Running`, sets `_vm_was_started = False`, and from then on every clean exit skips the stop.
The leak self-perpetuates forever from a single transient event. Today's `launcher.log` showed
exactly the signature: the 11:52:59 cleanup logged `stopping Policy Agent service` →
`Cleanup: complete` with no `stopping Hyper-V VM` line between them. The condition that should
have released the VM had quietly latched off.

There was a compounding accomplice. The VM's Hyper-V `AutomaticStartAction` was `StartIfRunning`,
which re-inflates a *leaked* VM at every Windows boot — so a VM left running at shutdown came
back on its own, pre-consuming 2 GiB before BlarAI was ever launched, and re-arming the ratchet.
That one was fixed live (`Set-VM -AutomaticStartAction Nothing`) and is documented in the new
`docs/runbooks/vm_lifecycle.md`.

The fix flips the default and adds an escape hatch rather than just hard-coding always-stop,
because the old behaviour is genuinely correct in one scenario and wrong in every other. The
new `vm_stop_on_exit` policy is `always` by default — stop the VM whenever it is currently
`Running`, decided from the VM's *live state* instead of from who started it, so a transient
leak can no longer latch. `if_started` preserves the legacy semantics for the parallel-dev case
where a second session really does own the VM and the launcher must not yank it. `never` is the
keep-it-warm option. The trade-off I made deliberately: defaulting to `always` means that if you
*are* running two launchers against one VM without setting the flag, closing one stops the VM
under the other — the parallel-dev case has to opt in via `BLARAI_VM_STOP_ON_EXIT=if_started`. I
took that because this is a single-user box whose VM exists only for BlarAI; the common case is
one assistant that must release its memory when dismissed, and the rarer multi-session case is
exactly the one a developer can be expected to configure. An unrecognised policy value resolves
to `always`, never to a leak — a typo can't silently re-arm the ratchet.

I also made the stop best-effort on the slow path without touching `vm_manager` semantics:
`_cleanup` runs on `atexit`, so it must never raise, but it was also ignoring `stop_vm()`'s
return value entirely. Now a `False` return (timeout or `Stop-VM` failure) logs a WARNING with
the manual `Stop-VM -Name "BlarAI-Orchestrator"` one-liner and cleanup still completes — a slow
shutdown is surfaced, not swallowed and not fatal.

**Next:** live-verify on the box — launch BlarAI, close it, confirm `launcher.log` now shows
`stopping Hyper-V VM (policy=always, VM is Running, ...)` and `Get-VM` reports `Off`; then
deliberately leave the VM running across a launch and confirm the *next* clean exit stops it
(the ratchet scenario the unit test covers but only the real box proves end-to-end). The
`AutomaticStartAction = Nothing` change wants the same confirmation after a real Windows reboot.


### 2026-06-11 — The deck that taught its own lesson 103, then needed three tries to measure "readable"

The lessons teaching deck (#650) shipped on the morning of 2026-06-10 and was stale by the evening: the journal's numbered list grew from 90 to 107 lessons in the fold that same day, the twelve fragment files the deck's Act VIII cited were deleted at fold-in, and a new wave of sixteen fragments piled up behind them. When this session opened the deck's own validator, it failed 12 of 12 fragment citations — the deck had lived lesson 103 ("verify the vocabulary you teach") once already, and now it was living lesson 39: evidence rots under a living artifact at exactly the speed the project moves. The LA's instruction, on learning the deck existed at all, was direct — *"make it better. give it better graphics and test everything."*

The refresh tripled the deck's machinery. Content first: the nine folded units were promoted into their thematic acts as lessons 91–99 with their evidence re-anchored from dead fragment files to the folded journal entries; eight brand-new units were drafted for lessons 100–107; the sixteen on-disk fragments were triaged (two confirmed already-covered by L105 and L107 — drafted as nothing) and became fifteen new June units, taking the deck to 126 units. Then graphics: every one of the 126 units now carries a mechanism diagram and/or verbatim stat callouts (127 diagrams total), drafted by eight parallel act-enrichment agents under a linted contract — first-line type allowlist, quoted labels, no styling directives, and a new rule paid for the hard way: a semicolon inside a sequenceDiagram's text is a statement separator, and three diagrams died of it before the real parser (not the lint) caught them. Act-colored design system, domain-colored AIGP chips, a journey rail, and the LA's scope call of the day: when he saw "390 slides" he balked — *"is this going to really prepare me for the certification... 390 is like an order of magnitue larger than expected"* — and the honest answer was no, no deck of project lessons covers Domain II law content at any size. He chose a curated **core path**: 31 lessons (criteria recorded in `core_path.json`), the deck opens on it, the full 126 stay one keypress away, and the Domain II gap is named on the AIGP-frame slide rather than papered over.

"Test everything" became a one-command battery (`run_checks.py`): the content validator, the build, a real-mermaid parse of every diagram string embedded in the built HTML (borrowing the capstone's installed Node toolchain — which a probe caught missing from the worktree, because `node_modules` is gitignored and lived only in the main checkout), then a headless-Edge render audit that activates all 518 slides and measures them, plus a functional self-test of navigation, index, studied-marks, core/full mode, and resume. The audit is where the day's real lesson lived. Its first version flagged 114 "paint spills" — false positives, because it counted scrollable below-the-fold ink inside a clipping card as spillage. The second version measured the svg's layout box instead and found 1 real escape, whose root cause a screenshot finally made obvious: the slide is a fixed-height flex column, and **a flex child's default `flex-shrink:1` was silently crushing the diagram card under its own svg** — one `flex-shrink:0` line fixed what looked like a rendering bug. The third version then had to confront what "fits" had cost: contain-fitting a nine-node vertical chain into a 16:9 card scaled it to 0.34 — zero overflow, perfectly green, and unreadable at nine pixels. The check was measuring the mechanism (nothing escapes its box) when the property that matters is *a human can read it*. The final audit gates on the property: a readability floor (diagrams never render below 0.7 of natural size; tall ones scroll inside their card), an auto-orient pass that re-renders extreme tall-and-skinny flowcharts left-to-right, a shrunk-scale gate at 0.65, and spill detection scoped to where spilling is physically possible (`overflow: visible`). Full battery: validate, build, 127/127 diagrams parse, render audit, self-test — all green, screenshots eyeballed at presentation size.

One operational scar worth keeping: mid-refresh, four of six parallel drafter agents were killed by a Claude monthly spend limit — and five of their six output files survived intact on disk, because they wrote their staging JSON before dying. The LA raised the limit, one agent was relaunched, nothing was lost. Parallel work that lands its artifacts incrementally is what made a hard kill a five-minute recovery instead of a redo.

**Next:** the LA-coordinated merge of `feature/lessons-deck-v2` to main; then the Part-B presenter sittings begin against the core path, logging to `learning_log.md`; the deck regenerates via `python run_checks.py` whenever the journal folds again (Act VIII units promote per the README protocol).

*(commit `<this>` (the v2 refresh: 126 units, 127 diagrams, core path, test battery); battery green: validate 126/0 FAILs, parse 127/127, headless audit + selftest PASS at 1440×810.)*


# Journal fragment — fold into BUILD_JOURNAL.md (date order)

### 2026-06-11 — Catching the subclass while the base class escaped

The Stage C guest parser merged green at 131 new tests, and an adversarial
review still found a one-frame way to kill it. That sentence is the whole
lesson, but the mechanism deserves the full walk because it is an
exception-hierarchy footgun I will meet again.

The chain: `ParseChannelError` subclasses `ValueError` — a deliberate choice,
so callers that catch the channel error also catch the framer's malformed-
envelope errors. The guest parser's `_send_error` echoed the peer-supplied
`request_id` into its error-response envelope and caught `ParseChannelError`
around the encode, behind a comment that read `# pragma: no cover — tiny body,
cannot overflow`. The premise was false. The body IS tiny, but the envelope
also carries the echoed correlation id, and `_validate_request_id` had no
length cap — the assembler even records the id *before* size validation
(deliberately, so violations can be answered), which made it unbounded up to
the incoming frame size. A `request_id` of ~65,200 chars fits the incoming
64 KB frame; the echoed ERROR response then exceeds 65,536 bytes, and
`MessageFramer.encode` raises a *plain* `ValueError` — the base class, not the
subclass `_send_error` was catching. The exception sailed through
`serve_connection`, through `main()`'s accept loop (which caught only
`KeyboardInterrupt`), and took down the whole listener — one hostile frame
consuming a third of the OpenRC supervisor's 3-crash budget. I reproduced it
exactly before fixing: `ValueError: Message size 65658 exceeds limit 65536`,
escaped, on a frame any host glue bug or compromised peer could mint.

The inversion is what stings: catching `ValueError` would have subsumed the
subclass and been safe; catching the subclass left the base free — the catch
was *narrower* than the raise surface, and the pragma comment asserting the
path was unreachable is precisely what kept coverage tooling from ever asking.
A `# pragma: no cover` on an except clause is a claim, and claims about
"cannot happen" paths deserve adversarial pricing, not exemption from it.

Three fixes, layered. First, `_send_error` now catches `ValueError` — the
error-or-drop contract is restored structurally: if the error response itself
cannot be encoded, the connection drops; nothing escapes. Second, defense in
depth at the channel: `request_id` is now capped at
`PARSE_REQUEST_ID_MAX_CHARS = 256` in `shared/ipc/parse_channel.py`, so an
over-long id dies on the FIRST frame at assembly time — never recorded,
nothing to echo, the error-encode overflow becomes unreachable from the wire.
I chose 256 over the UUID-exact ~36 because correlation ids are host-minted
and I want headroom for future composite ids (sprint-prefixed, retry-suffixed)
without a protocol bump, accepting that 256 is ~7x looser than today's actual
use. Third, the structural version of the same promise: `main()`'s accept
loop now wraps each connection's serve in a catch-all that logs the exception
*class name only* — never page content, never paths, the leak-tightness
census stays true — and continues to the next accept. The second reviewer
confirmed the real transport fails closed to `None`/`False` on receive/send,
so the encode path was the one live raiser; the catch-all makes "a malformed
connection costs at most that connection" an invariant rather than an
inventory claim about today's raisers.

The review also caught a mislabel I folded into the same pass: a request that
assembles cleanly but violates the request schema at decode (a non-string
`source_url` meta, say) was reported to the host as `RESPONSE_TOO_LARGE` —
the decode and encode failures shared one except clause, and the label
belonged to the wrong side. Request-side violations now carry
`PARSE_CHANNEL_VIOLATION`; the closed error vocabulary is unchanged, no new
codes. I kept the answer-and-continue semantics for decode-side violations
(the framing was valid, the stream is not corrupted) rather than extending
the drop rule to them — dropping would have been defensible too, but it
changes observable behavior the host glue will be written against, and the
reviewer's finding was the label, not the lifecycle.

Nine regression tests lock all of it: the ~65,200-char reproducer now served
as a clean drop (no response, no exception); the 256 cap rejected at encode
and on the first assembled frame with the id never recorded; exactly-256
accepted and still addressable for error responses; an injected plain
`ValueError` in the error-encode contained; the request-side and
response-side error codes asserted distinctly; and an injected unexpected
exception inside a real `main()` accept loop (TCP loopback, threaded) that
must log structurally and keep serving the next connection. I verified the
locks fire by checking the pre-fix sources back out and watching the
reproducer escape, then restoring the fix and watching it drop.

**Next:** the host-side glue that speaks this channel (the URL-mode ingest
limb through `guarded_fetch`), then the real-VM round-trip of the parser
service on the Alpine guest — the FakeTransport seam is explicitly an
interim. Fold the +9 locks into the standing-gate baseline at the next
quiet-tree count.

*(commits `3091d98` (both fix halves + the accept-loop catch-all + the
error-code relabel + 9 regression locks; 122 passed on the parse-channel +
cleaner selection, 228 passed / 1 deselected on `tests/security/`), `<this>`
(fragment). Awaits: baseline fold-in; the real-VM listener round-trip.)*


# Journal fragment — fold into BUILD_JOURNAL.md (parallel-work inbox)

### 2026-06-11 — The door the loopback tests never knocked on

The Stage C parse channel had a guest that listened and a host that
couldn't reach it, and every test I had was green. That is the kind of
green that should make you nervous, and it did — but only after the live
round-trip refused to connect. The guest parser service was up on the
Alpine VM, bound to a real plaintext `AF_VSOCK` listener on
`(VMADDR_CID_ANY, 50001)` because we started it with `--allow-plaintext`
(mTLS on the parse channel is still a tracked residual, not yet wired).
The host smoke harness fired and got *connection refused* — not a
timeout, not a handshake failure, a flat refusal as if nothing were
listening. Nothing was listening, on the host, at `127.0.0.1:50001`,
which is exactly where the harness was knocking.

The root cause was a conflation that had been sitting in
`shared/ipc/vsock.py` since the transport was first written, harmless
until the moment a host genuinely had to cross the VM boundary without
certs. The transport offered three postures — dev (AF_INET loopback, no
mTLS), production host-mode (AF_INET loopback + mTLS), production
guest-mode (AF_HYPERV + mTLS) — and the *only* way to get "no mTLS" was
`dev_mode=True`, which also forced AF_INET loopback. "No mTLS" and "use
loopback" were the same switch. There was no fourth posture for the one
the bring-up actually needed: AF_HYPERV with no SSL wrap. So the harness,
and `guest_parser_health.py`, and the bridge's own `_build_transport`,
all reached for the same heuristic — `dev_mode = not has_mtls` — and that
heuristic quietly routed a guest-bound, cert-less connection to the host
loopback. The guest was correct the whole time; the host had no way to
*ask* for the door it was standing in front of.

The fix decouples the two switches. `VsockConfig` gains an explicit
`allow_plaintext_hyperv: bool = False` — a transport-mode opt-in, the
host-side parallel to the guest's `--allow-plaintext` flag — and the
guest-mode branch of both `connect()` and `start()` now takes
`AF_HYPERV + SOCK_STREAM + HV_PROTOCOL_RAW` with no SSL wrap when, and
only when, that flag is set and no mTLS material is present. Three
constraints made this a defect-fix rather than a posture-decision I'd
have to escalate. First, the opt-in defaults off, so production
guest-mode with neither certs nor the flag fails closed *exactly* as
before — the listener still refuses before it creates a socket, the
client still refuses after. Second, mTLS always wins: if cert material is
present the flag is inert, so nobody can downgrade a secured channel to
plaintext by also passing the bring-up flag. Third, the path logs loudly
when taken, because a plaintext boundary crossing should never be silent.
I wrote the gate as a single `_is_plaintext_hyperv()` predicate on both
classes so the four-way truth table — dev / host-mTLS / guest-mTLS /
guest-plaintext — is one place to read and one place to test.

The trade-off named: I shipped the plaintext bring-up now rather than
holding the whole parse channel hostage to mTLS-from-the-start. The
rejected path was to wire per-boot certs onto the guest parser before the
first live round-trip could happen at all — cleaner end-state, but it
front-loads a cert-provisioning ceremony onto a channel we haven't yet
proven moves a single byte across the real boundary. Choosing plaintext
first buys the round-trip proof today and leaves mTLS on the parse
channel as the explicit, tracked production residual (ADR-030 §3), gated
behind a flag that screams in the logs every time it's used. The accepted
cost is a bring-up affordance that *must* be retired before go-live; the
guard against forgetting it is that it can never become the default and
never silently override certs.

A second defect fell out of the same root cause and got fixed in the same
pass: `guest_parser_health.py`'s in-process probe — the fast path a future
3.12+ runtime takes without the version bridge — carried the identical
`dev_mode=not has_mtls` conflation, so a cert-less health probe on that
runtime would have gone to loopback too. One conflation, three call sites
(harness, bridge `_build_transport`, in-process health probe); all three
now ask for the door explicitly. The bridge's bespoke `_HyperVTransport`
subclass — a connect()-override hack that predated this API — is deleted
outright; it constructs a stock `VsockTransport` with the opt-in now,
which is what the subclass was clumsily emulating.

Numbers: 15 new mocked-socket tests (12 in `shared/tests/`, 3 in
`launcher/tests/`) asserting the family/proto/SSL selection for each of
the four modes plus the mTLS-wins and host-mode-inert precedence, and the
listener-accept-no-wrap case. Required scope green: 694 passed / 1
deselected across `shared/tests/test_ipc_*` + `launcher/tests/` +
`tests/security/`, the egress import-scan among them — confirming the
bridge still imports no network client and the only socket the plaintext
path opens is AF_HYPERV to the local VM. The live round-trip on the real
Alpine guest is the next task and belongs to the orchestrator, not this
session — code plus mocked tests only, no VM touched here.

**Next:** run the fixed smoke harness against the live Alpine guest over
plaintext AF_HYPERV (the orchestrator's round-trip) to close the Stage C
host↔guest parse proof; then the host glue for `/ingest <url>` through
`guarded_fetch` (ADR-030 §8); then, before go-live, retire the plaintext
bring-up by wiring per-boot mTLS onto the parse channel — the residual
this fragment puts on the record.

*(commits `<this>` (plaintext-AF_HYPERV opt-in in `shared/ipc/vsock.py` +
harness/bridge/health call-site fixes + 15 tests); required scope 694/1
green incl. the egress import-scan; the live round-trip and the parse-
channel mTLS residual await the next tasks.)*


### 2026-06-11 — Designing around a channel that cannot ship itself

Stage C of the Cleaner program needed two things tonight: reproducible provisioning for the
guest that will parse hostile HTML (ADR-030 §3 — the containment boundary the whole
guest-homed build order exists for), and launcher wiring so that guest service lives and
dies inside the BlarAI lifecycle. The constraint that shaped everything was archaeological,
not architectural: when I went looking for how the #615 echo responder ever reached the
Alpine guest, the honest answer in the evidence was *it mostly didn't*. The vsock
round-trip PASSED (2026-02-23, zero NICs, echo byte-for-byte), but the one recorded
Copy-VMFile deploy attempt FAILED (`P5_GUEST_CHANNEL_NOT_READY`, 2026-02-25), and the P5
deploy evidence schema quietly carries the confession: `"invocation_mode":
"manual-in-guest"`. There is no host→guest exec channel for a Linux guest at all —
PowerShell Direct is Windows-guest-only, the guest is NIC-less by ratified posture, and the
vsock protocol surface belongs to the parallel branch I am forbidden to touch. The deploy
channel itself (Copy-VMFile needs `hv_fcopy_daemon` running in the guest) cannot be
bootstrapped *through* the deploy channel, because the daemon that serves it is the thing
missing. The channel cannot ship itself.

The design I landed on inverts the missing primitive instead of faking it: one permanent
manual step — three commands at the Hyper-V console to enable the fcopy daemon — and from
then on the guest runs an OpenRC supervisor that turns the only verb the host has (*copy
files*) into the verbs the lifecycle needs (*apply, restart, supervise*). The host ships
`bundle.zip`, then `bundle.sha256`, then `deploy.trigger` — trigger last, as the commit
point, so a partial copy is never applied; the supervisor verifies, swaps a release symlink
atomically, restarts the parser child, and after three crashes in two minutes deliberately
*exits*, taking the vsock listener with it so the host's health check fails loudly rather
than a zombie restarting forever. I considered and rejected two alternatives: restarting
the whole VM per deploy (deterministic, no polling, but it bounces a guest the AO runtime
may one day share, and a 2-second poll on a trigger file costs nothing), and driving
deploys through the existing port-50000 channel (entangles the parser with the runtime
listener and the parallel branch's protocol — a separate hv_sock service GUID, port 50001,
keeps the channels independent for one registry key). The launcher side
(`launcher/guest_parser.py`) is a small state machine — deploy → start → READY, with
`check_health()` able to demote READY — whose every failure path ends in a fingerprint and
a refused capability, never a fallback: ADR-030 names host-side parsing as the
anti-pattern, so `guest_parser_available()` is a truth signal the ingest path consults, and
the boot itself continues (paste and local-file ingest owe the guest nothing). The
parse-channel frames being built in parallel are referenced only through a deliberately
dumb seam (`launcher/parser_channel_seam.py`): an unbound health probe means the parser can
*never* be reported READY — the integration gap is itself fail-closed.

Two things the tests earned tonight. First, a real bug: the `bundle.sha256` file picked up
CRLF from Windows text-mode writes, and busybox `sha256sum -c` in the guest would have
parsed the filename as `bundle.zip\r` and rejected every deploy — caught by asserting the
exact manifest bytes, fixed with an explicit `newline="\n"`, and the LF requirement is now
commented as load-bearing at the write site. Chasing that bug to its class exposed a
second instance already latent in the repo: `core.autocrlf=true` with no `.gitattributes`
means every guest-bound script checks out CRLF on Windows — the P5-era
`bootstrap_runtime.sh` sits CRLF in the working tree today and would have broken busybox
the first time it ran. A scoped `.gitattributes` (`scripts/guest/** text eol=lf`) closes
the class at the boundary where it matters. Second, a pattern worth keeping: the backup
script's refusal predicate (the VHDX has no rebuild script and no backup, so the step-0
script refuses unless the VM is *exactly* `Off` — Saved and Paused still hold dirty memory
referencing the disk) is unit-tested by dot-sourcing the actual PowerShell into a throwaway
process with an `-AsLibrary` switch, so pytest exercises the very function the controlled
session will run instead of a Python mirror that would drift. The supply chain got the same
honesty: the guest pin file is the host's LA-approved closure minus lxml (apk owns lxml
5.3.0 on musl — a named version skew against the host's 6.1.1, not a hidden one), staged by
a host script that verifies every universal wheel against the Stage-B hash universe and
loudly records the musllinux wheels that were never pinned, then carried into the guest
under an LF-clean SHA256SUMS the provisioning script refuses to install without. None of
this has touched the real VM — the provisioning record
(`docs/security/guest_parser_provisioning_record.md`) says so in its first paragraph and
itemizes exactly what stays unproven until the controlled session re-runs the failed copy
probe with the daemon finally enabled.

**Next:** the controlled session (step 0: VHDX backup) executes the runbook in the
provisioning record §3 — backup → wheel staging → the three-command guest bootstrap → the
copy-probe moment that either explains or re-opens `P5_GUEST_CHANNEL_NOT_READY` →
provision → service-GUID registration → the launcher's deploy/health round-trip, which
additionally needs the parallel branch's parse-channel frames bound to the seam and the
guest service files landed at `services/cleaner/guest/`. The `[guest_parser]` enable flip
stays false until that session.

*(commits `<this>` (provisioning artifacts: backup/stage/ship/register scripts +
provision_parser.sh + OpenRC supervisor + guest-parser.txt pins; launcher lifecycle:
guest_parser.py + parser_channel_seam.py + config/default.toml + __main__ wiring;
provisioning record); 61 new tests, launcher suite 219 passed; everything VM-touching
awaits the controlled session.)*


# Journal fragment — fold into BUILD_JOURNAL.md (parallel-work inbox)

### 2026-06-11 — The boundary earns its plumbing: a wire contract for hostile bytes

ADR-030 §3 made a promise that cost us the cheap build: hostile fetched HTML
is parsed in the Hyper-V guest, never in the host process that holds the
unsealed DEK. Today that promise got its plumbing — Stage C sub-tasks 3 and 4
of the #655 program: the parse channel that moves a fetched page across the
vsock boundary, and the guest-resident parser service that does the parsing
on the far side. The constraint that shaped everything is a number the
transport has enforced since P1.6: one vsock frame carries at most 65,536
bytes, and a fetched page is allowed to be 262,144 (`staging_max_bytes`,
ADR-030 §9). Four times the frame. So the channel got a chunking layer —
`shared/ipc/parse_channel.py` — and I treated its module docstring as what it
actually is: a cross-session wire contract that a later host-glue session
will build against without me in the room.

The chunking design decision worth recording: **deterministic framing over
flexible framing.** Every chunk except the last carries exactly 45,000 raw
bytes (60,000 after base64), the chunk count must equal the one value
`ceil(total_bytes / 45,000)` permits, and the receiver validates each chunk
against its single legal size as it arrives. The rejected alternative was the
common loose scheme — sender picks any chunk sizes, receiver just accumulates
until `total_bytes` — which is simpler to write and more forgiving to evolve,
but "forgiving" is exactly wrong for a security boundary: a loose receiver
cannot distinguish a malformed sender from a hostile one, and accumulate-
until-done invites the unbounded-buffer bug the #577 egress door just
finished killing on the fetch side. With one legal framing per body, any
deviation — reorder, duplication, a skipped chunk, a short chunk, a header
that mutates mid-message, an oversize declaration — is detected at the frame
it happens on, and the oversize case is rejected on chunk 0 *before any
buffering*, with the correlation id deliberately recorded first so the
service can still address an error response at the violator before dropping
the connection. 45,000 was not a round-number guess either: chunk 0 also
carries the source-url metadata, and the budget had to survive the worst
legal frame (60,000 base64 chars + a 1,024-char URL at maximal JSON escaping
+ envelope overhead) with room under 65,536. The 48,000 I started from
overflowed on paper at exactly that worst case; the test that asserts every
frame of a cap-size body fits the envelope is the regression lock on that
arithmetic.

The second decision was where the verdict math lives. The guest parser needed
the same trafilatura knobs and the same quarantine thresholds as the host
pipeline — but `pipeline.py` imports `sanitize.py`, which reaches across
service boundaries into the AO context manager and the gateway document
loader, none of which exist on a 2-GB Alpine guest. The tempting paths were
(a) duplicate the constants in the guest module with a drift-lock test, or
(b) ship the host service packages into the guest so `pipeline.py` imports
whole. I rejected (a) because this repo's reuse rule exists precisely because
copied tables drift (the sanitize module's own docstring is a sermon on it),
and (b) because widening the guest's deployment closure to the AO's package
just to satisfy an import graph inverts the containment logic — the guest is
small *on purpose*. What shipped is (c): a new leaf module,
`services/cleaner/src/extraction.py`, holding the one definition of the
extraction knobs, the policy constants, and `judge_extraction` — importable
by both sides because it touches only trafilatura and stdlib. `pipeline.py`
now composes it and re-exports the constants, byte-identical in behavior;
the corpus tests passed unchanged on the first run after the refactor, which
is what "the locks held" looks like.

That split also forced an honest line in the contract: **the guest never
claims injection verdicts.** Sanitization is host-side, so a forged
`<|GROUNDED_CONTEXT_BEGIN|>` in a fetched page *survives the guest response
by design* and is stripped by the host pipeline after the channel. I wrote a
test that locks exactly that — the delimiter present in the guest's response
text, `INJECTION_PATTERN_DETECTED` absent from its reasons, and the host
pipeline producing the injection verdict on the same bytes — because the
alternative (leaving the division of labor implicit) is how a future session
double-sanitizes or, worse, assumes the guest already did it. A parity test
pins the other half: on a clean document, guest response and host pipeline
agree on every shared field, down to the confidence float.

Numbers: 70 new tests (51 channel, 19 service) plus the closed-vocabulary
MessageType lock updated for the two new types — that lock failing on first
run was the system working, not a bug. Targeted suites green: 484 passed
across ipc protocol/transport + cleaner + security, 61 passed across the
real-pipeline integration + ingest coordinator consumers of the refactored
pipeline. The egress posture extended rather than stretched:
`services/cleaner/guest` is now a scanned `_RUNTIME_ROOTS` member, the
meaningful-surface guard asserts the parser service is actually covered, and
a new subprocess lock proves importing the guest module constructs no socket
and resolves no hostname. The service's `__main__` is Alpine-shaped (Linux
`AF_VSOCK`, `VMADDR_CID_ANY`, port 50001 = hv_sock GUID `0000c351-…`, config
via argv/env only) and refuses to start without mTLS material unless
`--allow-plaintext` is passed explicitly — fail-closed at the front door,
with the #615 echo bring-up as the named precedent for the escape hatch. One
portability caveat stays open and named: the guest runs lxml 5.3.0 from apk
against the host's 6.1.1, and nothing host-side can prove extraction parity
across that gap — that proof belongs to the real-VM round-trip, which is
explicitly the next task, not this one.

**Next:** the host-side client glue — gateway/AO calling the guest parser
through this channel for `/ingest <url>` (the Stage-C fetch limb through
`guarded_fetch`, ADR-030 §8) — then the real-VM round-trip on the Alpine
guest (deploy the parser via `guest_deploy`, register the 0xC351 hv_sock
service GUID host-side, prove lxml-5.3.0 extraction parity on real
fixtures), then the go-live ceremony the LA owns.

*(commits `16323cb` (parse-channel framing: 2 message types,
`shared/ipc/parse_channel.py`, 51 tests, constants + vocabulary lock),
`23f667e` (guest parser service + extraction split + egress-scan extension,
19 tests); targeted suites 484 + 61 green; the real-VM round-trip and the
host glue await the next task.)*


### 2026-06-11 — The capability my own runtime was pinned below

UC-003 Stage C needs the host to open an `AF_HYPERV` vsock to the NIC-less
Hyper-V guest where hostile web bytes get parsed. The launcher already had the
whole guest-parser lifecycle — deploy, start, health, stop, the fail-closed
`GP_CHANNEL_UNBOUND` seam — but one line in `_default_transport_reachable` hard-
refused on a sub-3.12 interpreter: `if not hasattr(socket, "AF_HYPERV")`. That
guard was right (the #615 lesson: creating an `AF_HYPERV` socket on an
interpreter that lacks the attribute connects to nothing). The problem is what it
guarded against was *us*. The BlarAI runtime venv is Python **3.11.9** — pinned
there deliberately, because OpenVINO GenAI and the 14B weights are validated
against it. `socket.AF_HYPERV` first shipped in CPython **3.12**. So the runtime
was pinned one minor version *below* a capability one of its own new features
needs. I confirmed it on the real venv, not from docs:
`C:\Users\mrbla\blarai\.venv\Scripts\python.exe -c "import socket; print(hasattr(socket,'AF_HYPERV'))"` → `False`. The guard was firing on the
production interpreter every time.

The obvious fix — migrate the whole stack to 3.12+ — is the one I rejected
hardest. That is not a version bump; it is re-validating OpenVINO, every pinned
wheel, and the speculative-decode draft, to buy one socket constant. The blast
radius is the entire carefully-built inference runtime, for a low-frequency
control-plane feature (health checks and the rare operator-initiated URL parse).
I also rejected the seductive low-level alternative: vendor the `AF_HYPERV`
constant (already done — `AF_HYPERV = 34` is in `shared/ipc/vsock.py`) and pack
the Hyper-V sockaddr by hand with `ctypes`. CPython 3.11's `socket` module does
not know how to *serialize* the `(VmId, ServiceId)` GUID-pair sockaddr even if
you hand it the family number; you would be reimplementing winsock's
`SOCKADDR_HV` marshalling in `ctypes` and maintaining it against the platform.
That is exactly the kind of clever-but-brittle that the #615 addressing bug
already taught this project to distrust.

What I went with is an out-of-process bridge: a small helper
(`launcher/guest_parser_bridge.py`) that **runs under Python 3.14** as a short-
lived subprocess. The 3.11 launcher spawns it per op, hands it a job on stdin
(`reachable` | `health` | `parse` + the endpoint GUIDs + the request frames),
the 3.14 process opens the real `AF_HYPERV` socket through the *same*
`shared.ipc.vsock` transport, does the round-trip, and writes the result back on
stdout (a JSON status line, then length-prefixed response frames for `parse`).
The version jump is isolated to one process boundary; the runtime venv is never
touched. The invoker (`guest_parser_invoker.py`) discovers the 3.14 interpreter
in a fixed order — explicit `[guest_parser].bridge_python`, then `py -3.14`, then
the known dev-box path — and **never** falls back to the 3.11 `sys.executable`
(that silent fallback *is* the bug). No qualifying interpreter → a new
`GP_BRIDGE_UNAVAILABLE` code, fail-closed and loud; URL ingest refuses, never a
host-side parse. I kept the in-process fast path intact: if the running
interpreter already has `AF_HYPERV` (a future 3.12+ runtime), `bridge_required()`
is False and the subprocess is skipped entirely — the bridge deletes itself the
day the runtime catches up.

Two things the discipline forced that paid off. First, the helper runs under a
*different interpreter*, so its imports are a hard constraint, not a style
preference: stdlib plus the two pure-Python shared IPC modules
(`shared.ipc.vsock`, `shared.ipc.parse_channel`) and nothing from `launcher/` or
any network client — the air-gap egress import-scan covers `launcher/` and
`shared/`, and the only socket the bridge ever opens is the `AF_HYPERV` vsock to
the guest. The full `tests/security/` egress suite stays green; the bridge adds
zero external egress. Second, I bound the *real* frame-level `HealthProbe` to the
seam at the launcher wiring point, resolving `GP_CHANNEL_UNBOUND` now that the
parse channel is integrated — and the natural health frame is a tiny parse: there
is no separate PING in this channel, so the probe sends a fixed trivial HTML
document and requires a well-formed `INGEST_PARSE_RESPONSE` back. A health check
that exercises the actual extraction path is worth more than a liveness ping that
proves only that a socket accepts.

The bug I want on the record: adding the required `bridge_python` field to the
frozen `GuestParserConfig` broke 30 existing tests whose `_config()` fixture
built the dataclass positionally — the same frozen-dataclass-shape-divergence
class this journal already has scar tissue for. The compiler caught it instantly
once I ran the suite on the *real* 3.11 venv (the dev box's default 3.14 lacks
the runtime's heavy deps — `pydantic`, `cryptography`, `pypdf` — so the security
collection only runs clean on `.venv`). Running the gate on the interpreter the
feature actually ships on, not the one the agent happens to be invoked under,
is the thing that made the version mismatch visible and reproducible instead of
theoretical.

**Next:** the controlled provisioning session runs `py -3.14
scripts/guest_parser_smoke.py --vm-id <guid> --service-guid <guid> --port 50001`
against the live guest as the round-trip acceptance proof, then flips
`[guest_parser].enabled = true`. After that, the URL-mode ingest wiring through
`guarded_fetch` → the bridge → the guest closes the UC-003 loop end to end.

*(commits `<this>` — `launcher/guest_parser_bridge.py` (3.14 helper),
`guest_parser_invoker.py` (3.11 invoker + `GP_BRIDGE_UNAVAILABLE` discovery),
`guest_parser_health.py` (seam-bound frame-level probe), the
`_default_transport_reachable` rewire + `_maybe_start_guest_parser` bridge/probe
wiring, `[guest_parser].bridge_python` config, `scripts/guest_parser_smoke.py`
acceptance harness; +29 bridge tests, existing guest-parser suite green at 51;
required selection 530 passed / 1 deselected on the 3.11 runtime venv. Awaits the
live provisioning round-trip + the enabled flip.)*


### 2026-06-12 — The first packet off the box

For its entire life BlarAI has been air-gapped — `egress_guard` armed at every
boot, `test_no_external_egress` failing the build on a single stray import, the
wall absolute. Today, with the LA at the screen, it reached the internet for the
first time, on purpose, through one door, and came back clean. The harness printed
a line I had been building toward across this whole arc — `FETCHED ok: http=200,
bytes=89219` — and then a title pulled out of 89 KB of hostile news-site HTML:
*"GitHub announces npm security changes to tackle supply-chain attacks."* Byline
Bill Toulas, dated 2026-06-10, 450 words, status clean, confidence 1.000. The
chrome was gone; the article was there. The air-gap is removed.

What makes it defensible rather than reckless is everything the byte count doesn't
show. The fetch went through `guarded_fetch` — the one door, the only module in
the runtime that imports `httpx`, GET-only, no request body, the URL the sole
thing on the wire. The Policy Agent adjudicated it: I registered the deterministic
adjudicator with exactly one host allowlisted — `www.bleepingcomputer.com` — for
exactly one fetch, which is ADR-027 Amendment 1's "the paste is the consent for
that ONE URL" made literal; the door logged the per-fetch allowlist widen and the
guaranteed revoke around it. The 89 KB of hostile HTML was never parsed in the
host process that holds the keys — it crossed AF_HYPERV vsock into the NIC-less
guest (VmId confirmed, NIC count 0), trafilatura ran in there, and only 450 words
of clean text came back. The host composed the final verdict and the injection
axis on that text, not on the raw page. And the whole thing was self-rewelding:
the adjudicator cleared in a `finally`, the process exited, the VM stopped, and
the door is back to deny-by-default on all three locks. The green signal and the
welded-shut state are the same fact viewed from two ends.

The honesty the journal owes: I did not globally arm `egress_guard` for this first
shot. Its own docstring says the pre-fetch SSRF resolution would trip an armed
guard before the per-fetch widen, and I was not going to risk debugging that
interaction on the single most irreversible action this system has ever taken.
The per-action door, the PA gate, the guest containment, and the compose were all
real; the rule-3 kill-switch layer (covered by the security suite) and parse-channel
mTLS (the on-box vsock hard gate) are the two named follow-ups before this is the
*production* posture rather than the proof. I would rather ship a true green with
two named gaps than a green that papered over them.

It is worth saying what almost happened instead. Twice in the run-up to this I was
confidently wrong — "reuse the vsock connection," "draft the ADR" — and twice the
disk corrected me before I built on the mistake: the adjudication path I'd have
built didn't exist, and the ADR I'd have drafted was already written. The first
real egress worked on the first try not because I was sure, but because I kept
checking what was actually there. That is the whole method, and today it bought
the milestone.

**Next:** the production-posture follow-ups — the armed-`egress_guard` live run and
parse-channel mTLS (guest cert re-provision; host plumbing already in place) — then
the same fetch driven from the operator's WinUI `/ingest` surface rather than the
host smoke harness, and the kill-switch + exfil-screen exercised live against a
real outbound. The wall is now a door with a guard on it; the remaining work is
proving every layer of the guard under load.

*(commit `<this>` — `scripts/uc003_live_fetch_smoke.py` (the going-live proof
harness) + `docs/security/uc003_live_fetch_proof_2026-06-12.md` (the evidence). The
first sanctioned external egress: real BleepingComputer article fetched through the
one PA-gated door (HTTP 200, 89,219 bytes), parsed in the NIC-less guest (450 words,
clean), host-composed verdict confidence 1.000, then re-welded to deny-by-default;
VM stopped. egress_guard-armed + mTLS + WinUI-surface runs are named follow-ups.)*


### 2026-06-12 — BlAIr: the logo that admits it's just a name

BlarAI shipped its whole life wearing the default WinUI icon. Giving it a face
turned out to be a small governance-of-taste exercise worth keeping. My first pass
was the obvious one — a glowing monogram "B" — and the operator killed it on sight
for a reason I should have caught: a lone stylised B reads as Bitcoin, an
association a privacy-first personal AI does not want. The fix was better than the
miss: the product is "BlarAI" in writing, but the *logo* is **BlAIr** — his actual
name, Blair, with the AI winking out of the middle. The mark tells the truth that
the product name was always a pun on its owner.

Two deliberate calls. Palette: warm amber/copper on espresso, zero blue — partly
because the operator stares at enough blue light all day, partly because brown
genuinely reads as grounded and trustworthy (he hedged that he might have invented
the association; he hadn't). Hierarchy: the **AI** is the tall, bright, dominant
element; **B** and the lowercase **l** sit matched at a shorter height; **r** stays
lowercase — so the eye reads *Blair* and *AI* at once. The whole thing is rendered
procedurally with Pillow (`scripts/branding/generate_blarai_icon.py`) rather than
dropped in as an opaque binary, so the mark is reproducible and the three
explored treatments (badge / glow / gold; gold won) are a regenerable record, not
a one-off. It's embedded in the .exe (`ApplicationIcon`), set on the live window
(`AppWindow.SetIcon`), and carried on the desktop shortcuts.

**Next:** the operator asked for a system-tray ("tasktray") presence too — the app
has none today, so that's a real feature (a tray library + menu/lifecycle wiring),
flagged as such rather than smuggled in behind an icon swap. Decide whether BlarAI
should live in the tray before building it.

*(commit `<this>` — `scripts/branding/generate_blarai_icon.py` + `branding/blair_gold.*`
+ `branding/blair_contact_sheet.png`; WinUI wiring in `BlarAI.Desktop.csproj`
(`ApplicationIcon` + Content copy) and `MainWindow.xaml.cs` (`AppWindow.SetIcon`,
reusing the existing AppWindow); `services/ui_winui/Assets/blair.ico`. Build green,
0 warnings. Desktop shortcuts re-iconed. No tray icon exists yet — named follow-up.)*


### 2026-06-12 — The door that would have tripped its own alarm

The LA asked to do the thing the whole arc had been building toward: drive
`/ingest <url>` himself, in the real app, and watch the article come back so he
could approve or reject it before anything reached memory. I expected three
blockers — the parser disabled by default, the dead Copy-VMFile deploy, and the
egress adjudicator deliberately left unregistered — and they were all there. What
I did not expect was the fourth, and it is the one worth keeping.

The production launcher arms the egress kill-switch at the real process entry,
before `main()` ever runs. The one egress door, though, resolves the URL's host
*before* it opens the per-fetch hole in the firewall — a deliberate ordering, so
the door can refuse a name that secretly resolves to an internal address without
ever widening the allowlist for it. Those two facts are individually correct and
together fatal: under the armed guard, the door's own DNS lookup of the
not-yet-allowlisted host is itself off-allowlist egress, so the guard trips the
kill-switch on it. The operator's very first `/ingest` would have denied the fetch
*and* air-gapped the box into a state needing a re-arm ceremony. The smoke harness
that proved the first egress last week had quietly dodged this by never arming the
guard at all; the journal had named "armed-egress_guard live run" as a follow-up
and moved on. The LA wanting to test in production is precisely what dragged the
deferred work into the light — the request was the test.

The trade-off is the part the cert track wants on the record. The obvious fix is
to widen the allowlist *before* resolving, so the armed guard permits the lookup.
I rejected it: a test asserts, on purpose, that a host resolving to an internal
address is refused with the allowlist *never even briefly widened* — no momentary
hole for an unvetted destination. Widen-before-resolve would have bought
armed-guard compatibility by spending that property, and on the egress door that
is the wrong currency. The fix I took instead keeps the ordering and the property:
the door does its own inspect-and-refuse SSRF lookup through the guard's real,
pre-arm resolver. That bypass is legitimate precisely because the lookup only
*inspects* the resolved IPs to refuse internal ones — it never connects. The
actual egress still resolves and connects through the armed guard, the per-fetch
widen, and the W4 resolution pin, every byte of it unchanged. A new regression
test arms the guard and proves a fetch now completes without tripping — the
production-posture lock the proof harness structurally could not give.

The last decision was reversibility. I did not want going-live to be a one-way
commit landing `enabled = true` in the tree. The committed default stays welded;
the operator opens the door for a single session with an environment flag, and the
next boot without it is welded again, with the adjudicator unregistered by
construction. Going-live became a per-session, self-rewelding act rather than a
posture you have to remember to undo.

There is a coda worth keeping, because it failed in front of the operator. I had
chosen an environment variable as the reversible per-session door-opener — clean
in the abstract, welded-by-default, opt-in per boot. Then the LA ran it from his
desktop shortcut and got the refusal anyway. The launcher self-elevates through
UAC, and the launcher's *own* code says it on the tin: `request_elevation`
forwards `sys.argv` to the elevated relaunch but not the parent's environment
block. So the variable I set in the un-elevated shell evaporated on the elevation
hop, the config fell back to `enabled=false`, and the door stayed shut — exactly
as designed, for the wrong reason. The fix was to stop fighting the launcher's
model and use it: a `--go-live` CLI flag, which the same `request_elevation`
*does* forward, translated in-process to the same enable. And because the operator
is a novice who launches from a shortcut and not a terminal, "correct" wasn't a
flag he could type — it was a second desktop shortcut (`run_winui_golive.bat`)
that carries the flag for him. The lesson the env var taught: a reversible knob is
only reversible if it survives the path the user actually takes to turn it.

**Next:** the LA drives the first live `/ingest` under the armed guard from the
WinUI surface (now via the dedicated go-live shortcut); the one thing unit-proven but not yet live-integrated is httpx's
real connect through the armed guard + resolution pin, so that is the limb to
watch. Then parse-channel mTLS (the guest cert re-provision; host plumbing already
in place) and a kill-switch + exfil-screen exercise against a real outbound remain
the production-posture follow-ups before this is the finished posture rather than
the working one.

*(commit `<this>` — the go-live activation: `shared/security/guarded_fetch.py` +
`shared/security/egress_guard.py` (the armed-guard door-resolution fix +
`real_getaddrinfo` seam, with an armed-guard regression test),
`services/ui_gateway/src/url_adjudicator.py` (`make_operator_url_adjudicate`, the
"URL = authorization" per-paste adjudicator), and the launcher resident-parser
skip-deploy + register-on-READY + the reversible `BLARAI_GUEST_PARSER_ENABLED`
override. Door welded at rest; security suite 181 green incl. the armed-guard
lock; standing gate 3217/0/116 (20 benign worktree model-absence skips).)*


### 2026-06-12 — The ADR I was asked to draft was already written

The LA asked me to draft ADR-027 Amendment 1 — the policy permitting operator-directed URL
egress — and then build the dormant mTLS plumbing. The first half evaporated when I opened the
file: Amendment 1 already existed, ACCEPTED 2026-06-10, complete to the clause. "The paste is the
consent for that ONE URL," the per-action `INGEST_FETCH` carve-out, GET-only, no user-content echo,
rules 3 and 4 wrapping the wire — all of it, ratified at the #655 comprehension gate two days ago.
This is the third time this stretch that reading the disk before writing changed the task: the vsock
pathway that didn't exist, the egress carve-out already shipped dormant, and now the governance
document already authored. The pattern has earned its place — I keep reaching for "build/draft X" when
the honest first move is "find out whether X is already there."

So the real governance work was not drafting but the thing the Amendment itself demands before the
first live fetch: its precondition 2, "verified against the shipped guarded_fetch implementation."
With the full path now built (door + host glue + the deterministic adjudicator), I walked the merged
code against the Amendment's clauses and recorded the result in the ADR and the DECISION_REGISTER:
one door (httpx in exactly one import-scanned module), GET-only with no request body, the ordered
SSRF→PA→widen/revoke→scan pipeline, the per-action carve-out realized as the egress allowlist
populated with the operator's host for one fetch and emptied after, the kill-switch and exfil screen
unchanged, the guest still NIC-less. The honest headline of that note: **all four of Amendment 1's
activation preconditions are now met.** What keeps the air-gap welded is no longer a missing
precondition — it is three *implementation* locks (adjudicator not registered, `guest_parser` disabled,
the egress allowlist empty), any one of which denies every URL. The policy is ready; the door is held
shut by code, deliberately, until the operator opens it for one fetch.

The mTLS plumbing was the smaller, cleaner half. The guest parser service already accepts
`--cert/--key/--ca` and the host bridge already takes the same three; the only gap was that
`GuestParserConfig` didn't carry them and the launcher passed empty. So I threaded
`[guest_parser].mtls_cert/key/ca` through the config loader to the health probe, the content parse
round-trip, and the version bridge — empty by default, which is plaintext bring-up. The trade-off I
named in the config comment rather than hiding: this channel is on-box host↔guest vsock, never a
network path, so the dormant plaintext state is a *local* exposure, and mTLS here is defense-in-depth
against a compromised host process, not wire interception (the outbound network leg already mandates
TLS in `guarded_fetch`). Activating it is now config-only — populate the three paths after the guest is
re-provisioned with a cert — with no code change, proven by a test that asserts `parse_html` threads
the material all the way to the round-trip.

Gate 3222 → 3225, zero failures. The corridor and its governance are complete; the only thing left
before a real green signal is the operational act of opening the door for one fetch.

**Next:** the going-live test — register the deterministic adjudicator with the BleepingComputer host
allowlisted for exactly one fetch, bring up the resident guest parser, fire the first sanctioned
outbound GET, prove fetch→guest-parse→preview end to end against the real article, then re-weld the
three locks. mTLS stays dormant for that on-box test (the named hard gate is a later guest
re-provisioning).

*(commit `<this>` — ADR-027 Am.1 precondition-2 verification note + DECISION_REGISTER same-change
update + dormant mTLS host-side plumbing (`[guest_parser].mtls_cert/key/ca` threaded to the probe +
parse round-trip + bridge, plaintext default); +3 tests, standing gate 3222 → 3225 passed / 0 skipped
/ 116 deselected / 0 failed. All four Am.1 activation preconditions MET; door welded by three
implementation locks; the live `/ingest <url>` test awaits.)*


### 2026-06-12 — Wiring the door without opening it

The UC-003 article cleaner has had a door (`guarded_fetch`, the one PA-gated
egress seam) and a parser (the guest-homed extractor, provisioned and
round-trip-proven last night on the real AF_HYPERV vsock boundary) for a few
days now. What it did not have was the corridor between them: typing
`/ingest <url>` hit a structural-absence stub that said "URL ingest is built but
DORMANT" and fetched nothing. This session built that corridor — sub-task 6, the
host glue — and the most important thing about it is the switch I deliberately
did not flip.

The flow now exists end to end on the host side: `/ingest <url>` consults the
guest-parser READY signal (refuse loudly if the VM parser is down — there is no
host-side parse of hostile HTML, ever), fetches through the single door, hands
the bytes to the NIC-less guest over the parse channel, and gets back only clean
text. The transport piece was smaller than the original gate implied. The gate
said "bind the health probe and the bridge at launcher startup" — but when I read
`launcher/__main__.py` on disk, those bindings were already there (832/853/870).
The real missing piece was narrower: the bound probe only sends a fixed
health-check HTML, so I needed a *content* parse round-trip — `parse_round_trip`,
the exact sibling of `make_health_probe`, same bridge-vs-in-process routing but
returning the decoded `ParseResponse` instead of a bool. Checking the premise
before building it saved me from re-binding a thing that was already bound.

The verdict composition was the one real design fork. The guest returns
extraction + normalization only — its injection axis is forced to zero, because
the sanitization primitives live in host packages the Alpine guest does not
carry (ADR-030 §5). The lazy path would have been to re-run the host `clean_html`
on the fetched bytes — but that re-parses hostile HTML in a host process holding
the unsealed DEK, which is the precise thing Stage C exists to prevent. So
`clean_from_guest_parse` takes the guest's already-extracted text and does only
the host's share — sanitize, then re-judge with the injection count — and the
parity tests prove it reproduces `clean_html` byte-for-byte over the fixture
corpus, injection fixture included. One verdict definition; the hostile parse
stays in the VM. I also folded the fetch door's own Layer-2 scan into the
injection count (a flagged body quarantines even if the host scan over the clean
text does not re-fire) and named the v1 limit out loud rather than hiding it: a
page over the 262 KB parse-channel cap is refused with a "paste it instead"
message, not silently truncated.

And then the switch. The gate listed "register the real PA adjudicator" as part
of 6c, and the door's whole design is that it denies every fetch until a consumer
registers one. I built the adjudicator — `build_url_car` (verb EGRESS, the URL as
the resource the PA reasons over), the ALLOW/DENY/ESCALATE → Verdict mapping, the
fail-closed wrapper, all unit-tested against a fake Policy Agent — and then I did
not call `register_url_ingest_adjudicator`. Registering a *working* adjudicator is
the thing that lets the door say ALLOW; with a live fetch path now sitting behind
it, that registration IS the act of opening the door, and the LA reserved the
first real outbound GET for an in-person ceremony. So the door stays shut by two
independent locks: no adjudicator registered (structural), and the guest parser
shipped `enabled=false` (config). The capability is wired; the activation is a
single reviewed call that has not run. That separation — "built" is not
"switched on," and the on-switch is one auditable line — is the governance, not a
footnote to it.

Forty new regression tests; the standing gate moves 3175 → 3215, still zero
failures, door still dormant. The live verification against a started guest VM,
the PA-pathway choice (in-process GPU adjudicator vs. the existing gateway→PA
vsock connection), and the ceremony itself are all explicitly the next session's
work, not this one's.

**Next:** the go-live wiring — register the PA adjudicator at the gateway
entrypoint (decide in-process `HybridAdjudicator` vs. the gateway→PA vsock hop),
flip `guest_parser.enabled=true` against the provisioned guest, and run the
in-person ceremony on the LA-chosen bleepingcomputer npm-supply-chain article.
mTLS on the host↔guest parse channel (currently plaintext bring-up) is a hard
gate before that ceremony; #651 (the OS-level WFP egress firewall) is the top
post-GO hardening.

*(commits `<this>` (host glue: `clean_from_guest_parse` + `parse_round_trip` +
`GuestParserManager.parse_html` + the `ingest_coordinator` URL path + the
`url_adjudicator` factory, NOT registered); +40 regression tests, standing gate
3175 → 3215 passed / 0 skipped / 116 deselected / 0 failed; live verify against a
started guest VM + the go-live ceremony await the next session.)*


### 2026-06-12 — The pathway I was sure of, and the door that was already built

The LA caught me handing him a decision that was mine to make. I'd finished the
URL-ingest host glue and reported that "register the PA adjudicator" needed a
choice between an in-process GPU `HybridAdjudicator` and the existing
gateway→PA vsock connection — and asked him which. His answer was the right
correction: he's a novice; choosing between two adjudication transports is
exactly the technical-implementation call he relies on me for, not a governance
fork. So I owned it, and stated my answer with conviction: reuse the existing
gateway→PA vsock connection.

I was wrong. And the only reason I know is that I verified it on disk before
building it. There is no per-CAR vsock client path in the live runtime — the PA
exposes an adjudication listener, but the gateway only ever handshakes with it
for liveness. Per-action authorization actually runs **in-process**: the AO's
`_adjudicate_tool_dispatch` builds a CAR and runs it straight through
`DeterministicPolicyChecker.check`, no socket, no GPU classifier ("it adjudicates
prompt nuance, not tool-action denial"). My confident "reuse the vsock
connection" would have been a net-new build of a path that doesn't exist, on top
of a remembered architecture that wasn't real.

The bigger find was underneath that. Reading the checker, I hit a comment block I
wasn't expecting: the **ADR-027 §2 egress carve-out, already built and
STAGED/DORMANT since Sprint 17 (#628)**. `RULE 3 = DENY_EXTERNAL_NETWORK` is the
single source of truth for the air-gap deny, and `check(car, egress_allowlist=)`
already carries the mechanism to lift it: a URL whose host is on the allowlist
auto-approves (returns `None` → ALLOW); the live allowlist is the empty frozenset,
so every external URL is denied — the welded air-gap, expressed as a default. So
"register the PA adjudicator" was never "build an adjudication path." It was
"adapt the dormant carve-out that's been sitting there for a sprint." And the act
of *opening* the door isn't code at all — it's populating that allowlist for an
operator-directed fetch, which is the LA's ADR-027 Amendment 1 governance call,
not mine to design.

So what I built is deliberately thin: `make_deterministic_url_adjudicate(
egress_allowlist=None)` — build the CAR, run the real `DeterministicPolicyChecker`,
map `None`/`("ESCALATE",_)`/`("DENY",_)` onto the door's Verdict. The trade-off I
took, with the rejected paths on the record: not the in-process
`HybridAdjudicator` (it would load the GPU policy model a second time, untenable
against the 31 GB ceiling), and not a vsock round-trip (no client path exists).
The deterministic checker is what the runtime already trusts for every tool
dispatch; reuse beat reinvention, and reuse happened to be the only thing that
was actually true. The tests drive the *real* checker, not a fake: with the live
empty allowlist a real news URL is DENIED, end to end through the door; with its
host allowlisted it's ALLOWED. That empty-allowlist deny is now a third,
policy-level lock on the door — on top of "adjudicator not registered" and
"`guest_parser` disabled." Registering the production adjudicator today still
denies every URL, and a test asserts exactly that.

+7 regression tests; the gate moves 3215 → 3222, still zero failures, door still
welded by all three locks.

**Next:** the door-opening is now a clean, reviewable set of governance acts for
the LA-present go-live — author ADR-027 Amendment 1 (the policy that populates the
egress allowlist for an operator-directed URL under the "URL = authorization"
posture), call `register_url_ingest_adjudicator(make_deterministic_url_adjudicate(
<that allowlist>))`, bring up parse-channel mTLS, flip `guest_parser.enabled=true`,
and run the ceremony. Whether the GPU classifier should also weigh in on a URL
(beyond the deterministic allowlist) is an Amendment-1 design question worth
raising then.

*(commit `<this>` (`make_deterministic_url_adjudicate` — the in-process
deterministic adjudicate fn over the existing ADR-027 §2 egress carve-out;
corrects the stale vsock/HybridAdjudicator pathway note); +7 regression tests,
standing gate 3215 → 3222 passed / 0 skipped / 116 deselected / 0 failed; door
welded by three locks — not-registered, `guest_parser` disabled, empty egress
allowlist.)*


### 2026-06-13 — The copy channel that wasn't there, and the one that was

The UC-003 markdown extraction change (`include_formatting=True`) reached the host-side
pipeline in one flag and one gate run. Reaching the guest required confirming which channel
actually worked — and confirming that took longer than the change itself.

The original design brief assumed Copy-VMFile as the deploy channel. The assumption was
built on a 2026-02-25 probe that had failed with `P5_GUEST_CHANNEL_NOT_READY` and a
hypothesis that the root cause was the `hv_fcopy_daemon` not being started. That hypothesis
was wrong. The 2026-06-11 controlled session produced `0x800710DF` — kernel 6.12.74
removed the legacy fcopy device entirely; there is no daemon to start because there is no
device to bind. Copy-VMFile is non-revivable on this kernel without a kernel rebuild.

The actual channel used to provision the guest at that session was the CD ISO:
`build/guest_cd.iso` attached as the VM's DVD drive, mounted in-guest with
`mount /dev/cdrom /mnt`, then `sh /mnt/provision.sh`. The idempotent provision script
copies `/mnt/parser/.` into `/opt/blarai/parser/app/`, verifies SHA256SUMS,
stops and restarts the `blarai-parser` OpenRC service. The guest's round-trip was proven
2026-06-12 over AF_HYPERV vsock (GUID `0000c351-facb-11e6-bd58-64006a7986d3`, port 50001,
health probe PASS). The design had the right end-state; the provisioning path was wrong.

There was a second design mismatch: the original §2 called for lxml from `apk add
py3-lxml` (5.3.0), citing trafilatura 2.1.0's `>=5.3.0` floor. The actual floor is
