`>=6.1.1`; Alpine 3.21's apk package would have produced an import failure at parser
startup. The controlled session installed lxml 6.1.1 from pip wheels, same as the host.

The #662 work this session was documentation and ISO maintenance: verifying the channel
from the handoff brief, finding two drifted files in the ISO payload (`extraction.py`
missing the new `include_formatting=True` flag; `vsock.py` drifted from main), syncing
them, regenerating SHA256SUMS (37 entries, LF, binary `*` format), and rebuilding
`build/guest_cd.iso` via `build/guest_cd/build_iso.ps1` (22,675,456 bytes,
`-VolumeName BLARAI_PROV`). The provisioning record was rewritten from the original
"NOTHING executed" posture to the proven-channel posture, with the re-provision runbook
(§3) replacing the Copy-VMFile steps. Gate held at 3238/2/116 throughout — no regressions.

The structural lesson from this arc: a guest deploy channel is reusable infrastructure,
not a one-time detail of a single feature's controlled session. Images (UC-003 STEP 2),
every future parser fix, and any future trafilatura or dependency update all need the same
path. Naming it a ticket (#662), documenting it as a runbook (provisioning record §3),
and making the ISO rebuild a five-step autonomous procedure before LA contact — that is
what makes the channel durable rather than ad-hoc.

**Next:** LA mounts the rebuilt `build/guest_cd.iso` as the VM DVD, runs
`mount /dev/cdrom /mnt && sh /mnt/provision.sh` at the Hyper-V console (~5 min), and
verifies `rc-service blarai-parser status → started`. Next launcher boot with the guest
enabled drives the health probe. A live `/ingest <url>` returning a preview with rendered
headings (`# Heading`, `- list item`) is the acceptance evidence for the full UC-003
STEP 1 corridor. UC-003 STEP 2 (images) and any subsequent guest changes use the §3
procedure — agent-side build then LA ceremony.

*(commit `<this>` (build/guest_cd/ payload sync + SHA256SUMS + ISO rebuild +
provisioning record rewrite); gate 3238/2/116 post-payload, on feat/uc003-markdown-extraction.)*


### 2026-06-13 — The runbook that read clean and lied twice

The plan was tidy: rebuild the ISO, hand the LA a three-line ceremony, watch the
guest pick up `include_formatting=True`. I had even written the provisioning
record's runbook to look complete. The real Hyper-V console exposed two defects
in that runbook within the first two commands — and both are the kind that no
test in the standing gate could ever have caught, because they live precisely on
the host/guest boundary the unit tests mock away.

The first failure landed before the ceremony even started. The LA booted the VM
and it refused with `0x80070005` — access denied — on the ISO. The cause, traced
on the host: rebuilding `guest_cd.iso` *in place* creates a new file, and a new
file does not inherit the per-VM read ACL Hyper-V silently stamps onto media it
attaches (`NT VIRTUAL MACHINE\<vm-guid>:(R)`). My earlier verbal guidance had
said the in-place rebuild needs no re-attach. That was exactly wrong: the
re-attach is not a formality, it is the step that re-grants the ACL. A sibling
session caught and re-granted it with `icacls`; the durable fix is in the runbook
now — re-attach after every rebuild, full stop, with `icacls` as the manual
fallback.

The second failure was mine in the runbook text itself. I had written
`mount /dev/cdrom /mnt`. At the console that returned `Invalid argument` — EINVAL.
I diagnosed it on the host first rather than guessing: the ISO was structurally
valid (CD001 volume descriptor present, size sector-aligned), correctly attached,
ACL now good, VM running. Every host-side fact was clean, which is what told me
the fault was the mount command, not the media. A cold-booted busybox guest does
not auto-detect the CD filesystem; the bare mount relies on a probe that simply
doesn't happen. The fix was to stop relying on detection and name the filesystem:
`mount -t iso9660 -o ro /dev/sr0 /mnt`. That ran, provision.sh completed, lxml
6.1.1 / trafilatura 2.1.0 verified, the parser restarted.

The verification was the part I most wanted to get right, and it forced a real
decision. The obvious proof is to drive `/ingest <url>` and watch a headed
article come back. I rejected it. The egress door is welded shut by three locks
on purpose, and the prior go-live arc had already shown that the very first
`/ingest` under the armed guard is a one-way ceremony — not something to spend on
a formatting check. The structure change is on the *parse* limb, not the *fetch*
limb, so the honest verification is a host→guest parse of a fixture this process
already holds: no `guarded_fetch`, no adjudicator, no `egress_guard`, every lock
untouched. I wrote `scripts/uc003_markdown_verify.py` to do exactly that over the
real AF_HYPERV vsock channel through the production version bridge. It came back
`status=clean`, 212 words, confidence 1.000, with `# Markdown Structure
Verification`, `## Why structure matters`, `**critical warning**`, and three
`- ` list items — every marker the old flag-off code stripped. UC-003 STEP 1 is
now live end to end, host pipeline and guest parser both, proven off the egress
path.

The arc's worth is the gap it named: a runbook can read complete, pass review,
and still carry latent defects that only the real boundary reveals. Two host/guest
seams — the media ACL and the busybox mount probe — are invisible to a test suite
that mocks Hyper-V and never boots the guest. The console is the test those mocks
can't be.

**Next:** UC-003 STEP 2 (images) and every future guest change now ride a *proven*
repeatable channel — edit source, sync the payload mirror, regenerate SHA256SUMS,
rebuild + re-attach (re-grants the ACL), `mount -t iso9660 -o ro /dev/sr0`, run
provision.sh, then `uc003_markdown_verify.py`-style parse-only verification. The
egress door and the #659 locks remain welded throughout; going-online is still the
separate, deliberate LA ceremony it was.

*(commit `<this>` — `scripts/uc003_markdown_verify.py` + the
`docs/security/uc003_markdown_verify_2026-06-13.{md,json}` evidence + the
provisioning-record runbook corrections (mount command, ACL re-grant, verified
status). Guest verified GREEN; standing gate unaffected (script is a host tool,
not gate-selected).)*


### 2026-06-13 — Headings live; one flag, no drama

The ingest pipeline was producing flat text even when the source article had headings, bold leads, and numbered lists. Every section header collapsed to a plain line indistinguishable from body prose. The fix was a single flag: `include_formatting=True` added to the `bare_extraction` call in `services/cleaner/src/extraction.py`.

I had proposed a two-flag combination (`output_format='markdown'` plus `include_formatting=True`) at the comprehension gate, confident the tests would hold. The LA corrected me from a live empirical test before I touched a line: `output_format='markdown'` makes `bare_extraction` return `None` in trafilatura 2.1.0, which `extract_document` maps to `EXTRACTION_FAILED` — every article would have quarantined. The `include_formatting=True` flag alone is the correct knob; it yields `# Heading`, `**bold**`, and `- list item` in `.text` while keeping `with_metadata=True` and everything else unchanged. I ran the standing gate after the LA's correction, not before — that's the right order, and I recorded it as such here. The lesson is that empirical tests on the actual installed version beat reading the function signature.

The gate result: 3238 passed, 0 failures, 2 skips (known non-elevated symlink delta), 116 deselected — clean, and 13 above the last recorded baseline (from further main merges since that figure was written). The corpus substring asserts all held: `"Pat four chicken thighs dry"` still matches in a list item that now reads `- Pat four chicken thighs dry`, and the news article body — which has no sub-headings — comes through as clean paragraphs exactly as before.

One architectural fact worth recording: this change is immediately effective for the host-side pipeline (paste and file ingest). The guest-homed parser (URL ingest, running inside Alpine) has its own baked-in copy of `extraction.py`, so URL-ingest articles are still flat text until the guest code is updated. The channel for that update is not yet confirmed — the go-live session used a provisioning path whose mechanics need to be recovered from the journal fragments, the provisioning record, and the launcher log before the guest step is planned.

**Next:** recover the guest provisioning channel from `docs/journal_fragments/2026-06-11_stage-c-guest-provisioning.md`, `docs/security/guest_parser_provisioning_record.md`, and `launcher/logs/provision.json`; report findings to the LA; then plan the guest re-provision to carry the `include_formatting=True` flag through to URL ingest.

*(commit `<this>` (extraction.py: +1 flag; this fragment); 3238/2/116 gate-green on clean branch, 2:39.)*


### 2026-06-13 — The rebuild that locked the VM out of its own ISO, and the checkpoint that would have undone the cure

Two small operational truths surfaced on the same afternoon, both invisible until
the VM ran in anger, and both worth keeping because they are the kind of thing a
clean runbook hides.

The first: updating the guest parser's code means rebuilding `build/guest_cd.iso`
and re-mounting it in the guest. I had told the headers session it could rebuild
the ISO *in place* and skip the re-attach — the path was unchanged, so why touch
Hyper-V? That was wrong, and the operator's next launch paid for it. Rebuilding the
file replaces it on disk, and the new file does not carry the per-VM ACL grant
(`NT VIRTUAL MACHINE\<vm-id>:R`) that Hyper-V silently adds when it first attaches
a file. The VMMS account could no longer open the ISO, and `Start-VM` failed closed
with `0x80070005`. The launcher did exactly the right thing — refused to boot
rather than limp — but the cause was a permission I had told someone to stop
re-applying. The fix was a one-line `icacls` re-grant (or, equivalently, the
`Set-VMDvdDrive` re-attach I had said to skip, which re-grants automatically). The
lesson: a file path that looks stable to you is a fresh object to the OS the moment
you rewrite it, and the ACLs that made it work do not come along for free.

The second was hiding behind the first. When the failed start raised Hyper-V's
"Automatic checkpoint detected — Revert / Continue / Cancel" prompt, it exposed a
standing hazard: automatic checkpoints were enabled on this VM. The guest is
updated out-of-band by re-running `provision.sh` from the CD ISO; a stray *Revert*
on a lingering checkpoint would have silently rolled the guest back and undone a
re-provision with no error at all — the worst kind of failure, the one that looks
like success. The cure was not to revert anything: a clean launcher stop had
already merged the open checkpoint *forward* into the base VHDX (preserving the
current state, not discarding it), so disabling automatic checkpoints left a single
consolidated disk with no revert target. The footgun is gone, not deferred.

**Next:** #662 owns the full guest-update runbook (the ISO ACL re-grant is recorded
there as load-bearing); the disabled-checkpoints state is documented in
`vm_lifecycle.md` §6. STEP 2 (image ingest) rides the same proven channel.


### 2026-06-13 — Sizing the parser VM, and the maximum that costs nothing

The BlarAI-Orchestrator guest does exactly one job — run the NIC-less trafilatura
parser for UC-003 URL ingest. Everything that matters for answering the user (the
14B, the VLM, voice) runs host-side, so the 2 GiB I had pinned to that VM was 2 GiB
taken from the same 31.323 GB the model competes for. The task was to hand the idle
share back. I switched it to Hyper-V Dynamic Memory — floor 512 MB, startup 1 GB,
ceiling 2 GB — and measured what the parser actually needs before trusting it.

The measurement nearly lied to me, and the shape of the lie is the keeper. My first
read of the running guest — a twenty-second idle window straight after the parser
went READY — showed MemoryAssigned pinned at the 1 GB startup and MemoryDemand
reading a flat zero. That is, byte for byte, the signature I had been warned to watch
for: balloon driver absent, guest unable to give anything back. I had built the run
with a sustained sixty-second parse burst rather than a single parse precisely
because a 248 KB parse finishes in about 0.2 s while Hyper-V's demand metric updates
on a multi-second cadence — a lone spike is invisible to it. That same design is what
rescued the conclusion. By the burst phase the balloon had woken, reclaimed the
assignment from 1024 MB down to 512 MB (only ballooning can drop a guest below its
startup, so the driver was there all along — just ~60 s slow to engage), and reported
a real demand of ~199 MB idle, ~256 MB under continuous near-cap load. The early
snapshot was not the failure mode; it was the transient before the balloon engaged,
and the longer window outlived it. 195 consecutive near-cap parses ran with zero OOM,
and the parser reached READY 34.5 s into the cold boot, well inside the 120 s budget.

The trade-off I had expected to weigh dissolved in front of the data. I came in with a
plan to "measure, then maybe tighten the 2 GB max down." But Dynamic Memory sizes the
assignment from demand-plus-buffer clamped to the floor — and the measured peak
(256 MB) sits so far below the 512 MB floor that the guest floats at 512 MB no matter
what the ceiling says. The max is a number never reached, so lowering it would reclaim
nothing real and only strip the headroom that protects a future heavier parse. Keeping
it at 2 GB is free. The whole reclaim — 2048 MB → 512 MB, about 1.5 GB back to the
host — comes from the floor, not the ceiling. The config I had been asked to apply was
already the optimum; the measurement's job turned out to be confirming it and proving
the max costs nothing, not finding a tighter one. No code changed: `vm_manager` only
starts and stops the VM, so the `Set-VMMemory` definition persists across every launch.

**Next:** the VM is Off with the Dynamic Memory definition persisted — handing it back
to the headers/structure session for its live `/ingest` work. If a future parser ever
carries a heavier resident stack (a larger extraction model, OCR), re-run
`scripts/measure_guest_parser_memory.py` and re-check the floor; the ceiling will not
need touching until idle demand climbs toward 512 MB.

*(commit `<this>` (#661): `Set-VMMemory` DM 512/1024/2048 on BlarAI-Orchestrator;
`scripts/measure_guest_parser_memory.py` + `docs/performance/guest_parser_memory_2026-06-13.json`;
`PERFORMANCE_LOG.md` 2026-06-13 entry; `vm_lifecycle.md` §5. VM measured, then returned
to Off.)*


### 2026-06-14 — The screen lied while the audit chain told the truth

The editable ingest preview (#663 Workstream A) shipped, compiled clean, and was
honestly described as needing an LA live-verify because the `@winui` tier sits
outside the headless gate. That caveat earned its keep almost immediately: the
edit-before-approve flow had a display bug that no amount of green Python could
have caught. An operator opens the preview, trims the advertisement out of a good
article, clicks "Done editing" — and the rendered preview snaps back to the
*original* streamed body, ad and all. It looks like the edit was thrown away.

The reassuring half of the story is that it was not. Approve reads `EditableBody`,
the TwoWay-bound edit buffer; the structured `ingest_decide` channel carries that
buffer, and the AO's keyed audit chain (`|edited=1|clean=<keyed16>` + the
`car_hash` over the edited body, ADR-029) attests the curated text that actually
got stored. So the data was correct end to end the whole time. What was wrong was
purely the pane the human looks at to decide. That gap — between what the system
*did* and what the screen *showed* — is the most dangerous shape a bug can take in
a human-in-the-loop approval surface: the operator's trust is calibrated against
the display, and here the display contradicted the truth.

The root cause was a field divergence I should have seen when I wrote the feature.
There are two markdown carriers for the same article. `Text` is what the
read-mode `controls:MarkdownBlock` binds (`MainWindow.xaml` ~line 279); it
accumulates the streamed preview body token by token. `EditableBody` is what the
edit `TextBox` binds TwoWay (~line 285); it is the operator's working copy. The
`IsEditing` setter in `MessageItem.cs` toggled the two visibilities — edit box
on, render off, and back — but on the true→false transition it never folded the
working copy back into the rendered field. So "Done editing" hid the box and
re-revealed a `MarkdownBlock` still bound to the *unedited* `Text`. Two fields,
one of them stale, and the visibility toggle papered right over it.

The fix is four lines in the setter: capture the leaving-edit transition
(`_isEditing && !value`), and when it fires on an ingest preview, assign
`Text = _editableBody` through the existing `Text` setter (which already raises
`Text`/`TextVisibility`/`PlayVisibility`) before the visibility notifications go
out. Both fields are markdown source for the same article, so the copy renders the
edits faithfully — the read-mode pane now shows exactly what the operator typed,
and exactly what Approve will send. I deliberately did *not* touch the Approve
path or the edit-mode seeding: Approve was already reading the right field, and
the bug was display-only, so the smallest honest change is the display-only sync.
The setter is the right home rather than the `OnIngestEditToggle` click handler,
because putting it in the setter guarantees the sync on *every* true→false
transition regardless of who flips the flag.

The trade-off worth naming: I copy the buffer on Done-editing rather than making
the read-mode `MarkdownBlock` bind `EditableBody` directly. Binding the render to
`EditableBody` would also fix the symptom, but it would couple the steady-state
display to the edit buffer for every assistant turn and re-render the markdown on
every keystroke while the box is open (UpdateSourceTrigger=PropertyChanged) — a
worse fit than a single fold-back at the moment the operator says they are done.
Copy-on-commit keeps the streaming render and the editable render cleanly
separate and syncs them at exactly the one transition that matters.

I cannot visually confirm this. The proof that the rendered pane now shows the
edited body is an LA `@winui` live-verify in the running app — paste an article
with an ad, trim it, click Done editing, and confirm the render is the trimmed
text (then Approve and confirm `kb_status.py` stores the same). The headless
`dotnet build -r win-x64` is 0 warnings / 0 errors, which proves it compiles, not
that the pixels are right. That is the same boundary that let the bug ship in the
first place, and it is worth saying plainly rather than implying coverage I do not
have.

**Next:** LA live-verify the Done-editing render in the running app. The two
other tracked #663-A follow-ups are still open and were left untouched to keep
this branch focused — session-switch dropping the preview action buttons, and the
stale "/approve" hint text. Workstream B (display-only images) remains the larger
next step.

*(commit `<this>` (#663 Workstream A follow-up: Done-editing display sync —
`MessageItem.IsEditing` folds `EditableBody` into `Text` on true→false for ingest
previews); WinUI compiles 0/0 `dotnet build -r win-x64`; display-only, no
backend/Python change; LA `@winui` live-verify pending.)*


### 2026-06-14 — The images that may never load: building a feature welded shut

UC-003 Workstream B is "display-only images" — when the operator ingests an
article, snapshot its content images once, store them encrypted locally, and
render them inline in the preview they already curate. The whole thing had to be
built **dormant**: not one live image byte fetched, behind the three egress weld
locks plus a new fourth (`[knowledge].images_enabled=false`). The interesting part
of this entry is not that I built it. It is that I built a security feature whose
entire job, for now, is to refuse to do anything — and that the test that proved
it works is the test that proves nothing happens.

The handoff warned about "GAP A": the staging file is UTF-8 text and the
host↔guest parse channel is text-only/256 KB, so image bytes can't ride either —
a new binary transport was supposedly non-optional, multi-part code. Grounding it
on disk reshaped the gap into something smaller and cleaner. The guest emits
`![alt](url)` *text* refs (trafilatura `include_images=True`); the bytes never
cross the vsock channel at all. The binary transport is host-internal: fetch each
ref through a binary sibling of the one egress door, write a per-image encrypted
staging blob, rewrite the inline ref to a local `blarai-img://<id>` scheme, and
carry only metadata on the submit frame. That is the proven text-staging handoff,
mirrored. The "non-optional new transport" became `image_staging.py` next to
`ingest_staging.py` and an additive `images` field on `INGEST_SUBMIT`. Naming the
real shape before fanning out the build mattered: six file-disjoint modules
(door, transport, storage, cleaner, WinUI, config) went out in parallel against a
pinned contract and came back composing on the first integrated gate run, 0 fail.

Two design calls are worth keeping. First, the rewrite has to happen **before**
the preview composes, not after — otherwise the operator edits text with remote
URLs while the store holds local refs, breaking the #663-A "what you see is what's
stored" promise. So `_stage_and_submit` now mints the doc id, runs the image
corridor, and hashes the *rewritten* text; the preview, the editable body, and the
content hash are all the same bytes. Second, the display-only invariant is not a
policy, it is a structural lock: image bytes can only become unsafe if they reach
the embedder or a VLM, so the regression lock is a fail-closed type assertion at
the two `_embed` call sites — `embed() received non-string` raises. The
dormancy-weld and no-VLM review dimensions later found nothing, which is the
result you want from the locks, not from luck.

The failure I want on the record is the alt-text escape. trafilatura does not
escape image alt-text, so a hostile page can write `![x](javascript: alert)` and
break out of the markdown. The cleaner grew an always-on `escape_image_alt` pass —
*always-on*, not gated by the dormancy locks, because the refs sit in text the
operator reviews and WinUI renders today regardless of whether images are ever
fetched. The build passed. The leaf-module tests passed. The integration tests
passed. Then the seven-dimension adversarial review reproduced, end to end, that
`![x](javascript: alert)` — with a single space after the scheme — sailed through
both escape passes untouched, because the escaper's URL class stopped at
whitespace (`[^)\s]`) while the WinUI renderer's did not (`[^)]`), and the
renderer then built a navigable `javascript:` hyperlink with no scheme filter. A
comment in the escaper *claimed* it saw "the same boundaries the renderer sees."
It did not. A parity claim in prose is not parity in code. The fix was two regex
characters to make the escaper's grammar match the consumer's exactly, plus a
defense-in-depth http(s)-only allowlist on the renderer's link `NavigateUri` — but
the lesson is the green checkmarks. Three layers of passing tests did not catch a
one-character bypass of the one always-on security control; an adversarial reader
told to *refute* the contract did, in minutes.

And then I got to repeat the lesson on myself. The same review flagged that the
binary-door refactor had quietly changed the text decode path — it dropped httpx's
never-None utf-8 fallback, so a page with a garbage header charset *and* a
conflicting `<meta>` would now decode differently. I took the review's suggested
one-liner (`_charset_from_content_type(ct) or "utf-8"`) and wrote a regression
test reproducing the exact pathological body first. The test failed. The fix was
wrong: `x-unknown-garbage` is a present-but-unknown charset, so it is truthy and
`or` never fires. httpx's rule is "declared-if-*known*, else utf-8," and since the
decoder already tries the declared charset as candidate one, the correct fix is to
pass a literal `"utf-8"` as the fallback candidate. Writing the failing test before
trusting the patch is the only reason the fix-of-the-fix exists.

What ships is welded by four independent locks, any one of which refuses images on
its own; the corridor logic is built across every layer so it is not a design
stub, and gated so nothing runs — though enabling it is the whole go-live
ceremony, not a single flip: the gateway still defaults the flag off and does not
read it from config yet (a named residual). The standing gate moved 3288 → 3461,
zero failed, including twenty tests whose pass condition is that an exploding fetch stub
is *never called* and a `store_image` spy *never fires* while dormant. Two latent
correctness defects the review caught (a kept `blarai-img://` ref collapsing on a
mixed-ref edit; image at-rest AAD binding only the image id, not the parent doc)
are fixed with regression locks even though neither can fire until go-live —
because "dormant" is not a reason to ship a wrong at-rest contract.

The LA's call on consent is the governance spine of this (ADR-032, recorded on
#663 c.1088): danger is the technical controls' job — content-type allowlist,
magic-byte sniff, SVG-refuse, caps, display-only — not the operator's; a hostname
is not something a human can judge for safety. So consent is coarse and about
egress/privacy only: same-host images load under the article's own consent,
off-host hosts get one yes/no for the whole article, listed for disclosure, never
a per-host vetting chore.

The guide's independent review then caught what my seven dimensions had not — and
the miss is instructive. My review asked, of every escape, "can something dangerous
*survive*?" It never asked "does something benign get *destroyed*?" So it found the
whitespace bypass (dangerous-survives) and missed that the same escape pass was
rewriting every legitimate relative / `#anchor` / `mailto:` *link* in pasted and
re-cleaned article bodies to `about:blank#blarai-blocked` — live silent corruption,
because the neutralization was keyed to "not absolute http(s)" instead of to the
dangerous scheme itself. A sanitizer has two failure directions and a review that
only walks one of them is half a review. The guide also caught two doc-honesty
overreaches — "sub-32px dropped" (a defined-but-unused constant) and "wired
end-to-end / a single flip" (the gateway never reads the flag from config) — both
re-cast as the named go-live residuals they are. And re-running the *full* gate, not
just the touched subset, surfaced a one-in-256 flake of my own: a corrupt-blob test
that tampered the GCM tag by overwriting its last byte with `\x00`, a no-op exactly
when that byte was already zero. Determinism is not optional in a regression lock.
Then the guide re-reviewed the link fix itself and found *it* had opened a new
hole: my "scheme-less is benign" branch let a control-obfuscated active scheme
(`java\tscript:`, a leading-`\x00` `javascript:`) pass, because the scheme regex
fails to match across the control char — yet a WHATWG-normalizing renderer strips
exactly those characters and reconstitutes the active scheme. The same
grammar-mismatch, a third time, now in the *detection* direction; closed by reading
the scheme off a control-stripped probe (what the renderer would see) and failing
closed on the unparseable. A fix to a grammar-mismatch bug is itself
grammar-mismatch-prone — the parity has to be re-checked against the consumer at
every layer the obfuscation can hide in, not asserted once and trusted.

**Next:** the guide session re-reviews this branch and merges (no self-merge). Go-live
is its own LA-present ceremony with its own egress review — register the binary
adjudicator, flip `images_enabled`, re-provision the guest with `include_images`
via the #662 CD-ISO channel, fetch the first image, prove the corridor, re-weld.
Named go-live residuals (dormant, can't fire until then): approve-time promotion
and reject-time cleanup of image rows; the WinUI live-pixel render + on-hardware
visual confirm. Phase 2 (learn-from-edits) is a separate session — and grounding
corrected the brief: the deterministic rule surface it assumes ("the prune_xpath /
phrase list the cleaner already supports") does not exist and must be built.

*(commits `<this>` (UC-003 Workstream B display-only images, dormant: binary door
+ image_staging + knowledge_images + cleaner escape/rewrite + WinUI resolver +
4th weld lock + coordinator/AO corridor; ADR-032) + guide-review must-fix pass
(scheme-keyed link escape; doc-honesty residuals; deterministic corrupt-blob lock);
gate 3288 → 3461 / 0 failed, +173 incl. 20 dormant-posture + review/guide regression
locks; WinUI builds
0/0; go-live deferred to the LA ceremony.)*


### 2026-06-14 — The article I almost had to throw away whole

The pain was small and exact: an article came through the UC-003 (knowledge-bank
ingest) cleaner with a little advertisement riding along, and the only thing the
operator could do was reject the whole good piece because of the one bad
paragraph. Approval was all-or-nothing; the preview was read-only. Workstream A
of #663 makes the preview curatable — trim the noise, approve what is left.

The interesting part was not the UI. It was discovering, by reading the code
rather than trusting the design note, that "approve the edited body" had no
obvious place to live. `/approve` carries `{doc_uuid, decision}` and nothing
else — the article body is fixed at `/ingest` time, written to an encrypted
staging file and stored in `knowledge_docs.content`, and `approve()` only
re-reads that stored row to chunk and embed it. The whole architecture forbids
plaintext on the 64 KB IPC frame, so threading an edited body through the
decision frame was off the table by doctrine. The tempting move was a new
"approve-with-body" RPC. I didn't take it. The existing `submit_pending`
already dedup-REPLACES a pending row on a matching keyed `source_hash`
(DELETE + INSERT), and `approve()` already indexes whatever content the row
holds — so "approve the edited body" is just **re-submit the edited text, then
approve, unchanged.** No new AO/vsock wire verb, no new plaintext path, all on
fully-tested code. The only protocol change is one additive optional field
(`prior_content_sha256`) that exists solely to feed the audit chain.

The trap was in the paste case, and the code had already documented it against
me: a paste's `source_ref` is `paste:<content_sha256>`, so an edited paste hashes
differently, computes a *different* `source_hash`, and a naive re-submit would
NOT replace the pending row — it would mint a second orphan and, if it reused the
old `doc_uuid`, trip the AO's collision guard. The fix is to carry the original
`source_ref` forward verbatim and mint a fresh `doc_uuid`, so the source-hash
stays stable and the prior row is superseded. URL/file refs are stable already,
so the rule is uniform: on an edit, carry the slot's `source_ref` forward, mint
a new uuid. A regression test drives exactly that paste path and asserts the
re-submit's `source_ref` is the original, not `paste:<edited-hash>`.

The third thing I got wrong on paper and right on disk was the re-validation. The
upstream map's synthesis said to "re-run the edited text through the Cleaner
pipeline" — which, for a paste, runs trafilatura extraction. Running an
HTML extractor over already-cleaned markdown would mangle or re-strip the very
content the operator chose to keep. Reading `pipeline.py` settled it:
`clean_text()` is the paste path — normalize + injection-scan +
delimiter-neutralize + a fresh verdict + a recomputed `word_count`, and *no*
extraction. That is the honest, non-destructive re-validator for edited text.
Quarantine still only labels; the operator's approval is the override (the same
posture as `/ingest`), so a re-flag is surfaced in the reply, never a hard block.
That was the Lead Architect's call — advisory, not blocking — and `clean_text`
makes it cheap and truthful at once.

Trade-offs, on the record:
- **Re-submit over a new RPC.** Chose re-submit because it reuses the
  dedup-replace contract and keeps the body on the encrypted staging file,
  never the IPC frame; accepted that every edit mints a fresh `doc_uuid` and
  re-stages (a little more work than mutating in place).
- **Label, don't block, on a re-flagged edit.** Chose to keep the
  approval-override posture consistent with `/ingest`; accepted that an operator
  can approve an edited body the scan re-flagged (they are the curation
  authority, and the flag is surfaced).
- **Audit provenance packed into the free-form `resource` field, not new
  canonical columns.** An edited re-submit records `|edited=1|clean=<keyed16>`
  alongside a `car_hash` that is the keyed digest of the edited body — both
  KEYED (HMAC), never a plaintext membership oracle (ADR-029). Chose the
  free-form pack because promoting them to first-class `AuditRecord` fields
  would version the signed record format and force a `verify()` back-compat
  migration touching every PA and AO record; accepted that the provenance is
  encoded in a string rather than named columns. The chain still honestly
  attests the human curation.

One thing the build did not start with came out of the adversarial pass: the
first cut had Approve and Reject on separate paths, and a *transient* Reject
failure could retire the preview buttons while the document was still pending
gateway-side — a fail-closed asymmetry that strands the operator with no controls
and a ghost slot. The fix unified both into a single `ingest_decide` channel that
returns a `decided` flag: the WinUI retires the buttons ONLY when `decided` is
true (the slot actually cleared), and a transient failure keeps both the buttons
and the slot for a retry. That `ingest_decide` is a new host-local WinUI→backend
RPC method — the one piece of genuinely new wire surface this turn — but it adds
no AO/vsock verb and carries the edited body only as a structured param (never
prompt text, never `sessions.db`).

The edited body never touches `sessions.db`: it rides a structured
`ingest_decide` RPC param, not prompt text, so the #655 labels-only stub holds
on the approve side too. The egress weld (guest parser disabled, adjudicator
unregistered, empty allowlist) and the #659 go-live locks were not touched —
this is host + WinUI only, no guest, no egress, no live fetch.

Verification: the Python backend is green on the standing gate — 3254 passed /
20 skipped / 0 failed in the worktree (the 20 skips are the benign absence of the
gitignored `bge-small` ONNX model; they pass on main), +34 net-new regression
tests across the protocol field, the coordinator edit-then-approve seam (incl.
the paste-trap lock), the gateway approve channel + the one-shot preview-meta
stash, the dispatcher frame attachment + the `ingest_decide` RPC, and the AO
audit edit-provenance. The WinUI surface (Edit toggle + editable markdown-source
box + Approve/Reject buttons) compiles clean (`dotnet build -r win-x64`, 0/0) but
its click-through is an LA live-verify (the `@winui` tier is outside the headless
gate).

**Next:** LA live-verify the edit→approve flow in the running app (paste an
article with an ad, trim it, approve, confirm the curated body is what
`kb_status.py` shows stored). Then Workstream B (display-only images) per the
#663 brief — which needs a guest re-provision through the proven #662 CD-ISO
channel and its own egress review. Phase 2 (learn-from-edits) builds on this
turn's edit-capture seam.

*(commits `<this>` (UC-003 #663 Workstream A: editable preview / edit-before-approve —
backend + WinUI); +29 tests, standing gate 3249/20/116 green in-worktree; WinUI
compiles, LA live-verify pending.)*


### 2026-06-14 — The knowledge bank that could only be written to

Two controls I would defend separately, each correct on its own terms, met
in the knowledge bank and quietly turned it write-only. The LA found it the
honest way: he ingested a cyberattack article, opened a fresh session, asked
the assistant to recall it, and got back *"Response held by the output
validator — LEAKAGE_DETECTED."* You could save anything. You could get
nothing back. For a feature whose entire pitch is "save articles, recall
them later," that is a clean kill.

The collision is worth keeping because neither half is wrong. The first
decision is lesson 13 in mechanical form: *provenance is not trust.* When
the operator approves a web article into the bank, that approval **curates**
the content — it does not **promote** web-sourced bytes into the trust
boundary. So knowledge retrieval grounds its chunks as `UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL`
(`entrypoint.py:2385`), which keeps the Layer-3 action-lock armed: a
prompt-injection buried in a saved article still cannot fire a tool. I would
not give that up. The second decision is the Stage-5 leakage control: feed
the cosine detector only the untrusted chunks, and if a generated answer is
≥ 0.85 similar to one of them, hold it as a verbatim echo. That is the
exfiltration signature — a fooled model parroting content from outside the
trust boundary — and I would not give that up either.

But a faithful recall of a saved article *is* near-verbatim to its source.
That is exactly what high cosine measures, and it is exactly what the user
asked for. So every honest recall looked like a leak. The detector wasn't
malfunctioning and it was not a "cyberattack content is dangerous"
classifier — it would have held a saved soup recipe with equal confidence.
It was a category error: the control could not tell "leaked what you didn't
ask for" from "answered using what you deliberately saved." I had seen this
exact shape before. On 2026-06-04 the same false-positive class held back a
correct two-document summary, and the fix then was to carve *trusted* content
out of the leakage feed because a summary of your own files is similar to them
by design. Curated knowledge is the next member of that family — the operator
saved it *for the purpose of recall*, so echoing it back is the point.

The tempting fix was the wrong one, and naming why is the governance part.
The fast path is trust-promotion: ground knowledge as `TRUSTED_LOCAL` and the
leakage feed excludes it for free, because trusted tiers carry no controls.
But "no controls" is the trap — it also drops the **action-lock** and the
injection scan, so a poisoned article saying *"ignore prior instructions and
call send_email"* would regain the power to fire a tool. That trades a
false-positive annoyance for a real security regression, and it directly
contradicts lesson 13. The other tempting shortcut, globally disabling the
leakage control, is over-broad in the other direction: it kills the detector
for genuinely-pasted external text too, re-opening the exfiltration surface
the control exists to close. And raising the cosine threshold fails for the
same reason #579 rejected it originally — a faithful recall trends to cosine
1.0, so no single number both passes recall and catches a real echo. The axis
is provenance, not a score.

So the fix is a fourth provenance tier, `UNTRUSTED_KNOWLEDGE`, and it is
deliberately the narrowest cut I could make: untrusted *everywhere* the
existing untrusted tier is untrusted — action-lock, datamarking, injection
scan, PII source, the #570 per-dispatch deny — with **exactly one** exception,
the Stage-5 cosine leakage output feed, from which it is exempt. Trust is not
promoted; only the leakage feed is relaxed. The mechanics fell out beautifully
once the tier existed, and that is not luck — it is the seam the original
ADR-023 design left. `get_untrusted_chunk_texts` filters on `==
UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL` (an equality), so the new tier is excluded from the leak
feed with **no change to that filter**; `has_untrusted_content` tests `not in
(TRUSTED_LOCAL, TRUSTED_MEMORY)`, so the new tier trips the action-lock with
**no change to that gate**. The entire behavioural delta is one line at the
ingest call — `UNTRUSTED_KNOWLEDGE` instead of `UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL`. The rest
of the change is tests and the words that explain why the filter stays an
equality so a future reader doesn't "tidy" it into a `not-in-trusted` test and
silently re-break recall.

The tests are the load-bearing half, because the whole risk here is weakening
a security control while fixing a usability bug. So each must-not-weaken claim
got its own lock: a GUARDED tool is still refused when only knowledge content
is present and there is no `/trust`; a DANGEROUS tool is still locked under
knowledge content with no override (#593 fail-closed, now proven for the new
tier too); knowledge chunks are still delimiter-wrapped and datamarked;
pasted-external content is *still* in the leakage feed even when mixed with
knowledge in the same session. And the positive case the fix exists for: a
faithful recall that I rigged to score cosine 0.97 against its source now
**passes** PGOV and is delivered — proven by injecting a mock detector that
*would* fire, then asserting it is never even consulted because the knowledge
chunk never reaches the feed. The teeth test is the same setup with
`UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL` provenance, which *is* held at 0.97 — so the carve-out is
demonstrably provenance-scoped, not a global softening. 156 tests green across
the three touched AO modules; 180 across tools + pgov.

**Next:** Guide-session review + merge to main (this branch deliberately stops
at a committed, test-verified state). After merge, re-run the standing gate to
record the new count, then the real-hardware confirmation the LA cares about —
ingest an article, recall it in a fresh session, watch it come back instead of
being held. The dormant fetch/egress weld (#577/#655/#659) is untouched; this
was AO-side leakage/provenance only.


### 2026-06-14 — The toggle that promises RAM back, and the delete button that was already built

Two pieces of WinUI UX in one focused session (#660): a per-chat delete button,
and two voice toggles that load a model when you turn them on and free the RAM
when you turn them off. The interesting thing about both is what the investigation
found before any code was written.

The delete button was supposed to be "frontend + backend." It was frontend only.
The whole delete path already existed and was already tested — `delete_session`
on the encrypted store with an `ON DELETE CASCADE` that takes the turns with it
(`session_store.py:918`), the `_m_delete_session` RPC (`dispatcher.py:199`), even
the C# `BackendClient.DeleteSessionAsync` (`BackendClient.cs:60`). All wired, all
green, all dormant — there had simply never been a button to call it. So the work
collapsed to a trash icon revealed on row hover, a right-click menu (with Rename
folded in, since `RenameSessionAsync` was *also* built with no UI), and a
ContentDialog confirm. The lesson I keep relearning: read the backend before you
scope the backend. The ticket's "verify a delete path exists; if not, add one"
was answered by the *verify*, not the *add*.

The voice toggles were the opposite — they looked like a one-line "add an unload"
and turned out to need a whole runtime surface. Voice on this project is
boot-immutable: the launcher builds one `VoiceEngine` at startup, reading
`VOICE_ENABLED` exactly once, and injects it into the dispatcher as a single
immutable handle. There was a `load()` and no unload, and nothing anywhere could
mutate the engine after boot. A toggle that loads-on-enable / unloads-on-disable
can't sit on top of that; it needs the engine to grow `load_stt`/`unload_stt`/
`load_tts`/`unload_tts`, the dispatcher to grow `voice_set_stt`/`voice_set_tts`
RPCs, and the launcher to build the engine *path-aware-but-empty* so the models
can come up later without a reboot. I built all of that, mirroring the #611
embedding-cache idle-unload: unload drops the model attribute and runs
`gc.collect()`.

But here is the honest part, and I flagged it to the LA before building rather
than at measurement time: I cannot prove the RAM actually comes back. The #611
precedent freed plain numpy buffers — trivially reclaimable. These are a compiled
OpenVINO Whisper pipeline and an ONNX-Runtime Kokoro session, and whether the
C++/driver layer promptly returns device memory when the Python object finalizes
is a hardware question, not a Python one. What I *could* verify, and did, is that
there is no lingering Python reference that would make the unload a structural
no-op — the dispatcher reaches the model only through the engine, never caching
it, so dropping the attribute drops the last reference. That was the LA's STOP
condition (don't ship a no-op that lies about reclaiming); it doesn't trigger.
The genuine gate moved to a measurement: `scripts/benchmark_voice_ram.py` samples
RSS around a real load/unload and flags any half that returns less than half its
load delta. The harness runs clean against the empty case; the heavy real-model
run is the LA's on-hardware live-verify, and the PERFORMANCE_LOG entry is set up
with the exact procedure and a blank results table to fill — deliberately blank,
because inferring those numbers would be the kind of unmeasured claim the log
exists to prevent.

The third decision that shaped it: always-off-at-boot. The engine is empty at
every launch regardless of the persisted toggle state (which is kept only for
display). That preserves the RAM-safe posture the 100%-RAM-freeze history demands
— the toggles are an in-session convenience, never an auto-load.

Headless: 41 new/changed targeted tests green (engine load/unload cycle +
idempotency + gc-ran assertions; the two new RPCs round-tripping status; the
launcher now building a path-aware boot-off engine). What awaits the LA is the one
thing a headless agent genuinely cannot do — watch the RAM gauge drop when the
toggle goes off.

**Next:** LA runs `scripts/benchmark_voice_ram.py` on the Arc 140V (isolated, then
co-resident with the 14B), fills the PERFORMANCE_LOG results table, and confirms
the reclaim gate passes. If a half reclaims weakly, that escalates to the
killable-subprocess pivot (an architecture decision, deferred by design). The
WinUI `dotnet build` + the @winui tier verify the delete/rename affordances and
the toggle loading-rings on the real surface.


### 2026-06-15 — Finishing the door I welded shut: the headless blockers, and a grammar mismatch's third visit

UC-003 Workstream B — display-only article images — merged a day earlier built
fully dormant behind four weld locks. The LA woke, asked where the blockers stood,
and then: finish the headless ones. Seven items that can land as code without ever
firing the first outbound GET — wire the gateway flag so the config actually gates
the gateway, preserve a kept image across an edit-resubmit, give the image rows an
approve/reject lifecycle, validate forged `blarai-img://` ids at the renderer, lock
the binary door's SSRF + ESCALATE paths with tests, re-validate an image's MIME at
the storage seam, and finally give `MIN_IMAGE_DIMENSION_PX` the consumer it never
had. All of it had to keep the feature welded shut: not one live byte fetched.

The shape decision came first. I grounded all seven against the on-disk code in a
parallel read-only fan-out, then built them in ONE coherent hand rather than the
parallel-builder pattern that worked for Workstream B. The difference is the work:
Workstream B was six greenfield, file-disjoint modules against a pinned contract —
parallel was right. These were surgical edits to interdependent *existing* files
all sharing the `guarded_fetch` contract; four parallel worktrees would have drifted
on the door's public surface. The grounding paid for itself by reshaping the hardest
one. The plan for the edit-approve cascade was to thread a `surviving_image_ids` set
from the host into `submit_pending`. Reading the code showed that `submit_pending`
already receives the edited body as its `content` argument, and the edited body *is*
the survivorship signal — the operator keeps an image by keeping its `blarai-img://`
ref in the text. So the function derives the survivor set from its own content: no
new parameter, no entrypoint wiring, no cross-service import. Naming the real shape
before writing beat the a-priori plan, again.

The build composed and the gate passed green at the build step — zero failed, WinUI
clean, already well above the 3492 main baseline (the full run to 3571 came after the
verify fixes). That is exactly the moment Workstream B taught me not to trust, so the real work was the
five-dimension adversarial verification — and it earned its keep by catching a defect
in each of the two directions a gate can fail. The false-positive (legitimate thing
destroyed): a re-submit of a *rejected* source migrated a kept image with its
`rejected` label carried forward verbatim, and `approve()` only promotes `pending`
rows — so a kept-and-then-approved image was stranded `rejected` forever, a row out
of sync with its own parent. The fix is the semantics I had not thought through: a
re-submit opens a *fresh* decision cycle on a new pending doc, so the migrated state
resets to `pending` and the new doc's decision governs it. The false-negative
(dangerous thing survives): the forged-id gate `^[0-9a-f]{32}$` accepted a
trailing-newline id, because in .NET the `$` anchor matches before a final `\n`. The
live render path masks it — `ExtractImageId` calls `.Trim()` first — but
`IsValidImageId` is a public predicate whose contract reads "exactly 32 lowercase
hex, nothing trailing," and it was lying. `\A…\z` closes it.

That `$` hole is the honest failure to keep on the record, because it is the *third*
visit of one class. Workstream B shipped an alt-text escaper whose URL grammar
(`[^)\s]`) disagreed with the WinUI renderer's (`[^)]`) by one whitespace class; the
fix for that opened a control-char fail-open; and now the forged-id regex was correct
as a *pattern* and wrong as *.NET*. Each time the bug lived in the gap between a
sanitizer's grammar and the grammar that actually consumes its output — and this time
the consumer was the regex engine's own dialect of the metacharacters. I cannot even
claim surprise: my verification prompt explicitly told the reviewer to check the
`$`/newline case, which means I shipped a form I already suspected instead of reaching
for the airtight idiom up front. Pointing the screen at the risk is good — it caught
it in minutes — but reaching for `\A…\z` before the screen would have been better.

Two things I deliberately did *not* decide for the LA. The reject-retention posture:
should rejecting an article *retain* its encrypted image bytes (consistent with the
document text tombstone) or *purge* them? I shipped the reversible retain-default and
named the strict alternative in the code, because purge-vs-retain is a privacy posture
only he should set — and he set it: **DELETE-on-reject**, purging the rejected image
bytes at rest for data-minimization under the privacy-absolute mandate, keeping only
the doc text tombstone and the `INGEST_REJECT` audit record. (A neat consequence: with
images purged at reject, the rejected-source migration corner the verification flagged
can no longer arise — there is nothing left to migrate, so a re-ingest re-fetches fresh
images instead of resurrecting purged ones.) And the C# test harness: the forged-id gate is load-bearing and
ships with zero coverage, because there is no C# test project in the repo at all.
Standing up the first one is a build/CI platform decision, not something to take
silently inside a dormant pass — so I fixed the regex, ticketed the harness (#665),
and surfaced it. The boundary held cleanly: the three genuine defects (the
state-machine desync, the anchor, a "never raises" contract a non-string MIME could
violate) I fixed; the two genuine decisions (retention posture, a new test platform)
I escalated.

What ships is welded shut at rest — the dormancy reviewer proved it with a live repro:
flip *only* `[knowledge].images_enabled` to true and an image fetch still dies because
no PA adjudicator is registered, empty metadata, nothing stored. The standing gate moved
3492 → 3571, zero failed, WinUI builds 0/0. Nothing fetches. Nothing stores. The flag now
genuinely gates the gateway instead of being a lock backstopped by a wiring gap — a lock
that works is worth more than a lock that is bypassed-but-happens-to-be-covered.

The independent Guide review then sharpened a claim I had carried since Workstream B:
"four independent locks." It is not quite true. `fetch_external_binary` shares the ONE PA
adjudicator with the text door, and the live "URL = consent" adjudicator self-populates its
egress allowlist per URL — so the day text URL-ingest goes live (registering that
adjudicator), TWO of the four locks release for images too, leaving the image door held by
`images_enabled` alone. The LA's call (BED-1): a purpose-aware DENY. The shared adjudicator
now refuses the image-ingest purpose outright until a separate image go-live, so the image
path keeps two image-SPECIFIC locks — purpose-deny + `images_enabled` — that survive text
go-live. The honest correction matters more than the lock count: "independent" was doing
work the locks could not actually do, and a sentence repeated unexamined since Workstream B
is how that kind of overclaim ossifies. The four-locks language is now corrected wherever it
lived (ADR-032, this fragment, CLAUDE.md).

**Next:** the guide session reviews and merges (no self-merge). The headless work is
done; what remains is the LA-present go-live ceremony — register the shared PA adjudicator,
lift the image purpose-deny, populate the egress allowlist, flip the flag, #662 CD-ISO guest
re-provision, fire the first GET, prove the corridor, re-weld — plus the #4 set-membership
manifest, the WinUI live-pixel visual confirm, and the #665 C# id-gate tests. Caps =
20/article is the LA's sanity-check at the door.

*(commit `db650ee` (UC-003 Workstream B headless go-live blockers, still DORMANT:
gateway flag wiring + edit-approve image migration + approve/reject lifecycle +
forged-id format gate + binary-door SSRF/ESCALATE tests + store-time MIME
re-validation + sub-32px drop; ADR-032 Am.1 + DECISION_REGISTER; folding in the
adversarial-verify fix pass — .NET `\A…\z` id anchor, never-raise-on-non-str-mime,
+5 test-honesty strengthenings). `018dea3` LA-decision follow-up — DELETE-on-reject:
`reject()` PURGES image bytes at rest (data-minimization; doc text tombstone +
`INGEST_REJECT` audit retained), superseding the reversible retain-default. `<this>`
Guide-review fix pass — **BED-1** image purpose-deny (the shared PA adjudicator DENIES
`uc003-image-ingest` until a separate image go-live, so the image path keeps two
image-specific locks — purpose-deny + `images_enabled` — that survive text URL-ingest
go-live) + 2 doc-honesty must-fixes (the journal baseline; the non-existent "binary
adjudicator" → the shared PA adjudicator) + the four-locks language corrected across
ADR-032 / this fragment / CLAUDE.md. Gate 3492 → 3575 / 0 failed (the +1 coupling-lock test landed after this was written; the 20 worktree
bge-absent skips pass on main); WinUI 0/0; optional doc rewords (SL-2/SL-3/TD-5/FIG-1)
+ C# id-gate tests ticketed (#665 / #663 c.1106); go-live deferred to the LA ceremony.)*


### 2026-06-15 — The door that stayed shut when the wire went dead

Today was meant to be a quiet live-verify: confirm that the two UC-003 fixes
that merged on the 14th actually behave in production posture. They did. In a
fresh session I asked BlarAI about the "Chinese hackers hijack an auth flow,
spy on an isolated network for a decade" article — the exact query the Stage-5
leakage detector had swallowed before the carve-out — and the answer streamed
back faithfully, Operation Highland and the PAM/OpenSSH backdoors intact, with
no LEAKAGE_DETECTED hold. The Done-Editing display sync held too: edit the
preview, click Done editing, and the read-mode pane now shows the edit instead
of the cleaner's original. Both shipped features confirmed against the real,
model-loaded box, and `kb_status.py` showed the curated edit landed as a fresh
approved doc.

But the entry that earns its keep is the accident. Mid-ceremony the operator's
first URL ingest fired with wifi off. The Policy Agent auto-approved the pasted
host (the paste *is* the consent, ADR-027 Am.1), and then the door's own
pre-fetch DNS resolution failed with a `gaierror` — and the door DENIED,
fail-closed, rather than erroring its way open. Wifi came back; the next ingest
widened the egress allowlist for exactly one host:port
(`www.bleepingcomputer.com:443`) and auto-revoked it about a second later, fetch
window closed. I had reasoned about fail-closed on paper for the whole
air-gap-removal campaign. This was the first time I watched it refuse under the
failure it was actually built for — a dead wire in the middle of a live fetch —
and it refused correctly. A green happy-path go-live never exercises that
branch; the wifi-off mistake did, for free.

One bruise stays in. The first cold boot wedged at the prompt-flow preflight.
The 14B generated the self-test response and PGOV approved it (11:25:23), but the
launcher's `async for token in gateway.stream_tokens(...)` consumption loop never
closed, so Step 7 — launch the UI — never ran and no window opened. CPU, GPU,
and disk were all flat for minutes; it was genuinely wedged, not slow. A relaunch
cleared it and the warm-cache boot sailed through, so the working theory is a
cold first-compile timing race rather than a logic regression — but it blocked
the app from opening, which is high-severity if it ever reproduces. Logged on
#659 for root-cause-on-recurrence, with the `stream_tokens` close path as the
first place to instrument.

**Next:** Root-cause the preflight stream-completion wedge if it recurs
(instrument the `stream_tokens` close/end-frame path; suspect cold-compile
ordering). Then the operator's two stated wants — make the cleaner learn from
edits (Phase 2) and turn on display-only images — the latter gated behind its
own image-egress review before any live image GET.

*(Live-verify session 2026-06-15; recall carve-out merge `a96f683`, Done-Editing
fix `67634de`; go-live re-proof recorded on #659, verifies on #663/#664. No code
changed this session.)*

### 2026-06-15 — Hardening the door before the first image comes through it

The UC-003 image limb has been built end-to-end and proven dormant for weeks, but
the controls that have to exist *before* the first real image fetch — the
decode-time safety net and the egress-consent gate — were the gap. Pass A closes
that, still fully welded: every line I wrote this pass is reachable only after a
future LA-present ceremony flips `images_enabled` and lifts the BED-1 purpose-deny,
two edits I deliberately did not make. The whole point of building the defenses
*before* the door opens is that "dormant" is not "safe to defer" — when the first
GET finally fires, it must land on hardened code.

The most interesting design problem was a single threshold that needed two
predicates, split by who owns the fail-closed. The decompression-bomb ceiling
(W1/BED-3) had to be enforced in two places with *opposite* behaviour on an
unmeasurable image. At the coordinator's fetch-time gate, an unreadable header
must DROP — we cannot prove the image is under the ceiling, so we refuse to keep
it (that is also the W3/TD-4 ratification, which inverts Amendment-1 #7's
keep-not-drop posture). But at the at-rest store boundary, the mirror is an
*affirmative* "is this provably too big?" check, and an unreadable header is NOT
provably too big — it already passed the magic-byte gate. So I shipped two
functions: `dimension_above_max` returns False on `None` (the store mirror's
contract), and `image_dimensions_ok` fails closed to drop on `None` (the
coordinator's). Conflating them into one would have forced a bad choice: make the
store mirror fail-closed and every existing `store_image` test breaks (its fakes
carry valid PNG magic but no `IHDR`, so they're "unreadable"); or make the
coordinator gate keep-on-unreadable and leave the exact hole TD-4 was meant to
close. The split is the honest shape, and I wrote the boundary into both
docstrings so the next reader doesn't "simplify" them back together.

CD-1 — that pasted and file ingests fetch *no* remote images, only URL-ingested
articles do — was the decision that quietly rewrote the test surface rather than
just the code. The existing `test_ingest_images.py` drove the image corridor
through paste mode, because that was the only mode wired when the corridor was
first built. Under CD-1 that is now an impossible scenario: a paste never fetches.
The faithful move was not to delete that coverage but to move it to URL mode
(driving the `_finalize_clean` tail directly with `source_type='url'` and a
same-site article host, so images fetch without a consent prompt) and to ADD the
CD-1 paste/file no-fetch tests alongside. The dormant tests, which use
`images_enabled=False`, didn't move at all — the weld short-circuits ahead of
everything, which is exactly the invariant I then pinned with a
dormancy-preservation test: a consenting verifier plus off-site refs plus
`images_enabled=False` still strips and never fetches. That test exists to prove
the new consent seam created no path around the fourth weld lock.

For the consent seam itself I built a dedicated `image_egress_consent` module
mirroring `escalation_consent` rather than reusing the latter's registry. The
trade-off: a parallel module duplicates the registry + timeout machinery, but
off-site-image egress and a PA ESCALATE are two different operator questions with
two different prompts, and a verifier wired for one must never silently answer the
other. Keeping the same-site grain (exact host match, no eTLD+1 yet) isolated in
one function means the registrable-domain upgrade the LA is weighing for Pass B is
a one-line change, not a hunt.

Two things bit and stayed in. The W7 real-resolver test — replacing a tautological
test that re-implemented `bool(knowledge.get("images_enabled", False))` with one
that drives the real `_load_entrypoint_config` — first failed with a
`FrozenInstanceError: cannot assign to field '__traceback__'`, which is what you
get when a frozen-dataclass exception (`ConfigResolutionError`) propagates through
pytest's fixture teardown. The frozen-exception crash masked the real cause: my
hand-written test TOML omitted the required `[generation]` section. The lesson is
the old one — read the *first* exception, not the one the framework re-raises. And
the `_PNG_BYTES` catch: the existing image fakes were `b"\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n"` plus
filler with no valid `IHDR`, i.e. unreadable headers, which the old keep-on-
unreadable posture happily kept. The moment W3 made unreadable headers drop, those
fakes would have silently dropped every image in the success tests. Giving the
fakes real headers was the difference between a green suite that tests nothing and
one that tests the thing.

**Lesson:** *One threshold can need two predicates, split by who owns the
fail-closed.* When the same bound (here, the bomb ceiling) is enforced at a
permissive boundary and a strict one, the "can't measure it" case resolves
oppositely: the strict gate drops (cannot prove safe), the affirmative check
returns "not flagged" (cannot prove unsafe). Don't collapse them into one helper
to look DRY — name both and write the boundary into the docstrings, or a later
cleanup re-introduces the hole.

**Lesson:** *A policy decision rewrites the test surface, not just the
code.* CD-1 (offline ingest ≠ egress) made the old paste-mode corridor tests
encode an impossible scenario. Faithfully move such tests to the now-valid path
and add the new-rule tests beside them; deleting them loses real coverage, and
leaving them green-but-impossible is worse.

**Next:** Pass B is the WinUI side — the per-article consent *prompt* that
registers a real `image_egress_consent` verifier, the `ImageResolver.Resolve()`
decrypt→`BitmapImage` seam, the per-document set-membership id manifest, and the
first-ever C# test project (#665). Then the go-live ceremony itself. The eTLD+1
same-site grain is teed up for the LA before Pass B. This pass hands back unmerged
for Guide review.

*(Pass A on `feat/663-image-go-live-passA`: W1 bomb ceiling + store mirror, W2
truncated-drop, W3/TD-4 unreadable-DROP, W4/PRIV-2 pinned UA, W5/CD-1 off-site
consent seam + paste/file no-fetch, W6 sub-32px restated, W7 real-resolver test,
TD-2 launcher boot-wiring test; ADR-032 Amendment 2 + DECISION_REGISTER row.
Dormant throughout — no image fetched or stored. Standing gate re-run pending;
pre-pass baseline 3575/0/116.)*

### 2026-06-16 — Teaching the box to draw, with the door welded shut

UC-010 is the first capability BlarAI has that was never one of the nine. ADR-015
and the journal's §19 both named image generation an "honest future track" — a
thing we'd want, behind a separate diffusion model, someday. The LA decided someday
was now, and approved the plan with amendments. So this is the build that
deliberately *expands* the canonical vision rather than executing it — and I wrote
that down everywhere it touches (UC-010 §scope, ADR-033, the register row), because
a silent scope expansion is exactly the kind of thing future-me would mistrust.

The shape of the work was a relief: it is the `vlm.py` story told a second time.
A heavy GPU model that can't co-reside with the 14B, loaded on demand, evicted in a
`finally`, fail-soft on every error so an OOM degrades to a notice instead of
freezing the host. I had the template, the eviction discipline (#561's lesson:
hold the VLM and the host freezes), and the born-encrypted store pattern from the
UC-003 image work that landed the same day. The new module is a structural clone
with one addition — a `_model_kind` global, because a diffusion pipeline comes in
two flavours (text2image, image2image) and the budget holds exactly one resident at
a time, so loading the other kind has to drop the first.

Two things made me stop and think rather than type.

The first was the manifest. The whole point of `verify_all_manifest_entries` is
fail-closed weight integrity at load — and I went to reuse it and found it can't
see this model. It globs `*.bin` in the top directory and keys by bare filename.
A diffusers-OpenVINO model puts its weights in subdirectories — `unet/`,
`vae_decoder/`, two text encoders — and the bare name `openvino_model.bin` repeats
across five of them. The flat function would find nothing and collide on the
duplicates. This was a real gap the plan assumed away ("verify via
`weight_integrity.py`"), and the wrong move would have been to either skip the
verify or to bend the locked flat function that the 14B boot path depends on. I
added a sibling — `verify_all_manifest_entries_nested` — that treats manifest keys
as relative POSIX paths and walks the tree, reusing the proven `load_manifest` +
`compute_sha256` primitives and keeping the same extra-file rejection (now
recursive). The flat function is untouched. I'm flagging it in the hand-back as a
deviation-of-necessity rather than pretending the plan covered it.

The second was content safety, and it's the one I'm proudest of resisting. The
instinct on an "uncensored image generator" is to bolt on a classifier so the
project can say it has a guardrail. The LA's decision — and the honest engineering
— is that a *local* classifier at the legal boundary is security theater: there's
no distributable hash database for the one boundary that matters, and a prompt
denylist is bypassable in three different ways while it false-refuses legitimate
prompts. So ADR-033 says the true thing instead: the boundary is named explicitly,
it's the operator's sole documented responsibility, it's a recorded ACCEPTED-RISK,
the control is governance plus a one-time go-live attestation, and the
*consequences* are bounded structurally — operator-initiated, audited, no-egress,
no-distribution. Writing "robust local technical control here is not achievable"
into a security ADR feels wrong until you realize the alternative is writing a
comforting lie into one.

The dormancy held all the way down, which is the part that lets me hand this back
unmerged with a straight face. `enabled=false` ships; the model is gitignored so
the capability is absent by structure; `is_available()` is False on a clean
checkout and `/imagine` degrades to "generation unavailable" with no load attempted
— and there's a test that proves exactly that. The `generate_image` tool is GUARDED
and the PA allows a local `tool:generate_image` CAR with no rule change, so the
"lift the purpose-deny at go-live" is a config flip, not an adjudication edit —
the BED-1 pattern from the UC-003 image door, except there's no egress door to weld
here, just the `enabled` flag and the model's absence.

The standing gate caught two real couplings I'd have shipped broken: the IPC
expected-types test (two new `MessageType`s) and the system-prompt invariant (every
registered tool must be named in the prompt, or the model can emit a tool tag the
PGOV allowlist then has to reconcile). Both are good tests — the second especially:
it forced me to decide, honestly, what the model should be told about
`generate_image` (answer: that it exists and directs to `/imagine`, not that it
should hallucinate an image description). Gate green at 3669/0, +36 over the 3633
baseline, egress invariants intact ("exactly one runtime module imports a network
client" still passes — image-gen adds no network surface).

**Lesson:** *When a security primitive doesn't fit the new artifact, add a
sibling — don't bend the locked one.* The weight-integrity sweep is load-bearing for
the 14B boot path; the diffusers-OV nested layout needed a different walk. The cheap
move (skip the verify, or widen the flat globber and risk the boot path) trades a
real fail-closed guarantee for convenience. A clearly-named sibling that reuses the
proven primitives keeps both layouts honest and the locked path untouched.

**Lesson:** *Name the control you actually have, not the one that sounds
reassuring.* On an uncensored generator the reflex is a classifier; the honest
answer is that a local classifier can't hold the legal boundary and a governance
attestation can be made to mean something. Writing "no robust local technical
control" into the ADR is the mature posture — a comforting fiction in a security
document is worse than a named, accepted, structurally-bounded risk.

**Next:** the branch (`666-uc010-image-gen`) goes back for Guide review unmerged.
Go-live is the separate LA-present ceremony in ADR-033 §dormancy/go-live: the
one-time operator content attestation, then flip `[image_generation].enabled`, then
verify LIVE on the Arc GPU (text→image + img2img each produce a real image;
born-encrypted confirmed by a raw-column scan; a cap fires; the eviction sequence
observed under `log_memory`; WinUI renders `blarai-img://`; `/edit` refuses a URL),
then record the community-grade PERFORMANCE_LOG live numbers and a same-day journal
entry. The `blarai-img://` `/edit`-seed + `/save` bank-reader bridge (the gateway
reaching the AO's `generated_images` store) is the one wiring step left for those
two display-path features; text2image and local-file `/edit` work without it.

*(Branch `666-uc010-image-gen` from main `2b7e35c`; new `shared/inference/image_gen.py`
+ `services/ui_gateway/src/imagine_coordinator.py` + `generated_image_resolver.py`,
`generated_images` table + IPC pair + GUARDED tool + `[image_generation]` config +
ADR-033 + UC-010; +56 new tests (54 unit + 2 @hardware); standing gate 3669 passed /
0 failed / 20 skipped / 118 deselected; awaits the LA-present go-live ceremony.)*

### 2026-06-16 — The first C# test project, and the lesson the $-anchor never teaches you

BlarAI's WinUI front end has had C# code for months — `ImageResolver`, `BackendClient`, `MarkdownBlock` — but no C# tests. Not because the logic wasn't worth testing: the id-gate in `ImageResolver` is a security boundary. A forged `blarai-img://` id with a path-traversal payload, a query-param smuggle, or a trailing newline slipping through the `\z`-vs-`$` distinction would let a malicious knowledge-bank entry reach the (dormant) decrypt path without being caught. The gate was correct by inspection, but inspection is not a test. Vikunja #665 item-1 was the ticket to make it one.

The challenge was isolation. `BlarAI.Desktop.csproj` is a WinExe with `UseWinUI=true` and `WindowsAppSDKSelfContained=true`. A ProjectReference from a test project drags in the full Windows App SDK workload, which has no headless runner support — `dotnet test` would need the WinUI XAML compilation step, which needs a live Windows display surface and a proper RID-targeted build. The right cut was to split `ImageResolver` into two partials: `ImageResolver.Core.cs` holds the five dependency-free members (Scheme constant, IsImageRef, the `\A…\z` Regex, IsValidImageId, ExtractImageId, EscapeAlt) with only `System.Net` and `System.Text.RegularExpressions`; `ImageResolver.cs` retains the WinUI display seam (Resolve, and the new `ResolveAsync` live-pixel stub). The test project then `<Compile Include="..\Services\ImageResolver.Core.cs" Link="ImageResolver.Core.cs" />` — a single-file link, no ProjectReference, no Windows App SDK. 28 xUnit tests over the gate: the shape accepts, the forgeries reject, and the critical trailing-newline case calls `IsValidImageId` directly (not `ExtractImageId`, which Trim()s the newline away before validating — that code-path difference is exactly what the test is designed to witness).

The trailing-newline test is the one worth pausing on. In .NET, `$` matches before a trailing `\n` — a fact the regex documentation states clearly and almost nobody reads. So `^[0-9a-f]{32}$` would silently accept `<32hex>\n` as a 33-char string, because the dollar anchor lands before the newline rather than after the string. The `\z` anchor is the absolute end-of-string anchor; it rejects the trailing newline correctly. The original code already used `\A…\z` and a comment explained why (adversarial review, 2026-06-15). The test makes that protection loadbearing: a future refactor that swaps `\z` for `$` "for consistency" will now have a red test to stop it.

One unexpected wrinkle: the .NET SDK auto-globs `**/*.cs` under the project root. When `tests/` lives as a subdirectory of `services/ui_winui/`, `BlarAI.Desktop.csproj` picks up the xUnit test files and tries to compile them — producing CS0246 (unknown Fact/Assert) errors alongside the WMC0909 XAML errors. The fix was a `<Compile Remove="tests\**" />` exclude group in the app csproj. Without that, the XAML compiler also fails on the test types it cannot resolve through the DataTemplate chain, producing a cascade that looks like a code error but is actually a glob-scope problem. The solution is deterministic once you see the pattern; the lesson is to exclude sibling test directories explicitly in any WinUI project that lives in a directory next to a tests/ folder.

`dotnet build BlarAI.Desktop.csproj -r win-x64` builds clean (0 warnings, 0 errors) — the `-r win-x64` flag is required because `WindowsAppSDKSelfContained=true` demands an explicit Windows RID; without it the SDK target fires an error at the architecture-resolution step. This is an environment constraint that predated this change: the original csproj was already `WindowsAppSDKSelfContained=true`, so the bare `dotnet build` without `-r win-x64` would have failed on the baseline too. The partial split introduced no regression.

`ResolveAsync` — the async live-pixel seam that builds a `BitmapImage` from an `InMemoryRandomAccessStream` over decrypted bytes — is built but not called. It is the on-hardware go-live ceremony step: read encrypted `knowledge_images` row, FieldCipher-decrypt on the host side, feed bytes into `ResolveAsync`, get back an `ImageSource`. The headless id-gate is what this ticket covers; the render confirmation is reserved for the LA-present ceremony.

**Next:** fold this fragment into BUILD_JOURNAL.md at the next quiet-tree merge point. The immediate downstream is the UC-010 / UC-003 image go-live prereq cluster (#665 item-2 onward): the C# id-gate tests are now the first locked test, and the subsequent items (live-pixel render seam wiring, weight-manifest integrity for SDXL) can proceed with this foundation in place.

**Lesson:** The `.NET $ anchor accepts a trailing newline` — When anchoring a regex that validates security-sensitive ids in .NET, `$` is wrong and `\z` is correct. `$` matches before a terminal `\n`, so a 32-hex string followed by a newline passes `^[0-9a-f]{32}$`. The `\z` anchor is the absolute end-of-string position and rejects it. Name this in test comments so no future refactorer "simplifies" `\z` to `$`. The BlarAI id-gate test suite makes this protection loadbearing: if the regex is weakened, a test goes red.

*(commits `<this>` — ImageResolver partial split, BackendClient.ResolveImageAsync, first xUnit project BlarAI.Desktop.Tests.csproj, BlarAI.sln; 28 id-gate tests / 0 failed headless; WinUI app builds 0/0 with -r win-x64; live-pixel ResolveAsync wiring + SDXL manifest integrity remain for the go-live ceremony.)*

### 2026-06-16 — Two legs to a pixel, and the membership grain I built but didn't wire

The `/save` command had been refusing for a reason I could see in one line: the
gateway constructed the `ImagineCoordinator` with `generated_image_reader=None`,
so every stored-image path — `/save <id> <path>` and a `blarai-img://` `/edit`
seed — answered "store not available in this process." The fix was never "write
a reader"; it was "build the corridor the reader needs," and the corridor is the
part the topology made interesting. The encrypted `generated_images` /
`knowledge_images` stores live in the AO process. The named-pipe server, the
dispatcher, and the gateway all live in the *launcher* process. So resolving a
`blarai-img://<id>` to pixels is two legs, not one: WinUI → named pipe (4 MB
frame cap) → dispatcher → vsock (64 KB frame cap) → AO decrypt → bytes back the
same way. A decrypted PNG is up to 2 MiB. It does not fit a 64 KB frame. That
single fact dictated the whole design.

I modelled the vsock leg directly on the proven Stage-C parse channel rather
than inventing framing: a two-verb chunked channel (`IMAGE_RESOLVE_REQUEST`
carries only the id; `IMAGE_RESOLVE_RESPONSE` streams the bytes), total-bytes
declared on the first frame, per-chunk exact-size validation, a hard cap that
*equals* `image_staging.MAX_IMAGE_BYTES` (a coupling-lock test asserts the
equality so the two can never drift), and fail-closed assembly on every contract
violation. The one shape the parse channel didn't need and this one does is the
*placeholder*: a missing image — unknown id, decrypt-quarantine, dormant store —
is a normal, expected outcome a renderer draws as the inert alt box, not an
error. So a None result is a single small `found=false` frame, never an error
frame and never partial plaintext. I spent the assembler's strictness on
*proving* that distinction: a `found=false` after found-chunk data raises, a
found chunk after a placeholder raises, and the gateway reader collapses every
one of truncation / oversize-declaration / bad-base64 / cross-talk / transport
failure to the same None. The corridor cannot leak half an image.
