The reader had to be synchronous, which is a small thing that would have been a
real bug if I'd reflexively mirrored the async ingest transport call. The
dispatcher drives it via `asyncio.to_thread`, so by the time `_resolve_generated_image`
runs it is already on a worker thread with no running loop — wrapping the
blocking connect in another `to_thread` (as `_open_prompt_transport` does) would
have raised "no running event loop." I added a sibling `_open_prompt_transport_sync`
that does the same dev/host/guest selection without the thread hop. The receive
loop is bounded by `RESOLVE_MAX_CHUNKS + 1`, not `while True` — a hostile AO
cannot pin the reader forever even before the cap rejects an oversize body.

The piece I deliberately built-ahead-but-did-not-wire is `get_knowledge_image(doc_uuid, image_id)`
— the per-*document*-grain membership read, the strictest set-membership check
there is: an image stored under document A returns None for document B because
the AAD-bound decrypt is keyed to the *queried* doc_uuid, so a re-pointed
plaintext doc_uuid fails authentication and quarantines to None. It mirrors
`get_generated_image` exactly. But it is NOT on the render path, and I left it
off on purpose: the renderer carries an `image_id` today, not a `(doc_uuid,
image_id)` pair, and `resolve_generated_or_display_image` already satisfies the
brief's by-id membership (a well-formed-but-unstored id resolves to None → the
placeholder). Forcing per-doc plumbing onto the renderer now would be a render-path
change I was not asked to make and a decision, not a defect. It is documented in
a code comment and in the report as the ceremony successor — the primitive is
ready the day the render path learns to carry the document.

Everything is dormant and unit-tested off the real hardware. No model is loaded
on the resolve path — a dormancy test patches `image_gen.is_available()` to
*raise* and the AO handler still resolves, proving the read never so much as
asks the generation surface a question. No decrypted bytes touch disk or a log
on this leg; they live in locals and outgoing frames only. No network client was
added — the corridor is pipe + vsock, both kernel objects — so the
exactly-one-network-client air-gap invariant stays green. The live cross-process
round-trip and the live pixel on the Arc are the only things a headless build
cannot prove, and those are reserved for the ceremony.

**Next:** wire this into the WinUI `ImageResolver` C# seam against the
`resolve_image` pipe RPC (the chunk/end contract is locked to match), then prove
the full two-leg round-trip + a live pixel at the LA-present go-live ceremony.
When the render path learns to carry a `doc_uuid`, swap the display resolve to
the per-document `get_knowledge_image` grain — the primitive is already built and
tested.

**Lesson:** When a value cannot fit the transport frame, model the new
chunked channel on the proven one (parse_channel) rather than inventing framing —
and pin the cap to the canonical constant with a coupling-lock test so the two
siblings can never drift. A "missing" result is part of that contract: it must
be a first-class small placeholder frame, never an error and never a partial.

*(commits `<this>` (UC-010/UC-003 WS3 host resolve corridor + `/save` wiring,
dormant): `shared/ipc/resolve_channel.py` + the AO handler + the sync gateway
reader + `get_knowledge_image` built-ahead; +111 in the targeted selection, full
AO + gateway + shared dirs green; live round-trip + live pixel await the ceremony.)*

### 2026-06-16 — The verifier I almost broke for the whole fleet

The brief for the UC-010 image-generation go-live prerequisites read cleanly
enough: close the weight-supply-chain gap the #666 review had flagged by
extending the manifest verifier to cover the OpenVINO `.xml` topology and
`model_index.json` (today it only hashes `.bin`), sign the SDXL manifest like the
14B, turn `secure_delete` on so "purges at rest" stops being an aspiration, and
build the converged WinUI render bridge dormant. The original plan even said it in
so many words: *extend BOTH the flat and nested verifiers + the stager*. That
"BOTH" is the sentence that would have refused the next real boot.

The flat verifier (`verify_all_manifest_entries`) is the one the 14B, the Policy
Agent, and the draft model go through at every boot, and their signed manifests
are `.bin`-only. Teach the flat verifier to *require* an `.xml` and a
`model_index.json` and the very next signed-manifest boot fails closed against
manifests that were signed before the rule existed — and re-covering them isn't an
edit, it's a TPM re-stage-and-re-sign ceremony. The capability I was hardening to
protect the boot would have been the thing that bricked it. The catch came at the
comprehension gate, not from a test: the standing gate runs on synthetic fixtures
and never boots the real 14B, so a green gate would have sailed straight past it.
That is the whole argument for scoping a shared-primitive change to the path that
needs it. I scoped every change — the `.xml`/`model_index.json` coverage and the
`require_signed` signature gate — to `verify_all_manifest_entries_nested`, the
sibling with exactly one caller (image generation), left the flat verifier and the
flat stager byte-identical, and added a flat `.bin`-only regression lock so a
future agent who reads "we hash topology now" and tries to "finish the job" on the
flat path trips a test instead of the operator's boot. I went with nested-only
because the alternative — one verifier for everything — couples the image model's
threat surface to the fleet's boot path, and the boot path wins.

The signing is verify-side only, and that was a deliberate line. The real `.sig`
is a TPM artifact produced on the box at the ceremony, and the model is gitignored
absent in this build — so there is nothing to sign here. What I wired is the
*refusal*: `[image_generation].require_signed_manifest` (shipped `true`, defaulted
`false` in the dataclass for dormancy and back-compat) routes the nested load
through `load_manifest_verified`, and a missing-or-invalid `.sig` — or, the strict
part the brief asked for explicitly, an absent manifest when signing is required —
fails closed instead of the old skip-with-a-warning. The signing tool turned out
to already be model-agnostic, so the only real work there was a runbook that puts
the steps in the one irreversible order: stage nested, sign, *then* flip enabled.
Get that order wrong and you've flipped the lock against an unsigned manifest and
the load refuses — fail-closed doing its job, but a confusing way to start a
go-live, so the order is written down.

`secure_delete` was the smallest change and the most quietly satisfying, because
it made two documents honest. ADR-032's "rejecting an article PURGES its image
bytes at rest" and ADR-033's "reaped outright" were both, strictly, overstatements
— a SQLite `DELETE` unlinks the page, it doesn't zero it; the ciphertext lingered
in free pages until something reused them. `PRAGMA secure_delete=ON` at every
store-open (knowledge, substrate, session) makes the claim literally true, and the
free-page residual probe (SE-1) is what keeps it true. The probe earned its keep:
the first substrate draft was a false pass — a single-chunk document frees one
page that the small replacement INSERT immediately reuses, overwriting the
residual regardless of the PRAGMA. Only a multi-page document frees more pages than
the replacement can reclaim, so the residual survives unless `secure_delete`
zeroes it. A test that passes with the control removed is not a test; toggling the
PRAGMA off and watching eight assertions fail is.

The render bridge is where the design's neat picture met the topology's actual
wiring. The decrypt corridor — turn a `blarai-img://<id>` into pixels — is not one
hop. The launcher hosts the pipe server, the dispatcher, and the gateway in one
process; the encrypted store lives in the *other* process (the AO), reached only
over the 64 KB vsock. So a 2 MiB PNG can't ride a frame: WinUI → named pipe →
dispatcher → vsock (chunked, capped, reassembled under the same 2 MiB cap the
egress door uses) → AO decrypt → back. I built and unit-tested that seam dormant —
the channel, the AO handler against a fake bank, the `/save` reader that finally
un-refuses now that it has a bridge — and reserved the only thing a headless
machine genuinely cannot prove, the live pixel on the Arc, for the ceremony. The
first-ever C# test project (#665 item-1) came with its own small lesson worth
keeping: .NET's `$` anchor matches before a trailing `\n`, so the id gate uses
`\z`, and the test that proves it has to call `IsValidImageId("<32hex>\n")`
directly — routing through `ExtractImageId` would `Trim()` the newline away and the
anti-forgery assertion would silently never fire.

Everything ships dormant: `enabled=false`, the model absent, zero new egress (no
network client added; the corridor is pipe + vsock, both kernel objects), the
exactly-one-network-client invariant still green. Nothing here generates,
fetches, stores, or renders an image. The go-live is a separate, LA-present
ceremony with a runbook now waiting for it.

**Next:** Guide adversarial review of the dormant branch; then the LA-present
go-live ceremony per `docs/runbooks/uc010_image_gen_go_live.md` — stage the nested
SDXL manifest, sign it, record the content attestation, flip `enabled`, and run
the live GPU + live-pixel confirms that this headless build deliberately left for
the box.

**Lesson:** A hardening that touches a *shared* primitive must be scoped
to the path that needs it — "fix it everywhere" can refuse the very boot you were
protecting, and a green gate built on synthetic fixtures won't catch it; the
regression lock that proves the untouched path still works is not optional.

*(commits `<this>` (UC-010 go-live prereqs WS1/WS2/WS3, dormant); standing gate
+net-new over 3690; live GPU + live-pixel confirms await the ceremony.)*

### 2026-06-16 — The doc that promised a door that wasn't wired

UC-010 shipped dormant and Guide-reviewed, but the post-review pass surfaced the
familiar gap between what the docstrings claimed and what the code actually does —
the kind of drift that reads fine until someone trusts the comment instead of the
call site. Seven fixes, all doc-honesty or fail-closed hardening, zero behavior
change to the live (welded, dormant) path.

The two that mattered most were a matched pair of overclaims. The
`IMAGE_RESOLVE_REQUEST` protocol docstring and the host-side
`generated_image_resolver` both advertised a *two-store* by-id resolve — "decrypts
from the generated_images / knowledge_images stores." The truth is narrower:
`generated_images` resolves by id alone (UC-010), but `knowledge_images` is keyed
*per-document* (`get_knowledge_image(doc_uuid, image_id)`) and the renderer only
ever carries the bare `image_id`. The resolver's `getattr(bank,
"get_display_image_by_id", None)` fallback points at a method that *does not
exist* on the bank — so that whole branch is inert, a reserved seam, not a working
second lookup. I corrected the docs to say so and left a one-line comment at the
getattr marking it inert-by-construction. I deliberately did NOT add the missing
by-id knowledge method: choosing per-doc vs. global membership grain is a real
decision, not a defect, so it stays deferred for the LA, not silently invented to
make the docstring true.

The C# side had the inverse lie. `ResolveImageAsync`'s XML doc said the dispatcher
"does not yet register this method" and that the null return comes from the
`PipeBackendException` catch. Wrong on both counts: the Python dispatcher routes
`resolve_image` through `getattr(self, f"_m_{method}")` with no allowlist, so it
*is* dispatchable — it returns null in the dormant build because no decrypted bytes
exist upstream, which makes `_m_resolve_image` emit `end{found:false}` and the C#
returns null via the found-check, never the exception path. A doc that names the
wrong control as load-bearing is worse than no doc.

While I was in `ResolveImageAsync` I closed a genuine fail-closed hole: the
reassembly `MemoryStream` had no cumulative cap and `Convert.FromBase64String` was
unguarded, so a malformed-base64 `FormatException` would *escape* the method
instead of returning null — breaking the contract the method's own doc promises. I
added a `MaxResolvedImageBytes` const (2 MiB, citing
`image_staging.MAX_IMAGE_BYTES` as the source of truth), a per-chunk cumulative
check that refuses rather than reassembles unbounded, and a try/catch that turns a
decode error into null. Minimal, fail-closed, contract-honoring.

The remaining three were locks and a residual. I added regression tests for the
nested verifier's `_safe_join` traversal/absolute-path guard (a `../escape.bin` key
and an absolute key both refused with "escapes the model directory" — that guard
existed but was untested). I pinned the named-pipe `resolve_image` frame contract
with a fake-gateway test asserting the *exact* field names the C# reassembler
depends on (`data_b64`, `mime` on the first chunk only, `found`), so a future
rename breaks the gate here rather than silently on the on-hardware go-live
ceremony — the leg's only safety net. And I recorded an honest ADR-033 Residual (3):
the weight-manifest sweep covers `.bin` + `.xml` + `model_index.json` but not the
per-component `config.json` / scheduler / tokenizer files — a low-severity local
edge a future re-stage can widen, now on the record like Residual (2) instead of
quietly absent.

The capability stays double-dormant: `enabled=false`, model gitignored-absent, no
egress, the flat verifier and the signed 14B/PA manifests untouched. Standing gate
3770 → 3778 (+8: 6 frame-contract locks + 2 traversal guards); WinUI 28 green; the
`-r win-x64` build clean.

**Next:** the go-live prereqs that remain are LA-present, not agent-fixable — the
one-time content attestation, the tracked weight-manifest hardening (sign the nested
SDXL manifest, optionally widen to Residual-3's config/tokenizer files), and the
`secure_delete` posture call — all before the `enabled=true` flip.

**Lesson:** *A docstring that names the wrong control as load-bearing is a
defect, not a nicety.* Two of this pass's seven fixes were comments confidently
describing a door (a two-store resolve; an unregistered dispatcher method) that the
code never built or wired. The fail-closed C# hole hid behind one of them. Read the
call site, not the comment — and when the honest fix would require inventing a
deferred decision (the by-id knowledge method) to make the comment true, fix the
comment, flag the decision, and leave the seam inert.

### 2026-06-16 — The least-authority instinct that didn't pay rent

The Guide review of the UC-010 go-live prerequisites flagged that a generated image
resolves by id alone — `get_generated_image(image_id)`, no session predicate — and asked
the right question: should it be scoped per-session instead? The reflex answer is the
textbook one: least authority, scope it. I worked the trade-off before reaching for the
reflex, and the reflex was wrong here.

Per-session scoping (`WHERE image_id=? AND session_id=<current>`) adds exactly one thing:
access-control-by-requesting-context. The integrity story is untouched either way — the
AES-GCM AAD is bound to `session_id|image_id`, so a relocated or edited ciphertext fails
to decrypt and quarantines regardless of how the row was looked up. So per-session is a
*query-scope* choice, not a confidentiality one. And in this system — one operator, one
DEK, a DACL-locked pipe, display-only output, zero egress — the boundary per-session would
enforce is "the operator from themselves," which is not a real trust boundary. The id is
already a 128-bit unguessable `uuid4` bearer handle that only ever appears in the operator's
own transcripts; you cannot resolve an image you don't already have the id for, and having
the id means you had the transcript. Per-session would buy ~nil real security while costing
something concrete: you could no longer use yesterday's generation as today's `/edit` seed
(new session ⇒ seed won't resolve), and "my images" would silently fragment into
per-thread vaults.

So the call was global-by-id, and the deciding frame was ownership, not scope. Generated
images are *operator-owned creations* — one cross-session collection — whereas ingested
`knowledge_images` are *document-owned artifacts* that are meaningless outside their article
(rightly per-document, `get_knowledge_image(doc_uuid, image_id)`). The asymmetry the review
noticed isn't an oversight to "fix" into symmetry; it's two different data-ownership models
getting two correctly-different grains. The actual gap the review found was that the choice
was *implicit* — the prose had even elevated the per-document grain as "the strictest," which
made the unscoped generated path read as a weaker accident. The honest fix was to make the
choice explicit and on the record (ADR-033 §Display + the DECISION_REGISTER row), with a
revisit trigger: if BlarAI ever becomes multi-user, re-scope it — the per-document primitive
is already the template.

**Lesson:** Least authority is a default, not a dogma. Scope data access to a
boundary only when it is a *real* trust boundary; in a single-operator / one-key model "the
user from themselves" is not one, and scoping there spends capability for no security. Match
the grain to the data's ownership model (operator-owned → cross-session; document-owned →
per-document), and when a default is deliberate, *record it* — an unscoped default that
isn't written down reads as an oversight, which is the only real defect.

**Next:** the global-by-id posture is recorded and approved; the live resolve path is first
exercised at the UC-010 generation go-live ceremony (where `/edit` can seed from any prior
generation cross-session). No code change — the wired behavior already matches the posture.

*(commit `<this>` — ADR-033 §Display resolve-grain note + the DECISION_REGISTER ADR-033 row; doc-only, no test impact; LA-approved, #666.)*

### 2026-06-16 — The runbook that assumed the wrong python

The UC-010 go-live ceremony tripped on its second step. Staging the nested SDXL manifest
worked — 19 files hashed, manifest written — but the very next command, signing it, died on
`ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'cryptography'`. For a second it reads like a broken
signer or a missing TPM key. It was neither: the dev box's default `python` is 3.14 and has
no `cryptography`, while the project venv (3.11.9) has it (46.0.5), and the same TPM signer +
`BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` key already signs the production 14B manifest. Wrong interpreter,
nothing else.

What made it a trap rather than an obvious typo is that the runbook's commands all said the
bare `python`, and the *first* command — the stager — is hashlib-only, so it ran clean on
3.14 and gave the false signal that the environment was fine. The trap only sprang on the
first dependency-touching step. This same 3.14-vs-venv gotcha had quietly cost the review
agents runs more than once; this time it surfaced in the operator's hands during the
ceremony itself. The fix was small — pin the full venv path in all six runbook commands
(provision, stage, sign, verify, hardware tests) and add a top-of-runbook interpreter
callout naming why — but the lesson is the kind that compounds.

**Lesson:** An operator-facing ceremony runbook must pin its interpreter /
environment explicitly — a bare `python` (or `pip`, or any tool) is a latent trap on every
box that has a second interpreter on PATH, and the danger is sharpest when an early,
dependency-light step happens to succeed and masks it until a later step fails. Pin the exact
venv in the command, not in the operator's memory.

**Next:** the ceremony continues on the venv-pinned commands — sign → verify (`VERIFIED`) →
the one-time content attestation → flip `enabled=true` → live GPU verify.

*(commit `<this>` — runbook venv-pin across 6 commands + the interpreter callout; doc-only; surfaced live during the UC-010 ceremony; #666.)*

### 2026-06-16 — Content safety as a signature, not a classifier

Recorded the UC-010 one-time operator content-safety attestation — go-live ceremony Step 3,
the governance gate that sits in front of the `enabled=true` flip. The shape of this control
is the part worth keeping. For an uncensored local generator, content safety here is a
*signed accepted-risk on the record*, not a filter. The path-not-taken — a content
classifier — was rejected deliberately: ineffective locally, memory-costly against a tight
ceiling, and privacy-invasive (it would re-inspect the operator's own private output). In its
place the operator names the one boundary no local control can robustly enforce — the
legality of what is created, CSAM the absolute example — and signs sole responsibility for
it. That is a more honest governance posture than a filter that gives false comfort while
inspecting private content: it puts the accountability where it actually lives rather than
pretending a local model can adjudicate legality. The formal attestation is in the ledger
(`20260616_182807_uc010_content-attestation`); the technical prerequisites (the nested SDXL
manifest staged, TPM-signed, and VERIFIED under `require_signed_manifest=true`) are confirmed
by both the operator and the Guide.

**Lesson:** When a capability can't be made safe by a technical control — because no
local control is robust, or the control would cost more (memory, privacy) than it buys —
the honest governance move is a documented, operator-signed accepted-risk that names the
boundary explicitly, not a token filter that manufactures false assurance. Put the
accountability where it lives.

**Next:** Step 4 (flip `[image_generation].enabled=true`, leaving `require_signed_manifest=true`)
then Step 5 (live GPU verify on the Arc 140V). The full ceremony narrative lands as the
Step-6 same-day entry once the live verify is in.

*(commit `<this>` — ledger attestation entry `20260616_182807` + this fragment; doc-only; go-live ceremony Step 3; #666.)*

### 2026-06-17 — The go-live that was a config knob wired to nothing, then a memory ceiling

UC-010 image generation went live today, and almost nothing about it went the
way the runbook implied. The runbook described a clean ceremony — stage the
manifest, sign it, attest the content, flip `enabled=true`, verify on the GPU.
We did all that. Then every image came out as colourful noise. The lesson of the
day is that a *dormant* feature is a feature nobody has actually run, and
dormancy hides every integration seam at once.

**The dead knob.** First noise. I assumed too-few denoising steps (the config
shipped `steps=6`, a Lightning setting, but the provisioned model is base
RealVisXL V5.0 on a DDIM scheduler that wants ~30). I bumped it to 30. Same
noise. The launcher log settled it: `MEM[image_gen.t2i.before] ... steps=0`.
Every generation had run at *one* step. The gateway coordinator and the
`IMAGE_GEN_REQUEST` frame default `steps` to `0` ("operator gave no override"),
the AO forwarded that `0`, and `generate_text2image` did `int(steps) if steps is
not None else cfg.steps` — so `0` (which *is not* None) was honoured, and
`max(1, 0)` floored it to a single step. The `[image_generation].steps` knob had
been dead since the day it was written; bumping the TOML could never have helped.
The fix is a one-liner (`steps if steps and steps > 0`), but it only became
*findable* once a human looked at a real image and said "that's not Bart." The
screen caught what three layers of green tests did not — the `@hardware` go-live
test asserts the bytes decode as a PNG, and one-step noise *is* a valid PNG.

**Prompt-craft was a red herring; fidelity was the wall.** With real steps the
images were coherent but soft — mushy faces, dropped subjects. I spent a round on
prompt-craft (the 77-token SDXL ceiling, front-loading, dropping character names
that the model draws literally — it rendered an actual caped Superman). That
helped adherence but not fidelity. A close-up portrait experiment settled the
question decisively: the model renders a beautiful frame-filling face, so soft
faces in wide shots are a *pixel-budget* problem (a 50-pixel face has no detail
to give), not the INT8 model and not precision. That ruled FP16 re-provisioning
*out* for the face complaint before we spent a day on it.

**Hires-fix, and the memory ceiling I should have measured first.** The fix for
small faces is a hires pass: generate at 1024², upscale, low-strength img2img
refine. Built it, and it produced genuinely good images — *standalone*. Then the
operator ran it in the live app and it timed out. The log showed why, and it was
worse than an OOM: co-resident with the always-resident 14B, the 1536² refine
drove the box to 99.8% memory and *paged the 14B out to disk* (`proc_rss`
collapsed to 142 MB). It finished by thrashing for two minutes and blew the 90 s
timeout. The honest finding: a 1536² diffusion pass and the 14B cannot co-exist
in 31.3 GB. I should have measured the 1536² footprint before believing the
design — the standalone number (26 GB peak) plus the 14B (~14 GB) was always
~40 GB.

**Evicting the shared 14B — and the premise I had to verify on disk.** The chosen
fix (operator-approved, as it amends a locked ADR) is to evict the 14B for the
duration of a *hires* generate and lazily reload it. The premise I nearly took on
faith: "the 14B is the AO's model." It is not — it is one `LLMPipeline` shared by
*both* the Policy Agent and the AO through a single `SharedInferencePipeline`
wrapper. Eviction therefore touches the classifier too. Verifying on disk turned
a scary blast radius into a clean design: both consumers route `.generate()`
through that one wrapper and neither caches pipeline-instance state (the AO's
tokenizer is disk-loaded; the PA passes string prompts), so adding `unload()` +
lazy-reload-on-next-`generate()` to the wrapper is transparent to both. Base
1024² generates still fit co-resident, so they keep the 14B (no reload cost); the
eviction is gated to the hires path. The OS was already evicting the 14B by
paging — we just made it explicit and clean.

**Two more seams the dormant build hid.** `/save` worked only after I decoupled
the resolve corridor cap from the 2 MiB *fetch* cap (a 1536² PNG is ~3 MB). Then
`/edit` failed the same way one layer down: the seed-staging read cap was the
2 MiB egress cap, and a hires seed is ~3 MB — so you could not edit a hires
image. Same decoupling: a `/edit` seed is a local/generated image, not a
door-fetched blob, so it gets a 16 MiB cap while the egress door's 2 MiB
(BED-3 anti-bomb) stays exactly where it was.

**The key-rotation scare that was the control working.** A
`SESSION_ROW_DECRYPT_QUARANTINE` warning looked alarming mid-go-live. It was 11
days old, confined to the two oldest sessions (created before a dev→prod key
transition), and physically unrelated to image generation. The
`EncryptedSessionStore` was fail-closing correctly — refusing to surface a title
it could not authenticate. With approval (dev test data only) I backed up
`sessions.db`, removed the 2 orphan sessions + their 24 turns, left the global
`sessions_meta`, and verified `integrity_check ok` (34→32 sessions).

**Next:** live-verify `/edit` on a hires image and the first chat turn's 14B
reload; consider `hires_factor=1.25` if 1536² stays tight (it peaked at 294 MB
free even with the 14B evicted); fold this fragment + the ADR-033 §memory
amendment in at the next quiet point.

**Lesson:** *Measure the memory before you believe the design.* The
hires pass was correct and the quality was real, but it could never run in
production and only a measured `proc_rss` collapse made that legible — the design
review had nodded it through. Pair it with the older lesson that the screen
catches what tests miss (one-step noise passed a byte-decode assertion), and the
new corollary: *a config knob is dead until you trace it to its call site* — and
*verify whose resource you are about to evict* (the "AO's 14B" was the whole
system's 14B).

*(Commits: the UC-010 go-live + quality + hires + 14B-eviction + cap-fix
consolidation — see the squashed feature merge this lands under; standing gate
re-run at consolidation; live `/edit` + reload verify still owed by the
operator.)*

### 2026-06-17 — Giving the operator a way to see what they made

UC-010 went live last week: the operator can now `/imagine`, `/edit`, and
`/save` images that are born encrypted in the AO-resident `generated_images`
store. But there was a quiet asymmetry in that capability that bothered me the
moment I read the code — you could *create* images and *export one if you
already knew its id*, but you could not **see what you had made**. No listing.
No user-facing delete (only the AO's internal `delete_generated_image`, reachable
from no command). No record of which images had ever been written to disk. A
born-encrypted store the operator can write to but can't inventory is a store
that quietly accumulates forever. This Phase 1 closes that gap: a `/images`
command to LIST and DELETE, and a forward-looking "this was exported" flag.

The whole design pivots on one security line I did not want to cross: **a listing
must never decrypt anything**. The temptation is obvious — the operator would
love to see a thumbnail or the prompt that made each image. But the prompt and the
pixels are the two AES-GCM-encrypted columns, and the entire point of the
born-encrypted store is that decrypted bytes leave it by exactly two doors: the
resolve corridor (inline display) and `/save` (explicit export). A list is
neither. So `list_generated_images` reads only the cheap, non-content columns —
`image_id`, `session_id`, `mime`, `created_at`, `saved`, and a `length(data)`
SQL aggregate for the size hint. That last one was the small, satisfying call:
the operator wants a size in the listing, and `length(data)` gives the on-disk
ciphertext length (plaintext + the GCM nonce/tag) without ever touching the
cipher. A faithful size proxy, zero decrypt. I locked it with a test that
monkeypatches the bank's `decrypt` to raise `AssertionError` and asserts the
listing still succeeds — if a future change ever routes a list through a decrypt,
that test detonates. There is, by the same logic, *no* decrypt-quarantine path on
the list (unlike `list_pending`): nothing is decrypted, so nothing can fail to
decrypt, and a row with a corrupt blob still lists fine — its metadata is intact
and useful (you can still DELETE it), it just can't be *displayed*, which the
resolve corridor already handles with a placeholder.

The `saved` flag was the place I had to discipline myself against over-building.
The honest design question is "has this image ever been written to disk?", and
the lazy answer is an export ledger — every path, every timestamp. The LA had
already settled it in the brief: a SIMPLE boolean, not a log. That is the right
call and not just the minimal one — a path/timestamp ledger of where encrypted
images were exported is itself a little map of the operator's filesystem habits,
exactly the kind of metadata a privacy-absolute system should not be hoarding. A
bare 0/1 says what the operator needs ("I've got a copy of that one") and records
nothing it shouldn't. The flag is also explicitly forward-looking: existing rows
migrate to not-saved, because there is no on-disk record of past `/save`s and
*inventing* one would be a lie. I wired the mark into `/save` fail-soft — the PNG
is already on disk by the time the mark fires, so a mark failure (AO unreachable,
store error, a raising manager) is logged and swallowed; it must never undo a
completed save. Two tests pin that: one where the manager returns `ok=False` and
one where it *raises*, both asserting the file is still written and the reply
still says "Saved".

The migration taught me the SQLite gotcha I should have remembered: `CREATE TABLE
IF NOT EXISTS` is a no-op on a table that already exists, so adding a column to
the schema string only ever reaches a *fresh* database. A store created by last
week's build would silently never get the `saved` column. So the column needs an
explicit, idempotent `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` guarded by a `PRAGMA
table_info` existence check. I proved it the hard way in a test: build a store,
then *rebuild* its `generated_images` table in the pre-#667 shape (rename, create
without `saved`, copy the rows back, drop) to genuinely simulate an older DB —
not just trust that the ALTER is correct — then reopen through
`EncryptedKnowledgeBank` and assert the column appears, the old row survives as
not-saved, and it still decrypts (the ALTER is metadata-only; it touches no
encrypted blob). And that a second open is idempotent.

The wiring decision I'm happiest with is the one that produced *no* code: the
dispatcher. `/imagine`, `/edit`, `/save` already flow through the gateway's
getattr-guarded `handle_imagine_command` → `parse_imagine_command` →
`ImagineCoordinator.handle_command`. By teaching the parser one more verb
(`images`) and the coordinator one more branch, `/images` rides that exact path
with **zero dispatcher change**. I confirmed `/imagine` and `/images` never
collide as command prefixes (the stems diverge at char 6 — `…ine` vs `…es` — so
neither is a prefix of the other) and locked it with a test. The one place I had
to be careful was the parse order; I put `images` first in the verb tuple so the
longer stem is tried first, though the non-collision makes order moot. The
end-to-end test drives a `/images` string straight through the real
`handle_imagine_command` with a scripted AO behind it and asserts an
`IMAGE_LIST_REQUEST` actually went out — proving the dispatcher-less wiring works
top to bottom.

Two smaller fail-closed calls worth keeping. First, `/images delete` requires the
**full** 32-hex id as the confirm-of-intent: there is no modal in a chat surface,
so the full id *is* the confirmation, and a partial or forged id is refused
*before* any IPC is issued (a test asserts the gateway never even connects for a
forged delete). I reused the anchored `is_blarai_img_ref` / `re.fullmatch`
shape from `/save`, so a 32-hex id with a trailing newline — the classic
`$`-vs-`\Z` forgery — is refused too. Second, the IPC list frame is capped at 200
records newest-first with a `truncated` flag and a full `total`, so the listing
always fits one 64 KB envelope and a runaway gallery can never blow the frame; the
list-response encoder normalises every record onto a pinned key set, so an
accidental `prompt`/`data` key on a record is *dropped* at encode — the list frame
cannot carry decrypted content even by a wiring mistake (locked with a test that
asserts a smuggled `SECRET-PROMPT` is absent from the encoded bytes).

The standing-gate-relevant suites stay green: `shared/ services/ -m "not hardware
and not winui and not slow"` runs **3191 passed, 22 skipped** (the 22 are the
documented worktree env-skips — 20 missing-ONNX router tests + 2 symlink-privilege
— not introduced here). The delete path is the existing `secure_delete=ON` wipe,
unweakened; the egress door, the PA adjudicator, the at-rest DEK/AAD, and the
resolve cap are all untouched.

**Next:** Phase 2 is the WinUI gallery (not this branch) — it will consume the
same `IMAGE_LIST_REQUEST` metadata + the resolve corridor for thumbnails, so the
metadata-only contract this pass established is exactly what it needs. One open
posture call for the LA at review: whether the `saved`-flag + `/images` surface
warrants an ADR-033 amendment row (I treated it as a feature *implementation*
under the existing ADR-033, since it changes no locked decision — born-encrypted,
DELETE-on-discard, no-VLM, no-egress, content-safety all hold — but the call to
record it formally is the LA's). The by-id resolve grain (global vs
session-scoped) noted at go-live still stands; `/images` lists across all sessions
deliberately (the operator manages their whole gallery from chat), which is
consistent with that global-by-id posture.

### 2026-06-17 — The last leg to a pixel, and the "zero C# change" that was never true

Yesterday's WS3 fragment ended with an explicit instruction to its successor:
"wire this into the WinUI `ImageResolver` C# seam against the `resolve_image`
pipe RPC, then prove the full two-leg round-trip + a live pixel." This is that
successor. The corridor was already built and locked end to end —
`BackendClient.ResolveImageAsync` reassembles the chunked PNG, `ResolveAsync`
decodes bytes into a `BitmapImage` off an in-memory stream, and the id-gate in
`ImageResolver.Core.cs` refuses every forged ref. All three were built and
called by nothing. The gap was a single dangling wire, and the interesting part
was the shape of the connector, not its length.

The renderer is a thicket of `static` methods: `MarkdownBlock` rebuilds its whole
content tree on every `Markdown` change, so `Build → BuildProse → AppendInlines →
BuildImageInline` are all static, and `BuildImageInline` had no way to reach a
`BackendClient` instance. The honest options were (a) thread a client reference
through four static call sites, or (b) a process-wide static delegate set once at
init. I took (b): a `static Func<string, CancellationToken, Task<byte[]?>>?
ImageBytesResolver` on `MarkdownBlock`, wired in the `MainWindow` constructor to
`_backend.ResolveImageAsync`. In a multi-window app (a) would buy real isolation
and I'd have paid the threading cost; in a single-window personal app with exactly
one backend pipe per process it buys nothing, and the static keeps the renderer's
static-by-design shape intact. I wrote the trade-off into the delegate's doc so a
future multi-surface BlarAI knows why the static is there and when to revisit it —
the rejected alternative on the record, not just the choice.

The flow itself is the standard async-image-load done fail-closed. `BuildImageInline`
runs on the UI thread, so it id-gates synchronously (a non-`blarai-img://` scheme
or a forged id returns the inert alt `Run` immediately, never starting a resolve),
emits a sourceless `Image` in an `InlineUIContainer`, captures the UI
`DispatcherQueue`, and fires the resolve. The byte fetch happens off-thread; the
*decode* does not. That distinction is the one place I'd have shipped a bug on
autopilot: `BitmapImage` is a `DependencyObject` and `SetSourceAsync` must run on
the thread that will display it, so I marshal both the decode and the `Source`
assignment back through `TryEnqueue`. Get that wrong and you get a cross-thread
`RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD` at the exact moment the first real image arrives — invisible
to every headless test. Every failure branch — null/empty bytes, a corrupt decode,
a dead resolver, a closing window's vanished dispatcher — swaps the container's
child to the same alt-text placeholder, so a missing image, a forged id, and a
hostile blob all render identically and inertly. The `Image.Source` is only ever
an in-memory `BitmapImage`; there is no code path from a `blarai-img://` ref to a
`Uri`, a `file:`, or the network.

Then the bug that wasn't in the code but in the prose: two docstrings in
`imagine_coordinator.py` claimed "the WinUI renders `blarai-img://` inline with
zero C# change." That was never true — the renderer's old `Resolve()` returned
`null` unconditionally, so a generated-image reply rendered the placeholder and
nothing else. And the gateway's success reply was plain text, so the renderer's
image path never even fired: there was no `![alt](ref)` for `AppendInlines` to
match. Two layers both quietly defeated the feature the docstring advertised as
done. I fixed both — the reply now leads with `![generated image](blarai-img://<id>)`
on its own line (informational text + the `/save` hint still follow for the TUI,
which shows the embed as literal text) — and rewrote the stale claims plus the
`ImageResolver` "dormant, not part of this build" comments, which my own change
now contradicts. The "zero C# change" line is the kind of doc rot that reads as
confidence and hides a hole; killing it was as load-bearing as the wire.

What a headless build can prove, it does: 47 C# id-gate + render-gate tests
(`dotnet test`, +19 new), 24 imagine-coordinator tests (+1 locking the markdown
embed leads the reply — a plain-text reply would silently never render a pixel),
69 across the whole resolve corridor, and a clean `dotnet build` of the full WinUI
desktop project (x64; the default AnyCPU build fails on
`WindowsAppSDKSelfContained requires a supported Windows architecture`, which is a
platform-flag issue, not a missing workload — `-p:Platform=x64 -r win-x64` builds
0/0). What it cannot prove is the only thing left: the actual pixel on the Arc
140V. The async fill, the UI-thread decode, and `ResolveAsync` live in the WinUI
partials the strictly-isolated test project deliberately does not reference, so
their fail-closed behaviour is asserted by construction and confirmed by eye at
the ceremony. That is the honest boundary of this work.

**Next:** at the next WinUI-open session on the Arc 140V, run `/imagine <prompt>`
and confirm the generated image renders inline in the chat (the runbook's Step 5
item 1 — WinUI → pipe → dispatcher `resolve_image` → vsock → AO decrypt → bytes →
`BitmapImage`), then `/edit` and confirm the edited image renders. When the render
path eventually learns to carry a `doc_uuid`, swap the display resolve to the
per-document `get_knowledge_image` grain (the WS3 built-ahead primitive). UX is a
reasonable default — max-width-constrained, aspect-preserved, no interaction;
refine after seeing it.

**Lesson:** A "zero-change" or "already handled" claim in a docstring is
a load-bearing assertion — verify it against the code before trusting it, because
doc rot reads as confidence and hides exactly the hole you're there to close. Here
two layers (a null-returning resolver and a plain-text reply) each silently
defeated a feature the docs called done; the fix was as much killing the false
claim as writing the wire. Pairs with the verify-the-LA's-technical-premises
discipline: treat your own predecessors' "done" the same way.

*(commit `<this>` (UC-010 inline-image render wiring, #666/#665 Pass B): the
`MarkdownBlock` static resolver delegate + sync-placeholder/async-fill flow with
UI-thread `Source` marshaling, the `MainWindow` wire-up, the markdown-embed reply
in `imagine_coordinator._dispatch_generate`, the `InlineImageRenderGateTests`
project addition, and the doc-honesty correction of the false "zero C# change"
claims + the `ImageResolver` dormancy comments; C# 47/0, imagine 24/0, corridor
69/0, full WinUI desktop build 0/0 on x64; the live pixel on the Arc awaits the
WinUI-open verify.)*

### 2026-06-17 — The command the operator couldn't type, and the "saved" that wasn't true

UC-010 image management shipped Phase 1 with a green backend gate — `/images` to
list, delete, and read saved-status, wired end to end: the gateway coordinator
parsed the verb, two metadata-only IPC verbs carried it, the AO handlers answered
fail-closed, and the standing gate held at 3869. Then the operator typed `/images`
in the running app and got *"Unknown command."* The backend was complete and the
operator still could not reach the feature.

The WinUI keeps its own client-side allowlist of which slash commands to forward to
the backend (`BackendPassthroughCommands`). `/images` was not in it, so the command
never left the client — it fell straight to the local "Unknown command" path. This
is the *identical* gap `/imagine` had at its own go-live. Twice now a feature has
been backend-complete, gate-green, and merged, and the operator-facing surface
silently dropped it because one hand-maintained list was not edited. The one-line
fix (add `/images` to the allowlist, `5bde9a0`) is trivial; the smell is not. A
manual allowlist that must be edited per command is a defect generator — the durable
answer is to derive the passthrough set from the shared command registry, or
cross-check the two at build time, so a third new command cannot fail the same way.
I logged that as a tracked hardening follow-up rather than bolt it on under time
pressure.

The second half was subtler. With `/images` finally working, the operator saw all
15 generated images flagged **encrypted-only** — though he had saved most of them to
disk. Correct, and useless. The `saved` flag is forward-looking by construction: it
records a save only from the moment the feature existed, and the entire existing
library predated it, so there was no record to show. A boolean that means "was
exported at least once, going forward" cannot answer "what is currently safe on
disk" for anything older than itself.

The honest fix was to stop trusting the flag and check reality. With the app closed
and `knowledge.db` free, I replicated the AO's DEK unseal (the `TpmSealer` +
`dek_keystore.json` envelope the app opens automatically at boot), decrypted each
stored image, hashed its plaintext bytes, hashed the 52 PNGs in the operator's save
folder, and marked the exact SHA-256 matches. 15/15 images decrypted to valid PNG
magic (confirming the right key), and 8 matched a byte-identical file on disk. The
marks were cryptographically safe by construction — a wrong key or a non-match marks
nothing — so the only DB write was `saved=1` on images proven identical to a file
the operator already holds. I took a `knowledge.db` backup first regardless.

The result corrected the record in both directions: the operator remembered saving
"all but one," but disk truth was 8 — the other 7 were iteration-variants he
generated and did not keep (he saved `cafe1.png`, not the other two café takes;
`lunar_surface4–7`, not every lunar attempt), or were saved-then-overwritten under
the same filename so only the last survives. That divergence is the point. A
"saved" flag is memory; a content-hash reconcile against the actual save location is
truth, and the truest meaning of "saved" is "a byte-identical decrypted copy exists
on disk right now" — which a stored boolean can only ever approximate. Going forward
the flag auto-marks on `/save`; for the backlog, only the hash told the truth.

**Next:** Phase 2 — a WinUI gallery pane (thumbnails, per-image save/delete,
saved/locked badges) and the always-visible session-delete control the operator
flagged missing — on the LA's go. And the passthrough-allowlist hardening: derive or
cross-check the WinUI command set so a backend-complete command cannot reach the
operator as "Unknown" a third time.

**Lesson:** *Backend-complete is not operator-reachable.* A feature with a
green backend gate and a clean merge is still not done until the operator's actual
surface reflects it; a hand-maintained client allowlist that must be edited per
command is a defect generator (it silently dropped `/imagine`, then `/images`) —
derive it from the shared registry or cross-check at build time.

**Lesson:** *A "saved" flag is memory; a content hash is truth.* A
forward-looking "was-exported" boolean cannot describe a library older than itself,
and drifts from "currently safe on disk" even going forward. For a pre-existing
backlog, only a decrypt-and-hash reconcile against the real save location is
accurate — and it will correct the operator's memory, not just confirm it (8
byte-identical copies on disk vs. a remembered ~14).

### 2026-06-17 — The gallery that reused everything, and the allowlist that drifts in silence

UC-010 Phase 1 (#667) shipped the backend for managing generated images — a `/images` chat command that lists (metadata-only, `length(data)` size with no decrypt), deletes by full 32-hex id (the existing `secure_delete` wipe), and a forward-looking saved/encrypted-only flag. Phase 2 (#668) is the surface a human actually uses: a WinUI gallery pane. The striking thing building it was how little new machinery it needed. The display corridor already existed (`BackendClient.ResolveImageAsync` → `ImageResolver.ResolveAsync`, the same decrypt-and-decode seam the inline `blarai-img://` markdown render uses). The transport legs already existed (`TransportGateway._list_generated_images` / `_manage_generated_image`, driving the Phase-1 IMAGE_LIST/IMAGE_MANAGE verbs). The id-gate already existed (`ImageResolver.IsValidImageId`, the `\A…\z`-anchored 32-hex check). The gallery is a second front-end over a proven backend, not a new path — and that is exactly the property that keeps the security posture intact: born-encrypted at rest is unchanged, the list/manage wire stays metadata-only (no pixels, no prompts), and decrypted bytes cross only the resolve corridor (for a thumbnail) or the operator's explicit Save (the sanctioned export, ADR-033). No new decrypt path, no new egress.

The two new dispatcher RPCs (`_m_list_generated_images`, `_m_manage_generated_image`) are deliberately thin — they mirror `_m_resolve_image` exactly: the same `getattr`-guard so a stub gateway degrades gracefully, the same anchored id regex (`_RESOLVE_ID_RE`) so a forged delete id never reaches the born-encrypted store, the same fail-closed discipline (any exception → a clean empty/`ok=False` result, never an error frame, never a raise). The manage leg validates both the action (`delete`/`mark_saved` only) AND the id BEFORE the gateway is consulted, so a malformed request is refused without ever touching the store. An empty gallery is the safe degraded state; a refused delete is a refusal, not a crash. 13 dispatcher tests pin all of that with a fake gateway that records every call, so the "forged id never reaches the gateway" claim is asserted, not asserted-by-comment.

I made three judgment calls worth recording. First, the layout was the LA's decision (an overlay pane over the chat, not a split view) — I implemented exactly that: a `Grid.RowSpan=2` overlay with an opaque themed background so the chat stays intact underneath and an X returns to it. Second, where the LA's brief left the gallery's per-tile Save/Delete buttons as "hover-reveal OR small always-visible corner buttons — your call," I chose always-visible. That is not a coin-flip: this same sprint's WS4 exists *because* the per-session delete button shipped `Opacity=0` (hover-only) and the operator could not find it. Repeating that mistake one panel over would have been willful. The gallery buttons are always-visible corner actions; the fix for the session button is a dim-but-visible default (`Opacity=0.55`, brighten to 1.0 on hover, restore to 0.55 — not 0 — on exit). Third, Save is a client-side write, not a backend route: resolve the full-res bytes through the existing corridor, write them to the `FileSavePicker` path with `File.WriteAllBytesAsync`, then call `mark_saved`. This keeps the backend wire metadata-only and makes the operator's explicit Save the single sanctioned way decrypted pixels reach disk — which is precisely the ADR-033 §D `/save`-equivalent. The ticket itself specified this flow, so it was a confirm-not-invent.

A WinUI-desktop wrinkle that bites every time: `FileSavePicker` has no `CoreWindow` to attach to and throws without `WinRT.Interop.InitializeWithWindow.Initialize(picker, hwnd)`. The existing code already had a `Hwnd` property (`WindowNative.GetWindowHandle(this)`), so the fix was one line — but it is the kind of line that is invisible until the picker silently fails to appear. Worth noting the codebase deliberately uses a Win32 `GetOpenFileNameW` for *opening* files (to reach OneDrive cloud-only placeholders when de-elevated); for *saving* a brand-new file that concern does not apply, so the WinRT `FileSavePicker` is the right tool and the brief's explicit instruction.

The most portfolio-worthy piece is WS5, because it fixes a class of bug, not an instance. The WinUI composer carries a hand-copied C# array — `BackendPassthroughCommands` — of the slash commands it must forward to the backend rather than handle host-side. That array has silently dropped a newly-shipped backend command twice: first `/imagine`, then `/images`. Each time, a real capability was unreachable from the GUI until a human noticed. The root cause is structural: C# and Python cannot share a literal across the language boundary, so the array is a manual mirror with no enforcement. The durable fix is a single Python source of truth (`shared/ipc/slash_commands.py`, `BACKEND_PASSTHROUGH_SLASH_COMMANDS`) plus a gate-time test that reads the C# array straight out of `MainWindow.xaml.cs` as text — stripping `//` line comments first so a command merely mentioned in a comment is not miscounted — and fails loudly, naming exactly which canonical command is missing, if the C# set is not a superset. I verified the gate has teeth before trusting it: extracted the live tokens, simulated dropping `/images`, and confirmed the assertion fires with `missing == {'/images'}`. No runtime behavior changed; this is purely a wiring-correctness guard. The next person who adds a backend-parsed slash command will be forced to update the WinUI mirror in the same change, or the build goes red.

Verification was clean across all three surfaces: the Python standing gate 3864 passed / 0 failed (the 22 skips are the documented benign worktree skips — 20 `semantic_router` tests need the gitignored `bge-small-en-v1.5` ONNX model absent in an isolated worktree, 2 `test_runtime_config` symlink tests need an elevated shell), the C# headless project 59 passed / 0 failed (47 prior + 12 new `GalleryManagementGateTests` locking the client-side management id-gate), and the WinUI build 0 warnings / 0 errors with `-p:Platform=x64 -r win-x64 --self-contained`. What I could NOT verify is the live GUI rendering: a headless build proves the XAML and code-behind compile and bind, but it cannot render the overlay, decode a real thumbnail, or exercise the FileSavePicker. The gallery actually showing decrypted thumbnails, the Save dialog appearing, and a delete removing a tile are the operator's on-hardware step.

**Next:** the Guide reviews this branch adversarially and merges (no self-merge). On-hardware confirm for the operator: open the gallery from the new sidebar icon → see thumbnails of generated images → Save one (badge flips to ✓) → delete one (tile leaves). Downstream, the allowlist SSOT is now the home for any future backend-parsed command; the gate test guards it.

**Lesson:** A hand-copied cross-language allowlist drifts in silence — guard it with a gate test, not vigilance. The WinUI `BackendPassthroughCommands` array dropped a shipped backend command twice (`/imagine`, then `/images`) because it is a manual C# mirror of a Python-side truth with no enforcement, and a missing entry fails quietly (the command is handled host-side / errors instead of reaching the backend — no crash, no log, just a capability that is mysteriously unreachable). When a set MUST be mirrored across a language boundary that cannot share a literal, name ONE source of truth and write a gate-time test that parses the mirror out of the other language's source and fails loudly, naming the specific missing element. Verify the gate has teeth (simulate a drop, confirm it goes red) before trusting it. Vigilance is not a control; a red test is.

*(commits `<this>` — UC-010 Phase 2 gallery: 2 dispatcher RPCs + 13 tests (`test_dispatcher_image_management.py`), `GeneratedImageMeta` DTO + 3 BackendClient methods, the overlay-pane gallery (XAML + `GalleryImageItem` VM + Save/Delete/thumbnail-fill code-behind), the WS4 session-delete dim-visible fix, the WS5 allowlist SSOT (`shared/ipc/slash_commands.py`) + gate test + 12 C# `GalleryManagementGateTests`. Python gate 3864/0 (22 benign worktree skips); C# headless 59/0; WinUI build 0/0 x64. Live GUI render / Save dialog / delete-tile remain the operator's on-hardware confirm.)*

### 2026-06-17 — The gallery that tested green and showed nothing

I merged UC-010 Phase 2 with every gate green — Python 3884/0, C# 59/0, WinUI build 0/0 — and told the Lead Architect it was verified. He relaunched, opened the gallery, and it said *"No generated images yet."* There were fifteen images in the store. The feature I had just called done did not work, and the test suite had sworn it did.

I made myself not guess. First I proved the data was real: a read-only `count(*)` on the live `knowledge.db` returned 15 rows, 8 marked saved — exactly what the earlier reconcile left. Then I proved the wiring was real: `_list_generated_images`, `_manage_generated_image`, and `_resolve_generated_image` all live on the same `TransportGateway` class, and the dispatcher reaches them by dynamic `getattr(self._gateway, f"_m_{method}")` — so if inline render (which uses `_resolve_generated_image`) worked, the gallery's list method was reachable by the identical path. A stale or unwired backend was tempting to assume, but the evidence didn't support it. The smoking gun was in `launcher.log`, repeated on every gallery-open at 17:09: `dispatch 'list_generated_images' failed: Object of type coroutine is not JSON serializable`, at `dispatcher.py:870`. The backend was current; the code was wrong.

The bug is a one-word assumption. The gateway's list/manage legs are `async def`. The new dispatcher RPCs were modelled on `_m_resolve_image`, which calls its gateway leg via `await asyncio.to_thread(self._gateway._resolve_generated_image, image_id)` — correct, because `_resolve_generated_image` is a *synchronous* method and `to_thread` runs blocking work off the event loop. Copied onto an `async` leg, `asyncio.to_thread(async_fn, arg)` runs `async_fn(arg)` in a worker thread, which merely *constructs* a coroutine and returns it un-awaited. The dispatcher then handed that coroutine object to the JSON encoder, which refused it, and the fail-closed path turned the refusal into an empty list. Inline render (sync resolve) worked; the gallery (async list/manage) silently didn't — same file, same author, one wrong assumption about whether the callee needed a thread or an await.

Why did thirteen dispatcher tests miss it? Because the test's `_FakeGateway` legs were synchronous `def`. `to_thread(sync_fn)` works fine, so the buggy dispatcher passed every assertion. The mock's *shape* had diverged from the real object's shape — sync where the real thing is async — and so the suite validated a code path that production never executes. This is the frozen-dataclass mock-shape divergence from the early days of this project, back again in a new costume: a test double is only evidence about the system insofar as it matches the system. Mine matched the convenient shape, not the true one.

The fix is small and the test fix is the load-bearing half. The dispatcher now awaits the async legs directly (`await lister(session_id)`, `await manager(action, image_id)`), with a comment naming the trap so the next person doesn't reach for `to_thread` again. And the `_FakeGateway` legs are now `async def` — matching the real gateway — which does two things at once: it makes the corrected dispatcher pass, and it turns the original bug into a *caught* regression, because `to_thread` on an async fake now yields a coroutine that fails the `result["total"]` assertions. I proved that mechanism in isolation (`asyncio.to_thread(async_fn)` → `coroutine`, "never awaited") before trusting it. The operator's cosmetic ask rode along: the per-session delete button now stretches hard-right by the scrollbar instead of hugging the title, a one-line `HorizontalContentAlignment="Stretch"` on the row container.

The part worth keeping is the honesty. My "verified and merged" rested on a green suite that tested the wrong shape; the live-verify on real hardware caught what every automated check waved through. And the failure mode compounded it: the dispatcher's fail-closed-to-empty swallowed a genuine error into a state indistinguishable from "the store is empty." The error was right there in the log, but the UI lied by omission — it said *nothing here* when it meant *I broke*. That is the sibling of the allowlist lesson from this same sprint: a quiet failure is not a safe failure when it masquerades as a valid, ordinary state.

**Next:** the operator relaunches to pick up the fix and confirms thumbnails render, Save flips the badge, and delete removes a tile. A fail-loud follow-up — so a future serving error surfaces "couldn't load images" rather than an indistinguishable empty gallery — is tracked on the gallery-hardening ticket.

**Lesson:** *A test double standing in for an async object must itself be async, or it proves a path production never runs.* When a fake replaces a real collaborator, its method signatures — sync vs async, return types, raised exceptions — are part of the contract under test, not incidental scaffolding. A sync fake for an `async def` gateway let a `to_thread`-vs-`await` bug pass thirteen green tests and ship; the live run failed instantly. Mirror the real shape in the double (await-ability included), and where the shape is the very thing that can go wrong, make the double the strictest possible version of the real contract so the bug becomes a red test, not a production surprise.

*(commits `<this>` — `_m_list_generated_images`/`_m_manage_generated_image` await the async gateway legs directly instead of `asyncio.to_thread` (the coroutine-not-serializable fix, #668 live-verify); `_FakeGateway` + the boom-gateways made `async def` to match the real `TransportGateway` and to catch the regression; session-delete `HorizontalContentAlignment="Stretch"` right-align + a stale-comment correction. Verified: dispatcher 13/13, standing gate 3884/0/2/118, WinUI build 0/0. Live render/Save/delete remain the operator's on-hardware confirm.)*

### 2026-06-21 — Measuring whether releasing the 14B actually opens the door for the 30B

The headless-coding plan turns on a model swap: BlarAI's resident 14B has to get
out of the way so OpenVINO Model Server can load a 30B coder into the same 31.3 GB
of Lunar Lake unified memory. The whole design rests on one empirical question the
agentic-setup brief flagged but could not answer from outside — openvino #33896: on
this iGPU, GPU memory is not auto-released on idle, so it is entirely possible for
the system-RAM counter to read "freed" while the Arc-140V carve-out stays pinned,
which would make the swap silently page itself to death. Before trusting any of it,
I measured.

First the premise correction, because it changed the shape of the answer. The
session prompt — and the CLAUDE.md "VM isolation" story — say the 14B runs in the
VM. It does not. `deployment_mode="host"`, `device="GPU"`: the 14B is a host process
on the Arc 140V, and the BlarAI-Orchestrator VM is a NIC-less parser with no GPU
passthrough. The 14B and the 30B draw from the *same* unified pool — there is no
cross-domain barrier to fear. That turned the question into one purely of magnitude
and the #33896 carve-out return, not topology, and the keystone confirmed it before
anything else: the live 14B sat at ~8.7 GB on the iGPU per the per-PID GPU counter,
not in the guest.

The release path already existed — `SharedInferencePipeline.unload()`, built for
UC-010's hires-image eviction: drop the pipeline ref, one `gc.collect()`, lazy fresh
rebuild on the next generate. I drove it two ways. B1, a throwaway standalone process
loading the same pipeline; B2 — the one that actually counts — a flag-gated sentinel
hook inside the live long-lived launcher process, removed the moment the run
finished. Three cycles each, before/after on both dimensions: the gate-identical
`\Memory\Available MBytes`, cross-checked against Committed Bytes and the Free&Zero
list so a standby-cache shuffle could not masquerade as a real free; and per-PID
`\GPU Process Memory` for the carve-out itself. I deliberately refused the obvious
shortcut of killing the process to "free" the GPU — a process exit always returns
it and would have proved nothing about the in-process release that the swap actually
performs.

The carve-out comes back. In the live process `unload()` returned in ~1 second and
the per-PID GPU dropped 10,774 MB to a 677 MB floor and *stayed* there across the
60-second settle — no driver-held residue, no #33896, every cycle, zero drift. The
freed RAM was genuine: Committed Bytes fell ~11.3 GB and the Free&Zero list rose
~11 GB in lockstep. The single production `gc.collect()` was enough — the labelled
second-collect diagnostic freed nothing more. The reload came back coherent, not
garbled. The release mechanism is sound on this driver (32.0.101.8826, OpenVINO
2026.1.0).

But the honest part is the margin. Releasing the 14B took Available from ~11.6 GB to
~22.0 GB — it clears the 30B's 21 GB gate, but by about a gigabyte, and that gigabyte
is on loan from the ambient. The cold floor on this box drifted between 19.4 GB and
23.7 GB during the session purely from other processes; at the heavier end the same
release lands ~18 GB and goes short. And the freed amount is itself state-dependent —
~8.7 GB for an idle 14B, ~10.8 GB reloaded, ~11.8 GB once a conversation has built up
KV. So the precondition is met, not the swap: a release returns enough memory to the
right pool, in a lean-enough ambient, with thin margin.

The sharpest finding is one the measurement only surfaced by looking at the other
side of the swap. The fleet's `start-llm.ps1` enforces its 21 GB headroom check
inside `if (-not $Force)` — and the swap is required to pass `-Force` (to skip the
interactive prompt that would otherwise offer to stop the BlarAI VM). So during the
real swap *nothing enforces the gate*, and the only non-Force remediation is to stop
a VM we now know holds ~0.5 GB and is irrelevant to the host-side 14B. The swap
design cannot lean on start-llm to catch a shortfall; it has to own its own pre-load
Available check and its own remediation — trim ambient, or abort with a clear error —
before it ever issues the irreversible 30B load.

The certification ran the same day, and it is the part I nearly got wrong. The first
pass looked like a win — the real Qwen3-Coder-30B loaded in ~30 seconds and decoded at
37.4 tok/s (the ~87% override holds), took 15.3 GB of the iGPU at steady, and stopped
clean. I wrote "the door opens, swap viable with a thin ~0.7 GB margin" and committed
it. The reviewer refused the verdict and demanded two things I had skipped: the
*measured* load peak, not the modeled one, and a Committed cross-check instead of an
Available number inflated by ~10 GB of standby. Both corrections cut the wrong way.

So I sampled continuously *through* the staged load. Even with BlarAI fully **down** in
a lean 25.4 GB ambient, the 30B's dual CPU+GPU weight copy drove Available to **67 MB**,
Committed to **29.1 GB**, and the pagefile to **206,000 hard page-ins per second** for
six seconds before it recovered. The load is a near-whole-pool transient that thrashes
the box even in the best case. The brief's 21 GB gate is simply too low. And the bare
swap keeps BlarAI alive (its ~1.5 GB remnant) and loads from B2's directly-measured
~22 GB — three-plus gigabytes *tighter* than the 25 GB that already thrashed. The
honest verdict is the opposite of what I first committed: **the bare swap is not
viable.** It page-storms; from the swap's tighter pool it would death-spiral. My own
"~8 GB steady headroom" was Available-with-standby — the committed-real steady is one
to three gigabytes, going negative as the 30B's KV fills. The one corroboration I keep
is the §4 point, made twice over: the fleet's `-Force` skips the gate (the safety
classifier refused it for exactly that), and the gate it skips is too low anyway.

And then the operator's correction — he builds projects with this 30B routinely, so it
plainly works — which sent me lurching the other way into a third wrong call. I waved the
page-storm away as "just the model reading its weights" and declared the swap viable. Both
were over-reaches. The page-in spike is partly the legitimate fifteen-gigabyte read, yes,
but Available fell to sixty-odd megabytes: the load genuinely exhausts *physical* RAM. It
survives only because the commit limit — physical plus pagefile — sits above the 31.3 GB
of physical, so there is no out-of-memory error; but the box is at its physical edge, and
that edge *is* the page-storm. My "Committed 29.1 < 31.3 so memory never ran out" had
confused the commit limit with physical headroom. And his daily use proves the *standalone*
load on a leaned box, which is not the swap: the swap starts from a tighter pool — a bare
14B-release lands around 22 GB, a full teardown around 19.4 GB cold — both below the 23.1 GB
I ever actually watched the 30B load from. I still have not measured the swap. So the honest
landing is not a verdict; it is three cases held apart — release sound, standalone load
works‑but‑marginal, swap conditionally viable pending the one measurement I kept talking
around. Three swings now, each corrected from outside the numbers: a reviewer's skepticism,
then the operator's experience, then the reviewer drawing the line between the scenario I
measured (the swap's tight pool) and the one he runs (the leaned standalone box).

So I finally ran the measurement I had been circling. Live BlarAI, release the 14B in its
own process, and read what the 30B would actually have to load into — from the real ambient
the box happened to be in, not one I had quietly cleaned first. The bare swap landed at
20.1 GB: the release freed 11.3 gigabytes cleanly, but with BlarAI still resident the floor
sits two gigabytes under the cold baseline and three under the 23.1 GB I ever watched the
30B load from. Sub-threshold. So I did not load the 30B — from there it is a death-spiral,
and the gate the reviewer asked for is exactly the thing that should refuse it. The
swap-back leg held: the 14B rebuilt in twenty-one seconds, coherent. The honest CASE 3 is
therefore measured, not extrapolated — the bare swap is not viable, and a viable swap is one
that *actively* claws the box up past twenty-three gigabytes (full step-aside plus an ambient
trim) before it dares the load, and verifies it got there.

**Next:** the §4 gate is now designable against a measured number. A bare 14B-release is not
enough — it lands ~20 GB here, sub-threshold — so the swap must fully step BlarAI aside *and*
trim the ambient to clear ~23–25 GB, then verify before it loads, aborting otherwise (which
the measurement shows the gate correctly doing). The one load still unmeasured is the 30B
from a swap that has *reached* a viable headroom; it was deferred because it reproduces the
marginal standalone case already on record. A lighter load path — mmap/streamed weights, or a
smaller coder — would lift the whole question by shrinking the ~29 GB transient: a worthwhile
optimisation, possibly an OpenVINO upstream lead. The harness (`scripts/measure_14b_release.py`)
stays a reusable tool; the live hook was re-added for this measurement and removed again
(launcher reverted clean, no diff vs HEAD).

**Lesson 151:** *Confirm the measurement and the ground-truth fact are about the same
scenario before you let one override the other.* My instrument and the operator were never
in conflict — I had measured the swap's tight pool; he was describing the leaned standalone
box he runs daily. Treating his fact as a refutation of my number (or my number as a
refutation of his fact) is how I talked myself into successive wrong calls. The numbers were
real every time; the error was attaching a verdict to the wrong scenario. (An earlier draft
of this lesson — "when the instrument disagrees with the operator, the instrument is
suspect" — is itself too strong: that framing is how you rationalise the next bad call.)

### 2026-06-21 — Dispatching to a fleet you must not become

The headless-coding capability has a tempting wrong shape: give BlarAI a coder. The
agentic-setup side already built one — a 30B behind OVMS, isolated worktrees, a
deterministic verify gate, secret-scan, resample, review, auto-merge — battle-tested
on this exact host. The right move, and the one the brief insists on, is to DISPATCH
to it rather than re-implement it. So increment 1 of the wiring is deliberately
small: a `/dispatch` command whose backend enqueues a task to the fleet's documented
`add-fleet-task.ps1`, triggers `run-fleet.ps1`, and reads back the run's
`SUMMARY.txt` — calling the fleet's entry points, never touching its internals, never
hosting a coder. The fleet already refuses any repo under `~/BlarAI`; I mirrored that
refusal as a fail-fast guard so a dispatch can only target the operator's OTHER
projects, scoped to the configured projects dir.

The interesting constraint is milestone 1 biting from a new angle. The 14B and the
fleet's 30B cannot co-reside in 31 GB. So the brief's vision — "the 14B decomposes
the idea into tasks, then the fleet runs them" — is two phases separated by a model
swap, not one flow. Increment 1 therefore does NOT use the 14B at all: the
"decomposition" is a deterministic one-task framing, and the user's request becomes a
single fleet task. The 14B-driven decomposition — the part the brief itself warns not
to trust until last — rides on the swap automation, which is increment 2. I told the
operator exactly where my decomposition diverged from his wording rather than quietly
building the thing that can't run.

Architecturally I kept it gateway-direct. The imagine/ingest coordinators route
through the AO because their work needs the GPU pipeline or the encrypted store;
dispatch needs neither — it is deterministic host subprocess plus file reads, and the
gateway is already host-side. So `/dispatch` parses in the gateway, calls a shared
`shared/fleet/dispatch.py` engine directly, and replies — no new IPC verb, no AO
round-trip. The engine is the reusable piece: increment 2's swap driver calls the
same `run_fleet` (already written — launched detached with CREATE_BREAKAWAY_FROM_JOB
so it outlives a BlarAI teardown, and resumable by RunId either way).

The whole surface ships dormant. `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false` threads through the
AO config to the gateway exactly like the `images_enabled` weld-lock; with it false
the coordinator returns a clear notice and no subprocess is ever spawned. The
security frame is small but worth stating: a local PowerShell subprocess is local
exec, NOT network egress — the egress guard governs sockets, and is untouched here —
so the new surface adds no egress, fails closed (vector argv, no shell, bounded
timeout; any non-zero / timeout / exception becomes a clear failure, never a silent
success), and is target-scoped to the operator's projects.

25 new tests — 15 across the engine (target validation, the BlarAI-repo refusal,
SUMMARY/`RESULT:` parsing, fail-closed enqueue, the detached trigger) and 10 across
the coordinator (parse, dormant-when-disabled, run, status, fail-soft); the
pre-existing SSOT/WinUI allowlist gate (2 tests) turned green the moment `/dispatch`
was in both the Python constant and the C# mirror — the guard that has twice caught
a silently-dropped backend command. The one thing mocks cannot prove — that
BlarAI's enqueue argv matches the REAL `add-fleet-task.ps1` parameter contract — a
headless enqueue smoke settled: one task written to the live queue came back as the
exact `{repo, task, prompt}` schema, then the queue was restored byte-identical (no
live run triggered, the operator's pending task untouched).

**Next:** increment 2 — the host-level Option-A swap driver that increment 1
deliberately left to the operator: fully step BlarAI aside, load the 30B (the
standalone load already proven), run the fleet, swap back and reload the 14B with a
coherence check, all crash-recoverable and never ending at zero models. Then, only
then, the 14B decomposer (the brief's step 7), backed by templates and
pre-validated rulers, never self-certified. The pre-load headroom gate it needs is
the load-bearing one milestone 1 measured — set it to what actually loads (~the whole
pool), not start-llm's too-low 21 GB, and never the `-Force` path that skips its own
check.

**Lesson 152:** *Dispatch to the capability; don't become it.* The thinnest
correct integration here is a queue file and a summary read — but only because the
hard parts (isolation, verify, merge, the model itself) already live on the other
side of the fence. Re-implementing any of them inside BlarAI would have traded a
~200-line engine for a second coder to contain.

*(commit on branch `feat/fleet-dispatch-i1`: `shared/fleet/dispatch.py` engine +
`dispatch_coordinator` + the dormant `[fleet_dispatch]` config + `/dispatch`
SSOT/WinUI + gateway/dispatcher wiring; 25 new tests; full standing gate 3911
passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped; headless enqueue smoke proved the real
add-fleet-task.ps1 contract.)*

### 2026-06-22 — The swap you can only watch run on the real box

Increment 2 is the one genuinely hard part of the headless-coding work: the 14B⇄30B model swap.
The brief had a clean state machine for it (§4.2) — unload the 14B in-process, gate on ~21 GB,
load the 30B, run, swap back. Milestone-1 had already told me that plan doesn't survive contact
with this box: a bare in-process unload frees only to ~20.1 GB, below the 30B's ~22-23 GB load
peak, because ~6 GB of the live AO process (Python heap, the INT8 draft, the Level-Zero context,
the KV/embedding caches) is freed only by a process EXIT. So the design diverges where the
measurement forces it: full step-aside means the launcher process exits, and because a process
can't tear itself down and bring itself back, a detached host driver (breakaway, the increment-1
pattern) has to run the swap and relaunch the launcher. I told the LA exactly where I was leaving
the brief and why.

The discipline that paid off was building the whole thing with the side-effects injected. Every
dangerous operation — read free RAM, start-llm, run-fleet, Stop-Process ovms, relaunch the
launcher, the AO health probe, the out-of-band failure signal — is a `SwapOps` callable, so the
state machine itself is unit-tested without ever touching a real model. That let me prove the
invariants that matter for a destructive subsystem: NEVER-ZERO is structural (the teardown that
stops the 30B and restarts the 14B runs on every path — clean finish, gate-abort, settle-timeout,
load-fail, cancel, and even an unexpected exception that re-raises only after the 14B is back);
RESTART-AO has a bounded retry with a readiness probe and a loud out-of-band signal when the
backend genuinely won't come back (the most dangerous gap the LA flagged — zero models with a
down assistant that can't notify through itself); cancel is per-task; the watchdog sentinel is
always disarmed before OVMS is stopped. Forty-two tests, all at the seam.

Two of the LA's review gaps shaped the data model. Crash recovery is an idempotent reconciler
wired into every backend boot, anchored on a write-ahead swap-state file plus the fleet's
sentinel: disarm, stop any resident 30B, cold-load the 14B, report-or-interrupted — it converges
to "14B up, 30B down" after a soft crash or a reboot, and it's a no-op (no subprocess) on every
normal boot because it's existence-gated. And the swap-state file holds only `{run_id, session_id,
tasks, phase}` — never conversation content; the conversation reloads from the encrypted
sessions.db by session_id, so the privacy-absolute mandate isn't broken by a recovery file. A test
asserts the dataclass has exactly those fields, so the privacy property can't silently regress.

The honest edge of this increment is what I deliberately did NOT wire live. The investigation
resolved the two build-time confirmations into facts that change the shape: the backend runs
in-process in the launcher (there is no separable "backend" to bounce), and the WinUI has zero
