reconnect logic, so "keep the window up and reconnect" isn't available without new C# — the build
takes the LA's pre-authorized full-relaunch fallback, and the window closes for the swap and
reopens after. The final command-hookup — wiring the orchestration into the live /dispatch through
the gateway and launcher, with the launcher's step-aside callback — lands WITH the operator's
on-hardware step, because the launcher-exit→relaunch dance is only validatable on the real box and
wiring it unvalidated into the live boot path would add risk no test could catch. The swap
*subsystem* is done and proven; the last seam is welded on the hardware.

One number moved late: the LA set the pre-load gate to 21, not the 24 I'd recommended from the
milestone-1 margin. 21 matches start-llm's own gate and false-aborts less; 24 kept ~1-2 GB above
the measured load peak. I flagged the trade-off — at 21 a marginal load thrashes-then-loads rather
than hard-failing because the gate is graceful — and set it to 21 as directed, with the
alternative on the record. It's a config key, so it moves without a code change.

**Next:** the LA reviews the built subsystem; on approval, the on-hardware go-live wires the
command-hookup and runs the first real swap (flip enabled, /dispatch an idea, watch the 14B step
aside, the 30B build, the 14B come back), and #670 tracks the WinUI-reconnect follow-up for the
window-stays-up UX.

**Lesson 153:** *Inject the danger so you can test the logic.* A subsystem whose whole job is
irreversible side-effects (kill the model, load another, relaunch the app) is exactly the one you
must be able to drive in a test — make every side-effect a passed-in callable, and the invariants
(never-zero, disarm-before-stop, bounded-retry-then-signal) become assertions instead of hopes.
The real subprocess wrappers stay thin and are the only thing left to verify on hardware.

*(commits on `feat/fleet-dispatch-i2`: `c3c10aa` swap-state+reconciler+decompose, `1b2c291` the
driver state machine, `1eafa94` config+boot-hook+real-ops+detached-entry, + this commit's
orchestration + ADR-034 + register row; 42 increment-2 tests; full standing gate re-run for
regression.)*

### 2026-06-22 — The stage that wasn't there, and the check that never ran

Increment 3 of the headless-coding dispatch was supposed to be the friendly part. Increments 1 and 2 were the plumbing — enqueue a task to the agentic-setup fleet, automate the 14B⇄30B swap. Increment 3 is the layer that lets a non-developer (the operator, by his own description) hand BlarAI a plain goal — "a calculator an eight-year-old can use" — and get back work that is actually *checked*, without ever writing a test. The shape was clear from the brief: the 14B turns the goal into acceptance criteria, each criterion tagged by how it's verified — build, behavior, smoke, visual, human — and the objective ones ride the fleet's existing verify gate while the operator eyeballs the rest.

Two things turned a tidy feature into a lesson about honesty.

The first I found before writing a line of it, by actually reading the fleet I was dispatching to instead of trusting the brief's description of it. The brief's tier table mapped SMOKE — "launches, no crash, clean console" — onto "the fleet gate (launch + console scan)." So I sent an Explore agent through `new-agent-task.ps1` and `verify-project.ps1` to confirm the seam. There is no launch-and-console-scan stage. The fleet builds, runs `pytest`/`npm test`, runs `verify-project.ps1` (build/lint/typecheck), asks a review agent, and auto-merges. SMOKE-as-the-brief-imagined-it doesn't exist. That's the difference between mapping onto a system you assumed and mapping onto the one that's there. I delivered SMOKE as a behavior-tier "it imports and starts without raising" test that rides the real TESTS stage, and said so plainly rather than inventing a fleet stage to match a diagram.

The second the LA caught, and it's the one that matters. Reading the same scripts more closely: `verify-project.ps1` runs `dotnet build` for .NET, but there is no `dotnet test`, and the TESTS stage is `pytest`/`npm test` only. So for a C#/UWP app — the calculator, the running example — a behavior or smoke test *never runs*. It comes back `none`. The trap is obvious once named: an acceptance layer whose entire reason to exist is to prevent false confidence would, if it treated `none` as "fine," hand the operator a clean green report for math it never checked. The whole thing would become the rubber stamp it was meant to replace. So `criterion_status` returns `verified` only on an actual `pass`; `none` and `skip` become UNVERIFIED — "NOT AUTO-CHECKED, please verify yourself" — and the report renders that distinctly from a pass, with the .NET limitation stated *up front* at the confirm step so a clean-looking report can never mislead. I went with honest-asymmetric assurance — real behavior-gating for Python and Node, build-only for .NET, said out loud — over the alternative of adding `dotnet test` to the fleet, because the fleet's internals are explicitly not mine to change. The gap is named as a future item, not closed by reaching where I wasn't supposed to.

The LA also collapsed my command surface to one flow. I had proposed keeping increment 1's immediate "enqueue and run now" path as a power-user fast-path alongside the new confirm flow. The ruling: no. Every `/dispatch` goes through the acceptance layer and the mandatory confirm; "skip the model swap when the 30B is already loaded" survives only as an internal branch of EXECUTE, never as a surface that fires work without the criteria confirm. "Always confirm" means always — and a non-developer should never have a path that does work he didn't approve. I made the confirm mandatory by construction: there is no code path that reaches EXECUTE except the approve verb. A test asserts that a plan, a reject, and a status check all call the execute function zero times.

One more correctness call paid off a question the LA raised at the 3a review. My first `compile_prompts` baked the acceptance criteria into *every* task's prompt. For a single-task dispatch that's harmless, but the fleet runs each task in its own worktree that auto-merges independently — so every task writing the same acceptance-test files would collide at the per-task merges. The fix is a dedicated final `acceptance-tests` task that runs after the feature tasks merge: one test-writer, written against the complete code. Because the feature tasks auto-merge first, that task *reports and best-effort-repairs* rather than hard-blocking the feature merge — a post-hoc gate can't un-merge, and a hard pre-merge gate would mean changing the fleet's internals, which isn't mine to do. It costs an extra 30B run; correctness, a clean merge, and honesty about what the gate can do are worth it.

It ships dormant, as increments 1 and 2 did — `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false`, and the PLAN/EXECUTE wiring to the assistant deliberately unset, so an enabled-but-unwired `/dispatch` confirms and then says "wiring not connected" rather than firing. 3a is the pure core (`shared/fleet/acceptance.py`); 3b is the gateway confirm flow plus the honest report. The live AO-IPC round-trip and the launcher step-aside on approve are the on-hardware go-live, because the launcher-exit-then-relaunch dance is only validatable live.

The honest gap I'm leaving in, on the record: `criterion_status` trusts `TESTS: pass` at face value. A vacuous coder-written test that asserts nothing still reads `verified`. Closing that needs red-first or mutation validation — confirm the test actually fails on broken code before trusting its pass. That's Enhancement-1, deferred and tracked, named here so it isn't quietly forgotten the way deferred hardening usually is.

**Lesson 154:** *Read the system you're dispatching to before you map onto it.* The brief described a fleet stage (launch + console scan) that doesn't exist; an hour reading the actual `*.ps1` scripts turned a feature that would have silently under-delivered into one that's honest about what it can and can't check. A diagram of a dependency is a hypothesis; the dependency's source is the fact.

**Lesson 155:** *An unrun check is not a pass.* The most dangerous output of an assurance layer is a green light for something it never tested. When a gate can't run a check (no `dotnet test`, pytest absent), the honest status is UNVERIFIED, surfaced as loudly as a failure — never folded into "looks good." A verification feature that rounds "didn't check" up to "passed" is worse than no feature, because it manufactures the false confidence it was built to remove.

**Next:** the on-hardware go-live — wire the PLAN/EXECUTE AO-IPC round-trip and the launcher step-aside on approve, then run the first real goal→confirm→swap→build→report→open-the-app on the Arc 140V. Then Enhancement-1 (red-first/mutation validation) to close the trust-the-test gap. Runbook: `docs/runbooks/headless_coding_dispatch_go_live.md`.

*(commits `5300572` (3a — pure acceptance core, 43 tests), `1a2280f` (3b — single always-confirm `DispatchCoordinator` + honest report + ADR-035 + DECISION_REGISTER row), `1a803e7` (ADR-035 acceptance-task semantics + Enhancement-1 go-live note); merged to main `f8d0f5d`; on-main standing gate 4014 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped / 118 deselected; the live PLAN/EXECUTE wiring + first real run await the on-hardware go-live.)*

### 2026-06-22 — Wiring the dormant dispatch to a backend that can step out of its own way

Phase A took the headless-coding dispatch from "built but wired into nothing" to live-capable, without ever flipping the switch. The acceptance layer (increment 3) and the swap driver (increment 2) both shipped dormant; Phase A is the seam between them and the running backend — the part I'd deferred precisely because it's the fiddly, live-validated bit. The operator wanted it decomposed and shown piece by piece, dormant throughout, with the model flip reserved for an on-hardware shakedown. So it went in five reviewable commits: housekeeping, the PLAN round-trip, the EXECUTE core, the EXECUTE wiring, and the launcher glue.

The first lesson was the operator's, and it stung in the right way. Sub-part 1 made the fleet target config-driven — the compiled-in `~/agentic-setup` and `~/projects` became `[fleet_dispatch]` config keys, so a non-default box isn't hard-wired. I threaded it through the gateway and called #670 done. The operator didn't: the boot-time swap *reconciler* — the crash-recovery that restores the 14B after a half-finished swap — still built its config from the compiled-in fallback. On a default box it's invisible (fallback == config). But on a custom root, once swaps go live, the EXECUTE path would *write* swap-state under the configured root while the reconciler *read* the old one, so it would never find the in-flight swap and the never-end-at-zero recovery would silently never fire. A half-promoted config is worse than a hardcoded one: a hardcoded value is at least consistent; a config that only *some* consumers honor desyncs the writer from the reader. The fix was a small testable seam (`reconcile_at_boot_for_roots`) and a regression test that pins the writer's swap-state path equal to the reconciler's under a custom root. I'd audited the obvious consumer and missed the one that mattered for safety.

The reply-then-step-aside ordering is the kind of thing that's obvious once stated and silent if missed. EXECUTE fires the swap and then the launcher has to *exit* — fully, to free the 14B's GPU memory for the 30B. But the WinUI window dies with the backend. If the AO stepped aside before sending its "stepping aside" reply, the operator's window would close mid-sentence and they'd never see why. So the handler enqueues, hands off, sends the `EXECUTE_RESULT` reply, waits a configurable grace, and *only then* signals the step-aside — and only on success: an enqueue refusal sends the failure and leaves the 14B up. A test pins the event order to exactly `["reply", "step_aside"]`, and another proves no step-aside on the failure path. When a process is about to take itself down, flush the last word first; the order is load-bearing because the UI is mortal with the process.

The genuinely hard seam — the one that only truly validates on hardware — is that the AO runs on a daemon thread and a daemon thread cannot `sys.exit` the process. Calling the launcher's cleanup directly from it would self-join the AO's own serve-loop. So the step-aside asks the *main* thread to exit: `_thread.interrupt_main()` raises a `KeyboardInterrupt` up there, which unwinds to `atexit`→`_cleanup` (unload the 14B, free the GPU), the process exits, the detached driver loads the 30B and later relaunches the launcher. That whole chain — the interrupt actually terminating cleanly, the GPU actually releasing, the WinUI actually closing and reopening, the 30B actually *fitting* in the freed pool — is the live-only boundary, and I said so plainly in the ADR and every commit: the green suite proves the wiring and the ordering and the fail-closed paths, and proves *none* of the physical swap. That distinction is the whole point of writing the boundary down. The progress log exists for the same reason the boundary does: the operator is blind while the window is gone, so the AO and the detached driver both append a human-readable trail to the same run-id-keyed log — stepping aside, 30B loading, gate pass or abort, fleet running, swapping back, 14B restored — surfaced by `/dispatch status` after the box returns. (The operator caught that I'd built the log writer and the reader but wired nothing to *show* it — written into a void. Now `/dispatch status` reads it.)

One honesty correction worth keeping: a sub-part-2 commit claimed `os` "was never imported in entrypoint.py." It was — function-locally, twice — so no production path was ever broken; the real change was adding a *module-level* `os` for the new `_fire_swap`. The operator flagged the overclaim against the actual code. The accurate note: module-level `os` added for `_fire_swap`; the two now-redundant local imports tidied. Small, but the journal that rounds "I added an import" up to "I fixed a latent bug" is the journal that stops compounding.

It all ships dormant — `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false`, and a built-but-wired dormancy guard constructs the *real* gateway, proves both the PLAN and EXECUTE seams are wired, and proves the flag alone keeps everything inert. The flip, and the first real goal→confirm→swap→build→report→open-the-app, are the Phase-B shakedown on the Arc 140V — and that shakedown is expected to take more than one run, because the model swap has never been fired end to end.

**Lesson 156:** *When a value becomes config-driven, audit every consumer — a half-promoted config is worse than a hardcoded one.* Promoting `~/agentic-setup` to a config key looked done when the gateway honored it, but the boot reconciler still read the compiled-in fallback; on a custom root the swap-state writer and the crash-recoverer would silently disagree and the restore-the-14B recovery would never fire. A constant is at least consistent everywhere; a config that only some readers honor desyncs the writer from the reader exactly where it's most dangerous. Grep every consumer of the old constant before calling the promotion complete, and pin writer-path == reader-path with a test.

**Lesson 157:** *Reply before you exit; a daemon thread can't take down its own process.* When a backend has to terminate itself to free a resource, the last message to the user must be sent and flushed before the teardown, because the UI dies with the process — order is correctness, not polish. And the teardown itself can't come from a daemon thread (it would self-join or skip cleanup): it has to signal the main thread to unwind through the normal `atexit` path. Wire that signal as an injectable seam, test the wiring, and mark the actual process-death as live-only — a green suite can prove you asked to exit, never that the exit was clean.

**Next:** the Phase-B on-hardware shakedown — flip `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=true`, dispatch a tiny throwaway Python task, let the full swap fire, and iterate on the live-only list (the daemon→main signal terminating cleanly, the reply-then-exit timing, the driver surviving teardown, the real 30B fitting, the 14B coming back) until it's clean; then the first real task, de-risked. Runbook: `docs/runbooks/headless_coding_dispatch_go_live.md`.

*(commits `8a482b6` (sub-part 1 housekeeping — config-driven #670 root + prompt hardening), `a2f80b1` (the boot-reconciler config-root fix the LA caught), `e223a86` (sub-part 2 — PLAN round-trip), `003b0ec` (the fell-back degradation notice), `de0875d` (3a — EXECUTE pass-through + writer-root + relaunch capture), `970dd5b` (3b-1 — EXECUTE wiring, reply-then-step-aside, dormancy guard), `7579942` (3b-2 — launcher glue + driver progress + ADR-035 Am.1 + this entry's fragment), `6240b91` (the AO-side EXECUTE/PLAN dormancy-lock restore the LA caught at merge-time); merged to main `2333229`; on-main standing gate 4064 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped / 118 deselected; all DORMANT behind `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false` — the live swap is the Phase-B shakedown.)*

### 2026-06-22 — The swap assumed it was alone

Phase B finally ran the model swap on the box for the first time — and it Fail-Closed-exited before it ever loaded the 30B. Two real bugs, both the same shape: the swap-relaunch was built and tested as a single-instance, in-process operation, and the live box was neither. The operator had four launcher instances running.

The headline failure was a TLS handshake — `CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED` at the gateway↔Policy-Agent mTLS, a Fail-Closed exit at boot — and the cause took a careful read to state precisely, where the precision is the point. Per-boot cert provisioning (ADR-026) mints a fresh in-memory Certificate Authority on every launcher boot and writes the leaf certs into one shared `<repo_root>/certs/` dir, overwriting the last set — and there was no guard against more than one launcher. Four boots, four CAs, one directory: each stomped the others, so a service presenting a leaf signed by boot-A's CA to a peer trusting boot-D's CA fails verification. My first draft of the root cause called the BlarAI-Orchestrator VM "another stale-CA holder," and the operator corrected me, rightly. The VM did not contribute: `[guest_parser].enabled=false`, so the VM is not a per-boot-cert consumer, and the PA mTLS is host-side loopback. The failure was host-side multi-instance, full stop. A cert-resync-to-VM channel — which the bug report floated as one option — would have solved a problem that did not exist, so we did not build it. Naming the cause precisely is what kept us from building the wrong fix.

The second bug was quieter: the relaunch came up under system `Python311`, not the project venv, because `compute_relaunch_argv` defaulted to `sys.executable` — whatever interpreter started the firing launcher. And a third thread ran underneath both: the step-aside that is supposed to bring the old launcher down asks the main thread to exit via `_thread.interrupt_main()`, but if the WinUI native event loop is parked the interrupt is never delivered, so the old process never dies and the swap driver's settle deadlocks waiting on it.

Three fixes, all dormant. A single-instance guard — a per-checkout PID-file lock acquired before cert provisioning — so a second launcher refuses cleanly with a message that *is* the UX of the fix ("BlarAI is already running (PID N) — close that one first") and hard-exits without running `_cleanup`, because a refused instance that ran the always-stop VM cleanup would stop the *live* instance's VM. The guard confirms the holder is really a launcher (its cmdline runs `-m launcher`) before refusing, so a recycled PID after a crash reclaims the lock instead of falsely insisting BlarAI is already running — the one false-positive the operator would actually hit in a crash-relaunch. The venv fix makes the relaunch resolve the checkout's own `.venv` deterministically. And the guaranteed-termination watchdog forces the process down when the graceful interrupt doesn't land — because the load-bearing fix for a never-delivered signal is to stop waiting for it, not to wait longer; graceful first for a clean release, forceful as the guarantee, with a started-teardown flag so the wedged case is forced immediately while a real teardown is allowed to finish.

What's honest to keep on the record: A/B/C only clear the cert-and-instance wall. The swap's actual core — the 30B loading and *fitting* in the freed pool, building, and the 14B coming back — is still unproven, because run-1 never reached it. The forced exit terminating, the GPU releasing in time for the 30B (~25 GB free clean, the 14B ~8.7 GB → should fit), the swap-back — those are the live unknowns the next run tests for the first time. Twenty-two unit tests prove the wiring and the decisions; none of them prove the physical swap. That distinction is the whole point of writing it down — and the re-run is expected to surface fresh wrinkles past the wall, which is the shakedown working, not a regression.

**Lesson 158:** *A control tested in isolation can still rest on an unstated single-instance assumption.* The relaunch passed every unit test and the standing gate, then failed the first time it ran on the box because four instances shared one per-boot certs dir. "Concurrency = 1" must be made explicit and enforced (a single-instance lock before the shared write), not inherited silently; a shared per-boot artifact with no instance guard is a latent multi-instance bug.

**Lesson 159:** *When you depend on a signal being delivered, guarantee termination — don't widen the window.* The step-aside's `interrupt_main()` was never delivered (a parked native loop), so the old process never exited and the relaunch deadlocked. A wider window cannot wake a thread that will never wake; only a watchdog that forces `os._exit` when the graceful path doesn't complete guarantees it.

**Next:** the re-run from one clean instance, box leaned — the first real test of the swap mechanics (30B load + FIT + build + 14B swap-back), with the live-only list (forced exit terminating, GPU reclaiming) front of mind. Runbook: `docs/runbooks/headless_coding_dispatch_go_live.md`.

*(commits `<this>` on `fix/670-swap-relaunch-single-instance` — A `launcher/instance_lock.py` + the Step-1.4 guard, B `compute_relaunch_argv` venv resolution, C `launcher/step_aside.py` watchdog; 22 net-new tests; ADR-034 Amendment 1 + DECISION_REGISTER same change; all DORMANT behind `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false` — the live swap is the re-run.)*

### 2026-06-22 — The thirty-second load the driver never saw

The model swap fired for the second time, and this run carried clean through everything run-1 couldn't reach: a single-instance boot, the gateway↔Policy-Agent mTLS handshake on the first attempt, dispatch → approve → step-aside → the headroom gate → and the 30B coder model loading. A/B/C had cleared the cert-and-instance wall exactly as built. Then the driver hung at "loading the 30B" and sat there for ten minutes until the operator recovered it by hand.

The first explanation was the obvious one, and it was wrong. The box's Arc iGPU has roughly 16 GB for the GPU; the 14B needs ~8.7 and the 30B ~15, so they cannot co-reside, and the natural read was a GPU out-of-memory — the 30B trying to load onto a GPU the force-exited 14B hadn't released. It's a clean, physical story, and it was held confidently. So the instruction was to confirm it in the OVMS load log — and the log said something else. The 30B reached `state changed to: AVAILABLE` thirty seconds after launch, the error log was zero bytes, and start-llm had armed its watchdog sentinel and started its coding-proxy — every sign that the model loaded and start-llm *knew* it was ready. The 30B loaded and fit on this box. The hang was not the GPU.

What hung was the driver's own plumbing. `real_load_30b` ran start-llm through `_safe_run`, which captures stdout and stderr through a pipe and waits for them to close. But start-llm launches two long-lived processes — OVMS itself and a stateless tool-call proxy — and on Windows those grandchildren inherit the captured pipe. So even though start-llm loaded the 30B and exited zero, the pipe never reached end-of-file (the proxy was still holding it, and was *still* running as I wrote this), and the wait blocked forever — past start-llm's own deadline, past the subprocess timeout, because the timeout's cleanup tries to drain the very pipe that won't close. The 30B was ready at 15:44:38; the driver was deadlocked and never looked up. Had we "fixed the GPU," the deadlock would have shipped untouched.

The fix has two parts. The pipe one is small and exact: the start-llm launch now writes to a per-run log file with `close_fds` instead of an inheritable capture pipe, so a long-lived grandchild can't wedge the wait — and the generic `_safe_run` stays exactly as it was for the build/test/verify callers that genuinely parse their stdout (a regression test pins that it still captures). The GPU one is the part the wrong hypothesis got right for the wrong reason: even though run-2 didn't OOM, it only fit because the abrupt `os._exit` happened to free the 14B's GPU within OVMS's thirty-second load window — timing luck, since the confirmed-dead interrupt path means the graceful unload never runs. So the step-aside watchdog now explicitly releases the 14B's GPU context — a new exit-path release that bypasses the reload guard, bounded so a hung unload can't defeat the termination guarantee — before it force-exits, and the driver wait-verifies GPU-free before loading the 30B, logging it before and after so the handoff is measured rather than assumed.

The honest scope on that last part: this is an iGPU, so its GPU memory *is* system RAM, and the Windows counters under-report what OpenVINO actually allocates — half a gigabyte reported against a fifteen-gigabyte model. So "GPU-free" is read as system-RAM-free, the pool the iGPU draws from, and the new GPU gate effectively rides the same pool as the existing RAM gate. The graceful unload is the real guarantee; the gate and the logging are the measurement. Naming that plainly mattered more than dressing it up as a GPU-budget probe the hardware won't honestly give.

**Lesson 160:** *The log decides, not the hypothesis.* The hang looked like a GPU OOM and was confidently called one; the OVMS log showed a clean 30-second load to AVAILABLE, and the real cause was a captured-pipe deadlock that hung the driver after the model was ready. Verify the failure mechanism against the evidence before you fix it.

**Lesson 161:** *Capturing a subprocess's output deadlocks if it spawns a long-lived grandchild.* The grandchildren (OVMS + the coding-proxy) inherit the captured pipe on Windows, so it never EOFs and the wait blocks forever after the child exits. Redirect a daemon-spawning launcher's output to a file with `close_fds`; keep capture for short subprocesses whose stdout you read.

**Next:** the single re-run with both fixes, from a clean box (the orphan proxy killed, the GPU clear of the operator's standalone 30B). The pipe fix should carry the driver *through* — past "loading the 30B" to "fleet running" — and the graceful unload should hand the GPU over clean. Still live-only and unproven past here: the GPU context actually releasing, the 30B's build of the task, and the 14B coming back. Watch the instrumented GPU-free before/after and the progress trail through fleet-run → swap-back → 14B restored.

*(commit `<this>` on `fix/670-swap-core-pipe-and-gpu` — (1) `swap_ops._run_to_logfile`/`real_load_30b` file-redirect launch, (2a) `SharedInferencePipeline.release_gpu_for_exit` + the watchdog's bounded GPU-unload, (2b/2c) the driver GPU wait-verify gate + before/after instrumentation; 14 net-new tests; ADR-034 Amendment 2 + DECISION_REGISTER same change; all DORMANT behind `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false`.)*

### 2026-06-22 — The ruler that collapsed the wrong things first

The headless round-trip finally writes real code, and the first thing it showed me was
that it writes *too much of it*. Ask the 14B for "an `is_leap_year` function" and it
returned nine fleet tasks — `define-is-leap-year-function`, `implement-leap-year-logic`,
a task *per test case* (`test-…-2024`, `-1900`, `-2000`), `verify-edge-cases`,
`acceptance-tests`, and a bare `create-file` — each of which the fleet would hand its own
git worktree and its own 30-90s model-swap cycle. Thirty-three minutes for one line.
Pointed at "build an ecommerce site," the same reflex yields hundreds of micro-tasks and
the product is simply unusable. The decompose step had a deterministic "ruler," but all it
did was slugify, dedupe-by-exact-slug, and cap at eight — it never collapsed an atomic
split, so the explosion sailed straight through.

The architecture gave me the shape of the fix for free: the acceptance layer already
appends exactly one dedicated test task downstream, so the decomposer's job is only to
emit the *fewest coherent feature tasks*. Collapse the per-test-case / `define-` /
`scaffold-` / `create-file` splits; keep the features; let the layer add the test. Simple
enough that I nearly shipped it on a single green run. The reason I didn't is the only
reason this entry is worth keeping.

I ran a four-lens adversarial red-team against the rule before trusting it, and it
caught **two false-collapses I had already written** — both in the dangerous direction,
both invisible to my own happy-path tests. The first was a leaf-gate I was proud of: if a
goal *looks* small (names a single "function"/"rule"/"endpoint"), cap it to one task. It
worked on leap-year — and silently dropped real work everywhere else. "Write a linter
rule" decomposes to rule + visitor + fixer, three genuine features, and my cap guillotined
two of them. The second was subtler and more embarrassing: to stop the rule treating a
*named verification deliverable* as a structural test, I followed the panel's first
instinct and gated the test-drop on "does its object share a root with a sibling feature."
That fixed `verify-signature` paired with `build-tpm-client`… until the red-team noticed it
*didn't*, and that `verify_pcr_seal.py` is a real file in this very tree. The genuine fix
wasn't a *restriction* on when to drop — it was a *rescue*: a `verify`/`test`-led task
whose object is a cryptographic-integrity noun (`signature`, `checksum`, `seal`) or a
deployable artifact (`runner`, `generator`, `endpoint`) *is* the deliverable and stays a
feature, while an assertion phrase (`returns-…`, `-503`) marks it structural even when it
names an artifact. With the rescue in place I could delete the leaf-cap entirely — the
collapse alone right-sizes leap-year to one.

Then I ran a *second*, post-build adversarial pass — three lenses running the shipped rule
against new cases — specifically to break what I now believed was correct, and it earned
its cost immediately. The integrity-noun rescue was a *narrow allow-list*, and an entire
ordinary class of product features lives just outside it: `verify-email-address`,
`verify-otp-code`, `verify-age`, `verify-user-permissions`, `verify-manifest` — email
confirmation, OTP login, an age gate, an authorization layer, update integrity. Every one
classified as a structural test and got dropped, because "email"/"age"/"otp" aren't in any
allow-set. The worst shape: `verify-email` + `verify-sms` collapsed to a *single* survivor
and the discarded one wasn't even flagged to the never-zero-tests floor — a whole
deliverable vanished without a trace. The control made it undeniable: the *same* feature
slugged `implement-email-verification` survived untouched. The drop was driven purely by
which verb the 14B happened to pick. No allow-list of nouns can ever be complete, so the
real fix abandoned the list for a *test of shape*: a `verify`/`test` task is structural only
when, after removing the tokens it shares with a sibling plus assertion/number tokens, it
has **no deliverable noun of its own left**. `test-add` beside `implement-add-todo` keeps
nothing (drop); `verify-email-address` keeps `address` (a real noun → feature). Distinct
verify-features (`verify-email` + `verify-sms`) share no token, so neither is swept; only a
per-case *enumeration* — several test/verify siblings sharing a stem like
`test-iso8601-pt1h`/`-p1dt12h` — collapses together. After that change: zero false-collapses
across both red-teams' corpora, every disguised-feature goal surviving.

The trade-off I took, on the record: a slug alone cannot tell a standalone
`verify-pagination` (a test) from a standalone `verify-email` (a feature), so I tuned the
rule to **never drop the feature** and accept that three irreducibly-ambiguous standalone
per-case tests (`verify-pagination`, `test-constant-column`, `test-kitten-sitting`) survive
as documented over-keeps — single-digit, never a dropped feature, the ideal noted in the
eval beside each. Over-keep is the safe direction; squeezing those out would re-introduce
exactly the false-collapse the adversarial pass just spent itself eliminating. The eval has
teeth precisely because its over-split oracle (count + a fixed list of structural slug
patterns) is defined *independently* of the collapse code — so when I reverted the ruler to
prove it, nineteen right-sizing tests went red instead of the detector quietly dying with
the thing it was meant to detect. Same discipline for the "never end at zero tests" floor:
decompose now carries a `collapsed_test_intent` flag, and the live plan path injects a smoke
criterion when it dropped the model's tests but criteria-gen produced nothing testable —
shown green, then shown red with the floor reverted.

**Next:** Problem 2 — the swap-back teardown that never fires (wrap the run in
`try/finally` so the 14B is always restored, add an overall run timeout, order the
sentinel-disarm before the OVMS stop), then the hygiene pair (write-fresh swap queue +
worktree sweep on run end). Decomposition stays dormant (`[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false`)
until the operator's shakedown.

*(Problem 1 of the decomposition-and-swapback brief; `shared/fleet/decompose.py` right-sizing
rewrite + `acceptance.py` never-zero-tests floor; a design-time red-team (wf_5bc7e0bd) and a
post-build adversarial pass (wf_a5a11a2b) each caught a distinct false-collapse class; +60
eval tests over a 38-case red-team corpus incl. 6 verify-feature regression locks, +2 floor
locks, +1 leaf-predicate lock, 3 over-split-encoding tests updated; 3 documented standalone
over-keeps; full fleet-dispatch blast radius green; two green→revert→red mutation proofs;
dormant on commit. Awaits Problem 2 + the operator shakedown.)*

### 2026-06-23 — The teardown that ran on the next boot instead of mine

The shakedown logs told a quieter story than the brief did. The brief called Problem 2 a
"missing try/finally" in the swap teardown. But the swap-state file said `phase: RECOVERED`,
not `FAILED`, and the progress trail held the line *"The coder fleet is running your approved
tasks…"* and then nothing — no *"swapping the 14B back."* The teardown had never run at all.
The 14B came back, but the next-boot reconciler restored it, not the driver. So the driver
hadn't crashed in teardown; it had never reached teardown. It was alive and **blocked inside
the task loop** — `real_run_task` running the long-lived `run-fleet → opencode →
playwright-msedge` subtree through the capture-pipe `_safe_run`, the exact Windows
`capture_output` deadlock that `b778430` had already fixed for the *load* path and never
carried to the *task* path. A grandchild holds the inherited pipe, `communicate()` blocks past
the 3600s timeout, control never returns. The fix the brief named would have hardened a path
the code never reached.

That correction mattered because it pointed the work at the right place: de-deadlock the
task-run subprocess (file-redirect + a reuse-safe process-tree kill), put a real out-of-band
budget watchdog on the whole run, make the teardown `BaseException`-safe, verify the OVMS stop
actually took, and sweep the worktrees the fleet leaves behind. All dormant, all on an isolated
worktree.

The part I'll keep is what the **design red-team caught before I wrote a line**. Eight
adversaries attacked the *design*, and three CRITICALs survived dedup — every one of them a
case where I'd discharged a guarantee with the wrong primitive. My watchdog `stop()` used
`thread.join(timeout)`, which is a bounded *wait*, not a *kill* — the in-tree `step_aside.py`
precedent literally returns with the thread still alive on timeout, so a deadline landing at
teardown entry would let the daemon fire `abort()` *during* the relaunch and tree-kill a reused
PID now owned by the just-restarted AO. My PID-reuse defense was a `create_time` pre-read that
wasn't atomic with a kill that re-opens the PID by integer, and it guarded only the root, not
the deep descendant tree. And the quietest, worst one: my "fresh queue" fix was going to write
`[]` over the operator's shared `fleet-queue.json` — wiping his manually-queued overnight tasks
on a file the swap path never even reads. A no-destructive-data violation I'd have shipped,
caught by a reviewer attacking a design instead of a diff. The fix became: don't touch the
shared queue at all, just validate the repo directly.

So the obligations got discharged with primitives that actually hold — an unbounded join plus a
structurally-inert abort flag set under the holder's lock at teardown entry (the watchdog can't
*act*, not just can't *be joined*); the direct child killed via the held Popen handle (the OS
keeps the PID reserved while a handle is open) and descendants gated on `create_time > T0` with
no blind `taskkill` fallback. I documented the one residual I cannot close — a re-parented
grandchild PID is irreducibly racy, and the boot reconciler is the named backstop — rather than
implying it away.

The trade-off I took on the watchdog lock: `abort()` holds the holder lock through the
tree-kill's bounded reap (~3s), which can delay teardown entry by that much in the rare case a
budget fire coincides with teardown. I chose correctness (no wrong-child kill, no torn read)
over shaving that window, having checked there's no deadlock — `terminate_process_tree` takes
no other lock. The alternative (release before the reap) buys milliseconds and reopens a
target-selection race; not worth it for a one-shot detached process.

The numbers: full standing gate **4192 passed / 0 failed / 20 skipped** (the skips are the
worktree's missing gitignored `bge` model + the symlink privilege, both benign). Eleven
mutation reverts, each turning its targeted test red and restoring green — including the one
that taught me my own `begin_teardown` test was masked (the None-clear caught the mutation, so
the `_tearing_down` guard looked unnecessary until I wrote a test that registered a child
*after* teardown began). A second adversarial pass over the *built* code — seven reviewers,
one of which wrote a runtime race-probe that fired `abort()` on a worker thread while teardown
held the lock — came back MERGE-READY: both obligations hold against the cited lines, zero
must-fix defects. It earned its keep anyway by finding the one real gap left: the budget>0
production seam that wires `abort`/`request_stop` had no test (every existing `run_swap` test
took the disabled branch), so I added the binding-identity lock, plus three cheap correctness
hardenings (widening the audit-write catches to `BaseException`, guarding the backoff sleep)
that make the never-zero claim literally true for every teardown write. Built `enabled=false`;
the live swap-back stays the operator's on-hardware step.

And then the part that earns this entry its place in the file: my post-build pass came back
MERGE-READY, and the independent merge-gate didn't agree. It found a real never-zero hole I'd
missed — `restart_launcher()` and `backend_ready()`, the two calls that *are* the 14B restore,
were guarded by `except Exception` while the sleep right beside them was already
`except BaseException`. A `BaseException` there is reachable (the driver is spawned
`CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP`, so a Ctrl+Break lands as `KeyboardInterrupt`), and it escapes the
retry loop after one unverified attempt, skips the probe, and masks the original exception —
flatly contradicting the docstring two lines up that promises every step is
`BaseException`-guarded and the restore is unconditionally reached. I had written the guarantee
and then left the one call that matters most outside it, and my own adversarial pass read right
past it because it was reasoning about the watchdog and the holder, not re-reading the boring
retry loop. Three `except Exception → except BaseException` edits closed it; the lesson (folded
to the top of this file as #162) is that "I reviewed it adversarially" and "it is correct" are
different claims, and an independent gate is the cheapest place to find out which one you have.

**Next:** merged to `main` (`fe3604a`, `--no-ff`), the operator's uncommitted `enabled=true`
flip preserved across the merge and the on-main gate green at 4212/0. The first live `/dispatch`
on his box is what exercises the watchdog race for real — the unit tests pin the logic, the Arc
140V proves the concurrency. Tracked at #670; an AO-owned coherence smoke-check after the 14B
reload is the filed follow-up #673 (the air-gap import control rightly keeps an HTTP client out
of the detached driver).

*(commits `df7a825` (the build: task-run de-deadlock + out-of-band budget watchdog +
BaseException-safe never-zero teardown + verify-the-stop + provenance-aware worktree sweep +
no-destructive queue fix; new `shared/fleet/proc_tree.py`) + `523990a` (the merge-gate
restore-step `BaseException` fix); gate 4192/0 on the worktree → 4212/0 on `main`; 11/11
mutation reverts caught; DORMANT (`[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false`); merged `fe3604a`,
operator's enabled=true flip preserved; live swap-back awaits the operator.)*

### 2026-06-23 — The fix the brief pointed away from

The first live shakedown of headless dispatch worked — the swap-back fired on its own, the 14B
came home, the run ended RECOVERED — but it surfaced two rough edges, and the builder brief that
landed to file them down was authored without source access. It made a guess about where one of
them lived. That guess is the interesting part of this entry.

B1 was the lone-test sibling. `write an is_palindrome function` had decomposed into two fleet
tasks: `implement-is-palindrome` and a sibling `acceptance-tests` that then ran ~24 minutes
*failing*, because it got its own git worktree and the implementation wasn't in it. The brief's
diagnosis pointed at the right-sizing ruler in `decompose.py` — the collapse must have failed to
fold the lone test task into the feature. Plausible. It was also wrong, and the brief told me to
confirm the cause before touching anything, so I did.

I traced it. The collapse in `decompose.py` is *already correct*: for `is_palindrome` it returns
exactly one task and sets `collapsed_test_intent=True`. All three of the brief's candidate
hypotheses — a ≥2-feature requirement, an `acceptance-tests` slug that misses the taxonomy, a
≤2-task guard — are refuted by the code (`acceptance-tests` is in `_RESERVED_STRUCTURAL`; the
survivors loop drops every test-kind task whenever a feature exists; there is no count guard). The
second task is *re-created downstream*, in `acceptance.py`'s `compile_prompts`, which materialises
the behavior/smoke criteria into a dedicated `acceptance-tests` fleet task whenever a test-tier
criterion and a feature task both exist. That design is right for a *multi*-feature goal — one
final test-writer against the fully-merged code, no duplicate test files across per-task merges —
and wrong only for the single-feature case, where there is one worktree and one merge and the
separate task is pure overhead plus a doomed, implementation-less worktree.

So the fix moved a whole file from where the brief sent it. `compile_prompts` now folds the test
block into the lone feature task when there is exactly one, and keeps the dedicated final task for
≥2. The consequence I had to name out loud: the brief's "add eval-corpus cases and revert-the-
collapse mutation" would have locked nothing — the corpus exercises `decompose_request`, which
already right-sizes `is_palindrome` to one, so those cases pass with or without my change. The real
mutation-resistant lock had to sit at `compile_prompts`/`generate_plan`: revert the
`len(feature)==1` fold and four tests go red (`assert 2 == 1` — the separate task comes back). I
added the corpus cases anyway (they document the single-function intent and guard the decompose
layer), but the load-bearing lock is the acceptance-layer one. Four existing tests encoded the old
single-feature behavior and are now deliberate behavior changes, flagged as such — not regressions.
The LA, gating, noticed I'd surfaced all four rather than the one he'd named.

B2 was the verify-the-stop that cried wolf. The same shakedown wrote a `SWAP_FAILED` file claiming
OVMS was still resident after a forced stop, when in fact a ~15 GB model server simply unloads
slower than the old check-then-one-fast-retry window; the boot reconciler converged it moments
later and the operator saw RECOVERED. The mechanism is correct; the timing was too eager.
`_verify_ovms_stopped` now POLLs `ovms_alive` on a bounded window (mirroring the driver's existing
`_settle`/`_await_gpu_clear` injected-clock pattern) before escalating, and only signals after the
poll *and* a forced retry *and* a short post-retry poll all still show it resident. The trade-off I
accepted and named: in the genuinely-stuck case the 14B restore is delayed by up to the poll window
— but that restore is memory-doomed anyway (the 30B and 14B cannot co-reside), the boot reconciler
is the backstop, and the success path returns the instant OVMS is gone, which actually *helps* the
restore by guaranteeing the GPU memory is free by the time it runs. The unconditional never-zero
14B restore is untouched; B2 changes only *when the failure signal fires*. Revert the poll to a
single check and the slow-exit test false-fails again (`assert 2 == 1` — a forced retry it should
never have made).

Both ship dormant behind `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false`. The honest doc trail came along: ADR-035
got an Amendment 2 (and its DECISION_REGISTER row), and the stale "compile_prompts appends one
acceptance-tests task" claims in `decompose.py`/`acceptance.py` were corrected — they would have
literally contradicted the code. The lesson this earned (#163) is the one about the brief's "where":
a brief written without source access points at a plausible file, and the real cause can be a layer
downstream — confirm it against the code before you move.

**Next:** merged to `main` (`12bfe44`, `--no-ff`, parent `8a23731`); the operator's uncommitted
`enabled=true` flip + `MainWindow.xaml.cs` edit were preserved across the merge, and the on-main
standing gate is green at 4220/0. The live swap-back with the poll, and the first single-function
`/dispatch` producing exactly one folded task, are the operator's on-hardware confirmations at the
#670 Phase-B go-live.

*(commit `e5a08eb` — B1 fold in `acceptance.compile_prompts` + B2 poll in
`swap_driver._verify_ovms_stopped`; ADR-035 Am.2 + DECISION_REGISTER; new acceptance/swap/eval
tests + four deliberate behavior-change updates; both mutation directions captured green→revert→red;
worktree gate 4200/0 → on-main 4220/0; merged `12bfe44`, operator's flips preserved; DORMANT — live
swap-back awaits the operator.)*

### 2026-06-23 — The acceptance task that tested nothing for twenty-four minutes

On the rocket-calc dispatch the operator caught the resident 30B coder grinding for ~24 minutes
(about 2,150 s of CPU) against an empty workspace, stopping only at the 3,600 s per-task cap. The
decomposition had split his one-window "two number boxes, an Add button, a label that sums them"
into three tasks — `create-main-window`, `implement-add-functionality`, and a dedicated
`acceptance-tests` sibling. The two feature tasks PARKED (a separate review-agent defect left them
on their `agent/` branches, never merged to `main`). The appended acceptance task then ran in a
worktree branched from `main` — which still held only the README — and dutifully wrote tests for a
calculator that wasn't there.

I root-caused it in code rather than by assumption. `acceptance.compile_prompts` appends the
dedicated `acceptance-tests` task whenever there are >=2 features (its own docstring already named
this exact failure mode); `swap_driver._run_phases` then iterates `self._tasks` and calls
`run_task` unconditionally — only cancel/stop break the loop, no merge-dependency gate; and a
task's worktree defaults to base `main`, which a PARK leaves untouched. Three facts that compose
into a guaranteed empty-workspace grind.

The fix that mattered (FIX b) is a few lines of judgment, not cleverness: before running the task
whose slug is `ACCEPTANCE_TASK_SLUG`, look at the `outcomes` already collected this run; if any
preceding feature is not `MERGED`, append a synthetic `SKIPPED` outcome naming the parked task and
`continue`. Because the acceptance task is always last, "every prior outcome is a feature" holds,
so "skip unless all preceding == MERGED" is exact. I chose the in-memory driver guard over a
fleet-side worktree-artifact probe because it's the smallest correct change and it lives in BlarAI
where the regression test can reach it. The never-zero teardown is untouched — the guard is a
CODE-loop `continue`, and the 14B restore lives in `run()`'s `finally`, which a loop guard cannot
reach.

What the build taught me was to verify the reporting claim instead of asserting it. My approved
plan added a `SKIPPED` icon to `dispatch._format_report`. Tracing the actual path showed the swap
driver writes its report through `write_cumulative_report`, which already emits
`- acceptance-tests: SKIPPED (reason)` literally — `_format_report` is only reached by
`/dispatch status`, on `parse_summary` output, which never yields `SKIPPED` unless I also added
the deferred `_classify_result` branch. The icon would have been dead code. So verification SHRANK
the change: `dispatch.py` is byte-unchanged and the deferred round-trip bundle
(`_classify_result` + `_format_report`) stays whole. The same trace surfaced a pre-existing quirk
worth tracking — the cumulative `SUMMARY.txt` format (two-space `  - task: RESULT (detail)` lines,
no `RESULT:` line) doesn't match `parse_summary`'s regex, so `/dispatch status` reads "no task
results parsed" for ANY post-run cumulative report, not only a skipped one. Not mine to fix in
this brief (a report-format decision, dormant, out of scope), but recorded as a #670 follow-up.

The second fix is a fidelity lever, not an unblock. Once FIX b lands, an over-split no longer
strands the 30B, so the decomposer's habit of splitting one small app's UI from its behavior drops
from a failure to an efficiency cost. Part-1 adds a prompt rule keeping a single window and the
behavior of its controls as ONE task. The LA added the rule that turned out to matter most: the
rocket-calc run didn't just split UI from logic, it dropped the calculator's arithmetic entirely —
building window, resize, and styling with no task for the actual add. So Part-1b makes the PRIMARY
functionality non-optional: a rocket-themed calculator must build the calculator, not only the
rocket.

I deferred Part-2 (a deterministic ruler backstop) on the record, and the brief's own §5 caught me
into confirming why. §5 asked for a `must_collapse` corpus case proving the winui slugs right-size
to exactly 1. They don't, and can't: `create-main-window` and `implement-add-functionality` are
two sibling-distinct features, so the deterministic collapser keeps both at 2 — precisely the
over-collapse Part-2 was deferred to avoid. Every existing "-> exactly 1" test is a single-feature
goal. Forcing the case to 1 would have required the very backstop we chose not to ship. So the
prompt change stays validated by the live re-run, not by a deterministic eval it can't move, and I
added instead the boundary the prompt actually targets: a `must_survive` guard proving two genuine
screens (a settings window and a main window) stay two tasks. The execution-verified §4 decision
governs the aspirational §5 line.

Ships dormant behind `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=false`. Decompose eval 69 -> 70, the fleet
blast-radius sweep 275/275 green, and the skip guard proven mutation-resistant (disable it and
`run_task` fires on the empty-workspace task — the grind reproduces, then restored byte-clean). The
operator's uncommitted `enabled`-flip and `MainWindow.xaml.cs` edit were preserved untouched; the
commit is surgical.

**Next:** the parked-feature root cause (the review agent leaving feature tasks on `agent/`
instead of merging) is a separate fleet-side brief and the actual reason the workspace was empty —
FIX b is the safety net, not that cure. Track the two follow-ups surfaced here: the
`SUMMARY.txt` / `parse_summary` format mismatch, and the optional `'skipped'->SKIPPED` round-trip
(`_classify_result` + `_format_report`) deferred from this pass. The LA gates this branch
(ultracode adversarial pass) before any merge.

**Lesson 165.** *Verification can shrink a change, not just confirm it.* The approved plan
added a `_format_report` icon; tracing the real report path showed the driver never reaches that
function and the icon would be dead code — so the honest build was smaller than the plan, with
`dispatch.py` left byte-unchanged. Trace the actual consumer before adding code to serve an assumed
one; "verify, don't assert" sometimes deletes work.

*(commit `<this>` — FIX b acceptance-task gating in `swap_driver.py` + FIX Part-1/1b decompose
prompt rules; +2 `test_swap_driver.py` tests, +1 `must_survive` corpus case; eval 70, fleet sweep
275/275; ships dormant.)*

### 2026-06-23 — The stop button that was already wired, minus the button

A dispatch run can thrash for up to a thirty-minute circuit-breaker, and until today the
operator had no clean way to call it off mid-flight. That sounds like it should be a hard
problem — reaching across a process boundary into a detached driver that has already torn
BlarAI's backend down and loaded the 30B — but it wasn't. The hard part was already solved.
The detached swap driver's CODE loop has, since the #670 work, polled a cancel sentinel at
every task boundary: `if self._ops.cancel_requested(): break`, where `cancel_requested` is
just `cancel_path(config).exists()`. Tripping that file routes the driver straight into its
NEVER-ZERO teardown — 30B unloaded, 14B restored, and — this is the part I cared most about —
the in-progress task PARKS on its `agent/<slug>` branch rather than being discarded, because
the worktree sweep is deliberately force-FREE and refuses to remove a worktree with
uncommitted or committed-but-unmerged work. The mechanism was complete. The only thing missing
was a trigger: nothing operator-facing ever wrote that sentinel. So this was a wiring job, not
an invention, and I held myself to that — the whole point of the exercise was to NOT build a
second stop path when a tested one already existed.

What I added is a `stop` verb on the gateway coordinator. `parse_dispatch_command` now
recognises `/dispatch stop`, `handle_command` routes `kind == "stop"` to a new `_stop`, and
`_stop` does exactly two things: decide whether a run is actually live, and if so write
`cancel_path(self._config)` — the same file, derived from the same `FleetDispatchConfig`, that
the running driver polls. The "is it live" gate is the load-bearing judgment. I reused
`swap_state.is_in_flight` — the identical predicate the boot reconciler already trusts: a run
is ACTIVE iff a swap-state record exists on disk in a non-terminal phase (terminal being
`PHASE_IDLE` / `PHASE_RECOVERED`). With no in-flight record, `_stop` writes nothing and says
"Nothing is running to stop." That "writes nothing" half matters as much as the write: a
sentinel dropped while nothing is running would sit on disk and silently abort the NEXT
dispatch's first task — a stop that poisons a future run is worse than a stop that no-ops.

The trade-off I deliberated longest was whether `stop` should also clear the session's
pending-approval slot. I decided firmly that it must not, and the reason is a semantic
boundary I did not want to blur: a PENDING plan — decomposed, criteria shown, not yet approved
— has never reached the coder, and discarding it is `/dispatch reject`'s job. `stop` is for an
APPROVED, EXECUTING run. Conflating them would mean a `stop` typed while a plan happened to be
waiting would silently throw that plan away, which is a surprise the operator never asked for.
So `_stop` leaves the slot untouched; with only a pending plan and no live run it reports
"nothing running" and the plan survives. I wrote a test that asserts exactly that
(`test_stop_does_not_touch_pending_slot`) because it's the kind of distinction that erodes
under a careless future refactor.

The one thing I had to confirm rather than assume was sentinel cleanup. The driver does NOT
remove the cancel file after honoring it — I traced the only `_rm(cancel_path(...))` to
`prepare_and_launch_swap`, which clears it at the START of every EXECUTE ("a prior run's cancel
must not abort this one"). That means the design is already self-correcting and I needed no new
cleanup code: even the narrow boundary case — a run finishing on its own between my in-flight
read and my write — leaves at worst an orphaned sentinel that the next run's own prep wipes
before its CODE loop ever reads it. Reusing that existing cleanup, and documenting precisely
where it lives in the `_stop` docstring, was the right call over bolting on a redundant unlink
that could itself race the driver.

I proved the test actually binds the behavior rather than passing by luck: I removed the
`path.write_text(...)` line and re-ran, and `test_stop_active_run_writes_cancel_sentinel`
failed on `assert sentinel.exists()` while the five no-write-path tests stayed green; restoring
the line returned all to green. The relevant suites — `test_dispatch_coordinator.py` (now 32,
+7) and `test_swap_ops.py` (47) — pass at 79, the gateway suite at 512, and the WinUI
passthrough-allowlist gate stays green untouched because `/dispatch` is one passthrough entry
whose verb is parsed gateway-side, so a new verb needs no allowlist edit.

**Next:** the live confirm is the operator's on-hardware step — fire a real `/dispatch approve`,
then `/dispatch stop` mid-run, and watch the box come back with the partial work parked on its
`agent/<slug>` branch. A natural follow-up is surfacing the stop affordance in the WinUI itself
(a button that sends `/dispatch stop`) so the operator isn't typing the verb, and possibly a
`/dispatch status` line that reflects a cancelled run distinctly from a completed one.

**Lesson 166.** *Ship the trigger, not a second mechanism.* When a cross-process control
already exists and is tested (here: the driver's cancel-sentinel poll), the operator-facing
gap is usually just the thing that writes the signal — resist re-implementing the teardown.
The discipline that paid off was reusing the existing `is_in_flight` gate and the existing
`prepare_and_launch_swap` cleanup rather than inventing parallel copies that could drift or
race the real driver.

*(commits: this fragment + the `dispatch_coordinator.py` `stop` verb/handler + 7 new
`test_dispatch_coordinator.py` tests; coordinator suite 25→32, swap_ops 47 unchanged, both
green at 79; gateway suite 512 green. Live mid-run stop awaits on-hardware verify.)*

### 2026-06-23 — Confirming what I assumed before he commits half an hour to it

The dispatch confirm-preview now shows the operator the build shape (Part 1) and what
counts as done, but there was still a gap that only bites a *non-technical* operator. He
writes a detailed product prompt — "a calculator a kid can use", "a packing-list app for
weekend trips" — and that detail is real signal about WHAT he wants. What he cannot give
is technical direction: he can't tell the system which language, which framework, which
window-management approach. He shouldn't have to — the agent layer's AGENTS.md supplies all
of that. But between his product intent and a runnable spec there is always a set of gaps
the 14B has to fill by ASSUMING: decimals or integers only? a calculation history or not?
a fixed window or a resizable one? Those are product decisions, and until now they were
made silently. He approved a ~30-minute build without ever seeing the reads the system had
to make to turn his sentence into a plan. If one of those reads was wrong, he found out
half an hour later, looking at the finished app.

So Part 2 surfaces, in the same preview he already approves, the 14B's KEY PRODUCT
ASSUMPTIONS — under a plain-English header, "Here's how I read the parts you didn't spell
out (reject and add detail if I got one wrong)". The load-bearing design constraint, the
one I kept checking myself against, is PRODUCT-NOT-TECH. The assumptions he sees must be
things he can actually judge — behaviour, appearance, scope — never language/framework/
SDK/version/architecture. Showing a novice "Assumed Python with a Tkinter window" is worse
than useless: it's a question he can't answer, presented as if his approval means something.
So the generation prompt asks ONLY for product-level assumptions a non-developer could
confirm or correct, and EXPLICITLY excludes every implementation choice, with the
instruction that if an assumption is technical it should be left out entirely. And when the
goal was fully specified and nothing was assumed, it emits an empty array — no section at
all. The right number of assumptions for a tightly-written prompt is zero, and the preview
should look exactly as it did before in that case.

The wiring is deliberately lean, and the reason is the IPC. The spec already crosses the
gateway boundary as the `criteria` field — the AO encodes `plan.spec.to_dict()`, the framer
passes the dict through opaquely, and the gateway reconstructs it with
`AcceptanceSpec.from_dict`. So I carried the assumptions ON the spec: a new
`assumptions: tuple[str, ...]` field, defaulted empty, threaded through `to_dict`/`from_dict`.
That single change means assumptions ride the existing PLAN_RESULT frame with ZERO transport
change, ZERO AO-handler change, ZERO framer change — I proved it by pushing a populated spec
through the real `encode_plan_result` → `decode_plan_result` → `from_dict` round-trip and
watching the assumptions come out intact on the far side. Generation is a SECOND, separate
14B call in `generate_plan`, not folded into the criteria call. I weighed folding it in (one
model call is cheaper) and rejected it: folding risks the model blending an implementation
note into the criteria JSON, and it would have perturbed the criteria parse path that the
decompose eval and every criteria test depend on. A separate call keeps that contract
byte-identical — the decompose eval stayed green untouched — and lets the assumptions prompt
be ruthlessly product-scoped on its own. The call is fail-closed and non-blocking: a model
or parse failure yields no assumptions and the plan proceeds, exactly as a fully-specified
goal would. The render is guarded by `if spec.assumptions:`, so an empty spec is backward-
compatible to the byte.

Two judgment calls stayed in. First, `AcceptanceSpec.from_dict` originally coerced every
assumption item with `str(...)` — and my own adversarial test caught that turning a stray
`42` or `None` in a malformed payload into the literal assumption strings `"42"` and
`"None"`, inventing product reads the model never made and showing them to the operator as
if they were real. I dropped non-string items instead of coercing them, matching the
ruler discipline the rest of this layer already follows: the model proposes, the
deterministic parse disposes, and junk never reaches the approval gate unfiltered. Second,
I made every new test mutation-resistant rather than trusting one green run. I stashed the
production change and watched all fifteen feature tests fail for the right reasons —
`AttributeError: no attribute 'assumptions'`, the missing parser, the absent header — then
restored byte-identically and confirmed 165 green. The assumption-surfacing assertions
check the ACTUAL assumption strings on their own bulleted lines under the header, the
backward-compat ones prove the no-assumptions render is unchanged and the with-assumptions
render only INSERTS — it displaces nothing. A test that can't fail when the feature is
absent would have been a comment dressed as a guarantee, and the whole point of this layer
is that a non-developer can trust what it tells him.

The trade-off worth naming for the record: this is still display-only. There is no
question-and-answer turn, no new state machine, no chat hijack — the operator reads the
assumptions at the approval gate and rejects-and-refines if one is wrong, the same gesture
he already has. I considered an interactive clarifying-question exchange (ask him the
ambiguous ones, fold his answers back in) and deferred it deliberately. The confirm gate is
the one screen he must read to say yes; the right move first is to make the system's
interpretation VISIBLE there, cheaply and without a new turn, and see whether visibility
alone closes the misread loop. An interrogation turn is a heavier instrument, and it can be
added on top of a visible read-back later — it can't be added under a hidden one.

**Next:** watch the first real on-hardware dispatches with assumptions live — confirm the
14B actually keeps the assumptions product-level (no implementation leakage past the prompt's
net) on genuine operator prompts, and only THEN weigh whether the underspecified ones warrant
an optional clarifying-question turn that folds his corrections back into the decomposition.

### 2026-06-23 — Showing the operator the build before he signs off on it

The dispatch confirm-preview had a quiet honesty gap. When a novice operator types
`/dispatch calc | a calculator a kid can use`, the 14B at PLAN time already does the
hard part — it decomposes the goal into concrete tasks (`PlanResult.tasks`, the
compiled `{repo, task, prompt}` dicts) AND derives the acceptance criteria. Those
tasks cross the IPC intact and `dispatch_coordinator._plan` was already holding
`plan.tasks` in the pending slot. But the thing we put in front of the operator —
`render_criteria_preview` — only ever showed the *acceptance criteria*: the
plain-English "here's what I'll treat as DONE." It never showed the *shape of the
build*. So the operator was approving the WHAT-counts-as-done while blind to the
HOW-it-gets-built. For a non-developer who can't read a task decomposition off a
prompt and can't drive the coder himself, that's the wrong half to hide: the
decomposition is exactly the part he most needs to sanity-check before anything
irreversible fires. The data was already computed and already in hand at the call
site — this was a surfacing gap, not a missing-feature gap.

The fix is deliberately small and display-only. `render_criteria_preview` grew one
new keyword-only param, `tasks: list | None = None`, defaulting to `None` so every
existing caller and every existing test renders byte-for-byte as before — I locked
that with an explicit equality assertion (`tasks` omitted `==` `tasks=None` `==`
`tasks=[]`), because "backward-compatible" is a claim that should be *proven*, not
asserted. When tasks ARE passed, a short "Here's how I'll build it (N task(s)):"
block renders near the top, right under the goal line and ABOVE the automatic
checks, so the operator reads the build shape first. Each line is just the task's
humanized slug — the `task` key, hyphens/underscores to spaces, first letter
capitalized (`add-calc` -> `Add calc`) — and pointedly NOT the full task prompt. A
compiled prompt carries the baked-in acceptance-test block and anti-mirror header;
dumping that at a novice would be noise that buries the signal. The shape is the
signal. The only wiring change was the one line in `_plan` passing
`tasks=plan.tasks` into the render.

I made the new tests mutation-resistant rather than trusting a single green run.
The "with tasks" assertions check the actual humanized substrings (`Add calc`,
`Acceptance tests`) and that the build plan sits above the criteria, plus a negative
assertion that the sentinel prompt text never leaks into the preview. Then I proved
the green wasn't vacuous: I stashed both code edits, re-ran, and watched both
"with tasks" tests fail for the right reasons — the acceptance one with a
`TypeError` (no such kwarg yet) and the coordinator one on the missing header —
before restoring (byte-identical to a pre-stash backup) and confirming 74/74 green.
A test that can't fail when the change is absent isn't a test; it's a comment.

The trade-off worth naming: I considered surfacing the per-task prompt detail
(collapsible, or a count of criteria folded into each task) so the operator could
see more. I rejected it. The whole acceptance layer exists to keep a non-developer
from being misled, and more text in the approval gate works against comprehension,
not for it. The right grain for "should I approve this build?" is the list of named
steps, not the coder's internal instructions. If the operator wants depth, the
place to add it is the next turn — a clarifying-question exchange — not a denser
wall in the one screen he has to read to say yes.

**Next:** Part 2 — the 14B clarifying-question turn: when the goal is ambiguous,
have PLAN ask the operator one or two plain-English questions and fold the answers
into the decomposition before presenting this now-visible plan shape, so the build
he approves is the build he actually meant.

### 2026-06-24 — Giving the 14B back the platform knowledge we were throwing away

The matured dispatch pipeline parked on a pure-product goal — "a calculator that
looks like a rocket" — after a thirty-minute churn (run `20260624-084153-bd`). The
trace was unambiguous: the fleet's scaffold picker is deliberately conservative, it
refuses to guess a language from an ambiguous goal, and the operator's prompt was
pure product intent with zero technical signal. So nothing seeded, the 30B authored
a WinUI project from nothing, proliferated a rogue `Program.cs` and a second test
project, and hit a compiler error it could not climb out of. The irony is sharp:
the smartest component that actually *does* understand the platform — the 14B at
decompose time — was being asked to decompose and then having its platform
understanding thrown straight in the bin. This increment is the BlarAI half of the
fix: the 14B now emits a coarse, enum-constrained build-signal during PLAN, and the
deterministic fleet (the LA's separate lane) maps that signal to a concrete
scaffold. The operator still writes pure product; the 14B *is* the system, not the
operator, so it is allowed to carry the technical read.

The shape of the implementation was decided for me by a pattern already in the
tree, and I leaned into it hard rather than inventing my own. The acceptance layer
already makes the 14B emit *product assumptions* as a separate, second model call —
deliberately separate so the criteria JSON contract stays byte-identical and the
criteria parse path and its tests never move. I made the build-signal a *third*
such call, for exactly the same reason. The temptation was to fold the surface
classification into the criteria prompt and save a model round-trip; I rejected it
because the whole value of the separate-call discipline is that it cannot regress
the contracts that already have tests. Keeping `build_plan` on a separate call meant
every one of the existing decompose / criteria / assumptions tests passed untouched
— 110 of them green on the first run after the change, which is the proof that the
separation held, not a hope. The signal rides on the `AcceptanceSpec` (round-tripped
in `to_dict`/`from_dict`, so it crosses the gateway IPC inside the spec dict with no
transport change at all — the framer already forwards the spec as arbitrary keys),
and its three goal-level fields (`surface`/`complexity`/`language_hint`) are stamped
onto every compiled task object in `compile_prompts`, at the single exit, so all
three task shapes carry them uniformly.

Fail-closed had to be absolute and I traced it to the letter: any parse failure,
missing field, or unknown enum value collapses to `unknown`/`None`, never a raise,
never a block. The load-bearing guarantee is that a `surface == unknown` dispatch is
byte-identical to today — the fleet sees no usable signal and falls back to its
current conservative no-seed path — so the change can only ever help, never make a
working dispatch worse. There is a subtlety I want on the record: a model that emits
no JSON object at all yields `build_plan is None` (the spec carries nothing), whereas
a model that emits an object lacking a valid surface yields a fully-formed dict with
`surface: unknown`. Both render no platform line and seed no scaffold, so both are
safe, but they are *different internal states* and I locked each one separately
rather than letting the test suite blur them.

The honest part of this entry is the verification, where my own mutation probe
caught a soft spot in my own test. I follow the BlarAI bar — for every new assertion,
prove it goes RED under a deliberate mutation, never just trust the green. I wrote
six mutations: accept a bad surface enum, drop the per-task threading, suppress the
preview line, drop the signal from the smoke-floor copy, never attach the signal,
and have the no-object path return a dict instead of `None`. Five went red exactly
as designed. The sixth — dropping `build_plan` from the `_ensure_test_floor` copy —
stayed *green*, and that was the useful failure. The reason is that in
`generate_plan` the floor runs before the build-signal is attached, so my
"survives the floor" test was asserting the end state, which the later attach
satisfies regardless of whether the floor itself carried the field. The end-to-end
test could not fail when the floor's carry was deleted, which by the project's own
definition means it was a comment, not a test. I kept the floor's carry anyway —
it mirrors the `assumptions` carry that is already there and is correct-by-
construction for any future reordering — and I added a *direct* unit test of
`_ensure_test_floor` that drives the carry tightly; that one goes red under the
mutation, as it must. The lesson is one the journal already half-knows in other
forms: an end-state assertion is not a guard on the intermediate step that produced
it, and a mutation probe is the only thing that tells you which kind of test you
actually wrote.

The EXECUTE-to-queue-write trace was the other thing I refused to assume. I followed
it on disk, not from the docstrings: PLAN's `compile_prompts` stamps the fields onto
each task dict; `execute_swap_dispatch` hands the approved tasks to
`prepare_and_launch_swap`, which writes them into the swap-state `current.json` via
`asdict` + `json.dumps` (whole dicts, no field filtering); the detached driver reads
them back whole; and `real_run_task` writes each one into the per-task
`task-queue.json` that `run-fleet.ps1` consumes. I drove a real plan through all
three writes and read the files back — the signal fields land in `task-queue.json`
alongside `repo`/`task`/`prompt`, and I locked that with two cross-module tests in
`test_swap_state.py`. The fleet boundary is exactly `run-fleet.ps1` reading that
file: that is where BlarAI's ownership stops and the LA's lane (the `-Surface`
parameter on `add-fleet-task.ps1`, the `Resolve-BuildProfile` map) begins. Worth
noting for the trace's honesty: the live `/dispatch` path never touches
`add-fleet-task.ps1` at all — that script is only reached by the deterministic
`enqueue_task` helper, which the swap path deliberately stopped calling to avoid
polluting the operator's shared overnight queue. So on the path that matters,
BlarAI owns the write all the way to `task-queue.json`.

Ships dormant — `[fleet_dispatch].enabled` is untouched, and the operator's
intentional working-tree flips were never staged. The live proof is the LA's
consolidated on-hardware `/dispatch` at the end of the three increments, where the
real 14B's first decompose answers the one question no injected fake can: does it
actually classify "a calculator that looks like a rocket" as `desktop-gui`.

**Next:** the fleet half of Increment 1 (the LA's lane) — `Resolve-BuildProfile` +
teaching `Resolve-TaskScaffold` to prefer the profile's scaffold and fall back to
today's heuristic only on `unknown`, plus the `-Surface` parameter on
`add-fleet-task.ps1` that reads the field this increment now writes. Then Increments
2 (the structural fail-fast gate) and 3 (the core/shell staged scaffold), and the
one consolidated on-hardware `/dispatch` that proves the whole chain end to end.

**Lesson 167.** *An end-state assertion is not a guard on the step that
produced it.* When a value is set in one place and re-set (or defaulted) in another
on the same path, a test that checks only the final value will stay green even if the
first place is deleted — it cannot tell you the intermediate carry works. The
mutation probe is what exposes this: if deleting the intermediate step leaves the
test green, the test is guarding the wrong thing, and the fix is a *direct* test of
the intermediate unit, not a more elaborate end-to-end one. Paid for here by the
`_ensure_test_floor` build-signal carry, whose end-to-end "survives the floor" test
could not fail when the carry was mutated away.

### 2026-06-24 — Building the headless WinUI so the box can test itself

The `/dispatch` pipeline shipped behind a confirm-gated chat surface that only the WinUI can
drive: type `/dispatch <repo> | <goal>`, read the PLAN preview, answer the Inc-4 clarifying
question if the 14B flagged an ambiguous platform, approve, then watch the model swap to the 30B
and the fleet build. That is exactly right for the operator. It is exactly wrong for an
autonomous sweep — the Dispatch LA wanted to fire the same dispatch across a stack of project
types hands-off, and there was no way in that did not involve a human at a window. So this session
built the missing seam: a reusable harness under `tools/dispatch_harness/` that drives the SAME
flow with no GUI. The one rule I held to the whole way was that it is a *driver*, not a second
implementation — it constructs the real `TransportGateway` (dev_mode, loopback :5001,
`fleet_dispatch_enabled`, the agentic-setup/projects roots threaded exactly as the launcher
threads them) and calls the same `gateway.handle_dispatch_command(...)` the WinUI's backend
dispatcher calls. Zero BlarAI runtime modules changed; the diff is purely additive.

The brief warned that the architecture map carried some guessed API names, and that warning paid
off twice. `TransportGateway.handle_dispatch_command(session_id, text)` turned out to be real and
already the WinUI's exact entry — a clean command-string-in, reply-string-out seam, so the harness
never has to touch the IPC at all. And `MessageFramer.encode_plan_request` /
`encode_execute_request` — the two names the map flagged as guesses — are real and already wired
end to end (gateway → AO `_handle_plan_request` / `_handle_execute_request` → `generate_plan` /
