`execute_swap_dispatch`). The lesson re-confirmed: confirm every signature against the code before
building on it. I read the gateway, the coordinator, the AO handlers, `shared/fleet/dispatch.py`,
and `swap_ops.py` end to end first, and it meant the harness slotted in without a single
adapter-shim for a wrong name.

The hard part — the part the brief called non-negotiable — was monitoring. The naive version
polls `SUMMARY.txt` and waits out the breaker. That is the dev-cycle-speed killer: a determined-
doomed run strands the 30B resident for the full `swap_run_budget_s` (5400 s here) before anyone
notices. So the monitor is SMART. I went and read the real fleet artifacts on disk — a completed
rocket-calc run from earlier today (`20260624-120231-bd`) and a *stalled* one
(`20260624-142352-bd`) — to ground the doom predicate in what the fleet actually writes, not what
I imagined it writes. The completed run's `journal.log` runs RUN-START → TASK-START → TASK-END →
RUN-END and took **59 minutes** to PARK (a real success — parked-on-branch is the operator's
recovery handle, not a failure). The stalled run froze at `[1/5] Building` with no TASK-END and no
SUMMARY, its `run-fleet-*.log` and `journal.log` mtimes dead. That contrast *is* the predicate:
a run is doomed only when, across two snapshots a grace window apart, none of journal.log /
run-fleet-*.log / ovms-*.out.log advanced AND no coder/dotnet/opencode child is burning CPU AND
no SUMMARY exists. Elapsed time is never the signal — a healthy build can be quiet for a while and
a real run can take an hour. The overall timeout is only the separate backstop. On a doom verdict
the harness fires the clean `/dispatch stop` (the coordinator's cancel-sentinel path) so the 14B
is restored and partial work parks, rather than waiting out the breaker.

The brief also named a specific false-positive trap, and reading the real coder agent log proved
it true: the `.agent.log` echoes the seed documentation, which literally contains words like
"parked" and "CS0246". Token-grepping that file for failure would fire on the *instructions*, not
the outcome. So the monitor never reads agent-log content — it reads fleet-log mtimes + process
CPU + the structured SUMMARY only. I split the whole thing so the doom decision is a pure function
of two `RunSignals` snapshots (`classify_run_health`), trivially unit-testable with hand-built
mtimes/CPU/flags, and the live `RunMonitor` is just the thin I/O shell that gathers signals from
disk and psutil. That separation is why the doom logic has nine direct unit tests including the
exact stalled-rocket-calc shape.

The honest failure of the session: the first dry-run of the Inc-4 clarifying path *crashed*. The
harness logs a progress line with a `→` arrow, and the Windows console code page (cp1252) can't
encode U+2192, so the whole job died with a `UnicodeEncodeError` before it ever approved. It was a
cosmetic glyph taking down a functional path — the worst kind of paper cut. The mock (pytest,
UTF-8 capture) was green; only running the real CLI on the real console caught it. Fixed by
ASCII-ing the arrow and, more durably, reconfiguring stdout/stderr to `errors="replace"` at CLI
start so any em-dash / ellipsis / smart-quote the coordinator emits degrades to `?` instead of
crashing a sweep. Small bug, but it is the "screen caught what the test missed" pattern again, and
it is worth keeping in.

Verification before handoff: the full standing gate is green at **4351 passed / 20 skipped / 118
deselected** on this isolated worktree (1:52), the +49 harness tests landing cleanly (the 20 skips
are the known gitignored-`bge-small` router env-skips + the non-elevated symlink pair, both of
which pass on the main checkout). The dry-run drives the whole flow against a fake in-process AO
and prints the report with no GPU — both the clear-surface path and, with `--dry-run-ambiguous`,
the clarifying-question path. The live end-to-end run against the real stack + the 30B is the LA's
gate (it doubles as the rocket-calc fix confirmation), so I left that for the box.

**Next:** the LA runs the live dispatch — start BlarAI (`python -m launcher --go-live`, which is
what wires the AO's swap context), then
`python -m tools.dispatch_harness --repo rocket-calc --goal "a space rocket calculator" --clarify-answer 1`
— and confirms the harness drives PLAN → approve → swap → monitor → SUMMARY end to end, stopping
fast if the run determines-doom. Once the sweep proves out across a few project types, the small
follow-ups are a richer per-project `examples/sweep.json` and (optional) wiring the harness's
machine-readable report into a saved sweep artifact.

**Lesson 168.** *Ground a "smart" heuristic in the real artifacts before you code the
predicate.* The doom-detector is only trustworthy because I read an actual completed run and an
actual stalled run on disk first — the 59-minute-then-PARKED success and the frozen-at-[1/5]
failure — and built the stop condition from the contrast between them (stale fleet-log mtimes +
idle coder CPU + no SUMMARY), not from a guess about what "hung" looks like. The same reading is
what let me honor the brief's false-positive warning concretely: the agent log echoes seed docs
containing "parked"/"CS0246", so the predicate reads fleet-log mtimes + process state, never
agent-log tokens. A monitoring heuristic written from imagined signals would have killed healthy
long runs and waited out doomed ones; one written from the real files does neither.

### 2026-06-24 — The load that should not have fit, and the trust grain for code I did not write

The headless-coding dispatch swapped the resident 14B out for a 30B coder and ran a real
build on the Arc 140V (run `20260624-120231-bd`). Two things from that run belong in the
BlarAI record — one a hardware fact I had wrong in my own head, one a governance call.

**The 30B load did not OOM, and for a reason worth understanding.** A ~29 GB committed
transient landing on a 31.32 GB box "should" have failed. It did not, because OOM on Windows
fires when *commit charge* exceeds the *commit limit* — not when the working set exceeds
physical RAM. The commit limit here is physical RAM + pagefile = 31.32 + ~11 = 42.32 GB, so a
29 GB transient sits comfortably under it. What actually happened — measured at the
milestone-1 swap gate (2026-06-21) and corroborated by this run's pagefile peak (3.36 GB
ever-used) — is that *physical* headroom exhausted (Available RAM cratered to ~67 MB,
Committed peaked ~29.1 GB) and ~3.3 GB of modified pages spilled to disk in a brief
~200k-pages/s storm. The load survives on three levers: swap-first (releasing the 14B frees
~11 GB of baseline), the pagefile lifting the commit ceiling from 31 to 42 GB, and the 87%
Intel Shared-GPU-Memory override giving the iGPU a ~27 GB window into the unified pool. The
correction I had to make to my own earlier mental model: "Committed 29.1 GB < 31.3 GB physical
so RAM never exhausted" was wrong — 31.3 GB is *physical*; the commit limit is higher
(pagefile), and physical headroom *did* exhaust. The swap is therefore gated on a *headroom*
check, not an OOM catch: this box will not OOM, it will page-storm, so the gate must read
Available-RAM headroom and abort below threshold (which it correctly does). Community-grade
numbers: `PERFORMANCE_LOG.md` (2026-06-24) +
`docs/performance/dispatch_swap_telemetry_2026-06-24.json`.

**Code the AI writes is UNTRUSTED, not TRUSTED_LOCAL — a provenance call, named.** The operator
asked where dispatch output (the 30B's generated project) sits in BlarAI's provenance model
(ADR-023). I recommended UNTRUSTED — datamarked, action-locked — and the trade-off is worth
recording because it is exactly the kind the cert track wants on the record. The path NOT taken
is TRUSTED_LOCAL, and it would be defensible: the realistic injection risk is low (an air-gapped
box, the operator's own model, the operator's own goal). I went UNTRUSTED anyway because
provenance is the deciding factor — TRUSTED_LOCAL means the operator authored or placed the
file, and machine-generated unvetted output is provenance-closer to "untrusted external" — and
because the cost of containment is near zero here: datamarking does not stop the 14B from
reading and discussing the generated code, it only blocks that code from driving the 14B's
tools, so the read-and-discuss use case loses nothing while a propagated injection loses its
teeth. It also future-proofs the dormant egress path (#577): if dispatch ever pulls external
code, UNTRUSTED is already the right default. Recorded on #679, which also carries the
persistence trace: loaded/grounded file content lives only in the in-RAM grounding context
(`context_manager._sessions[].grounded_chunks`, no persistence method) — it is NOT duplicated
to disk; the encrypted `sessions.db` persists conversation turns only.

**Next:** the file-read feature (#679) inherits both calls — UNTRUSTED provenance grain +
ephemeral in-RAM grounding (no disk duplication). The next swap capture arms a pre-sized Intel
UT window + a sub-second sampler before approval, to publish this run's load-trough cleanly (it
was lost this run to an over-long capture that buffered in RAM and did not flush on kill).

**Lesson 169.** *OOM is a commit-limit event, not a physical-RAM event — gate memory-risky
loads on measured Available-RAM headroom, never on catching an allocation failure that a
pagefile-backed commit limit will not raise.* (The swap would page-storm, not OOM; the headroom
gate is the real control — the milestone-1 / dispatch-swap arc paid for this.)

### 2026-06-24 — Asking one question, and only one, and never letting the model write it

Increment 1 gave the 14B back its platform knowledge. Increment 4 is the operator's
own idea built on top of it: when the model genuinely cannot tell whether a goal is a
desktop app, a web page, or a phone app, ask. The whole risk of "ask the operator" is
that it slides into a per-detail quiz, and a novice quizzed on every detail rubber-
stamps — which is worse than not asking, because now the rubber-stamp is laundered as
a decision. So the entire design is a discipline against that slide. The 14B does not
get to ask anything. It only FLAGS the ambiguity and lists the platforms it is torn
between; the system owns the question text and the answer→surface mapping out of a
small curated map. Small models write leading and irrelevant questions, so the model
that is best at *detecting* the fork is deliberately not allowed to *phrase* it. The
scope bar I held the map to is three-pronged and I wrote it into the code so a future
session has to argue against it on the record: ask only for a decision that (a)
materially forks the build, (b) the 14B can't assume, and (c) the operator can answer
in product terms. Platform/device is the one decision in v1 that clears all three.
Everything else stays assume-and-show-in-the-preview — the same instinct as routing
consent to the coarsest meaningful grain.

The load-bearing call in Part A was a quiet one about a dict key, and it is the kind
of thing that decides whether "strictly additive" is true or just claimed. The brief
said "non-ambiguous surface → candidates forced to `[]`." Read literally that means
every parsed `build_plan` grows a fifth key. But increment 1's parser emits a precise
four-key dict, and there are exact-equality tests pinning that shape, and the spec
crosses the gateway IPC *as that dict* — so unconditionally adding a key is not
additive, it is a wire-format change dressed as one. I resolved it by treating the
gating contract (#7 in the brief — byte-identical for a clear surface) as the harder
invariant and the "forced to `[]`" as its semantics: candidates are attached to the
parsed dict ONLY when the fork is real and the surface stays `ambiguous`. For every
clear or unknown plan the dict is byte-for-byte what it was yesterday, every existing
payload reconstructs unchanged, and the kill-test that proves it (`_parse_build_plan`
of a clear plan equals the exact old four-key dict) goes red the instant someone makes
the key unconditional. That is the test I most wanted to exist, because "additive" is
the entire safety case and a claim is not a guard.

The fail-closed coupling is the other place the design defends itself. An `ambiguous`
flag with fewer than two real candidates is meaningless — there is no fork to ask
about — so the parser coerces it straight back to `unknown`, which is today's no-seed
behavior. A model that hedges with "ambiguous" and then names one platform, or names
nothing, or names garbage that all filters out, gets exactly the same treatment as a
model that said nothing: the question never fires. I like that the failure mode of the
new feature is *the absence of the new feature*, not a half-built interactive state.

Part B was where I made the judgment I am most willing to be wrong about, and I want
the reasoning on the record rather than the answer. After the operator picks a
platform I have to thread the chosen surface back into the plan. The obvious move is
to re-run `compile_prompts` with the refined spec — but `compile_prompts` does two
things, and one of them (folding the acceptance-test block into the lone feature task)
is *not idempotent*: run it twice and the task prompt carries the test block twice.
The surgical truth is that only `surface` changed, and the only thing that needs
re-deriving is the per-task build-signal stamp, which `_thread_build_fields` does by
`.update()`-ing each task in place — and that one *is* idempotent and leaves the
folded prompt alone. So the answer path re-threads, it does not re-compile. The brief
even pointed at this ("it's enough to thread the chosen surface into the build_plan
that rides the task"), but I only trusted it after I traced what `compile_prompts`
would actually do to a second pass. The integration test that the chosen surface lands
on `pending.tasks[0]["surface"]` *and* reaches `execute_fn` *and* the persisted record
is the proof the thread survives all the way to where the fleet reads it.

The honest part, as ever, is a test that failed for a reason worth keeping. My
out-of-range-answer fallback test asserted that a bad answer leaves the task surface
as `unknown` — the safe coercion — and it failed, reporting `ambiguous`. The code was
right in spirit but I had taken a shortcut: on the fallback branch I passed the held
tasks straight through to the preview without re-threading, so they kept the raw
`ambiguous` surface the fake had stamped. In the live flow `compile_prompts` would
have stamped `ambiguous` and the fleet-boundary coercion would have caught it, but my
fallback path skipped the stamp entirely, so the defence never ran. The fix was to
re-thread on the fallback branch too, against the still-ambiguous build_plan, so the
coercion to `unknown` actually fires before the un-refined plan can reach the fleet.
The test was telling me the truth: a defence-in-depth coercion only protects you on
the paths that actually run it, and I had a path that didn't. (The other failure that
run was my own test casing — I lowercased the reply and compared it against a
mixed-case label; a test bug, not a code bug, fixed in the assertion.)

One thing I checked rather than assumed, because it decides whether any of this works
in the wired flow: does the ambiguity signal even reach the gateway? The coordinator
runs gateway-side; the 14B runs AO-side; PLAN crosses the wire. I traced it — the AO
sends `criteria=spec.to_dict()`, `to_dict` already serialises `build_plan`, the
gateway rebuilds via `AcceptanceSpec.from_dict`, and `candidates` is just a key inside
the build_plan dict that round-trips wholesale. So increment 4 needs no new IPC verb
at all: the signal rides the channel increment 1 already opened, and the answer is
gateway-side state riding the existing EXECUTE frame. I almost added a protocol field
out of reflex and stopped, because adding one would have been gratuitous surface for a
thing that already crosses cleanly — and I locked the property with a PLAN-seam test
so a future refactor that drops `build_plan` from the wire fails loudly instead of
silently disabling the question.

Ships dormant — `[fleet_dispatch].enabled` is untouched and the operator's intentional
working-tree flips were never staged (I built on an isolated worktree off `main` and
never went near the main checkout). The one thing no injected fake can answer is whether
the real 14B flags ambiguity *well* — does it say `ambiguous` for a genuinely
platform-agnostic goal and stay clear-headed for "a desktop calculator with buttons",
or does it reach for `ambiguous` as a hedge and over-ask. That is the LA's off-dispatch
live decompose, and it is the right gate: the plumbing and the system's question are
proven green here; the classifier's judgment is the on-hardware open item.

**Next:** the LA's off-dispatch live PLAN probe (does the loaded 14B classify
surface/ambiguity sensibly — the precondition the build-signal architecture set for
increment 4), then the consolidated on-hardware `/dispatch` test that proves the whole
1→4 chain, then the fold-in of this fragment into `BUILD_JOURNAL.md` at the next quiet
tree. The clarifying map stays at the platform case until a real goal earns a second
entry against the three-bar test.

**Lesson 170.** *Defence-in-depth only defends the paths that run it.* A
fail-closed coercion (here, "an unresolved ambiguous surface becomes `unknown` before
it reaches the fleet") is worthless on a code path that bypasses the step that applies
it. The fallback branch held the pre-coercion tasks and passed them straight through,
so the safety net the rest of the flow relied on simply wasn't in the net's path; the
test that asserted the safe end-state is what exposed it. When you add a guard, audit
*every* branch that produces the guarded value — the easy branch and the error/fallback
branch both — and make a test exercise the fallback specifically, because the fallback
is exactly the branch a tired implementer routes around the guard.

### 2026-06-24 — Making the clarifying question actually fire: a live probe, a few-shot prompt, and the operator's UX call

Increment 4 (the confidence-gated clarifying question) shipped built-correct — gated, merged,
4300/0 — but when I probed the real 14B on the Arc 140V it was DORMANT in practice. With the
as-merged `_BUILD_PLAN_TEMPLATE` the model *guessed* `desktop-gui` for every platform-agnostic
"app" (todo, chat, note-taking, workout-tracker) and never reached for `ambiguous`. The template
was the cause: it told the model "a wrong guess is better than over-asking," so it never asked.
The design's §9 risk, realized — and a reminder that a green unit gate proves the wiring, not the
capability. The live model probe is the real acceptance test.

The fix was empirical, not theoretical. A balanced FEW-SHOT prompt — four clear examples
(desktop / web / mobile / command-line) plus two ambiguous ones (`todo app`, `chat app`) and a
Conservative rule (flag `ambiguous` ONLY for a bare "app" with no platform word) — flipped it: on
a 15-goal probe (6 genuinely-ambiguous, 9 across every clear surface) the 14B scored **15/15 with
zero over-asking and zero under-asking**. The aggressiveness is the operator's UX call, and he
chose Conservative — he is asked only on the genuinely-platform-forking goals, never on one that
names or implies its platform. That is the whole point: ask only what a novice can actually
answer, never a per-detail quiz (the same coarsest-meaningful-grain principle as egress consent).

One stray-brace quirk surfaced and earned a durable fix. The 14B occasionally appends an extra
closing brace (`{"surface":"web","candidates":[]}}`), and the greedy `\{.*\}` extractor over-grabbed
it into invalid JSON → the whole build-signal lost (fail-closed, but a needless miss — it cost the
'landing page' goal in the probe). Replaced it with a depth-aware, string-aware balanced-brace
extractor (`_first_json_object`) that recovers the first valid object regardless of trailing junk.
The probe went 14/15 → 15/15, and it hardens *every* build-signal parse against the model's
occasional sloppiness, not just this one goal. Locked with regression tests.

**Next:** the clarifying question now fires live (built-correct + the 14B triggers it correctly).
The remaining confirm is end-to-end in a real dispatch — the operator answering the question in
the PLAN preview — which folds into the test-dispatch sweep.

**Lesson 171.** *A built-correct feature can be dormant in practice — the live model probe,
not the green unit gate, is what tells you the capability actually exists. Prompt behaviour is
empirical: for a small model a few-shot example beats a paragraph of instruction, and the
de-risk probe is the acceptance test.*

### 2026-06-24 — The second model, wired as a choice and blessed before it speaks

UC-010 has drawn photoreal images since the #666 go-live. The dispatch arc asked it for
something different: an *illustration* model, so the headless coder can embed a real brochure
graphic instead of a 🚀 emoji. The model — Playground v2.5, an SDXL-architecture aesthetic
checkpoint — was already converted to OpenVINO-INT8 and validated on the Arc 140V by an
earlier session. What it lacked was a way into the runtime. This entry is about giving it that
way without disturbing the photoreal capability that is already live, and about a discipline
moment the git index handed me along the way.

The wiring is deliberately small, because `image_gen.py` was already model-agnostic — it loads
whatever `model_dir`/`weight_manifest` the launcher resolves. So a second model is not a second
code path; it is a *selection*. I added a `model_variant` label (`photoreal-sdxl` default,
`illustration-playground` the new option), an enum-validated config key that resolves which
dir/manifest pair the launcher hands to `ImageGenConfig`, and a resident-swap on that label so
the module unloads-and-reloads when the variant changes — the same "one diffusion model resident
at a time" rule the existing kind-swap (t2i↔i2i) already enforced, now extended across variants.
The photoreal path is byte-identical: the default variant resolves the same base keys it always
did. The illustration variant ships **double-dormant** — un-selected (the selector defaults to
photoreal) *and*, until today, manifest-unsigned. Selecting it is a deliberate edit; the resource
swap-sequence the operator made load-bearing (one model per phase, never co-resident in 31.3 GB)
is honoured because nothing here makes two models co-reside.

Then the operator said: get the ceremony done — stage and TPM-sign the new weights, if it can be
done safely. The honest answer was: only after proving three things, because "sign the weights"
hides a trap. The nested manifest stager walks the model dir with `rglob("*.bin"/"*.xml")`, so a
GPU-compile cache left in the directory by the validation runs (the dir had bloated 3.3→7.0 GB)
could have been hashed *into* the manifest — and the verifier rejects any unlisted `.bin`/`.xml`
under the tree, so a manifest contaminated by regenerable cache files would fail the very next
load. I checked the swept set against the known-good SDXL manifest before signing: exactly the
same 19 files (9 `.bin` + 9 `.xml` + `model_index.json`), no strays — the cache evidently uses a
different extension that neither the stager nor the verifier touches. Only then did I sign, reusing
the *existing* non-exportable `BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` TPM key with the CNG SILENT flag (no PIN,
no UAC, no new key), writing the detached `.sig`/`.pub` into the gitignored model dir. I verified
the full runtime gate — `verify_all_manifest_entries_nested(..., require_signed=True)`, the exact
call `_verify_weights` makes — returns all-verified over 19 entries, and that a one-byte tamper of
the manifest is rejected fail-closed. Signing is supply-chain integrity, not a content claim: it
asserts "these are the bytes we intend," it does **not** flip the variant live, and it does not
stand in for the operator's content attestation, which (with selecting the variant) remains the
go-live ceremony. I went with signing-now because it is reversible, autonomous-safe, and removes
the one piece of go-live friction only the hardware can produce — leaving the live decision wholly
the operator's.

The discipline moment: I reflexively `git add -A`'d to take the diff, and the index swept in a
change that was **not mine** — `[fleet_dispatch].enabled = false → true`, the dispatch go-live
flip, sitting uncommitted in the working tree from the operator's own dispatch work. The doctrine
is explicit that flipping that flag is the operator's on-hardware step, and a session that
commits a foreign capability-enabling change inside an unrelated feature branch has quietly
hijacked a governance decision. I caught it reviewing the staged diff, unstaged everything
(non-destructive), and used `git add -p` to commit only my own hunk of `default.toml`, leaving
the flip exactly where I found it. The lesson is not "be careful with git" — it is that in a tree
carrying other sessions' uncommitted work, the staged diff is a thing you *read*, never a thing
you *assume*, and `-A` is the wrong reflex there.

Standing gate green at **4379 passed / 0 failed / 118 deselected** with `LOCALAPPDATA` redirected;
the eight new tests cover the variant default, the cross-variant resident-swap, the
unload-clears-variant reset, the enum fail-closed, and the variant→paths selection both ways.

**Lesson 172.** *In a working tree holding other sessions' uncommitted changes, read the
staged diff and commit only your own hunks — `git add -A` is the wrong reflex.* A reflexive
`git add -A` on this shared tree swept in a foreign `[fleet_dispatch].enabled=true` capability flip
that doctrine reserves for the operator; the staged-diff review caught it and `git add -p` landed
only the owned hunk. The index is evidence to inspect, not a net to cast.

**Next:** Phase 2b — the asset-generation + rembg cutout pipeline (isolated 3.11 venv, build-time
fleet tool outside UC-010 governance), then Phase 3's VLM design loop (render spike → near-term
off-screen render → critique/fix wired into the swap driver, VLM as a loop signal, the operator's
eyeball still the final aesthetic verdict). The illustration variant stays dormant-by-selection
until the operator selects it and records the content attestation.

*(commit `<this>` — UC-010 illustration model-variant wiring: `image_gen.py` variant swap +
`entrypoint.py` resolution/validation + `default.toml` selector; +8 tests; gate 4379/0. Playground
v2.5 manifest staged + TPM-signed + round-trip-verified out-of-tree (gitignored `models/`). The
pre-existing `[fleet_dispatch].enabled` flip deliberately left unstaged.)*

### 2026-06-25 — Letting the model do the half it's good at

Phase 2a gave the dispatch a second model to *choose*; Phase 2b is about making that model's output
actually usable. Playground v2.5 has a split personality I'd already seen and the operator had
already ruled on: it draws a decent flat *subject* and then fills every empty pixel around it with
busy mosaic and dot clutter — the well-known SDXL horror of negative space. You can fight that with
prompts and a negative prompt, and I did, but you don't win; the model wants to decorate. The
operator's call was to stop fighting the background and *remove* it — `rembg` first, a flat-vector
LoRA only if more is wanted later. This entry is about building that and proving it, and about the
quiet good sense of the decision once you see it work.

The insight the cutout encodes is a separation of concerns: the model is good at the subject and
bad at the background, so don't ask it to be good at both — take the subject and throw the
background away. A `u2net` segmentation isolates the flat subject onto transparency in under a
second, which also happens to be exactly the asset shape an embedded app graphic wants (a logo on a
card, an icon on a surface — never a full-bleed square). The first proof made the point cleanly: a
512² rocket generation came out as the brief predicted — passable rockets (two of them, and some
garbled pseudo-text, at low CPU settings) sitting in a mosaic of coloured noise — and the cutout
erased all of that mess and handed back the rockets on white, ~64% of the frame now transparent.
The artifacts that remained were the *generation's* (duplicate subject, low-step noise), not the
cutout's. That distinction is the whole finding: rembg fixed the background problem completely, so
the only lever left to pull is generation quality, which is steps, resolution, and a singular
prompt — not a different cutout.

Two constraints shaped *how* it runs. First, isolation: `rembg` and its onnxruntime went into the
throwaway 3.11 conversion venv, never BlarAI's runtime `.venv` — the same discipline that bit us
once before when a diffusers install silently moved numpy under the runtime's feet. `pip check`
came back clean, numpy held at 2.4.6, and the OpenVINO pipeline and rembg import together in one
process. Second, the device: the Arc 140V is occupied — OVMS has the dispatch's 30B coder resident,
and Playground co-resident with a 30B is the 32.5 GB ceiling breach we already measured. The
standing rule forbids me stopping OVMS without an explicit task anyway, so the proof runs on CPU.
That is slower (≈141 s for a 512² image; longer at 768²) but it is the *honest* place for the
standalone tool to live — the GPU asset path belongs inside the Phase-3 model-swap sequence, where
the 30B is swept out of the way first, not stolen out from under a running dispatch. Naming that as
a deliberate boundary rather than a workaround matters, because the same boundary is the governance
one: this asset generator is a build-time fleet tool that writes plain PNGs, *outside* the
born-encrypted UC-010 store and outside the go-live ceremony, its content-safety resting on the
operator's prompt control. The operator owns that and accepts it for dev/legal app assets; it is a
chosen posture, not a gap.

There is a smaller lesson in the filename I had to delete. Writing the reference doc, I passed a
Windows path with a backslash before `uc010` — and `\uc0` is a unicode escape, so the file landed
as `docs<U+C010>_dispatch_asset_pipeline.md` at the repo root instead of under `docs/`. There is a
memory about exactly this; I tripped it anyway. Forward slashes in every tool-call path, always —
the backslash is never worth the risk on this platform.

**Lesson 173.** *Decompose a model's output along the axis where it is strong vs weak, and
discard the weak half rather than fighting it.* Playground draws good subjects and bad backgrounds;
a one-second cutout turns a mediocre-background model into a usable asset source, and reframes the
only remaining quality lever as generation (steps/res/prompt), not post-processing. The cheap
separation beats the expensive attempt to make one model good at everything.

**Next:** Phase 3 — the VLM design loop: render spike (foreground capture) → the near-term
off-screen `--render-to-file` render (de-risking spike with a fallback, never blocking the loop) →
critique/fix wired into the swap driver, with Qwen3-VL as a loop *signal* and the operator's eyeball
the final aesthetic verdict. The asset generator gets a version-controlled home in `agentic-setup`
and the ASSETS swap point (unload-and-confirm-freed before the 30B loads) at that wiring.

*(Phase 2b: `asset_gen.py` build-time tool [gen + rembg cutout] in the isolated 3.11 venv;
`docs/uc010_dispatch_asset_pipeline.md`; pipeline proven on CPU [load 9 s, 512² gen 141 s, cutout
<1 s, background removed cleanly]. No BlarAI runtime code changed; the tool is dormant until the
Phase-3 dispatch wiring.)*

### 2026-06-25 — The signal that never becomes a verdict

The dispatch loop can build. It can run tests. What it cannot do — and what a
VLM can — is look at a screenshot and tell you whether the app actually meets
the visual criteria the operator approved before the build started. That gap is
what Phase 3 of the VLM design loop closes.

The "brain" I built here is deliberately narrow. The module `shared/fleet/critique.py`
does exactly three things: it builds a crisp, parseable prompt that asks Qwen3-VL
to judge a screenshot against the goal and each visual criterion; it parses the
VERDICT/FEEDBACK response in a way that fails soft on any garbage output; and it
runs the loop-signal decision in a single pure function (`should_iterate`) so
there is one place where the continue/stop choice lives. The `__main__` CLI prints
a JSON object and exits 0 even on failure, so the PowerShell fleet loop can read
the `should_iterate` field without error-path gymnastics.

The hardest design constraint was the one the Lead Architect locked at the top of
the brief: the VLM is a loop signal, never a verdict. That distinction is not just
a policy rule — it needs to be structurally enforced so a future agent can't
accidentally "fix" it. The regression lock in `TestVisualTierAlwaysEyeball` proves
it: a visual `AcceptanceCriterion` put through `criterion_status` with a pass-pass
`TaskReport` still comes back `STATUS_EYEBALL`. No path through the code can turn
a VLM critique into a `verified`. The lock runs in the standing gate.

The fail-soft contract needed care. Every caller of `critique_screenshot` must get
a `VlmCritique` back — never an exception, never a None, never a hang. I went with
the same layered defense `vlm.py` uses: import error → `_FAIL`; missing file →
`_FAIL`; `describe` returns None → `_FAIL`; `describe` raises → `_FAIL`; parse
raises (paranoid) → `_FAIL`. The `needs_work=False` in each `_FAIL` case means
the loop stops, which is exactly the right behavior when the VLM is broken.

The parse design chose structural keywords over free-text scoring. I considered
asking the model to rate each criterion on a 1–5 scale and iterating on anything
below 4, but that approach fails when the model forgets the schema — and small
models forget schemas. A leading `VERDICT: PASS` / `VERDICT: NEEDS_WORK` line is
detectable with a single regex even if the rest of the response is mangled. The
unparseable default is `needs_work=False` (stop iterating) rather than
`needs_work=True` (keep going), because an infinite loop on garbage output is
worse than stopping too early.

The additive `visual_criteria_texts` helper in `acceptance.py` keeps the
critique loop from having to know about `AcceptanceSpec`'s tier model directly.
It is four lines and a docstring. I did not touch `criterion_status`, the tier
constants, or any existing data path.

40 targeted tests pass, all deterministic (no real VLM). The standing gate on
this worktree hit 4399/0/118 — 0 failures, zero regressions introduced.

The code is DORMANT: nothing wires `critique_screenshot` into a live caller.
Phase 3 go-live is an integrator step: wire `visual_criteria_texts` + the
critique loop into the dispatch coordinator, verify on a real screenshot.

**Next:** the integrator wires the critique loop into the dispatch coordinator —
at minimum, a post-build screenshot path that calls `critique_screenshot` and,
if `should_iterate` returns True, enqueues another FIX task before the final
report. The memory model (14B eviction during a VLM call) is the open hardware
question; the existing `vlm.unload()` + lazy-reload pattern handles it, but the
co-resident peak needs a live measurement on the Arc 140V before the loop is
budget-trusted.

*(commit <this>; 40 new tests in `shared/tests/test_fleet_critique.py`; new
`shared/fleet/critique.py`; additive edit to `shared/fleet/acceptance.py`;
standing gate 4399/0; dormant — no live caller wired.)*

### 2026-06-25 — Watching the machine watch its own work

Phase 3 of the VLM design loop is the one with a genuinely unsolved problem at its centre:
to let Qwen3-VL critique a built app, you first have to get a *picture* of that app — headlessly,
on a build machine, ideally without a window flashing onto the operator's screen. Everything else
(the critique brain, the loop signal, the acceptance lock) was tractable Python. The render was the
question mark. This entry is about answering it, and about the moment the loop actually looked at a
flawed app and said the right thing.

The architecture decision came first, and it was a decision about where *not* to cut. The dispatch's
detached model-swap driver is the most safety-hardened code in the fleet — a never-zero teardown
that restores the 14B on every path, every step BaseException-guarded. The tempting reading of the
plan was to teach that driver to interleave a third and fourth model (the image model, the VLM) into
its swap sequence. I declined. The per-task build loop — where "build, look, fix, rebuild" naturally
lives — is in the fleet's `new-agent-task.ps1`, not the driver. So the critique loop lives there,
driven by a pure, fully-tested BlarAI-side core, and the driver's invariant is left untouched. The
trade-off I accepted: the asset-generation phase (which genuinely needs the 30B swapped *out*, since
Playground + 30B was the measured 32.5 GB breach) stays a separate, OVMS-gated step rather than a
driver task-kind. The critique, by contrast, needs no swap at all — during a dispatch the 14B is
already gone, so the VLM (~5 GB) co-resides with the 30B (~18 GB) under the ceiling.

The render proof was the good kind of surprise. WinUI 3 Desktop won't render an element that was
never in a live visual tree — `RenderTargetBitmap` returns 0x0 if you try to be clever and never
show a window. The honest answer is minimal-visibility, not zero-visibility: create the window,
`SetWindowPos` it to (-32000, -32000) so it lives off every monitor, `ShowWindow` with
`SW_SHOWNOACTIVATE` so the compositor runs but focus never moves, then render. I built the seed,
ran `App.exe --render-to-file out.png`, and got `RENDER-OK: 57x82`, exit 0, no window on screen, the
active foreground untouched. The 57x82 is just the empty seed's tiny content — but the *mechanism*
works, which means the loop can run unattended overnight without ever grabbing the operator's screen.
That was the de-risk the whole phase hinged on.

Then the part that earns its place in this file. The operator told me, mid-build, that the rocket
calculators sitting in `projects/` are flawed outputs — not finished apps. Which made one of them the
perfect test: I captured a real screenshot of the "Space Rocket Calculator" (a garish solid-red
background, a keypad, a blank display) and ran the *real* `critique_screenshot` against it with
Qwen3-VL loaded on CPU. Fifty-two seconds later it came back `NEEDS_WORK` with: *"the bright red
background is visually overwhelming and clashes with the yellow and orange buttons, creating a
garish, unprofessional appearance… the button layout appears misaligned and cramped… the number
display area is completely blank."* It caught the exact flaws a human would. The loop signal fired.
That is the whole thesis of Phase 3 proven on real data: the small coder can't invent design, but a
vision model can *see* when the design is wrong and hand back something the coder can act on.

The failure that stays in: the foreground-capture fallback (Tier 2) shipped broken and the tests
didn't catch it. Its inline `Add-Type` references `System.Drawing`, which on PowerShell 7 forwards
through an endless assembly cascade (`System.Drawing.Common` → `Primitives` → `Private.Windows.GdiPlus`
…) and fails to compile at runtime — but the PS unit tests *stubbed* the capture, so 63 green tests
sailed past a runtime compile error. Classic mock-passes-prod-crashes. The fix is to run the GDI
capture under Windows PowerShell 5.1, which still has `System.Drawing` natively; the deeper fix is a
test that actually exercises the compile path so a stub can never hide it again. Tier 1 (headless)
works, so the loop degrades correctly regardless — but the lesson is the test, not the workaround.

Everything is DORMANT: the critique core is merged with no live caller, the render entry lives only
in the seed, the loop module is unwired. The components are built and the two hard things —
render, and the VLM seeing a real flaw — are live-proven. What remains is the wiring that fires the
loop inside a real dispatch (the `new-agent-task.ps1` hook + threading the visual criteria from the
acceptance spec to the fleet), and the OVMS-gated asset-generation swap. Those are go-live steps.

**Lesson 174.** *A stubbed test of a shell-out or a compiled-inline type proves the wiring, not
the runtime — exercise the real compile/exec path at least once.* 63 green PowerShell tests hid a
`System.Drawing`-on-PS7 `Add-Type` compile failure because every test stubbed the capture. The cheap
guard is one un-stubbed test that actually compiles the type / runs the command on the target shell.

**Next:** wire the loop into the live dispatch — the `new-agent-task.ps1` post-merge hook calling
`critique-loop.ps1`, fed by visual criteria threaded from `acceptance.visual_criteria_texts` through
the queue task to the fleet; then the OVMS-gated ASSETS swap (asset-gen up front, unload-and-confirm
before the 30B reloads). The first real dispatch that builds an app, screenshots it, and iterates on
the VLM's feedback is the on-hardware go-live.

*(Phase 3 integration: live-proved the headless `--render-to-file` render [exit 0, no focus steal]
and the real `critique_screenshot` on a real flawed app [Qwen3-VL CPU: load 4 s, critique 52 s,
correctly flagged the garish design]; perf in `docs/performance/qwen3-vl-8b-int4-cpu-critique-2026-06-25.json`.
Agent-built render mechanism + critique-loop module [agentic-setup]; the critique core merged earlier
today [`fd85db3`]. Dormant — no live caller wired.)*

### 2026-06-25 — The seam the unit tests couldn't see

The VLM design loop's auto-FIX was the last limb of Phase 3: after the coder
builds and the app merges, render it, let Qwen3-VL critique it against the
request's visual criteria, and — if the critique says it's rough — feed that
feedback back to the coder for a better version, bounded, over and over. I had
137 PowerShell unit tests green on the loop logic, 75 on the capture, 40 on the
critique brain. By the usual bar it was done. The Lead Architect's instruction
was sharper than the usual bar: *"test end-to-end too, not just unit tests. test
the seams to make sure the flow crosses them properly."* So I stopped trusting the
green checkmarks and ran the whole thing live on the Arc 140V, a real dispatch
styling a real calculator. Two bugs surfaced that not one of those 252 tests
could have caught, and both lived exactly on a seam.

The first was the capture. In every unit test and the earlier render spike the
WinUI app rendered headless in about a second. In the live run the `[6/6]`
critique tried to render the just-built app and the call timed out at 30s, fell
through to the foreground tier, failed there too, and degraded — correctly, by
design — to a structural-only check. No pixel critique, no iteration. The render
wasn't broken: rebuilt warm it still did 784x529 in one second. What the unit
tests never modelled was *the resident-30B memory and GPU pressure*. At `[6/6]`
the 30B coder is still loaded and free RAM is ~5 GB; a cold App.exe's first
WinUI-compositor init under that pressure stalled past a 30s budget that had only
ever been measured on an idle box. The fix was small — 90s and a warm-up retry,
the second attempt being warm — but the lesson is that a timeout tuned on an idle
machine is a different control on a loaded one.

The second was the one worth keeping. With the capture fixed, the re-run got
further: the render succeeded, Qwen3-VL loaded co-resident with the 30B (the
memory fear didn't materialise — opencode exits post-merge and leaves room), the
critique returned *needs_work* against the strict criteria, the auto-FIX callback
fired — and threw: `Add-VisualFeedback is not recognized`. The whole design
iteration silently skipped, fail-soft, with a clean merge and a polite "open the
app and eyeball it." The callback couldn't see a function that is unmistakably
defined and dot-sourced. The cause is a PowerShell scope gotcha I had walked
straight into: the rebuild closure used `.GetNewClosure()` to freeze the
variables it captured ($wt, $OrigPrompt, the log path), which is reasonable — but
GetNewClosure rebinds the block's *function* lookup to the global scope. One level
deep — every unit test, run as `pwsh -File` — the script scope is reachable and it
works. The real fleet runs *two* levels deep (`run-fleet` calls `& new-agent-task.ps1`),
and there the dot-sourced fleet-lib functions live in a nested script scope the
global-bound closure cannot see. The 137 unit tests passed because their mock
callbacks were defined in-scope and invoked one level deep — they never crossed
the boundary the production call crosses on every single run.

That is the textbook shape of the failure this journal exists to keep: the mock
passes and production crashes, and the gap between them is not logic but
*environment* — nesting depth, residency pressure, a scope the test harness never
reproduces. I'd have shipped this as "done, 137/137 green" without the
end-to-end mandate, and the design loop would have been dead on the first real
dispatch with nobody the wiser, because it failed soft. The fix is a plain
scriptblock — it binds to the script scope that has the functions, and the
captured vars are script-scope and stable between definition and the immediate
call below, so the freezing GetNewClosure bought was never needed. I locked it
two ways so it can't quietly return: a static guard (AF10 — no scriptblock in the
file is GetNewClosure'd) and a behavioural one (CB1 — a nested `&`-invoked child
proves a plain callback resolves a script-scope function while a GetNewClosure'd
one throws, reproducing the live bug). The suite went 137 → 140.

I went three rounds chasing the root cause — the one-level repro stubbornly
*worked*, which was itself the clue: the bug only exists at the nesting depth the
unit harness never uses. Matching the repro to production's actual two-level
invocation is what finally reproduced it, the same discipline that the
headless-swap measurement taught earlier this month — measure the scenario the
ground truth actually runs in, not the convenient one.

The clean re-run (seamtest3, both fixes in) then proved the whole cycle on the
Arc 140V, end to end: build → review (which itself iterated, FIX-FIRST then a
second pass) → merge → render → critique-0 *needs_work* → auto-FIX #1 → critique-1
→ auto-FIX #2 → critique-2 → auto-FIX #3, three passes each *applied + re-merged
+ verify=pass*, bounded at MaxIter 3, no "not recognized" anywhere. The
screenshots tell the story the report can't: critique-0's number display was a
small unbordered "0"; after FIX #1 it was large, bold, and panelled, and the
equals button had gone red → green for accent — both verbatim from the VLM's
feedback. By critique-2 it was still reworking the palette. The VLM never
declared itself satisfied — it returned a detailed, specific critique at every
pass (display border, right-alignment, grid spacing, the DEL button's lack of
distinction) and the loop stopped only because the budget ran out, not because a
machine called the design done. That is the design working as intended: the VLM
is a loop *signal* that catches real, nameable flaws and drives the coder to
address them; the operator's eyeball remains the only thing that says "ship it."

**Next:** the loop is genuinely demonstrated, not just unit-asserted — the branch
merges to the dispatch main. Going live behind the operator's UC-010 ceremony
stays unchanged; this is the fleet-side design tool, dormant until the
`-EnableVisualCritique` opt-in. The natural follow-on is tuning MaxIter and the
"good enough to stop" threshold against more real tasks, and feeding the same
loop the *code* review findings (already proven to iterate) and the design
findings from one place.

**Lesson 175.** *Unit-green is not seam-green.* A test that mocks the
callback and runs one level deep cannot catch a failure that lives in scope
binding, nesting depth, or residency pressure. When a control's behaviour depends
on its environment — how deeply it's invoked, what else is resident, a timeout
tuned on an idle box — only an end-to-end run at the real depth and real load
crosses the seam. Mandate the end-to-end pass for any loop with a callback or a
resource-contended step; the green checkmarks from in-scope mocks are necessary,
not sufficient.

*(agentic-setup commits `279b41a` (auto-FIX + capture warm-up retry), `35f3b6c`
(GetNewClosure->plain fix + AF10/CB1 regression locks; verify-critique-loop 140/0,
verify-capture 75/0). Live-proof run on the Arc 140V: 3 auto-FIX passes all
applied + re-merged + verify=pass, MaxIter-bounded, critique-0/1/2 PNGs captured
the visual progression. BlarAI critique brain shipped `5eff0b7`; render+seam proof
in the sibling 2026-06-25 fragments.)*

### 2026-06-26 — The live test that found four real bugs the theory had explained away, and taking the unreliable judge out of the path

The brief said: build the critical coding loop and the critical design loop, and live-test the
dormant Phase-3 design loop on hardware. The honest arc is that the live test did most of the
teaching, and almost everything it taught contradicted a confidently-held prior hypothesis.

I reproduced the Phase-3 design loop end-to-end first: a themed kid-friendly space landing page
dispatched to the 30B with six visual criteria, so the end-of-run choreography (unload the 30B,
screenshot the page with the GPU free, run the 8B VLM critique, feed it back to the 30B) would have
something to look at. It parked. Debugging, it parked again, differently. Across those runs the
system surfaced four real defects, and the prior session's whole framing for the design loop's
flakiness — "headless msedge silently fails under concurrent OVMS GPU load" — was wrong on every
count.

The capture never produced a pixel, even with the GPU completely free. Two compounding bugs:
`msedge --screenshot` silently no-ops (exit 0, no file) when `--hide-scrollbars` is present or when
`--window-size` precedes `--screenshot` — the invocation had both — and, deeper, msedge's launcher
process exits 0 immediately and hands the screenshot to a detached worker that writes the PNG about
four seconds later, so the code's "check the file the instant the process exits" always missed it
and fell through to a WinUI-oriented structural floor that doesn't even understand web. I pinned both
empirically (variants A/E/F wrote 0 bytes; B/D wrote ~140 KB; variant G, the Start-Process form,
wrote the file 4 s after `WaitForExit` returned exit 0). The fix is to poll for the file, not trust
the process. With it the capture worked first try, and the VLM correctly reported "the rocket is
missing — only a star, not a rocket" against criterion 2: a real defect, exactly the signal the loop
exists to produce.

But the design loop only fires on a merged app, and the dispatch kept refusing to merge green,
test-passing work. The blocker was the same each time and it sat in the critical path before the
merge: the per-pass 30B self-review. On the first run it went sticky FIX-FIRST and then timed out;
on the re-run the 30B simply stopped generating mid-review (ovms output idle 215→287 s, with no
idle-timeout to kill it). A read of its 614-line transcript showed it ignoring its own "read-only,
don't run tests" instruction — re-exploring the whole repo, running the tests, trying to start the
server — and the prompt was asking it to "find problems" and "suggest a fix as a snippet," which the
2026 literature (arXiv 2603.00539) names as the framing that makes a reviewer over-correct
conforming code. The model wasn't malfunctioning; it was doing exactly what an over-engaged,
mis-prompted, same-model reviewer does.

The judgment call, which the operator endorsed: stop trying to merge *over* an unreliable signal and
take it out of the path. The deterministic gate (build + tests + verify, soon + property-based tests
+ mutation) drives the merge; when the gates are green the per-pass self-review is skipped entirely
(a pure, tested `Test-ShouldRunReview`), and the cross-model 14B critic reviews once, post-merge,
where a swap is already cheap. The review still runs where it actually decides the merge — build-only
ecosystems like WinUI with no behavioral test gate — and for that case I rewrote the prompt
research-informed (severity taxonomy, report only CRITICAL/MAJOR, test-aware skepticism — "the gate
is green, so NO DEFECTS is the common correct answer" — a binary production-malfunction gate, no
proposing fixes). The operator's instinct was the sharp one: don't just route around the broken
signal, fix the signal.

Two smaller defects fell out of the same runs. The harness false-doomed the long GPU-bound review
because its progress detector watched the journal/run-fleet/ovms logs and the coder's CPU but not
the review log (a GPU-bound review reads as idle CPU) — fixed by adding the review/design/swap-progress
log mtimes to the progress signals, with a mutation-guard test that the new signals never mask a
genuine stall. And the 30B opened a foreground browser (`start http://localhost:3000`) mid-build,
which the command-timeout plugin now rewrites to a no-op.

The through-line: the project's master principle — deterministic execution is the judge, the LLM is
a signal — is usually stated about whether to trust the model's verdict. The lesson this session
paid for is that it is also about *where the signal sits*. An unreliable judge in the critical path
blocks good work even when you have correctly demoted it to "just a signal," because the path still
waits on it. Move it off the path.

**Next:** the in-flight green-gates re-run (p5) should reach the design phase for the first time;
then the rest of the coding loop — the property-based + sampled-mutation gate (tools cached offline),
the 14B `_critic_phase` swap, two-clock timing on the new steps — and the deterministic geometry/CLIP
anchor for the design loop, before folding to main with the operator's flips preserved.

*(commits: agentic-setup `e35fd57`/`9550200` capture, `01f046b` merge-on-green, `6aced8f`
browser-hygiene, `85163f2` skip-review-on-green + critic.md, `f283f2e` review.md; blarai `9957a25`
harness-doom, `30a408f` spec-blind PBT. Verified: merge-decision 27/27, Test-ShouldRunReview 8/8,
184 blarai acceptance+harness. Vikunja #687.)*

### 2026-06-26 — The critic that costs nothing when silent

The cross-model 14B code critic is now wired into the swap driver as `_critic_phase`. It fires post-merge, before the VLM design loop, and reviews the 30B's merged diff through the 14B's eyes — the model that has no stake in the 30B's choices. The whole build is DORMANT: `_noop_critic` is the default, and the calling sequence for every existing test is byte-identical to before the commit.

The design principle that made this tractable: the critic does NOT call `stop_ovms()`. The live `real_run_critic` loads the 14B via `start-llm.ps1 -Force` internally, which replaces whatever is in OVMS (typically the 30B) without the driver needing to know. That one decision kept the legacy call-sequence tests green without modification: `_noop_critic` records nothing, so the existing exact-list assertions see the same `["load", ("task", ...), ..., "disarm", "stop", "restart"]` they always have. The design phase IS the reason for the `stop_ovms()` calls — it unloads the 30B to free the GPU for the headless capture. The critic doesn't need that; it runs a completable agent session against whatever model is loaded.

The NEVER-ZERO discipline is structural here too: `_critic_phase` is wrapped in `BaseException` just like `_design_phase`, any fix-lap task that raises is swallowed at the outer level, and the cancel/budget guards fire at phase entry and at each iteration boundary so an operator cancel skips the critic cleanly.

The signal/gate distinction matters. The critic is post-merge — the deterministic gate already decided. `should_iterate` from the critic is a SIGNAL: the fix lap runs, but the operator can still look at what happened. `critic_signal` is stored on `SwapDriverResult` the same way `design_signal` is: the last result dict, not a verdict that controls the machine's destiny. The machine always converges to 14B-up; the critic's verdict informs the next human look, not the teardown.

On the ops side: `real_run_critic` loads the 14B, confirms the port is up, runs `critic-run.ps1` via `_run_to_logfile` (never a captured pipe — the #670 run-2 deadlock lesson), reads the verdict from the log, and returns the dict fail-soft on any failure. The `_parse_critic_result` function scans for the LAST `VERDICT: MERGE` or `VERDICT: FIX FIRST` line (last-wins in case the critic reasons aloud before concluding), collects numbered blockers that follow, and returns `None` (fallback) if no verdict is found. The fallback is `{"should_iterate": False, "verdict": "UNCLEAR", "findings": "critic unavailable"}` — same shape as `_noop_critic`, so the driver's critic phase is symmetrically fail-soft at every layer.

The critic script (`critic-run.ps1`) runs the git diff in PowerShell (since `critic.md` has `bash: deny`) and embeds the diff in the prompt, truncated at 8000 chars with a Read-tool note for large files. The critic agent gets what it needs without running any command itself.

What I accepted in exchange: a fix lap after a FIX FIRST verdict runs the 30B again, which means the 14B is loaded by the critic, then the 30B reloads for the fix, then the 14B reloads for the re-critique. Three model swaps per iteration. The operator accepted this tradeoff explicitly when approving the design (#687): the cross-model bias removal is worth the extra swap overhead on the rare path where the critic finds a real blocker.

**Next:** the critic is DORMANT pending go-live (task 3 / two-clock timing, then task 7 Phase B merge). The live activate step is: register `run_critic=...` in `build_swap_ops` (already done — it will fire automatically when the driver runs) and confirm a critic pass end-to-end on the first real dispatch.

*(commits `d2d00ef` blarai worktree + `5b815e1` agentic-setup; 12 new critic-phase tests + 1 DORMANT-safe regression lock; 131/131 swap tests green, all legacy tests unchanged.)*

### 2026-06-26 — Moving the design critique to where the GPU is actually free

The dispatch's post-merge design critique was running per-task, while the 30B
coder was still resident on the Arc 140V. The headless `msedge` capture that
feeds the VLM needs the GPU, and under that contention it failed *silently* —
so every web/GUI app fell through to a structural floor and the critique was
skipped. The design loop was blind on exactly the surfaces (web, desktop-gui)
it exists to judge. The operator's call was unambiguous: better to wait for the
models to swap and have a working process than a fast one that fails constantly.

So the critique now runs as an END-OF-RUN phase (`swap_driver._design_phase`,
new `PHASE_DESIGN`), after the CODE loop, where it can unload the 30B itself and
hand the whole GPU to the headless capture + the in-process VLM. The teardown's
later `stop_ovms` becomes a harmless idempotent no-op. It only fires when a task
declares a visual surface WITH real `visual_criteria_json` (not `"[]"`) AND
something actually merged — a non-visual or empty run is a clean no-op, which is
why every pre-existing exact-call-list test stayed byte-stable (their tasks carry
no `surface`).

Two judgment calls are worth keeping. First, mid-build the scope was corrected:
the phase had to CLOSE THE LOOP, not just record a one-shot signal. So it is a
bounded `critique -> feed the feedback back to the coder -> fix -> re-critique`
loop (`max_design_iterations`, default 2). Each lap genuinely swaps models —
`stop_ovms` to critique, then `load_30b`+`wait_ready` to run one coder fix task
whose prompt *embeds the reviewer's feedback verbatim*. That per-lap swap latency
is the deliberately-accepted cost of a process that works over one that thrashes
a contended GPU. The VLM `should_iterate` DRIVES the loop but is never the
verdict — every progress line is phrased as the reviewer's suggestion ("open the
app and judge for yourself"); the operator's eyeball is the verdict, the same
loop-signal-not-verdict rule `critique.py` already documents.

Second — and this is the one that paid for itself — I started to attach the
design note to the merged task's `TaskOutcome.detail` so the cumulative
`SUMMARY.txt` would carry it. Then I actually read `_classify_result`: it
keyword-scans the RESULT line, and "blocked"/"not merged" *anywhere* on it flips
the classification. Appending free-text VLM feedback into a line that the
harness/gateway RE-PARSE at swap-back is precisely the #686 cumulative-summary
shape bug, re-armed. So I took the brief's sanctioned alternative — a dedicated
`SwapDriverResult.design_signal` field plus the restart-surviving progress trail
(which `/dispatch status` already shows) — and left the frozen `TaskOutcome`
untouched. The operator still gets the full signal; the parser can't choke on it.

The whole phase is wrapped to swallow any `BaseException` — a failed
capture/critique/reload/fix breaks the loop cleanly and the NEVER-ZERO 14B
restore is unconditionally reached. The live `real_run_design_loop` invokes
`critique-loop.ps1` through the logfile-redirect runner (not a captured pipe a
stray msedge grandchild could deadlock, the #670 run-2 lesson), single-quote-
escapes the operator's goal/criteria into the PowerShell command, and fail-softs
to "design critique unavailable" on any error. Still DORMANT — no live caller
reads `design_signal` and `[fleet_dispatch].enabled` is untouched.

**Next:** the LA's on-hardware go-live step — flip the dispatch enabled, run a
real web/GUI dispatch, and watch one capture+critique+fix lap actually land on
the Arc 140V (the live capture is the one thing the injected-fake tests can't
prove). If `max_design_iterations` wants to be operator-tunable from
`default.toml` rather than a driver default, that's a thin spec-thread follow-up.

*(commit on `worktree-agent-af9edd184715bd0c2`: `swap_state.PHASE_DESIGN`,
`swap_ops.real_run_design_loop` + `build_swap_ops` wiring,
`swap_driver._design_phase` bounded loop + `SwapOps.run_design_loop` /
`SwapDriverResult.design_signal` / `max_design_iterations`; +17 tests
(10 driver, 7 ops); test_swap_driver + test_swap_ops 104/0, full
swap/fleet/dispatch/critique/acceptance sweep 659 passed / 3 env-skip / 0 failed.
Live capture awaits the LA go-live.)*

### 2026-06-26 — The "done" signal that fired once per task

The capstone the day before had run the whole dispatch pipeline live — mutual-TLS to the AO,
the 14B planning, the 14B→30B swap, a real build — and then reported PARKED and exited while a
*second* task was still being built in the detached swap driver, with the 14B not yet restored.
I saw the harness say "done," went to look, found the orphaned build, and killed it — which is
the worst possible move, because the swap-back was about to fire on its own. The harness's
premature report is what made me interfere with a run that would have finished cleanly.

The root cause is a classic "completion signal that isn't terminal." A multi-task decomposition
doesn't run one `run-fleet` over an N-task queue — the swap driver calls `run-fleet` once *per
task*, and each invocation overwrites `state/fleet-runs/<id>/SUMMARY.txt` with only that task's
result. The headless harness's `RunMonitor` keyed COMPLETE off a single predicate — *does
SUMMARY.txt exist?* — so it returned the instant the first task's summary appeared, oblivious to
the tasks behind it and to the swap-back ahead of it. A per-step artifact was being read as a
whole-run terminal signal.

The fix is to make the harness wait for the *actual* terminal signal, which already exists: the
swap-state phase. The driver writes a write-ahead `{run_id, phase}` record through RESERVE →
LOAD-30B → CODE → UNLOAD-30B → RESTART-AO → RECOVERED, and the monitor was already reading that
phase — but only for a diagnostic label. Now COMPLETE requires both a SUMMARY *and* a terminal
phase (RECOVERED/FAILED/CANCELLED/IDLE — every task processed and the 14B back). Two corollaries
fell out of letting the monitor actually reach the end of a run for the first time. First, the
doom detector (stop a stalled coder fast) must only fire during the CODE phase — the swap
setup/teardown phases have no coder, so a quiet log there is the driver winding down, not a
doomed run; without that guard the monitor would have started shooting the swap-back it was now
finally waiting for. Second, after it *does* issue a stop (a doomed coder or the overall
timeout), it keeps polling until the swap reaches a terminal phase before returning — so the
harness never reports "done" mid-swap-back on that path either, bounded so a wedged restore can't
hang it. The empty phase string — a dry-run with no swap pipeline at all — stays
SUMMARY-authoritative, so the fake-AO test path is unchanged.

What I'm keeping: the swap-back itself was never the bug. The detached driver's `finally` restores
the 14B unconditionally, after every task, regardless of whether the harness is still watching —
that part was already robust. The bug was purely that the harness *reported* the run finished
before it was, and a confident-but-early "done" is dangerous precisely because a human (or an
orchestrating agent) acts on it. A completion signal has to be the thing that's true once, at the
end — not the thing that's true after every step.

**Live-verified, and the verification earned its keep — three times over.** A solo palindrome
dispatch confirmed the harness reports COMPLETE only after the swap-back ("14B is back", 72 polls /
463s, MERGED) — the old harness exited at the first per-task SUMMARY, minutes earlier. But getting
there surfaced two more defects that only a real run shows. First, a crashed run (an OOM — I had
foolishly run a memory-heavy test gate in parallel with the 24 GB 30B; lesson: never run heavy work
beside a resident large model) reached a terminal swap phase having written NO cumulative SUMMARY,
and my first fix (COMPLETE needs a SUMMARY *and* a terminal phase) HUNG the harness — so a terminal
phase now completes with or without a SUMMARY (outcome NONE if none). Second, the swap-state file is
per-box, so a stale prior-run RECOVERED could falsely complete a fresh run the instant it started —
so the monitor now only trusts a phase whose run_id matches. Then the *successful* run surfaced a
third: the swap teardown's cumulative SUMMARY used a different shape than the per-task one, so the
parser the harness + gateway share classified the merged run as NONE — the writer now emits the
canonical shape. Three bugs, none of which a mock would ever have shown; the live run was the test
that mattered.

**Next:** fold this fragment into `BUILD_JOURNAL.md` at the next quiet point and lift the proposed
lesson into the numbered list.

*(commits: `c9945fd` monitor phase-aware completion + doom-only-in-CODE + swap-back wait; `1f17dd7`
terminal-phase-without-summary + run_id-scope the swap-state; `a33cf51` cumulative SUMMARY uses the
run-fleet shape so the shared parser reads it. 126 swap_ops + fleet_dispatch + dispatch-harness tests
green; two live palindrome dispatches — the harness waited for the swap-back and reported MERGED.
Vikunja #686.)*

### 2026-06-26 — The system that parked good work, and learning to watch it

I went into this thinking the dispatch's problem was code quality — the 30B builds a
compiling app but it isn't good enough. Three live dispatches (a tip calculator, a
themed landing page, a CSV-to-JSON CLI) proved the opposite, and the inversion is the
whole lesson: the system parks *good, building, verify-passing* work on *transient*
events, not quality failures. The tip calculator's create-app task built a working app
with eight passing tests — and parked because the review agent hit its 10-minute clock.
The landing page built clean and the reviewer said MERGE — and parked because the coder
couldn't `rm` its own temp file (the headless sandbox auto-rejects "ask" prompts), spun
to the idle circuit-breaker, and that process flag vetoed the merge the reviewer had
already approved. The CLI parked because the 14B over-decomposed one cohesive tool into
seven tasks it couldn't finish in the budget. Every park was a clock or a stall, never a
flaw. The fear was the wrong fear: the thing was too conservative, not too lax.

So the fixes follow one principle — the deterministic gates (build, tests) are the judge;
a review timeout or a coder circuit-breaker speaks to the *process*, not the work, and
must not park what the gates and the reviewer vouch for. `Test-ShouldMerge` now separates
hard blocks (no-changes / secret / test-fail / verify-fail / an explicit FIX-FIRST — always
park) from soft process signals (timeout / circuit-breaker — never park on their own). I
let the coder manage files in its disposable worktree (the merge-gate is the real backstop;
sensitive paths and `rm -rf /*` stay denied — and that one needed the LA's explicit sign-off,
because widening an autonomous agent's destructive permissions is a governance call a
classifier rightly blocked until he approved it). And the quiet killer: the 30B ignores the
"never run a foreground server" instruction and hung `node src/server.js` for the full
five-minute command cap, repeatedly — so the instruction is now *enforced* by capping
server commands to a ten-second probe. The proof is the re-run: the create-app task that
parked twice merged cleanly, all three tasks, thirteen passing tests I ran myself.

The methodology lesson cut deeper than the code. I'd fired a dispatch and waited for the
whole-run signal — and a per-task park sat unnoticed for twelve minutes while I watched the
wrong thing. The operator caught it: *"task 1 was already finished and parked again yet you
didn't automatically do anything. very wasteful."* He was right. The logs were never
missing — each `.agent.log` is a complete event stream of every command and its output —
I just wasn't watching them. So I built `watch.py`: a readable live feed of the coder's
every action plus each per-task verdict, with `--stop-on-park` that trips the cancel
sentinel the instant a task parks. Fire-and-wait-ninety-minutes became watch-and-react-in-
seconds. The trade-off I'm keeping on the record: loosening the merge gate to trust green
deterministic gates over process hiccups *does* merge work a timed-out reviewer never fully
vetted — I accepted that because the tests are real and the operator opens the app, and
because the alternative (parking good work) is the failure he actually hit. An explicit
negative review and every security block still park, always.

**Next:** the critical-loops build is handed to a fresh session (`docs/handoffs/critical-loops-handoff-brief.md`,
research at `docs/research/agentic-code-design-quality-loops-2026-06.md`, Vikunja #687) — a
rigorous coding loop (property + mutation testing as the real gate; a *cross-model* 14B critic
instead of the 30B grading itself; spec-blind independent tests; bounded execution-grounded
fixes) and the design loop refined the same way, with the two-clock timing model (progress +
ceiling) at every level. *(commits `52c8f51` (blarai: F5 + watcher + Phase-3 + research),
`2c2c691` (agentic-setup: F1/F2/F3/F6 + command-timeout + F4); dispatch tests 133/0,
merge-decision 23/23; A's re-run MERGED 13/13; Phase 3 dormant, live GPU capture awaits.)*

### 2026-06-26 — The lever I'd built three reviewers to avoid finding

The critical-loops session ended in a good place — the design loop fired end-to-end on the p6
page, clean work merged, everything landed on main — and then the operator asked the question
that mattered more than any of the fixes: is this thing fundamentally flawed, is there
engineering we're missing, or are the local models simply not smart enough? He told me to use
my expertise and trusted sources, not my impressions. So I did the homework — two independent
research passes, every headline number re-fetched against primary sources — and the answer
reframed the whole effort.

It was all three of his hypotheses at once, in a specific and uncomfortable ratio. The
*philosophy* was right: a deterministic build/test gate as the final judge, with the LLM and the
VLM as signals, is the rare asset that makes a weak local model usable at all — most systems
lack it, and we'd built it well. The *models* are a genuine, length-dependent ceiling: a ~30B
coder that fits in 31 GB builds a simple app end-to-end only 15-35% of the time, single digits
once it's multi-feature, and the success rate falls roughly as p^N with the number of steps —
METR measured ~100% on tasks under four minutes collapsing to under 10% past four hours. The
"drew a star instead of a rocket, then spun for sixty minutes" failure I'd been treating as a
harness bug was textbook: a capability-ceiling hit followed by the exact thing weak models are
worst at — recovering from their own mistakes. But the part that stung was the engineering
finding. We had poured effort into the *review* side — a 30B self-review, a cross-model 14B
critic, a VLM design loop, three review surfaces — and the evidence is blunt that this is the
saturated half: METR clocked added scaffolding at +8 points, statistically insignificant, while
the single highest-leverage lever for a weak model, best-of-N parallel sampling with the gate as
the selector, sat completely unpulled on top of the verifier we'd already paid to build. A
*weaker* open model than ours went from 15.9% to 56% on SWE-bench Lite just by sampling more and
letting tests pick the winner — beating the frontier's single-shot score. And locally the usual
"N× the cost" objection evaporates: the marginal sample is electricity, not API dollars.

The trade-off I put to the operator, and he accepted: re-weight from the review side to the
generation side. The alternatives I named and we rejected were real ones — keep refining the
critics (the data says that's the saturated half), accept the ceiling and stop (abandons the
capability he wants), or drop the local constraint for this feature (abandons the entire point of
BlarAI). What we chose instead: build best-of-N to *replace* the serial retry loop — because that
loop asks the weak model to self-correct, its worst skill, where N independent fresh attempts
route around the weakness entirely — feed the spec-blind acceptance tests to the coder as input
rather than only as the gate, right-size the task envelope to short checkpointed steps, evaluate a
purpose-built agentic coder at INT8, and freeze the review side. The thing that makes this more
than a tactical pivot is what compounds: generation-coverage-plus-verification rides the model
capability curve upward as local models improve (METR has that reliable-task horizon doubling
about every seven months), where another reviewer never would. So the recent critic and VLM work
isn't wasted — they're cheap signals for what the gate can't judge — the *next* marginal build
just shouldn't be a fourth one.

**Next:** finish the #687 critical-loops queue (#6 design loop / #685 + #9 critic swap live-verify),
then the ACCEPTED generation-side re-weight — Vikunja epic #688, starting with best-of-N (#689).
Full cited analysis in `docs/research/dispatch-capability-and-leverage-assessment-2026-06.md`.

### 2026-06-26 — Shipping the proven thing live, not dormant-with-a-flip

The dispatch had been gate-green and proven end-to-end on the Arc 140V for sessions — natural-language
goal to 14B decompose to step-aside to 30B build to language/test gate to auto-merge to swap-back, run
after run — yet it still shipped `enabled = false`, with the operator carrying an uncommitted
`enabled = true` flip that I re-preserved on every single merge. He finally called it: *stop making the
mature functions dormant.* He was right, and the mistake is worth keeping.

"Ship dormant" is the correct default for code that is **unproven** or **security-sensitive** — it earned
its keep on the image-generation go-live (a one-time content attestation gated the flip) and on the
air-gap egress door (welded shut by the absolute-privacy mandate). But I had turned it into a blanket
reflex and applied it to a feature that demonstrably works. The cost wasn't hypothetical: the repo
*lied* about what functioned, and the operator had to babysit a working-tree flip indefinitely. The fix
was one commit — `fleet_dispatch.enabled = true` (`20c9553`), and I could land it with confidence because
I'd already measured the standing gate at 4550/0 *with that exact state in the tree*, so going live broke
nothing.

The distinction that should have been explicit from the start, and now is: **proven → live; unproven-on-
hardware → prove (live-verify) then live; air-gap/privacy egress → welded until the operator's deliberate
go-online ceremony.** Three buckets, not one. The dispatch was bucket one and should have been live long
ago. The 14B critic is bucket two — it has never run on hardware (its #9 live-verify is still pending), so
it stays env-gated only until that proves out. The web-search/URL-ingest egress is bucket three — it stays
shut by the operator's own privacy mandate, released by the Kagi-key ceremony he's already decided on, not
by my caution.

**Next:** prove the 14B critic on hardware (#9) and then ship it live; release the egress at the operator's
Kagi-key go-online ceremony — both bucketed, neither left dormant by default.

### 2026-06-27 — The four bugs the green suite swore weren't there

The critical-loops code went to `main` already proven by units: pixel_lint 25/25 plus 5 killed
mutants, verify-critique-loop 193/193, the swap diagnostic green, the standing gate at 4250/0. By
every test I had, #6 (the deterministic design gate) and #9 (the cross-model 14B critic) and #8
(the build-only review) were done. Then I ran them on the hardware, and the live verify found
**four real defects in a row** — every one of them invisible to a suite that was entirely green.

The first showed up the instant the first #9 dispatch tried to merge: every Python coding task
parked at *test collection* with `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'app'`. The seeded scaffold's
own test does `from app.core import …`, and its docstring cheerfully claims "the fleet runs pytest
with the project root on PYTHONPATH" — but the `pyproject.toml` never actually set `pythonpath`. The
gate runs the console-script `pytest` (via `uv run --no-project`), which, unlike `python -m pytest`,
does *not* put the cwd on `sys.path`. So the comment was aspirational and the config was a lie, and
no unit test would ever catch it because the test was *about the scaffold's intent*, not the gate's
behaviour. One line — `pythonpath = ["."]` — and the seed went from ModuleNotFoundError to 2-passed.

With Python merging, #9's critic finally swapped (coder-30b → qwen3-14b, both ways, clean) and the
ACTIVE/DORMANT diagnostic I'd added fired exactly as designed — proving the env had reached the
detached `swap_driver`, the thing the LA had specifically warned was a false-dormant trap. And the
critic produced… nothing useful: `agent "critic" not found, falling back to default` and `The diff
content is missing`. Two more bugs. The agent file existed in `configs/agents/` but had never been
*synced* to `~/.config/opencode/agents/` where opencode actually looks. And the diff command —
`git diff <base>...HEAD` — is *empty* when you run it post-merge on the base branch, because HEAD
